✓  A revitalized near-south neighborhood, formerly McRee Town ✓  Just north of the Missouri Botanical Garden, along Tower Grove Avenue ✓  Avg. rating: 4.5/5 across 8,957 reviews

## Botanical Heights Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Botanical Heights, St. Louis

Botanical Heights is a revitalized near-south St. Louis neighborhood — once known as McRee Town — that sits just north of the Missouri Botanical Garden, with Tower Grove Avenue running through it as a busy commercial spine of bakeries, fitness studios, and home-services companies.

Tucked into the near-south side of the City of St. Louis, Botanical Heights is a small but busy neighborhood of roughly four-tenths of a square mile. It is bounded by **Chouteau Avenue** to the north, **Interstate 44** to the south, **39th Street** to the east, and **Vandeventer Avenue** to the west. **Tower Grove Avenue** threads north to south through the heart of the neighborhood and carries much of its commercial life, while interior and edge streets such as McRee Avenue, Park Avenue, Blaine Avenue, and Central Industrial Drive fill in the rest.

For most of the twentieth century this place was called **McRee Town**. Built in the early 1900s, it began as a mixed-use district where small manufacturing and distribution businesses stood alongside rows of housing. The neighborhood thrived until the late 1960s, when the construction of Interstate 44 carved through the area and physically split it from the Shaw neighborhood to the south. That highway is still the southern edge of Botanical Heights today.

Through the later decades of the century, McRee Town slid into decline. Rising vacancy, absentee landlords, and crime took hold, and by 2000 roughly 56 percent of the area’s structures were vacant or deteriorated. The City of St. Louis described the neighborhood at its low point in stark terms, and many of its original residents — a community that by the 2000 Census was largely Black and heavily renter-occupied — were living amid serious disinvestment.

The turning point came through the **Garden District Commission**. In 1998, Jonathan Kleinbard founded this independent nonprofit, whose board drew from residents and institutions near the Missouri Botanical Garden, to steer a planned renovation. In 2000 the City adopted a redevelopment plan, and in 2004 and 2005 one of the most extensive land-clearance efforts seen in St. Louis in decades cleared six blocks of McRee Town. It was during this period that the neighborhood was rebranded **Botanical Heights**, taking its new name from the world-famous garden just to the south.

On the cleared blocks, **McBride & Sons** built roughly 143 to 150 new single-family homes and townhomes, priced from about $155,000 to $400,000 and all sold by 2007. Beginning around 2010, the developer **UIC** added contemporary modern infill housing west of Thurman Avenue, and adaptive-reuse projects followed — among them **Rockwell Beer Co.**, which opened in a former electrical-supply building on Vandeventer, and the **MaTovu Jewish Neighborhood Center**, which reopened in a rehabbed 1920s building in 2018. City Garden Montessori School also put down roots here, moving to Tower Grove Avenue in 2012.

The transformation has not been without debate. The Garden’s role in clearing the original housing stock has been criticized as gentrification, and that history is part of an honest picture of the neighborhood. What stands today is a centrally located, largely rebuilt community whose population has grown modestly since 2010 and whose corridors now host an eclectic mix of bakeries, a brewery, fitness and recording studios, home-services companies, and small makers. The blocks of the former McRee Town redevelopment, anchored by Tower Grove Avenue and shadowed by the Missouri Botanical Garden, are the setting for the businesses featured in this guide.

Like the rest of St. Louis, Botanical Heights has a humid subtropical climate with four distinct seasons. Summers are warm and humid, with July high temperatures averaging near 90°F, and the area sees about 41.7 inches of rain spread across the year. Spring and early summer bring the region’s severe-weather season, as St. Louis sits within the broader stretch of the country prone to tornadoes — a reminder that local roofs, trees, and outdoor businesses are built to weather sudden storms. 

Winters are cold but rarely extreme, with January high temperatures averaging around 40°F and roughly 16.6 inches of snow falling in a typical year. The mix of hot, stormy summers and chilly, sometimes icy winters shapes everyday life here, from the kinds of home-services calls Botanical Heights businesses field to the seasonal rhythm of foot traffic along Tower Grove Avenue.

A street scene in Botanical Heights. Photo: “Botanical Heights (6)” by Paul Sableman, licensed under CC BY 2.0 via Wikimedia Commons.

## Boulevard Heights — St. Louis Neighborhood

Neighborhood

Boulevard Heights

Coordinates

38.5598° N, 90.2854° W

Ranked by 2,889+ Google reviews. Updated June 2026.

✓  A brick-home neighborhood at the far-south edge of St. Louis ✓  Bordered by Carondelet Park, along the Gravois, Loughborough & Morganford corridors ✓  Avg. rating: 4.9/5 across 2,889 reviews

## Boulevard Heights Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Boulevard Heights, St. Louis

Boulevard Heights is one of the southernmost neighborhoods in the City of St. Louis — a quiet, residential pocket of brick homes near the city limits, bordered by Carondelet Park to the north and threaded by the Gravois, Loughborough, and Morganford corridors that hold its shops, restaurants, and local services.

Boulevard Heights sits at the far-south end of St. Louis, hard against the southern city limits. It is bounded by **Gravois Avenue** on the west, **Holly Hills Boulevard and Carondelet Park** on the north, **South Grand Avenue and I-55** on the east, and the city limits on the south. Covering roughly 1.5 square miles, it is a compact, almost entirely residential neighborhood whose commercial life clusters along its edges rather than its interior streets.

The land here carries an old name: **Morganford**. The portion east of Morganford Road was once part of the **Carondelet Commons** — the shared common-field lands of the village of Carondelet, which the City of St. Louis annexed in 1870. The broad, parkway-style streets along the north edge, including Holly Hills Boulevard, echo the “Heights and Boulevard” naming style that ran through many early-twentieth-century south-side subdivisions.

Americans and **German immigrants** began subdividing the area into residential lots in the early 1870s. That German heritage — shared across much of the south side — shows up in the neighborhood’s church history. **St. Lucas Evangelical Lutheran Church**, founded in 1905 at 7100 Morganford Road, was described as the first Slovak Lutheran church west of the Mississippi, anchoring a German and Slovak story that still threads through the area.

Though some building went up along Grand Avenue before 1910, **most of Boulevard Heights was built out after World War II**. That timing gave the neighborhood its defining look: a dense, steady fabric of mid-century **St. Louis brick homes** on quiet residential blocks. As one of the city’s southernmost neighborhoods, it meets the city limits and the River Des Peres corridor at the edge of St. Louis.

Parks frame the neighborhood on nearly every side. **Carondelet Park** — the city’s third-largest park, purchased in 1875, with its lakes and historic boathouse — borders Boulevard Heights to the north. **Willmore Park**, donated in 1947 and sitting just west across the River Des Peres, adds lakes, a paved loop, and an accessible playground. **Christy Park** brings recreational fields inside the area, and the regional **River Des Peres Greenway** follows the channel along the city’s southern edge.

Day-to-day commerce gathers on the corridors. **Gravois Avenue** runs the west edge, the **Loughborough and Morganford** pockets hold restaurants, dental and real-estate offices, and neighborhood shops, while interior and south-side streets such as Robert, Blow, Tennessee, and Eugene host trades and small studios. Just to the north sits the historic **Bevo Mill** landmark in the adjacent Bevo Mill neighborhood.

Today Boulevard Heights remains what its post-war buildout made it: a settled, family-oriented neighborhood of brick homes and tidy blocks, close to big parks and easy interstate access, with a working main-street life spread along its bordering avenues. The ten businesses below — ranked by Google rating — represent some of the most established and best-reviewed local options in and around the neighborhood.

The boathouse at Carondelet Park, which borders Boulevard Heights to the north. Photo: “Boathouse at Carondelet Park” by DiamondRemley39, licensed under CC BY-SA 4.0 via Wikimedia Commons.

Boulevard Heights shares the St. Louis humid subtropical climate, with four distinct seasons. Summers are warm and humid — July highs average near 90°F — making the shaded lakes and paved loops of Carondelet and Willmore parks a welcome retreat. Spring and fall are mild and comfortable, the easiest stretches for walking the River Des Peres Greenway or running errands along Loughborough and Morganford. 

Winters are cold, with January highs around 40°F and roughly 17 inches of snow in a typical year, and the metro sits within Tornado Alley, so spring storms are part of the calendar. The neighborhood’s sturdy post-war brick homes are well suited to the swing of seasons, holding up to summer heat and winter cold alike.

# Carondelet Neighborhood Guide – South St. Louis History, Parks & Businesses

## Discover Carondelet’s Parks, Landmarks, and Local Services

Updated May 2026.

## Carondelet Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

Carondelet, St. Louis

Carondelet serves as the city’s southernmost and most historically distinct riverfront neighborhood — founded in 1767 by French settler Clément Delor de Treget as an independent village three years after St. Louis itself, anchored by the 180-acre Carondelet Park (1875), the National Register-listed Des Peres School where Susan Blow opened America’s first public kindergarten in 1873, the Carnegie-funded 1908 Carondelet Branch Library with its 1934 WPA mural, the historic Sisters of St. Joseph Motherhouse (1836), and the bluff-top Bellerive Park overlooking the Mississippi River — offering restaurants, professional services, real estate, faith institutions, family services, and historic preservation trades for residents and businesses across the far-south St. Louis corridor.

Carondelet sits in the extreme southeastern corner of the City of St. Louis at **38.5581° N, 90.2798° W** in zip code **63111**. Its 1.45-square-mile footprint is bounded by the **Mississippi River** to the east, **River des Peres** to the southwest, **Interstate 55 and Grand Boulevard** to the west, and **Walsh and Eichelberger Streets** to the north. The neighborhood’s signature corridors are **South Broadway** (the north-south spine running from the river bluffs to downtown), **Michigan Avenue** (with its concentration of historic churches and the Carondelet Branch Library), **Virginia Avenue**, **Loughborough Avenue** (the east-west connector to Loughborough Commons and I-55), and **Bellerive Boulevard**. Carondelet is bordered by **Patch** to the south (originally part of Carondelet itself), **Holly Hills** and **Boulevard Heights** to the west, and **Mount Pleasant** and **Dutchtown** to the north. At the 2020 census, the neighborhood had **7,734 residents**. The racial makeup was 43.1% White, 39.3% Black, 0.5% Native American, 1.5% Asian, 9.9% Two or More Races, and 5.6% Some Other Race; 9.6% of the population was of Hispanic or Latino origin.

Carondelet’s history begins three years after St. Louis itself was founded. **Clément Delor de Treget**, a Frenchman born in Cahors in southern France, traveled upriver from Sainte Genevieve in **1767**, obtained a land grant from St. Louis Commandant Louis Saint Ange, built a stone house at what is now the foot of Elwood Street, and laid out what became **Delor’s Village**. Soon joined by other French settlers from Cahokia and Kaskaskia, the village was successively called **Louisbourg** (for King Louis XV/XVI), **Catalan’s Prairie** (for early settler Louis Catalan), and finally in **1794 Carondelet** — in honor of Baron François Louis Hector de Carondelet, the Fleming appointed Spanish governor of Louisiana, after Treget strategically flattered him to secure renewal of his militia captain’s commission. The village’s nickname **Vide Poche** (“Empty Pocket”) reflected both its inhabitants’ reputation for sending St. Louis visitors home with empty pockets after card games and horse races, and its earlier meaning in New France as “the mill.” Another nickname was **Pain de Sucre** (“Sugarloaf”), a reference to its bluff topography.

Delor laid out the **Carondelet Commonfields** from present-day Virginia Avenue west to Morganford Road, with a southern Commons of pasture land extending all the way to River des Peres and beyond, eventually stretching to a mile beyond what is now Jefferson Barracks under Lieutenant Governor Zenon Trudeau in 1796. The Spanish census of 1796 showed 181 citizens; by 1850 the population had grown to 1,265. Carondelet was **incorporated as a town** by the County Court on **August 27, 1832**, with its town hall at Bowen Street and Broadway under a large elm “meeting tree.” The State Legislature **incorporated Carondelet as an independent city on March 1, 1851**, with first mayor **Dr. William Taussig**, a Bohemian immigrant medical doctor. The village remained an independent municipality for nearly two decades before being **annexed by the City of St. Louis on April 4, 1870** — making Carondelet, along with the Patch (which was also originally part of Carondelet), home to some of the oldest housing stock in the entire city.

The 19th century turned Carondelet into a major **industrial and shipbuilding town**. The Iron Mountain Railroad brought iron ore from Potosi and Pilot Knob to Carondelet, where the **Carondelet Marine and Dock** and a dozen other ironworks turned it into pig iron and then steel. Most consequentially, during the American Civil War, engineer **James B. Eads** (who would later build the 1874 Eads Bridge) constructed a number of **Union ironclad gunboats** at Carondelet shipyards along the Mississippi — one of which, the **USS Carondelet**, was named for the village and proved crucial to the Union naval campaign on western rivers. Loyalties during the war were split: older French inhabitants generally favored the Confederacy while the more recently arrived Germans were Republican Northern sympathizers. Henry T. Blow led the local pro-Union effort; Captain John S. Bowen rallied Southern sympathizers and later died from wounds sustained at Vicksburg.

Carondelet produced one of America’s most consequential 19th-century families. **Henry Taylor Blow** was a U.S. Representative from Missouri, U.S. Ambassador to Venezuela and Brazil, and President of the Iron Mountain Railroad. His family had been among the former owners of the slave **Dred Scott** and supported Scott’s freedom suit; in **1857 Henry T. Blow emancipated Scott and his wife Harriet** after once again becoming their owner. Blow donated land and funds for schools, churches, and civic institutions throughout Carondelet, including the original Blow School (designed by William B. Ittner and rebuilt in 1904 as today’s **Lyon at Blow School**). His daughter **Susan Elizabeth Blow**, who had studied Friedrich Fröbel’s methods in Germany, founded what is regarded as **the first continuous public kindergarten in the United States** at the **Des Peres School** (6307 Michigan Avenue) in **September 1873** in arrangement with St. Louis Schools Superintendent William T. Harris. The Des Peres School building is on the National Register of Historic Places and today houses the **Carondelet Historical Society**, restored to its 1870s appearance at a cost of $2 million.

Carondelet’s religious and cultural fabric is exceptional. The **Sisters of St. Joseph** arrived from France in **1836**, opened the first school in Carondelet, and built their Motherhouse on the bluffs along Minnesota Avenue (a designated City Landmark dating to 1840-era origins). From this single convent emerged **Fontbonne University**, **St. Joseph’s Academy**, and the **St. Joseph Institute for the Deaf**. The **Saints Mary and Joseph Chapel** at 6304 Minnesota Avenue, on a hilltop site set aside for church purposes by Delor himself in 1767, traces its line back to the 1775 log Chapel of St. Francis Xavier and the 1819 Our Lady of Mount Carmel and St. Joseph of the Angels church. The twin-steepled **St. Anthony of Padua Church** (1910) on Meramec Street is a major south-city landmark. **St. Paul’s Episcopal Church** on Michigan Avenue (1868-1870) and the **Carondelet Christian Church** round out the Protestant tradition, and the 1869 former North Public Market is now home to the **Quinn Chapel AME Church** (added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1974).

Public green space is anchored by **Carondelet Park**, opened on the centennial of American Independence on **July 4, 1876** on land formerly held by Alexander Lacey Lyle — whose 1850s house (the **Lyle Mansion**) still stands within the park as the city’s oldest building still in active use. At nearly 180 acres it is the **third-largest park in St. Louis** and includes two annually-stocked fishing lakes, the recently rebuilt **Carondelet Rec Complex** with indoor aquatic center, gymnasium, weight room, and walking trails, plus pavilions, baseball/softball/soccer fields, tennis courts, and a boathouse. Within the neighborhood itself, the bluff-top **Bellerive Park** was dedicated in **1908** with sweeping views over the Mississippi River, and the adjacent **Sister Marie Charles Park** on the Saint Louis Palisades extends the riverfront experience. The 4-acre **Alaska Park** serves the southern part of the neighborhood. The 1908 **Carondelet Branch Library** on Michigan Avenue, a **Carnegie-funded** building, houses a 1934 WPA mural “The Founding of St. Louis” by Robert Rigsby.

St. Louis Public Schools serves Carondelet through **Lyon at Blow School** (K-8, in the 1904 William B. Ittner-designed Blow School building) and **Woodward Elementary School** (PreK-5, designed by Rockwell M. Milligan and built in 1921). **St. Cecilia School and Academy**, a private Catholic K-8, also serves neighborhood families. The architectural fabric — some of the oldest in the city, with most housing built between 1880 and 1930 — ranges from modest shotgun cottages to substantial brick mansions, with the western section along Dover Place, Wilmington Avenue, and Bellerive Boulevard frequently named among the finest residential pockets in all of St. Louis. The historic urban core remains remarkably intact along Michigan and Minnesota Avenues, anchored by the Sisters of St. Joseph Motherhouse, the Saints Mary and Joseph Chapel campus, and the Carondelet Branch Library.

Carondelet’s 21st-century commercial life is concentrated along the Grand Boulevard and South Broadway corridors and at **Loughborough Commons** (Schnucks, Lowe’s, Office Max, Starbucks, Qdoba, Panera, and other anchors at Loughborough and I-55), with the historic **Ivory Theatre** at the southern tip of the neighborhood, the “**City of Carondelet**” mural in the middle of Ivory Avenue, **Bodach’s Games**, and the annual **Carondelet Garlic Festival**. The **Carondelet Community Betterment Federation** (CCBF) at 6408 Michigan Avenue is the active neighborhood organization, and the **Carondelet Historical Society** in the restored Des Peres School preserves the broader Carondelet area’s heritage (which historically includes the Patch, Mount Pleasant, Holly Hills, and parts of Dutchtown and Boulevard Heights). Interstate 55 runs through the neighborhood and famously divides it; despite the rough cut, Carondelet remains the southernmost gateway from the city to Jefferson Barracks and the south county corridor.

Image: Historic Carondelet neighborhood streetscape — founded in 1767 by French settler Clément Delor de Treget as an independent village three years after St. Louis itself, and annexed by the City of St. Louis in 1870 — Wikimedia Commons / CC BY-SA 3.0 by Onegentlemanofverona

Carondelet sits on the bluffs at the southeastern edge of the City of St. Louis, where the topography drops sharply down toward the Mississippi River around Michigan Avenue. The climate follows a classic Midwest four-season pattern. Hot, humid summers with highs regularly reaching the upper 80s°F drive peak outdoor activity at Carondelet Park’s nearly 180 acres, the Carondelet Rec Complex aquatic center, Bellerive Park’s river views, Sister Marie Charles Park, and the annual Carondelet Garlic Festival. Cool springs and falls are the busiest seasons for the historic-house walks along Bellerive Boulevard, Wilmington Avenue, Dover Place, and the Michigan-Minnesota Avenue church corridor. Cold winters with periodic snow and ice shift commerce indoors to the Ivory Theatre, Bodach’s Games, the Carondelet Branch Library, the Loughborough Commons retail anchors, and the South Broadway dining corridor. The bluff-top elevation gives the neighborhood slightly cooler river-influenced summer evenings than inland south-city corridors. The neighborhood’s 1880s-1930s brick and stone construction — some of the oldest housing stock in St. Louis — has weathered well over a century and a half of Missouri climate with remarkable durability.

## Carr Square — St. Louis Neighborhood

## Carr Square Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Carr Square, St. Louis

Carr Square serves as a redevelopment-driven Near North Side neighborhood positioned between the new NGA West campus and the Washington Avenue / Locust Street creative office corridor — offering data center consulting, creative coworking, legal process serving, digital marketing, and historic mixed-use office space for residents, professionals, and growing tech and marketing firms across the metro.

Carr Square sits on the Near North Side of St. Louis at **38.6373° N, 90.2021° W**, occupying the zip code 63106 just north of downtown. The 2020 Census recorded a 94.9% Black population with 2.1% White, 2.0% two-or-more races, and a small Hispanic/Latino presence. The neighborhood’s official boundaries are **Cass Avenue** on the north, **Carr Street** on the south, **North Tucker Boulevard / North 13th Street** on the east, and **North Jefferson Avenue** on the west. Its immediate neighbors are St. Louis Place to the north, Columbus Square to the east, Downtown West to the south, and JeffVanderLou to the west — placing Carr Square within a 10-minute walk of the Gateway Arch grounds, the Edward Jones Dome, and the new National Geospatial-Intelligence Agency (NGA) West campus just north on Cass.

Carr Square’s history is one of successive immigrant waves and 20th-century housing experiments. **German Protestants** first inhabited the area in the early 19th century, settling around the original Carr Square itself. Around 1842, a group of **Irish immigrants from County Kerry** settled to the northeast near 18th Street and O’Fallon, creating the legendary “**Kerry Patch**” — a tight cluster of one-room shacks built under squatter’s rights on land owned by the sympathetic Irish-American **Mullanphy family**, who refused to displace their countrymen. The Kerry Patch Irish quickly became St. Louis’s police, firemen, lawyers, doctors, editors, and political fixtures, leaving a cultural mark on the city that long outlasted the shacks. After 1870, **Polish immigrants** supplanted the Irish in Kerry Patch, founding **St. Stanislaus Kostka Parish** in 1878 — the church building completed in 1880 still stands on the National Register of Historic Places and as a St. Louis City Landmark.

By the 1880s, German Protestants began migrating outward and Orthodox Jews succeeded them in Carr Square. By 1920, the neighborhood had taken on a polyglot character of Russian and Balkan immigrants. The Depression and World War II then brought a major wave of **African-American migration** from the rural South into the central city — arrivals who often found themselves living in 19th-century tenement buildings subdivided into smaller and smaller units, in conditions so dense that an official 1947 city survey found that 33,000 homes in St. Louis still had communal toilets, and Carr Square earned the grim nickname “**the lung block**” for its tuberculosis rates.

It was against this backdrop that Carr Square and adjacent Columbus Square became the most consequential testing grounds for public housing in American history. **Carr Square Village**, completed in 1942 under the New Deal’s Housing Act of 1937, was the first generation of St. Louis public housing — a low-rise development for African-American families that opened as a sibling project to the racially segregated Clinton-Peabody for white families on the South Side. In 1954, just south of Cass Avenue and bounded by Jefferson, 20th Street, and Carr Street, the federal government opened **Pruitt-Igoe** — 33 eleven-story modernist towers designed by **Minoru Yamasaki** (later the architect of the original World Trade Center) on a 57-acre site, originally intended to be segregated as the Wendell O. Pruitt Homes (for African-Americans) and the William Igoe Apartments (for whites). When the Supreme Court’s Brown v. Board ruling forced integration, the project opened to nearly all Black tenants. **Vaughn Family Apartments** went up just to the east during the same era. By the 1970s, Pruitt-Igoe’s televised demolition (beginning March 16, 1972) became internationally known as “the day Modern architecture died,” and Carr Square Village pivoted to become one of the first public housing projects in the country to convert to **tenant management**.

Today, the 57-acre Pruitt-Igoe site is largely a wooded fenced lot whose future is being shaped by the **National Geospatial-Intelligence Agency (NGA) West campus** rising just north on Cass Avenue. The NGA project — a federal intelligence facility expected to bring thousands of jobs and billions of dollars in investment to Carr Square and the surrounding Near North Side — is the single largest economic catalyst the neighborhood has seen in over a century, and **Ponce Health Sciences University** has announced plans for an $80 million medical school facility on the former Pruitt-Igoe ground. Around the site today, **Preservation Square Apartments and Townhomes** provides nearly 700 new rental units, while the older Murphy Park, O’Fallon Place, and Vaughn redevelopments continue to stabilize the residential base.

Carr Square’s civic infrastructure includes three city parks — **De Soto Park** (the largest, 17.38 acres at the south end of the former Pruitt-Igoe site, with a paved walking trail and soccer field), **Murphy Park**, and **Loretta Hall Park** — totaling more than 30 acres of green space with soccer fields, softball and baseball diamonds, full-court basketball, picnic facilities, and an asphalt walking trail. **St. Stanislaus Kostka Church** continues to anchor the neighborhood’s eastern edge as the last Polish parish in St. Louis, an independent lay-board-run congregation with a Polish Heritage Center on site. The neighborhood’s schools include the **Gateway Schools complex** (Gateway Elementary, Gateway Michael, and Gateway Middle on 20 acres of the former Pruitt-Igoe site, which opened in September 1995), **Carr Lane Visual and Performing Arts Middle School**, **Jefferson Elementary**, and the private Catholic **Central Catholic St. Nicholas School**.

Because Carr Square sits at the geographic hinge between downtown St. Louis, the NGA campus, and the redeveloping Washington Avenue / Locust Street office corridor, many of the businesses that serve its residents and professionals are located along these adjacent corridors — particularly the historic mixed-use buildings of Washington Avenue south of Cole Street and the Locust Central Business District just south of the neighborhood. The listings on this page reflect that practical reality, drawing from the broader service area that Carr Square residents and professionals actually use.

Image: Pruitt-Igoe housing complex, Carr Square, St. Louis — USGS aerial photograph, March 3, 1968 — Wikimedia Commons / U.S. Geological Survey, Public Domain

Carr Square sits in the dense Near North Side urban core, where the four-season Midwest climate shapes the rhythm of the neighborhood’s redeveloping commercial and residential blocks. Hot, humid summers with highs in the upper 80s°F drive the use of De Soto Park’s soccer fields and the outdoor programming at adjacent Columbus Square; cool springs and falls are the high seasons for NGA-area construction milestones and Washington Avenue office leasing; cold winters with periodic snow shift commerce indoors to the creative office spaces, coworking studios, and historic mixed-use buildings that line Washington Avenue and Locust Street just south of the neighborhood. The area’s flat topography between the Mississippi bluffs and the city’s street grid means it remains highly walkable and transit-accessible year-round, with multiple MetroBus connections and a quick MetroLink ride to downtown.

#7

2223 Locust St, St. Louis, MO 63103, USA · (314) 652-2220

## Central West End — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/central-west-end

Neighborhood

Central West End

Coordinates

38.6395° N, 90.2547° W

Ranked by 1,900+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  10 of 10 are Professionally Managed Workspace ✓  All businesses verified via Google Business ✓  Avg. rating: 4.7/5 across 1,900 reviews

## Central West End Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Central West End, St. Louis

The Central West End serves as one of St. Louis’s premier mixed-use neighborhoods — anchored by the Cortex Innovation Community, the Washington University Medical Center, the Cathedral Basilica, and the historic gated mansions of Westmoreland and Portland Places — offering coworking and lab space, managed IT, signage, upscale grocery and dining, specialty coffee, ice cream, and international trade consulting for residents, scientists, students, and professionals across the metro.

The Central West End sits at **38.6395° N, 90.2547° W** across the 63108 and 63110 zip codes, just east of Forest Park. The neighborhood’s boundaries are **Delmar Boulevard** on the north, **I-64 / US 40** on the south, **North Vandeventer Avenue** on the east, and **Union Boulevard** with the eastern edge of Forest Park on the west. Recognized by the American Planning Association as one of **America’s Great Neighborhoods**, the Central West End is consistently rated the most walkable neighborhood in St. Louis and one of the most architecturally distinguished urban districts in the Midwest.

The Central West End’s development is inseparable from **Forest Park**, established west of the city in 1876 as a 1,300-acre civic park. When St. Louis extended its western boundary past Forest Park in the 1880s, the area immediately north of the park became the preferred address for the city’s industrialists, brewers, and merchants — especially as St. Louis raced to become the host city for the **1904 Louisiana Purchase Exposition** (World’s Fair). In 1887, civil engineer **Julius Pitzman** — the master planner of Forest Park itself — was retained by the Forest Park Improvement Association to plat what would become the neighborhood’s defining feature: the gated private streets of **Westmoreland Place** and **Portland Place**, modeled on Pitzman’s earlier work at Benton Place, Vandeventer Place, Kingsbury Place, and Washington Terrace.

By 1888, the two sister streets were laid out on the former Cabanne Dairy Farm, landscaped by Forest Park designer **Maximillian G. Kern**, and gated with sentinel gatehouses designed by some of St. Louis’s most prominent architects. Listed together on the National Register of Historic Places in 1974 as the **Portland and Westmoreland Places Historic District**, the two streets hold 94 mansions in Italian palazzo, French chateau, English country estate, Georgian Revival, Renaissance Revival, and Italianate styles. Residents over the decades included Charles Stix (founder of Stix, Baer & Fuller), Edward Faust (vice president of Anheuser-Busch), shoe magnate George Warren Brown, and contributors to the financing of Charles Lindbergh’s 1927 transatlantic flight. The two streets remain privately owned, gated, and governed by resident boards of trustees to this day — some of the very few World’s Fair-era private streets still operating in America.

The Central West End is equally defined by its literary and cultural legacy. The poet **T. S. Eliot**’s family home stands on Locust Street; playwright **Tennessee Williams** grew up in the neighborhood; Beat writer **William S. Burroughs**’s childhood home sits on Pershing Avenue (formerly Berlin Avenue); and novelist **Kate Chopin** wrote here. A.G. Edwards and Ralston Purina were both founded or led from Central West End addresses, and the neighborhood’s mansions hosted some of the planners of the 1904 World’s Fair itself. The **1959 St. Louis tornado** damaged parts of the district, and insurance proceeds helped fund the gas-lit reinvention of the commercial blocks — giving rise to the **Gaslight Square** art-and-nightlife district that established the Central West End’s national reputation as an artistic incubator.

Dominating the neighborhood’s skyline is the **Cathedral Basilica of Saint Louis** (the “New Cathedral”), completed in 1914 at Lindell Boulevard and Newstead Avenue. Designed by the firm of Barnett, Haynes & Barnett in a striking Neo-Byzantine / Romanesque Revival style, the cathedral houses one of the largest mosaic installations in the Western Hemisphere — a 41.5 million-tessera interior completed over 76 years (1912 to 1988). Designated a basilica by Pope John Paul II in 1997, the Cathedral Basilica was honored with a papal visit in January 1999. Other neighborhood institutions include **Saint Nicholas Greek Orthodox Church** with its popular Labor Day festival, **Rosati-Kain High School** (the Archdiocesan girls’ preparatory school founded in 1911), and the historic **Chase Park Plaza Hotel**.

The modern Central West End is also defined by the **Cortex Innovation Community**, the 200-acre tech and bioscience district that has reshaped the southern portion of the neighborhood. Anchored by the **4220 Duncan** and **4240 Duncan** Cambridge Innovation Center (CIC) campuses, Cortex provides specialized lab space, coworking suites, and a global innovation network for biotech startups and tech firms. Just south, the **Washington University School of Medicine** and **Barnes-Jewish Hospital** form one of the largest academic medical campuses in the country — including the **Siteman Cancer Center**, ranked among the top cancer centers in the United States by U.S. News & World Report. The **World Chess Hall of Fame** on North Maryland Avenue completes the cultural roster, housing the largest publicly accessible chess piece on Earth (the 14-foot, 6-inch **World’s Largest Chess Piece**) and rotating exhibitions on the game’s history and art.

The neighborhood’s commercial heart runs along **Euclid Avenue**, where more than 75 independent businesses — including the 125-year-old **Straub’s Market** (founded 1901), **Clementine’s Naughty & Nice Ice Cream**, **Left Bank Books** (the largest independently owned full-line bookstore in St. Louis), and **Northwest Coffee Roasting** — share the corridor with cafes, art galleries, and patios. The geography of the Central West End is uniquely advantageous: positioned on level ground above the formerly flood-prone River des Peres (now culverted under Forest Park), the neighborhood is eminently walkable, MetroLink-accessible via the Central West End and Cortex stations, and within minutes of downtown, Clayton, the Delmar Loop, and the Cathedral Basilica complex.

Image: Cathedral Basilica of Saint Louis, Central West End, St. Louis — completed 1914, designated a basilica 1997 — Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 2.5

The Central West End sits on the level upland above Forest Park, where the four-season Midwest climate shapes the rhythm of the neighborhood’s patio culture and event calendar. Hot, humid summers with highs in the upper 80s°F bring peak energy to Euclid Avenue patio dining, Forest Park festivals, and the Saint Nicholas Greek Festival on Labor Day weekend; cool, brilliant falls and springs drive the Central West End House & Garden Tour, the Cortex Innovation Community’s annual conference calendar, and the heaviest restaurant and gallery traffic on Euclid; cold winters with periodic snow shift commerce into the Cathedral Basilica’s holiday concerts, the Chase Park Plaza ballrooms, and the indoor lab and coworking spaces of the Cortex campus. The neighborhood’s walkable, level grid and MetroLink access keep it among the most weather-resilient retail and professional districts in the city.

## Cheltenham — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/cheltenham

Coordinates

38.6267° N, 90.2817° W

Ranked by 3,834+ Google reviews. Updated June 2026.

✓  A historic clay-mining & firebrick district in southwest St. Louis ✓  Part of the Dogtown cluster, just south of Forest Park ✓  Avg. rating: 4.6/5 across 3,834 reviews

## Cheltenham Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Cheltenham, St. Louis

Cheltenham is a small, history-rich neighborhood in southwest St. Louis — a former clay-mining and firebrick district tucked just south of Forest Park, and part of the wider Dogtown cluster. Its modern streets are quiet and residential, but the ground beneath them once fed one of the largest fire-brick industries in the country.

Cheltenham sits in the southwest corner of the City of St. Louis, bounded roughly by **Oakland Avenue** on the north, **Manchester Avenue** on the south, **Macklind Avenue** on the east, and **Hampton Avenue** on the west. Forest Park lies immediately to the north, and the neighborhood is woven into the “Dogtown” cluster alongside Clayton-Tamm, Franz Park, and Hi-Pointe. At roughly a third of a square mile, it is one of the City’s smaller official neighborhoods.

The area’s recorded history reaches back to around 1798. In the 1830s a **Sulphur Springs resort** opened here, and the neighborhood took the name **Cheltenham** after the English spa town of the same name — the birthplace of the resort’s manager. For a brief moment Cheltenham was a place people traveled to for its mineral waters, long before it became a place defined by its clay.

Cheltenham also hosted an unusual social experiment. Between **1858 and 1864** a band of French utopian communards known as the **Icarians** — followers of Étienne Cabet — settled here. In February 1858 the remaining members purchased a few hundred acres and set up communal workshops for tailors, joiners, blacksmiths, and shoemakers. Internal disputes over strict governance, including votes to reinstate bans on smoking and drinking, splintered the group, and the depleted commune disbanded by 1864, with some families later joining the Icarian settlement in Iowa.

What truly defined Cheltenham was **clay**. Beneath the area lies the bowl-shaped **Cheltenham Syncline**, a geologic formation south of Forest Park holding large reserves of high-quality fire clay. The 1852 extension of the **Pacific Railroad** line to Cheltenham supplied the transport that, paired with the clay and an immigrant workforce, turned the district industrial almost overnight.

The firebrick industry grew quickly. The earliest and largest firm, the **Laclede Fire Brick Company**, was founded in **1844**, and about a dozen companies followed. The **Cheltenham Fireclay Works** — operating on the site from 1855 and incorporated in 1867 as **Evens & Howard Fire Brick Co.** — produced fire bricks, sewer pipe, and gas retorts that shipped as far as Quebec and Africa. By 1895 roughly 50 brick yards operated in the area, making St. Louis the state’s chief clay-products center.

The clay and brick boom drew **Irish and Italian immigrants** whose families helped establish the neighboring **Dogtown** and **The Hill** communities. Today the kilns are long gone, and Cheltenham is a modest residential and light-industrial pocket — but the Manchester and Macklind corridors that frame it still carry working businesses, from manufacturers and trades to studios and an STLCC campus on its northern edge by Forest Park.

A quiet residential street in Cheltenham. Photo: “Tiny Cheltenham Street” by Paul Sableman, licensed under CC BY 2.0 via Flickr.

Cheltenham shares St. Louis’s humid subtropical climate, with warm, green springs giving way to hot, humid summers when July highs average near **90°F**. Spring and early summer bring frequent thunderstorms, and the region sits within the broader severe-storm belt, so businesses here plan for heat, humidity, and the occasional strong storm from roughly April through September. 

Winters are cool rather than harsh, with January highs around **40°F** and modest annual snowfall near 16 inches against roughly 42 inches of yearly precipitation. For the trades and industrial firms that line Cheltenham’s Manchester and Macklind edges, that mix of freeze-thaw cycles and humid summers is exactly the kind of weather that keeps plumbers, plasterers, and surface-treatment shops steadily at work year-round.

## Clayton Tamm — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/clayton-tamm

Ranked by 1,635+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  10 of 10 verified Clayton-Tamm corridor providers ✓  All businesses verified via Google Business ✓  Avg. rating: 4.8/5 across 1,635 reviews

## Clayton-Tamm Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Clayton-Tamm, St. Louis

Clayton-Tamm sits at the heart of St. Louis’s beloved “Dogtown” — a working-class Irish-American enclave directly south of Forest Park and the Saint Louis Zoo, anchored by St. James the Greater Catholic Church on its dramatic hill, the 1942-era Pat Connolly Tavern with its iconic neon Budweiser sign, Bob Cassilly’s Turtle Park, and the annual Ancient Order of Hibernians St. Patrick’s Day Parade down Tamm Avenue — offering historic neighborhood taverns, craft breweries, family-owned hardware, custom display manufacturing, technology consulting, and walkable retail for residents and businesses across the west-central St. Louis corridor.

Clayton-Tamm sits along the western edge of the City of St. Louis at **38.6264° N, 90.2933° W** in zip code 63139. Its boundaries form a compact rectangle: **Oakland Avenue and Interstate 64/U.S. Highway 40** to the north (separating it from Forest Park), **Hampton Avenue** to the east, **Manchester Avenue** (Old Route 66) to the south, and **Louisville and Dale Avenues** to the west. The neighborhood’s defining streets are **Tamm Avenue** (the residential and parade spine), **Clayton Avenue** (the commercial east-west spine), **Oakland Avenue** (the northern boundary along the Zoo), and **Manchester Avenue** (the southern industrial-historic edge). The name comes from the intersection of Clayton Avenue and Tamm Avenue, the center of the neighborhood’s business district. Clayton-Tamm is one of five official city neighborhoods — along with **Franz Park, Hi-Pointe, Cheltenham, and a small slice of Ellendale** — that together form the informal area known as **Dogtown**, which is not itself an official St. Louis neighborhood. The neighborhood sits within zip code 63139 alongside surrounding Hi-Pointe to the west, Franz Park and Cheltenham to the east, and Forest Park directly across I-64 to the north.

The earliest development in what would become Clayton-Tamm was a **1785 Spanish land grant to Charles Gratiot**, a Swiss-born early settler related by marriage to St. Louis’s founders, “to cultivate wheat, hemp, corn, tobacco, etc.” The grant was formalized in 1798 and reaffirmed by the US in 1808 after the Louisiana Purchase. Major growth came in 1852, when the **Pacific Railroad** extended into Cheltenham, opening the area’s clay-rich subsurface to industrial-scale mining. The first clay mines were established in 1845, and by the early 20th century two major operations were running in Clayton-Tamm: the **Roberts Mine** and the **Gittins Mine**, the latter reaching 100 feet deep just west of Tamm Avenue. The mining industry drew waves of Irish, Italian, German, French, and Balkan immigrants who built the housing stock around the mines. The Pacific Railroad, the 1849 St. Louis Fire (which triggered stricter brick-building codes), the 1876 founding of Forest Park, and the 1904 World’s Fair all accelerated the build-out of the neighborhood that would soon be called Dogtown. The clay mined here supplied much of the red brick that gives St. Louis its **“Brick City”** nickname.

The Dogtown name itself is the subject of one of St. Louis’s most enduring legends. While a popular tale ties it to Filipino Igorot tribespeople at the 1904 World’s Fair allegedly hunting dogs for ceremonial purposes, the **Dogtown Historical Society** — through historian Bob Corbett — has documented the name’s appearance in a *Missouri Republican* article dated **August 14, 1889**, five years before the Fair. The most credible origin describes coal miners displaced from Forest Park when the city acquired the land in 1876, who built shacks south of the new park and used watchdogs to defend their squatters’ homes — giving rise to the “Dogtown” label. The neighborhood’s housing stock primarily consists of early-20th-century **red-brick workers’ cottages, two-family flats, and Craftsman bungalows** packed densely along tree-lined streets. Closer to Clayton and Tamm Avenues, rows of original miners’ cottages reflect the area’s industrial origins; further out, three-bedroom Craftsman bungalows and frame homes intermingle with brick construction. Clayton-Tamm is notably hilly by St. Louis standards — neighboring Hi-Pointe to the west is named for sitting on one of the city’s highest points, and Clayton-Tamm’s own slopes are unusual in this otherwise flat Mississippi River city.

The neighborhood’s defining institution is **St. James the Greater Catholic Church** at 1360 Tamm Avenue, founded as a mission in 1860 to serve the Irish Catholic miners. The current **English Gothic Revival church**, with its cut ashlar stone facing and dramatic hilltop perch on some of the highest ground in Dogtown, was built during the Great Depression entirely funded by parishioners. **St. James the Greater School**, the attached private K-8 Catholic school across Tamm Avenue from the Sisters of Charity Seminary, has served the parish for over 160 years. Clayton-Tamm contains no public schools — residents are served by the Saint Louis Public School District. The neighborhood’s other religious landmark is the **1911 Memorial Congregational Church**, now home to St. Louis Bible Fellowship. Each March 17, Tamm Avenue becomes the route of the **Ancient Order of Hibernians St. Patrick’s Day Parade**, established in 1984 and held in Dogtown since 1986 — widely regarded as the more authentic of St. Louis’s two St. Patrick’s Day parades, drawing tens of thousands for Irish Schools of Dance, pipe bands, and clan floats. The parade kicks off at Tamm and Oakland in front of Pat’s, runs the length of Tamm Avenue to Manchester Road, and remains a multi-generational celebration of Irish-American heritage.

Public green space in Clayton-Tamm itself is famously limited — none of St. Louis’s 108 official city parks fall within the neighborhood’s boundaries. But the trade-off is extraordinary: **Forest Park** lies directly across Interstate 64 to the north, one of the largest urban parks in the United States, drawing roughly **13 million visitors annually** and home to the **Saint Louis Zoo** (free admission), the **Saint Louis Art Museum**, the **Missouri History Museum**, the **Saint Louis Science Center**, **The Muny** outdoor theatre, the **Norman K. Probstein Golf Course**, and miles of trails. Connecting Clayton-Tamm to the park is **Turtle Park**, the beloved sculpture playground at Oakland and Tamm Avenues, opened in **August 1996**. Sculpted by **Bob Cassilly** — the artist behind City Museum — and funded by philanthropist **Sonya “Sunny” Glassberg** as a gift to St. Louis’s children, the park features seven oversized concrete turtles native to Missouri (a 40-foot snapping turtle, a soft-shelled turtle, a red-eared slider, three box turtles, and a stinkpot turtle), seven concrete eggs, and a sinuous snake taking a bite out of the Tamm Avenue overpass. Cassilly borrowed a live snapping turtle from the Saint Louis Zoo to model the largest sculpture, and Turtle Park was voted “Best Picnic Spot” by the *Riverfront Times* in 2011.

The intersection of Clayton and Tamm forms the unofficial **“Downtown Dogtown”** — a tight commercial core of independent bars, taverns, breweries, and small specialty shops centered on the **Pat Connolly Tavern** at 6400 Oakland Avenue (in continuous operation since Irish immigrant Pat Connolly opened it in **1942**, marked by an iconic neon Budweiser V-9 sign), **Seamus McDaniel’s**, **Felix’s Pizza Pub**, **Heavy Riff Brewing Company** (opened 2013 in the former Gewinner’s grocery space), **Tamm Avenue Bar**, **Hogtown Smokehouse**, **Mac’s Local Eats**, and **Sugaree Baking Co**. The 2010 Census recorded **2,251 residents** in Clayton-Tamm, a 9% decline from 2000, with 1,415 housing units at an impressive **90% occupancy rate**, split roughly **53/47 owner/renter**. The 2020 Census reported a population that remained 85.2% White, 4.6% Black, 2.2% Asian, 6.8% two or more races, and 4.8% Hispanic/Latino; surveys show **over 20% of residents claim Irish ancestry**, the highest concentration in the city. The median age is 32, with a strong young-adult presence aged 25-44. Median home sale prices over the trailing 12 months are around **$220,000**, with three-bedroom cottages near Clayton and Tamm Avenues running $215,000 to $300,000.

Clayton-Tamm’s compact, walkable footprint and its position south of Forest Park, between the corporate density of Clayton and the residential pockets of South City, give the neighborhood a distinctive role as the commercial “downtown” of broader Dogtown. The intersection of Clayton and Tamm is the town square; the Pat Connolly Tavern is the unofficial living room; the AOH Parade is the calendar’s most important date; and the brick cottages climbing Clayton-Tamm’s unusual slopes are physical evidence of the immigrant mining families who built them. Many of the businesses serving this corridor straddle the wider Dogtown area or the nearby Class A office market in Clayton, reflecting the practical service ecosystem Clayton-Tamm residents and professionals use every day. The listings on this page draw from that broader Clayton-Tamm and Dogtown footprint, presenting the bars, breweries, retail, professional services, and consulting providers in their natural geographic relationship to the corridor.

Image: Ancient Order of Hibernians St. Patrick’s Day Parade in Dogtown — the more authentic of St. Louis’s two St. Patrick’s Day parades, held annually on March 17 down Tamm Avenue in Clayton-Tamm — Wikimedia Commons / CC BY-SA 2.5 by Agent-88

Clayton-Tamm sits on hilly terrain south of Forest Park, where the city meets some of its highest ground and the climate follows a classic Midwest four-season pattern. Hot, humid summers with highs regularly reaching the upper 80s°F bring peak outdoor activity to Turtle Park’s sculpture playground, the dog-friendly patios at Tamm Avenue Bar and Heavy Riff Brewing, the Saint Louis Zoo just across I-64, and the Pat Connolly Tavern’s tree-shaded patio overlooking Turtle Park. Cool springs and falls are the busiest seasons for walks through “Downtown Dogtown,” St. James the Greater services on the hilltop, Forest Park trail use, and Felix’s Pizza Pub’s outdoor seating.

March 17 is the calendar’s anchor — the Ancient Order of Hibernians St. Patrick’s Day Parade runs rain or shine down Tamm Avenue, often in cold, gusty early-spring weather that doesn’t deter tens of thousands of spectators. Cold winters with periodic snow shift commerce indoors to the Pat Connolly Tavern, Schnarr’s Hardware on Clayton Road, the Class A office spaces in adjacent Clayton, and Heavy Riff’s rock-themed taproom. The neighborhood’s late-19th and early-20th-century red-brick miners’ cottages — built from clay mined locally — have weathered well over a century of Missouri seasons with characteristic durability.

## Clifton Heights — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/clifton-heights

Ranked by 521+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  10 of 10 verified Clifton Heights / Hampton-Arsenal corridor providers ✓  All businesses verified via Google Business ✓  Avg. rating: 4.7/5 across 521 reviews

## Clifton Heights Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Clifton Heights, St. Louis

Clifton Heights serves as one of southwest St. Louis’s most topographically distinctive Victorian-era residential neighborhoods — an 1885 Julius Pitzman-platted Methodist subdivision atop the Gratiot League Square, anchored by the 4.4-acre Clifton Heights Park and its iconic lake (originally a private park dedicated to the City in 1912), the Fry Memorial Methodist Church (now a boutique inn), and the curving streets that flow down through Queen Anne and Arts & Crafts homes toward the park — offering general contracting, plastering, marketing, industrial supply, and real estate services across the Hampton and Arsenal corridors.

Clifton Heights is one of St. Louis’s 79 official neighborhoods, located at **38.6143° N, 90.2916° W** in southwest St. Louis City. The neighborhood is bounded by **Interstate 44** on the north and west, **Hampton Avenue** on the east, and **Arsenal Street** on the south. Surrounding neighborhoods include **Ellendale** to the north and west, **The Hill** and **Southwest Garden** to the east, and **Lindenwood Park** to the south. The neighborhood sits within **ZIP code 63139** and lies in the **4th Ward** of the City of St. Louis. The neighborhood spans approximately 0.48 square miles and is home to roughly 2,836 residents (2020 census). Clifton Heights has arguably the **most unique topography in all of the city** — a large hill that naturally slopes north and west toward the ancient valley of the **River des Peres** (long since channelized along the railroad and I-44), with a natural “dimple” or mini-valley at its center forming the bowl that holds **Clifton Heights Park** and its small lake. The unconventional **winding streets** are uncommon for St. Louis, where the rectilinear street grid rules in most of the city.

The area that now constitutes Clifton Heights was originally part of an approximately 3-mile by 3-mile tract of land called the **Gratiot League Square**, granted to the Swiss-born merchant **Charles Gratiot** in **1798**. Gratiot had requested the land grant in **1785** from the authorities of New Spain, who then governed the region, so that he could “establish a habitation on the River des Peres, to cultivate wheat, hemp, corn, tobacco, etc., etc.” This land was relatively remote from the village of St. Louis, which then consisted of only a few dozen structures along the Mississippi riverfront and common land that extended a couple of miles to the west and south. After the **1803 Louisiana Purchase**, Gratiot’s ownership of the land was recognized by the U.S. government in its process of reviewing the Spanish land grants. The land in present-day Clifton Heights retained its rural character until the middle of the 19th century, when the arrival of railroads and the discovery of coal and clay deposits transformed much of the area. While these mines and factories are more closely associated with the **Dogtown** neighborhoods and **The Hill**, some were established within Clifton Heights as well.

In **1851**, ground was broken in St. Louis for the **Pacific Railroad**, using land granted to the newly formed company by the state of Missouri. As construction proceeded westward along the valley of the River des Peres, trains began running in **1853** along roughly the northwestern boundary of present-day Clifton Heights. The development of Clifton Heights as a residential neighborhood took off in the **1880s**. An 1876 map shows just a handful of streets in present-day Clifton Heights; by **1884**, the area was served by **streetcars** along Arsenal Street to the south and (New) Manchester Road to the north. A major catalyst of this development in the northern part of the neighborhood was a community of Methodists led by **Benjamin St. James Fry**. The group hired the Prussian-born surveyor and city planner **Julius Pitzman** in **1885** to lay out their subdivision, which covered an area listed by the Recorder of Deeds as **D. W. Graham’s Sulphur Springs Tract**. Pitzman — whose work also influenced the development of Lafayette Square’s Benton Place (1866), Westmoreland and Portland Place, and several other St. Louis private streets — designed the distinctive curving streets surrounding the park that give Clifton Heights its uniquely Victorian, almost Webster Groves-like feel. The original construction included a Methodist church on Clifton Avenue, named the **Fry Memorial Methodist Episcopal Church** after Fry’s death in 1892. The church on that site, built in the early 20th century after the original burned, was sold to private owners in **2004** and is now a boutique inn.

Commercial buildings began to appear in the neighborhood by the turn of the 20th century. One example is the **Clifton Pointe Building** at the corner of Columbia Avenue and Clifton Avenue, built between **1903 and 1907**. At the time of a 1991 survey by the Landmarks Association of St. Louis, a Rexall Drugs store occupied the building; as of 2025, it is home to a hair salon and law office. In the **1920s and 1930s**, another wave of home building occurred. Based on information from building permits, scores of Clifton Heights homes were constructed during this period by local builder **Harry C. Vollmar**, whose family had lived in the neighborhood since at least 1910. The last part of Clifton Heights to be developed for residential use was the **Clifton Hills subdivision** north of Southwest Avenue and west of Tamm Avenue in the **1940s and 1950s**. When **Interstate 44** was built in St. Louis in the late 1950s and 1960s (in service by 1972), about **50 of the neighborhood’s houses ended up confined between the new highway and the railroad tracks**, and a small park called **Frisco Park** was effectively eliminated. I-44 now constitutes both a physical and psychological border for Clifton Heights. In **1991**, the Landmarks Association of St. Louis conducted a 455-page survey to assess the historical significance of the architecture within the neighborhood.

The **4.4-acre Clifton Heights Park** at the geographic center of the neighborhood — located between Columbia Avenue, Sulphur Avenue, Elizabeth Avenue, and the winding Clifton Avenue — began life as a **private park** developed for the residents of the **1885 Clifton Heights Subdivision**. The original 4.4-acre tract was **dedicated to the City of St. Louis in 1912**, with an additional 1.25 acres added during the early 1920s. The City later bought adjoining property to the south and east, enlarging the park to its present size. Today the park features a charming lake with **ducks, geese, two fountains, a viewing deck, and a “boat house,”** as well as a basketball court, playground, and walking paths that wind down through the surrounding Victorian homes toward the lake at the bottom of the bowl. The lake is part of the **St. Louis Urban Fishing Program**, although it is not regularly stocked like the program’s nine other park lakes. In **2018**, the City of St. Louis released a master plan to guide future improvements to the park, prioritizing infrastructure repair, restoration of the park’s Victorian character, and improved accessibility. The **Clifton Heights Neighborhood Association** (cliftonheights-stl.org) holds several annual events in the park, including an Easter egg hunt, a fall festival, and a candy cane hunt.

Clifton Heights is also home to the **Mason School of Academic & Cultural Literacy** on Southwest Avenue (an SLPS pre-K to 6th grade project-based magnet school designed by district architect **Rockwell M. Milligan** and opened in **1921**; former U.S. Representative and 1988 / 2004 presidential candidate **Richard Gephardt** grew up on Reber Place and attended what was then called Mason Elementary), **Epiphany of Our Lord Catholic Parish** (founded 1911, celebrated 100 years in 2011), **Mount Tabor United Church of Christ** on Arsenal Street (formed 1928 by a merger of two congregations dating to 1889), an **AMVETS Post 6**, and a Drury Inn and Suites at the intersection of I-44 and Hampton Avenue. The neighborhood’s key business anchors today include **Independents Graphic & Display** at 6331 Knox Industrial Drive (the 35,000-square-foot custom printing, sewing, prototyping, and POP-display manufacturer), **The St. Louis Plastering Company LLC** at 6841 Manchester Avenue (the high-rated specialty plaster contractor for the neighborhood’s Victorian and Arts & Crafts homes), **Hof Construction, Inc.** at 3137 Jamieson Avenue (the 100-year, multi-generational family-led general contractor and construction-management firm with GSA, educational, and institutional project experience), **S. M. Wilson & Co.** at 2185 Hampton Avenue (the century-old employee-owned construction-management firm specializing in healthcare, industrial, retail, and municipal projects), and **Midvale Industries** at 6310 Knox Industrial Drive (the 1901-founded aerospace, metalcasting, and metal-finishing supplier serving the national industrial base from St. Louis for more than 120 years).

Clifton Heights Park — the 4.4-acre park and lake at the heart of the Clifton Heights neighborhood, St. Louis, Missouri. Photo: St Louis Near Me Directory.

Clifton Heights sits in southwest St. Louis at 38.6143° N at the intersection of Interstate 44, Hampton Avenue, and Arsenal Street, with seamless access to Forest Park, the Hampton Avenue commercial corridor, the Cheltenham / Hill / Southwest Garden industrial corridor along the River des Peres, and the Shrewsbury-Lansdowne I-44 MetroLink station on the Blue Line. The neighborhood experiences St. Louis’s full humid continental climate: hot, humid summers, cold winters, and an active spring severe weather season. 

Peak outdoor activity falls during spring, summer, and fall, when **Clifton Heights Park’s** 4.4 acres of rolling Victorian-era topography draw heaviest neighborhood use at the playground, basketball court, boat house, viewing deck, two fountains, the lake (open to catch-and-release fishing), the curving walking paths that wind down through the Queen Anne, Arts & Crafts, Colonial Revival, American Foursquare, Prairie School, and Tudor Revival homes, and the Clifton Heights Neighborhood Association’s annual **Easter egg hunt**, **Fall Festival**, **Candy Cane Hunt**, and shred event. 

Cool seasons drive intense indoor activity at **The St. Louis Plastering Company LLC** on Manchester Avenue, **Hof Construction** on Jamieson Avenue, **S. M. Wilson & Co.** on Hampton Avenue, **Independents Graphic & Display** on Knox Industrial Drive, **Midvale Industries** on Knox Industrial Drive, **Abstrakt Marketing Group**, **St Louis Real Estate Center** on South Big Bend in adjacent Richmond Heights, **St Louis Business Forms Inc** in High Ridge, and the indoor venues of **Mason Elementary**, **Epiphany of Our Lord**, **Mount Tabor United Church of Christ**, **AMVETS Post 6**, the Fry Memorial Methodist boutique inn on Clifton Avenue, the Clifton Pointe Building (1903–1907), and the neighborhood’s surrounding Hampton / Arsenal / Southwest / Watson / Manchester / Knox Industrial / Jamieson corridors. The neighborhood’s 1880s–1950s housing stock — including its Queen Anne Victorians on Pitzman’s curving streets around the park, its 1920s–1930s Vollmar-built homes, its Arts & Crafts bungalows, Colonial Revivals, American Foursquares, Prairie School cottages, and Tudor Revivals — has weathered more than 140 years of Missouri climate with the durability of the German immigrant masons and Methodist craftsmen who built them.

## College Hill — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/college-hill

Ranked by 714+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  10 of 10 verified College Hill corridor providers ✓  All businesses verified via Google Business ✓  Avg. rating: 4.9/5 across 714 reviews

## College Hill Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 College Hill, St. Louis

College Hill anchors a historic, hilly stretch of North St. Louis built on Saint Louis University’s 1836 College Farm and the 1820s riverfront estate of Captain Lewis Bissell — anchored today by the towering 1871 Corinthian-column Grand Avenue Water Tower (designed by George I. Barnett) and the 1887 Bissell “New Red” Water Tower (designed by William S. Eames), the 1820s Bissell Mansion (one of St. Louis’s oldest standing brick homes), Mount Grace Convent (home of the “Pink Sisters”), and the William B. Ittner-designed 1912 Bryan Hill Elementary — offering chambers of commerce, professional consulting, business publications, marketing services, and culinary destinations for residents and businesses across the North St. Louis corridor.

College Hill sits in north St. Louis at **38.6733° N, 90.2195° W** in zip code 63107. The neighborhood’s boundaries form an irregular pentagon: **East Warne Avenue and Adelaide Avenue** on the northwest, **Interstate 70** on the northeast, **Ferry Street** on the southeast, and **West Florissant Avenue** on the southwest. The neighborhood’s defining streets are **East Grand Boulevard** (the historic commercial spine bisecting the southern hill), **West Florissant Avenue** (the southwestern boundary), **Blair Avenue** (running south of the Bissell Water Tower), and **Bissell Street**. College Hill is bordered by **Hyde Park** to the south, **O’Fallon** to the west, the **Mark Twain/I-70 industrial corridor and Bissell Point sewage treatment plant** to the east (separating it from the Mississippi River), and **Mark Twain neighborhood** to the north. The neighborhood’s near-perfect street grid is bisected by two hills — the larger southern hill anchored by the Grand and Bissell Water Towers, and the northern hill anchored by Mount Grace Convent. The 2020 Census recorded the neighborhood as **92.2% Black, 4.3% White, 1.7% Asian, and 0.6% Hispanic/Latino**, with a 2010 Census count of **1,871 residents** — a 36% decline from 2000 reflecting decades of population loss across North St. Louis.

The neighborhood’s name comes from its former use as the **College Farm of Saint Louis University**. The area, generally bounded by Warne, Carter, and Linton Avenues and Broadway, was acquired by SLU in **1836** for garden and recreation purposes, though the University ultimately decided not to build a campus there. The land was subdivided into residential lots in the early 1870s. Before SLU’s purchase, the area was rural, consisting of several large farm estates owned by **Colonel John O’Fallon** (whose 600+ acre holdings became the present **O’Fallon Park**), **Dr. John Gano Bryan, Edward Hempstead, and Captain Lewis Bissell**. Bissell — born in Connecticut in 1789, nephew of General Daniel Bissell, and a War of 1812 veteran who came to St. Louis in 1808 to serve under his uncle at Fort Bellefontaine — built his Greek Revival and federalist mansion atop a Mississippi River bluff between 1823 and 1828 on what became known as **Bissell Point**. He partnered with Colonel John O’Fallon to supply goods to frontier troops, eventually acquiring approximately 1,500 acres at Bissell Point.

The neighborhood’s most distinguishing architectural features are its **two historic standpipe water towers** — engineered to equalize the surging water pressure from the new Bissell Point pumping station, and now among only seven such surviving towers in the entire United States (with St. Louis home to three of them, the third being the 1898 Compton Hill Tower). The **Grand Avenue Water Tower** at 20th Street and East Grand Avenue was placed in service in 1871, designed by **George Ingham Barnett** (the first St. Louis architect to receive training abroad) and considered the largest perfect Corinthian column in existence at **154 feet** tall — in the late 1920s, lights were placed at its top to serve as aviation beacons for what became Lambert Field. The **Bissell “New Red” Water Tower** at Bissell Street and Blair Avenue, built in 1886-87 at a cost of $79,798 from red brick, light gray stone, and terra cotta, stands **194 feet** high — designed by **William S. Eames** (founder of the St. Louis chapter of the American Institute of Architects), often likened to a Moorish minaret. Both towers are listed on the National Register of Historic Places; in 1997, the Gateway Foundation added nighttime lighting to both, making them visible from the interstates.

The **Bissell Mansion** at 4426 Randall Place — one of St. Louis’s oldest standing brick homes, possibly the oldest — is a nearly 6,000-square-foot, four-bedroom, four-bathroom Greek Revival and Federalist structure with walls at least a foot thick, intact stained glass, a dumbwaiter, fireplaces in each room, and a central staircase. Captain Bissell lived there for over 40 years until his death in 1868 at age 79. The home was nearly demolished in the early 1950s for the construction of the **Mark Twain Expressway** (now I-70), but preservationists saved it. It became a designated **St. Louis City Landmark in 1971** and operated for over four decades as the **Bissell Mansion Restaurant & Murder Mystery Dinner Theatre** before closing at the start of the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020. The home is widely reported to be haunted by Captain Bissell himself.

College Hill’s other major institutions reflect its early-20th-century immigrant religious heritage. **Mount Grace Convent and Chapel** at East Warne Avenue, home of the contemplative cloistered **“Pink Sisters”** (Sister Servants of the Holy Spirit of Perpetual Adoration, named for their distinctive pink habits), was founded in 1928 and anchors the northern hill of the neighborhood. **St. Paul’s Lutheran Church** on East John Avenue dates its congregation to 1862, with the present church building constructed in 1926. **Bryan Hill Elementary School** at 2128 Gano Avenue, built in 1912, was designed by **William B. Ittner** — the prolific Building Commissioner for the St. Louis Public School District who designed 45 elementary schools and 5 high schools for the district starting in 1897. **Lowell School** at 1409 Linton Avenue (built 1926) now houses the **KIPP St. Louis Triumph Academy Charter School**. The St. Louis Academy at East Linton Avenue and 21st Street opened in September 2001 on the former Perpetual Help Catholic School site.

The neighborhood’s housing stock dates predominantly from between 1880 and 1920 and is dominated by **red-brick townhouses, four-family flats, and single-family brick dwellings** — many with comparatively large yards, with properties near the crest of the hillside bluff overlooking the Mississippi River and its surrounding valleys. The architecture is notably varied; College Hill developed slowly over decades, producing blocks where homes from 1870 sit next to homes from 1950. Two unusual clusters of **wood-frame houses** survive in violation of the city’s post-fire brick-only building codes — one cluster around West Florissant on DeSoto Street, another on Conde Street at College Street. Nearly half of residential units are owner-occupied. Historically, commercial activity was concentrated along East Grand Avenue near the Old Water Tower and along a strip on West Florissant Avenue. The neighborhood’s 1849-platted predecessor settlement, **Lowell**, originally extended from Adelaide Avenue, Broadway, East Prairie Avenue, and the river — annexed by the City of St. Louis without ever being incorporated.

College Hill’s modern business activity has been deeply shaped by the **North Spring Empowerment Center**, which borders the neighborhood and serves as the home of the **Heartland St. Louis Black Chamber of Commerce**. The neighborhood has access to MetroLink and Metro bus service via the Grand Avenue corridor, with quick I-70 access at Adelaide Avenue, West Florissant, Broadway, and East Grand exits — placing College Hill within minutes of downtown, the airport, and Interstates 55, 44, and 170. With its 1,871 residents (2010), unique combination of two nationally landmarked water towers, the centuries-old Bissell Mansion, the William B. Ittner-designed Bryan Hill School, the Pink Sisters at Mount Grace, and the layered architecture spanning eight decades, College Hill represents one of St. Louis’s most historically significant North Side neighborhoods. The listings on this page draw from the broader College Hill and surrounding service ecosystem.

Image: The Bissell Street Water Tower (the “New Red”), built 1886-87 — the 194-foot Victorian standpipe tower designed by William S. Eames, anchoring College Hill and listed on the National Register of Historic Places — Wikimedia Commons / CC BY 3.0 by Onegentlemanofverona

College Hill sits on hilly terrain in north St. Louis above the Mississippi River bluffs, where the city follows a classic Midwest four-season pattern. Hot, humid summers with highs in the upper 80s°F bring activity to O’Fallon Park just south of the neighborhood, the green spaces around the Bissell and Grand Avenue Water Towers, and the larger yards typical of College Hill’s late-19th-century brick homes. Spring and fall draw walkers along East Grand Boulevard and Blair Avenue, where the two landmark Victorian water towers stand visible from I-70 below.

Cold winters with periodic snow shift commerce indoors to the chamber and consulting offices around the North Spring Empowerment Center, the Heartland St. Louis Black Chamber of Commerce, and the Mount Grace Convent. The neighborhood’s 1880-1920 red-brick townhouses, four-family flats, and single-family dwellings — many with foot-thick walls like the nearly 200-year-old Bissell Mansion — have weathered more than a century of Missouri seasons with characteristic durability. The Gateway Foundation’s 1997 nighttime lighting of both water towers makes them visible from the interstates year-round, anchoring the neighborhood’s identity even in the depths of winter.
