## Columbus Square — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/columbus-square

Ranked by 543+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  10 of 10 verified Columbus Square corridor providers ✓  All businesses verified via Google Business ✓  Avg. rating: 4.6/5 across 543 reviews

## Columbus Square Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Columbus Square, St. Louis

Columbus Square anchors a small but historically deep stretch of near-north downtown St. Louis built on the city’s first wave of German Catholic, Irish, and Italian immigrant settlement — anchored today by the 1844 Shrine of St. Joseph (the only Vatican-authenticated miracle site in the Midwest and the only example of Baroque church architecture in St. Louis), the William B. Ittner-designed 1906 Patrick Henry Downtown Academy, the iconic Vess Bottle visible from I-70, the 1927 Cass Bank and Trust building, and the layered 1936 Neighborhood Gardens / 1952 Cochran Gardens / 1985-87 Columbus Square Townhomes and Castles housing legacy — offering coworking, marketing agencies, business brokerages, and chambers of commerce in the corridor that bridges Downtown St. Louis and the Bottle District.

Columbus Square sits just north of Downtown St. Louis at **38.6372° N, 90.1957° W** in zip codes 63101 and 63106. Per the City of St. Louis, the neighborhood’s boundaries are **Cass Avenue** on the North, **Interstate 70** on the East, **Cole Street** on the South, and **N. Tucker Boulevard / N. 13th Street** on the West. Columbus Square is bordered by **Old North St. Louis and the Bottle District** beyond Cass to the north, **Carr Square** to the west (separated by Tucker), the **St. Louis Post-Dispatch building and Downtown St. Louis** across Cole Street to the south, and I-70 plus the Mississippi River industrial corridor to the east. The neighborhood sits within a short walk of the **Gateway Arch** grounds and the Central Business District. The 2020 Census recorded Columbus Square as **88.7% Black, 5.8% White, 0.6% Asian, 3.3% Two or More Races**, with 2.2% Hispanic/Latino, and a 2010 Census population of **1,869 residents**. In 2010, Columbus Square ranked last among the 79 St. Louis neighborhoods for median household income at **$18,800**.

Columbus Square was settled by **successive waves of immigrants**. The first wave — a group of **German Catholics** — settled in the vicinity of 11th and Biddle Streets in the 1840s. The first **Irish immigrants** then colonized the land around 6th and Biddle, an area that subsequently became St. Patrick’s parish. After 1840, the area from the river west to 12th Street was built up with high-density tenement quarters and two- and three-story row houses for newly arriving immigrant laborers. Many of these structures fell to commercial and industrial development after the Civil War; those that remained had deteriorated into overcrowded slums by 1870, with tenement enclaves carrying nicknames like “Castle Thunder,” “Crabber Alley,” and “Wild Cat Chute.” An **Italian community** emerged near 7th and Carr Streets after the turn of the century — making the southern portion of today’s Columbus Square (along with the area along Cole Street) the **original “Little Italy” of St. Louis**. By 1920 the whole area north and west of downtown took on a polyglot character, with immigrants from Russia and the Balkan countries adding to the mix.

The neighborhood holds the distinction of being the birthplace of much of St. Louis Italian culinary history. The **Bommarito family** operated St. Louis’s first Italian bakery at 7th and Carr Streets, plus a spaghetti factory at 10th and Carr. The legendary **Tony’s restaurant** — created by Anthony Bommarito in 1946 as a small café — was originally Tony’s Spaghetti House and later Tony’s Steak House, growing from this Little Italy corridor to eventually become St. Louis’s top restaurant. By the mid-1900s most of the Italian community had relocated to **The Hill** on the south side of the city, but the Little Italy foundation in Columbus Square is commemorated on the SW corner of 11th & Carr by a plaque to **Frank A. Cammarata Sr. and his wife Antonia “Lena” Cammarata** — Italian immigrants who contributed to the original development of the neighborhood.

The neighborhood’s defining religious landmark is the **Shrine of St. Joseph** at 1220 N. 11th Street. Founded in 1843 by Austrian Jesuits to serve the rapidly expanding German Catholic immigrant community, the church was built on land donated by **Mrs. Ann Biddle** — wealthy widow and daughter of **John Mullanphy**, St. Louis’s first millionaire. The cornerstone was blessed by Bishop Kenrick on April 14, 1844, and the church was dedicated in August 1846 with Father James Van de Velde (later Bishop of Chicago) officiating. The Shrine is the **only example of Baroque church architecture in St. Louis** and the **only church in the Midwest to host a Vatican-authenticated miracle**. On March 16, 1864, dying German immigrant **Ignatius Strecker** was instantly cured after kissing a relic of Blessed Peter Claver — a healing the Vatican formally declared authentic in 1887, becoming one of the two miracles required for the canonization of Peter Claver in 1888. A second miracle followed when parishioners who pledged $4,000 toward a monument during the 1866 cholera epidemic were all spared, leading to the installation of the **Altar of Answered Prayers** in 1887. The third major expansion in 1881 added the twin towers; the **Friends of St. Joseph** saved the church from demolition in the late 1970s.

Public schooling in Columbus Square centers on **Patrick Henry Downtown Academy** at 10th Street, opened in 1906 and designed by **William B. Ittner** — the prolific St. Louis Public Schools architect responsible for 45 elementary schools and 5 high schools. Other architectural and visual landmarks include the **Cass Bank and Trust** building at 13th and Cass Avenue (a 1927 Beaux Arts structure that housed a bank founded in 1906 by St. Louis businessmen, nominated for the National Register of Historic Places), and the iconic **Vess Bottle** — the giant orange soda bottle that has become a Columbus Square landmark, visible as drivers exit I-70 onto Broadway. The neighborhood also includes three small parks: the 0.45-acre **Columbus Square Park** (established 1980), the 4.30-acre **Father Filipiak Park** (established 1980, named for a beloved local priest), and the 1.27-acre **Fourteenth Street Mall** (established 1976).

Housing in Columbus Square reflects nearly a century of public and subsidized development. After truck terminals and industries replaced many of the original tenement homes in the early 20th century, housing staged a comeback with the erection of the **Neighborhood Gardens apartments in 1936** — an early example of garden-style multifamily housing. **Cochran Gardens public housing** followed in 1952. The **Columbus Square Townhomes** and **the Castles** were both constructed between 1985 and 1987, and the former Columbus Square apartments (now known as **CitySide**) date from the same era. The 1980s townhomes are noted for hugging the sidewalks and framing the 19th-century Shrine of St. Joseph quite well, contributing to a streetscape that maintains historic continuity despite the dramatic 20th-century clearance.

Columbus Square’s modern commercial profile reflects its position bridging Downtown and the Old North St. Louis corridor. Few traditional retail businesses operate within the boundaries — a **Schnucks** grocery served the area until closing in 2000, leaving an enormous abandoned surface parking lot at 10th and Cass that falls within the **McKee NorthSide plan’s Phase B** redevelopment boundaries. The **St. Louis Post-Dispatch** building sits just across Cole Street from Columbus Square, while the former Rams stadium-area surface lots and the **Bottle District** (Paul McKee’s NorthSide plan) sit just north and east. The renaissance of Downtown St. Louis, the proximity to **One Metropolitan Square** (the tallest tower in St. Louis), and the corridor’s adjacency to the Gateway Arch grounds make Columbus Square a small but strategic professional crossroads. The listings on this page draw from this broader Columbus Square / Downtown St. Louis professional corridor.

Image: The Shrine of St. Joseph entrance at 1220 N. 11th Street — the 1844 Austrian Jesuit-founded Baroque church, site of the only Vatican-authenticated miracle in the Midwest and the only example of Baroque church architecture in St. Louis — Wikimedia Commons / CC BY-SA 3.0 by Marcus Qwertyus

Columbus Square sits just north of Downtown St. Louis along the Mississippi River bluff, where the city follows a classic Midwest four-season pattern. Hot, humid summers with highs in the upper 80s°F bring activity to the Gateway Arch grounds a short walk south, the small Columbus Square Park, and the 4.30-acre Father Filipiak Park. Spring and fall draw walkers along the corridor between the Shrine of St. Joseph, the Vess Bottle landmark visible exiting I-70 onto Broadway, and the 1927 Cass Bank and Trust building at 13th and Cass.

Cold winters with periodic snow shift commerce indoors to the coworking and office buildings along the Downtown / Columbus Square edge — STL Fusion on Washington Avenue, One Metropolitan Square (the tallest tower in St. Louis), and the St Louis Business Center. The 19th-century Shrine of St. Joseph, with its Baroque architecture and Altar of Answered Prayers, hosts winter Mass and weddings year-round. The neighborhood’s mix of 1936 Neighborhood Gardens, 1952 Cochran Gardens, and 1985-87 Columbus Square Townhomes and Castles housing has weathered Missouri seasons for nearly a century, anchoring the streetscape against changing weather and changing fortunes.

## Compton Heights — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/compton-heights

# Compton Heights Neighborhood Guide – South St. Louis History, Parks & Businesses

## Explore Compton Heights Parks, Landmarks and Local Services

Updated May 2026.

## Compton Heights Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

Compton Heights, St. Louis

Compton Heights serves as one of the city’s most architecturally distinguished near-south neighborhoods — laid out in 1888-1889 by Julius Pitzman as one of America’s earliest planned residential developments, anchored by the National Register-listed 179-foot Compton Hill Water Tower (1898), the 36-acre Compton Hill Reservoir Park, more than 200 mansions on the curving Hawthorne and Longfellow Boulevards, and the seamless transition into the South Grand business district — offering historic mansion tours, professional services, restaurants and bars, fitness and wellness, faith institutions, real estate, and event venues for residents and businesses across the near-south St. Louis corridor.

Compton Heights sits on the near-south side of the City of St. Louis at **38.6127° N, 90.2394° W** across zip codes **63104 and 63118**. Its boundaries form a compact 0.28-square-mile rectangle: **Interstate 44** to the north, **Nebraska Avenue** to the east, **Shenandoah Avenue** to the south, and **Grand Boulevard** to the west. The neighborhood’s signature streets are the gracefully curving **Hawthorne Boulevard** and **Longfellow Boulevard** (named by Pitzman for poets Nathaniel Hawthorne and Henry Wadsworth Longfellow), plus **Russell Boulevard**, **Compton Avenue**, and the boundary corridors. Compton Heights is bordered by **Tower Grove East** to the south, the **Compton Hill Reservoir Square** sub-area and **Tiffany** neighborhood to the east, and is across Grand from the Midtown and Tiffany corridors. At the 2020 census, the neighborhood had **1,396 residents** at a density of roughly 5,000 per square mile. The racial makeup was 72.6% White, 16.5% Black, 0.2% Native American, 3.6% Asian, 6.3% Two or More Races, and 0.7% Some Other Race; 3.5% of the population was of Hispanic or Latino origin.

Compton Heights occupies the northwest corner of what was previously the **St. Louis Commons**, the great communal field that the French colonial villagers used for grazing livestock, cutting firewood, and hunting game. Its initial land partition was received in the 1850s when the city limits were expanded to include the area just southwest of downtown. The prairie at that time was dotted with springs, limestone outcroppings, and rolling hills, and it had first belonged to prominent St. Louisans including **James S. Thomas**, **George I. Barnett**, and Missouri Botanical Garden founder **Henry Shaw**. The **Compton Reservoir** was completed in **1871** as the city’s population continued to expand, on what was then the highest point inside the 1855 city limits.

The Compton Heights subdivision proper — embracing Hawthorne and Longfellow Boulevards and the adjacent blocks between Grand and Nebraska — was laid out in **1888** by surveyor and urban planner **Julius Pitzman** “to correct errors he made in designing Vandeventer Place.” Pitzman, who had already designed Benton Place in Lafayette Square and would later design Forest Park and more than 40 other private streets, incorporated several unique features into his Compton Heights design: **gracefully curved streets** to create pleasant vistas and reduce traffic flow, generous landscaped setbacks, and a planning philosophy that viewed “nature as neighbor not as an enemy to be subjugated by some rectilinear grid.” The original **Compton Hill Improvement Company** was formed quietly in 1888 with a $400,000 investment (about $10 million in today’s terms) in the form of $100 shares by 33 individuals, with shipping magnate **Henry C. Haarstick** as Board President and Julius Pitzman as Secretary.

The second of Pitzman’s innovations was a contracting concept new to Missouri: **residential deed restrictions**. Lawyer **Edward C. Kehr**, one of the original shareholders, drew up the covenants — the **first such restrictions in the state** — which specified no more than one single-family residence per lot, a uniform setback from the street, fence restrictions, lot-grade restrictions, and a minimum construction cost. The Missouri Supreme Court did not uphold the principle for nearly three decades, but the deed restrictions still apply to any lot originally sold by the Compton Hill Improvement Company and proved decisive in protecting Compton Heights during the 1930s, ’40s, and ’50s, when large homes elsewhere in St. Louis were converted into rooming houses. Many of St. Louis’s first families settled here: corporate leaders of **Anheuser-Busch**, **Falstaff**, **Magic Chef**, **Monsanto Corporation**, and **Pet Incorporated** were among the early founders, and a number of homes still remain in the families of the original builders more than a century later.

The neighborhood’s most recognizable landmark, and one of the most iconic structures on the entire St. Louis skyline, is the **Compton Hill Water Tower**. Completed in **1898** at a cost of $48,000 from a design by architect **Harvey Ellis** (working for the firm of George R. Mann), the **179-foot French Romanesque Revival** standpipe tower of rusticated limestone, buff brick, and terra cotta stands on Grand Boulevard at Russell on one of the highest elevations in the city. It is the youngest and only public-accessible of the three remaining standpipe water towers in St. Louis (the others being the Bissell Street and Grand Avenue towers), and one of only seven such structures remaining in the entire United States. The tower was an immediate tourist attraction at the **1904 World’s Fair**, when as many as 5,000 visitors a day climbed its 198 iron spiral steps to the observation deck for 360-degree views of the city. Taken out of service as a standpipe in 1929, the tower was added to the **National Register of Historic Places in 1972** and underwent a $19 million restoration from 1995-1999. At the south end of the surrounding **Compton Hill Reservoir Park** (36 acres, established 1867) stands **The Naked Truth**, the 1914 bronze sculpture by Wilhelm Wandschneider commissioned by the German-American Alliance in honor of three editors of the German-language newspaper Westliche Post.

Compton Heights contains **more than 200 historic homes** of exceptional and varied architectural interest, the largest constructed during the boom that surrounded the 1904 World’s Fair. Architectural styles include **Victorian, Romanesque Revival, Colonial Revival, Tudor, and Arts and Crafts**, with large brick mansions, ornate stonework, and expansive lawns lining Hawthorne Boulevard, Longfellow Boulevard, Russell Boulevard, and Compton Avenue. The most famous of these is the **Magic Chef Mansion** at 3400 Russell Boulevard, designed by Ernst Janssen in 1908 for the founder of the Quick Meal and Magic Chef Stove Company and open today for public tours. The **Fleurs-de-Lys Mansion** has been converted into a luxury inn. The neighborhood’s **1966 ceremonial entrance markers** with their tiara emblems — designed by Compton Heights resident Frank T. Hilliker in collaboration with artist Rodney Winfield (of National Cathedral “Space Window” fame) — remain landmarks at the neighborhood’s key entries.

Compton Heights was designated a **Local Historic District in 1978**, and the broader **Compton Hill Historic District** — comprising Compton Heights, Compton Hill Reservoir Square, and part of Tower Grove East — became a Certified Local Historic District in 1979; the entire neighborhood is also a national historic district. The **Compton Heights Neighborhood Betterment Association** maintains the deed restrictions, organizes Mayor-led motorcades around the boundary on milestone anniversaries, and operates the historic **Compton Heights Concert Band** — founded by Frank Hilliker to provide music for neighborhood events, today employing 50 volunteer professionals and student musicians for 25 annual concerts, many at Tower Grove Park. Although Compton Heights itself contains no public schools, residents have access to **Shenandoah Elementary School** just across the southern boundary in Tower Grove East, along with **Long International Middle School** and **Roosevelt High School** within the St. Louis Public Schools district.

Because Compton Heights is a residential-only district by deed restriction, the businesses that serve its residents and the surrounding corridor are concentrated immediately outside its borders: along the **South Grand business district** (international restaurants, coffee shops, retail), the **Shenandoah Avenue corridor** (Crafted, Shugga’s, Van Goghz, Tanner B’s, Shaved Duck Smokehouse, Lona’s Lil Eats), and the nearby **Grand Center Arts District** with the Fox Theatre, Powell Symphony Hall, and Saint Louis University. The neighborhood is approximately four miles from downtown St. Louis, less than 10 minutes from Busch Stadium and the Fox Theatre, and directly adjacent to **Interstate 44**, which forms the northern boundary and provides fast access to Interstate 55, Interstate 64/U.S. Highway 40, and Interstate 270 throughout the metro region.

Image: The 179-foot Compton Hill Water Tower (1898), designed by Harvey Ellis in the French Romanesque Revival style and listed on the National Register of Historic Places — the centerpiece landmark of the Compton Heights neighborhood and one of only seven surviving standpipe water towers in the United States — Wikimedia Commons / Public Domain

Compton Heights sits on one of the highest elevations within the 1855 St. Louis city limits, where the climate follows a classic Midwest four-season pattern. Hot, humid summers with highs regularly reaching the upper 80s°F drive peak outdoor activity in Compton Hill Reservoir Park, on the curving Hawthorne and Longfellow Boulevards, at the open-air Magic Chef Mansion tours, and along the South Grand patios just west of the neighborhood. Cool springs and falls are the busiest seasons for tree-lined streetscape walks, the open Saturday-and-full-moon water tower viewings (when restoration permits), Compton Heights Concert Band performances at Tower Grove Park, and the neighborhood’s seasonal house tours and historic preservation events. Cold winters with periodic snow and ice shift commerce indoors to the Shenandoah Avenue restaurants (Crafted, Van Goghz, Tanner B’s, Shaved Duck Smokehouse, Lona’s Lil Eats), the South Grand international corridor, and the Grand Center cultural venues just north across I-44. The elevation gives the neighborhood slightly cooler summer evenings and somewhat more exposure to winter wind than the river-bottom corridors. The 1880s-1910s brick, stone, and terra-cotta construction of the Compton Hill Water Tower, the Magic Chef Mansion, and the Hawthorne and Longfellow mansions has weathered nearly a century and a half of Missouri climate with remarkable durability.

## Covenant Blu Grand Center — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/covenant-blu-grand-center

Ranked by 11+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  Home of the 1929 Fox Theatre & Powell Symphony Hall ✓  Grand Center Arts District & CID ✓  Avg. rating: 4.6/5 across 11 reviews

## Covenant Blu-Grand Center Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Covenant Blu-Grand Center, St. Louis

Covenant Blu-Grand Center serves as the premier cultural and artistic hub of St. Louis — home to the Grand Center Arts District (the city’s “Broadway” corridor), anchored by the 1929 Fox Theatre, Powell Symphony Hall, the Sheldon Concert Hall, the Contemporary Art Museum, St. Alphonsus “Rock” Church (1867), Third Baptist Church (1885), Saint Louis University, and the Grand Center Community Improvement District — offering theaters, museums, breweries, restaurants, professional services, and arts organizations across midtown St. Louis.

Covenant Blu-Grand Center is one of St. Louis’s 79 official neighborhoods, located in the center of the city at **38.6435° N, 90.2427° W**. Its official boundaries run north along **Dr. Martin Luther King Drive**, south along **Olive Street and Lindell Boulevard**, east along **North Compton Avenue**, and west along **Vandeventer Avenue**. The neighborhood is bordered by **JeffVanderLou** to the north, **Vandeventer** to the southwest, **Midtown** and **Saint Louis University** to the south, **Central West End** to the southwest, and **The Greater Ville** to the northwest. The 2020 census recorded the neighborhood’s population at **2,710 residents**. Notable internal streets include **North Grand Boulevard, Washington Avenue, Delmar Boulevard, Locust Street, Olive Street, Lindell Boulevard, North Spring Avenue, and North Theresa Avenue**. The neighborhood includes the famous “Bright White Way” corridor of Grand Boulevard between Lindell and Delmar.

The neighborhood’s most consequential cultural era began in the **1920s**, when Grand Boulevard between Lindell and Delmar emerged as St. Louis’s “Bright White Way” — a glittering corridor of nightclubs, ballrooms, restaurants, and **six grand theatres** that dominated the city’s nightlife from the 1920s through the 1950s. The most spectacular of these was the **Fox Theatre** at 527 North Grand Boulevard, built in **1929 by movie pioneer William Fox** (founder of 20th Century Fox) as the second-largest theatre in the United States with **5,060 seats** (4,500 after restoration). It was designed by architect **Howard Crane** in a striking **“Siamese-Byzantine Baroque”** style with a 90-foot grand lobby modeled after ancient Indian religious buildings, columns, crimson and gold accents, a signature elephant carpet, and a 2,000-pound chandelier. The Fox opened on January 31, 1929, was one of five spectacular Fox Theatres built in the late 1920s (alongside Brooklyn, Atlanta, Detroit, and San Francisco), and was designated a **St. Louis City Landmark in 1975** and added to the **National Register of Historic Places on October 8, 1976**.

After decades of decline, the Fox Theatre was **completely restored in 1982** in a one-year, $2 million-plus restoration spearheaded by St. Louis philanthropist **Mary Strauss** — the elephant carpet was rewoven, the chandelier was lowered and relamped, and all 4,500 seats were renovated and reinstalled to bring the theatre “as closely as possible to its 1929 state of magnificence.” The Fox reopened in September 1982 and has anchored the modern Grand Center Arts District ever since. The neighborhood’s primary coordinating body, **Grand Center Inc.**, was established in **1981** as a non-profit urban redevelopment corporation; it manages the **Grand Center Community Improvement District** with over 100 property and business owners and maintains a master database of hundreds of arts organizations, restaurants, and community institutions throughout the district. The **Kranzberg Arts Foundation** operates several eclectic contemporary venues including **The Marcelle Theatre** and **High Low**, providing affordable performance and gathering space for grassroots artists and smaller arts organizations.

The Grand Center Arts District’s performing arts anchors today include **Powell Symphony Hall** (home of the St. Louis Symphony Orchestra), the **Sheldon Concert Hall**, the **Grandel Theatre**, **Jazz St. Louis** (formerly Jazz at the Bistro), the **Centene Center for the Arts**, and the **Metro Theater Company** (a nationally recognized professional theater for young audiences and families). The visual arts cluster includes the **Contemporary Art Museum St. Louis (CAM)**, the **Pulitzer Arts Foundation**, the **Saint Louis University Museum of Art (SLUMA)**, the **Vaughn Cultural Center**, the **Bruno David Gallery**, and the **Kranzberg Arts Center**. The **Moto Museum** uniquely dedicates an entire facility to motorcycles. Part of the neighborhood falls within the **Midtown Historic District**, listed on the National Register of Historic Places.

The neighborhood’s historic religious institutions include the **Third Baptist Church** at the heart of the Arts District (congregation established 1850, present building completed 1885) and the **St. Alphonsus Liguori “Rock” Catholic Church** on North Grand Boulevard — a striking Gothic Revival landmark whose cornerstone was laid in **1867** and whose walls were built of locally quarried limestone, earning its nickname “The Rock” and designation as a **St. Louis City Landmark**. The neighborhood’s educational anchors include the **Clyde C. Miller Career Academy** high school, the **Grand Center Arts Academy** (opened 2010, an arts-focused independent public charter school serving grades 6–12), **Cardinal Ritter College Prep High School** (private co-ed Catholic), **Loyola Academy of St. Louis** (private Catholic middle school for boys), and **Preclarus Mastery Academy** (5–12 independent charter). Just south of the neighborhood lies **Saint Louis University**, whose campus extends along Lindell Boulevard.

The neighborhood’s community life is anchored by annual events centered on **Strauss Park** at the intersection of Grand and Washington. Summer brings the free **“Thursdays @ the Intersection”** outdoor concert and movie series; spring features the **Art Walk** on the third Friday in May with simultaneously open museums and galleries plus live entertainment; fall hosts the **“Dancing in the Street Festival”** on the last Saturday in September with 85+ performances spanning ballet, hip-hop, flamenco, salsa, belly dance, tap, and more across multiple outdoor stages; and the annual **First Night** celebration runs each New Year’s Eve. According to the 2020 census, 97.3% of the neighborhood’s housing stock is studios or one- and two-bedroom units (one of the highest concentrations of small living spaces in America), 92.1% of real estate is classified as apartment complexes or high-rise apartments (among the highest in the nation), and 95.2% is renter-occupied — reflecting both the district’s student population (Saint Louis University, Grand Center Arts Academy) and its growing urban-redevelopment housing pipeline. Many historic residences in the neighborhood predate 1939, with new construction also occurring between 2000 and the present.

Image: The Fox Theatre at 527 North Grand Boulevard — designed by architect Howard Crane in Siamese-Byzantine Baroque style and opened January 31, 1929 by movie pioneer William Fox as the second-largest theatre in the United States, restored in 1982 by St. Louis philanthropist Mary Strauss, designated a St. Louis City Landmark 1975, and added to the National Register of Historic Places 1976 — Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA

Covenant Blu-Grand Center sits on the central plateau of midtown St. Louis at 38.6435° N, with Grand Boulevard’s historic “Bright White Way” corridor running north-south through its heart. The neighborhood experiences St. Louis’s full humid continental climate: hot, humid summers with frequent 90°F days, cold winters with regular freezing temperatures, and a notably active spring severe weather season including real tornado corridor exposure. Peak outdoor activity falls during spring and fall, when Strauss Park’s “Thursdays @ the Intersection” outdoor concert and movie series, the May Art Walk, the September “Dancing in the Street Festival”, the Saint Louis University campus events, and the Fox Theatre marquee draw the heaviest neighborhood and visitor use. 

Cool seasons drive intense indoor activity at the Fox Theatre Broadway series, Powell Symphony Hall concerts, the Sheldon Concert Hall, Jazz St. Louis, the Grandel Theatre, the Contemporary Art Museum, the Pulitzer Arts Foundation, the Saint Louis University Museum of Art, the Kranzberg Arts Center, High Low and The Marcelle, the Moto Museum, and the area’s restaurants including Stage Left Grill, Curtain Call Lounge, and Urban Chestnut Brewing Company. Winter shifts commerce indoors to the First Night New Year’s Eve celebration, the indoor arts venues, the Saint Louis University academic year, and Saint Alphonsus Liguori “Rock” Church and Third Baptist Church services. The neighborhood’s 1920s “Bright White Way” theatre district, the 1867 Rock Church, the 1885 Third Baptist Church, the 1929 Fox Theatre, and the modern Kranzberg-era contemporary venues anchor more than 150 years of layered midtown architectural and cultural heritage.

## DeBaliviere Place — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/debaliviere-place

Ranked by 60+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  Home of Kingsbury Place & Washington Terrace (1890s private streets) ✓  Forest Park-DeBaliviere MetroLink hub (Red & Blue Lines) ✓  Avg. rating: 4.7/5 across 60 reviews

## DeBaliviere Place Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 DeBaliviere Place, St. Louis

DeBaliviere Place serves as one of St. Louis’s most architecturally elegant Central Corridor neighborhoods — built on an original 1776 Spanish land grant to Madame Marie Louise Papin, anchored by the historic private streets of Kingsbury Place and Washington Terrace (laid out by Julius Pitzman in the 1890s with iconic gates designed by Harvey Ellis), the Forest Park-DeBaliviere MetroLink station (where the Red and Blue Lines split), and the dense 1910–1925 apartment corridor along Pershing Avenue — offering real estate, interior design, professional consulting, private care, and Forest Park-adjacent businesses across the Central West End’s western edge.

DeBaliviere Place is one of St. Louis’s 79 official neighborhoods, located in the city’s Central Corridor at **38.6494° N, 90.2845° W**. Its official boundaries run north along **Delmar Boulevard**, south along **Lindell Boulevard** (overlooking Forest Park), east along **Union Boulevard**, and west along **DeBaliviere Avenue**. The neighborhood is bordered by the **West End** and **Visitation Park** to the north, the **Central West End** to the east, **Forest Park** to the south, and **Skinker-DeBaliviere** to the west. The 2020 census recorded the neighborhood’s racial composition as 55.0% White, 25.0% Black, 10.9% Asian, 6.9% Two or More Races, 1.9% Some Other Race, and 0.3% Native American, with 5.0% of Hispanic or Latino origin. Major internal streets include **Pershing Avenue, Waterman Boulevard, Kingsbury Place, Washington Terrace, Clara Avenue, Clemens Avenue, Enright Avenue, and DeBaliviere Avenue** itself. The 2010 census counted 3,466 residents in zip code 63112.

The land that became DeBaliviere Place was originally part of a **2,720-acre Spanish land grant** made in **1776 to Madame Marie Louise Papin** — granted in response to her request for a farm on the banks of the River des Peres. By the mid-1800s, ownership had passed to a roster of names still embedded in the surrounding street grid: **Peter Lindell, William McPherson, Pierre Chouteau Jr., Jules DeMun, William Forsyth, John C. Cabanne, and Thomas K. Skinker**. Most of the land west of Kingshighway remained rural in nature with large estates and farms. By 1875, Compton and Dry’s *Pictorial St. Louis* showed the west side of Union dotted with country houses, including the Waterman family’s large Victorian mansion with a square tower near Forest Park, and the home of **Daniel Bell**, whose estate at the southwest corner of Delmar and Union was later developed into Washington Terrace.

The transformation from rural estates to elegant urban neighborhood was driven by the anticipation of the **1904 World’s Fair**, for which **DeBaliviere Avenue served as the main entrance**. The northern part of the neighborhood’s private streets — **Kingsbury Place** and **Washington Terrace** — were platted in the early 1890s for the **Bell Place Realty Company** by famed surveyor **Julius Pitzman** (the same Pitzman who laid out Compton Heights, Westmoreland Place, and Portland Place). The famous Washington Terrace entrance gatehouse — designed by journeyman draftsman **Harvey Ellis** in 1894 in a striking French Norman style with conical towers reminiscent of the gates to the medieval German city of Lübeck — was built by architect **George R. Mann**. The structure features two flanking pillars topped with terra cotta lions made by **Winkle Terra Cotta of St. Louis** and wrought-iron arches (called “overthrows”) crowning the drives. The white stone classic-style gates of Kingsbury Place, the nude bronze statue “Awakening Spring,” and the Kingsbury Place fountain were all completed in **1908**.

Most early development took the form of large mansions on these private streets, designed by some of St. Louis’s most respected architects including **Hellmuth & Hellmuth, Hellmuth & Spiering, Mauran, Labeaume, Russell and Crowell**, and **Isaac Taylor** (the chief architect of the 1904 World’s Fair, who designed one of the Washington Terrace mansions). The private streets feature Italian Renaissance, Tudor Revival, Georgian Revival, Victorian Revival, French Eclectic, Colonial Revival, Beaux Arts, and Richardsonian Romanesque homes — with notable examples including 6 Kingsbury Place (1912, Italian Renaissance by Hellmuth & Hellmuth), 20 Kingsbury Place (1911, Italian Renaissance), 15 Kingsbury Place (1906, three-story Beaux Arts), 39 Kingsbury Place (1909, Colonial Revival by Hellmuth & Spiering), 63 Kingsbury Place (1915, Colonial Revival by Hellmuth & Hellmuth), and 48 Washington Terrace (1909, Tudor Revival by Hellmuth & Spiering). **Washington Terrace became a St. Louis City Landmark in 1973**, and the combined **Kingsbury-Washington Terrace City Historic District** was created in **1983** by Ordinance #56581. The larger **Waterman Place-Kingsbury Place-Washington Terrace Historic District** was listed on the **National Register of Historic Places in 2007**, with 223 contributing buildings, four contributing structures, and a contributing site across 66.7 acres. The district is also the westernmost part of the **Central West End Historic District**, established in 1974.

After the 1904 Fair, construction shifted to the elegant apartment buildings along **Pershing Avenue, Waterman Boulevard, and Delmar Boulevard**. Most of the apartments on the south side of Pershing — one of the most densely populated blocks in the entire St. Louis region, with buildings ranging from **five to 17 stories tall** — were built between **1910 and 1925**. A streetcar ran down Pershing connecting commuters to downtown jobs. Between 1920 and 1950, the **“DeBaliviere Strip”** (DeBaliviere Avenue itself) was a major commercial street featuring chain drug and food stores, the **Apollo Theater**, the iconic **Parkmoor Restaurant**, and **Garavelli’s Restaurant**. After World War II, the Strip declined into cheap bars and nightclubs whose decline spread to surrounding blocks. The 1950s westward migration from the demolished Mill Creek Valley project area created stress on the housing stock, and by the late 1970s a Fortune magazine article described the neighborhood as “the dangerous, semideserted mess” where “you needed an armed guard to go there.”

In **1977**, the **Pershing-Waterman Urban Renewal project** initiated by **Leon Strauss’s Pantheon Corporation** took control of many properties and successfully redeveloped DeBaliviere Place as an upper-middle-class district — an effort that, while controversial for displacing many low-income African-American residents, was a major factor in stabilizing the western Central Corridor. Later phases along Delmar facing the Regional Medical Center (now St. Louis ConnectCare) included affordable housing for elderly residents, and the **Kingsbury Terrace public housing apartments** were built in the early 1980s. Today the neighborhood is anchored by the **Forest Park-DeBaliviere MetroLink station** — the only stop in the city where both the Red and Blue Lines split, providing service to Lambert Airport via UMSL, Shrewsbury via Clayton, and Scott Air Force Base via Downtown. The neighborhood is across the street from **Forest Park** (with the Missouri History Museum and the Norman K. Probstein Golf Course), adjacent to the Central West End, the Delmar Loop, Washington University, and **Barnes-Jewish Hospital** (the city’s largest employer). The neighborhood is served by the St. Louis Public School District and home to the LEED Platinum-certified **Crossroads College Preparatory School** on DeBaliviere Avenue. Notable houses of worship include the **Westminster Presbyterian Church** at Delmar and Union and the **Grace and Peace Fellowship** on Delmar near Clara (which runs a winter women’s shelter and food pantry). A DeBaliviere Place Special Business District levies a tax of $0.85 per $100 valuation through 2034 to fund dedicated security and infrastructure oversight.

Image: The 1894 Washington Terrace gatehouse — the French Norman clock tower designed by Harvey Ellis and built by architect George R. Mann, marking the south entrance to the historic Washington Terrace private street platted by Julius Pitzman in the early 1890s, designated a St. Louis City Landmark 1973 and part of the National Register-listed Waterman Place-Kingsbury Place-Washington Terrace Historic District — Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA

DeBaliviere Place sits on the rolling Central Corridor terrain at 38.6494° N, just north of Forest Park’s 1,300-acre treasure. The neighborhood experiences St. Louis’s full humid continental climate: hot, humid summers with frequent 90°F days, cold winters with regular freezing temperatures, and a notably active spring severe weather season including real tornado corridor exposure. Peak outdoor activity falls during spring and fall, when the Forest Park trails immediately south of Lindell Boulevard, the Missouri History Museum, the Norman K. Probstein Golf Course, summer concerts in the park, the private streets of Kingsbury Place and Washington Terrace (with their architectural tours during the Central West End Association House & Garden Tour each May), and the Forest Park-DeBaliviere MetroLink plaza draw the heaviest neighborhood and visitor use. 

Cool seasons drive intense indoor activity at the neighborhood’s dense apartment buildings along Pershing and Delmar, the Crossroads College Preparatory School, the Westminster Presbyterian and Grace and Peace Fellowship congregations, and the adjacent Delmar Loop dining and Central West End restaurant corridors. Winter shifts commerce to the Loop, the Central West End, and the indoor venues at Washington University and Barnes-Jewish Hospital just minutes away by MetroLink. The neighborhood’s 1890s private-street mansion stock, the 1910–1925 Pershing/Waterman/Delmar apartment buildings, and the modern infill projects around the MetroLink station have weathered more than 130 years of Missouri climate with the durability typical of the Central Corridor’s World’s Fair-era construction.

## Downtown — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/downtown

Ranked by 751+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  10 of 10 verified Downtown St. Louis corridor providers ✓  All businesses verified via Google Business ✓  Avg. rating: 4.9/5 across 751 reviews

## Downtown Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Downtown St. Louis

Downtown St. Louis is the central business district of the city and the original 1764 founding site of St. Louis — anchored today by the 630-foot Gateway Arch (the world’s tallest arch and the tallest monument in the Western Hemisphere), the Old Courthouse (site of the Dred Scott case), Busch Stadium, Ballpark Village, the Dome at America’s Center, the Basilica of St. Louis King of France (the Old Cathedral), the Enterprise Center, and the Washington Avenue Loft District — offering coworking, marketing agencies, B2B lead generation, business consulting, and career development services in the corridor that draws nearly 4 million annual Arch visitors and houses a growing residential population of approximately 9,000.

Downtown sits at the heart of St. Louis at **38.6250° N, 90.2018° W** in zip codes 63101, 63102, and 63103. Per the City of St. Louis, the neighborhood’s boundaries are **Cole Street** on the North (with a northeastern extension to I-70 / Carr Street / Leonor K. Sullivan Boulevard), the **Mississippi River** on the East, **Chouteau Avenue** on the South, and **Tucker Boulevard (12th Street)** on the West. Downtown borders **Columbus Square and the Bottle District** to the north (across Cole Street), the **Mississippi River and East St. Louis (Illinois)** across the river to the east, **LaSalle Park and Soulard** to the south (across Chouteau), and **Downtown West** to the west (across Tucker). The St. Louis Downtown Neighborhood Association reports a resident population of **approximately 9,000** and growing each year, with **81% renting and 19% owning**. The neighborhood is part of the central corridor of the city, which in 2020 was **50.9% White, 32.6% Black, 8.3% Asian, 5.9% two or more races, and 4.3% Hispanic/Latino**.

Downtown St. Louis was originally laid out in **1764** by French fur trader **Pierre Laclede Liguest** and his 13-year-old scout **Auguste Chouteau**, who selected the site as a fur trading post for its high ground (not prone to flooding) and proximity to the confluence of the Mississippi and Missouri Rivers. Although named after King Louis IX of France, the early settlement was called “Laclede’s Village” by its inhabitants, with three principal north-south streets that are now First, Second, and Third Streets. By 1766 the village had a population of about 300 and 75 buildings; by 1804 (the Louisiana Purchase year), it contained 180 houses and 900 people. After the **1817 arrival of the steamboat**, St. Louis became a vital center of American commerce. The city was incorporated as the “Town of St. Louis” in **1809**. Downtown streets shed their old French names in 1822 and were renumbered (north-south) and renamed after trees (east-west) following the Philadelphia plan.

The single most defining feature of modern Downtown St. Louis is the **Gateway Arch** — the 630-foot stainless-steel weighted-catenary monument designed by Finnish-American architect **Eero Saarinen** in 1947, with construction running from February 12, 1963 to October 28, 1965 (the monument opened to the public on June 10, 1967). The Arch is the world’s tallest arch, Missouri’s tallest accessible structure, the tallest monument in the Western Hemisphere, and a National Historic Landmark — receiving **nearly 4 million visitors annually**. It sits within the **192.83-acre Gateway Arch National Park** (the smallest national park in the United States), which also includes the **Old Courthouse** — the Greek Revival 19th-century building where the **Dred Scott trials** originated. The Park’s $380 million **CityArchRiver renovation**, completed in 2018, added **Luther Ely Smith Square** (a “park over the highway” capping I-44) and a 140,000-square-foot underground museum, finally reconnecting the Arch grounds to Downtown after decades of highway separation.

The Arch’s construction came at significant historical cost. Between 1939 and 1941, city leaders demolished **40 city blocks** along the riverfront — what had been the largest array of cast-iron buildings in the world — clearing 82.5 acres of “blighted” land that was, in reality, a bustling district of 290 active businesses employing nearly 5,000 workers, nearly 200 residences, and a large African American population. For decades after the demolitions, the cleared riverfront sat as a giant gravel parking lot. The later **Mill Creek Valley clearance** (1959) displaced more than 20,000 residents, 95% of whom were Black, and destroyed nearly 300 Black businesses. Today the Arch stands as both an internationally celebrated symbol and an enduring reminder of mid-century urban renewal’s racial inequalities.

Beyond the Arch grounds, Downtown is anchored by an exceptional concentration of sports, convention, and corporate infrastructure. **Busch Stadium** (home of the St. Louis Cardinals, opened 2006) sits beside **Ballpark Village** — a $100 million first-phase mixed-use district that opened before the 2014 MLB season with bars, restaurants, the Cardinals Hall of Fame and Museum, and 720-space parking. The $260 million second phase added a 29-story residential tower (**One Cardinal Way**, 297 units), a 10-story office building, an 8-story Live! by Loews hotel, and over 200 annual events. The **Enterprise Center** hosts the St. Louis Blues NHL franchise; the **Dome at America’s Center** anchors the convention complex, which includes Cervantes Convention Center, the St. Louis Executive Conference Center, and the Ferrara Theatre. The **Basilica of St. Louis King of France** (the Old Cathedral, 1834) sits within the Arch grounds and remains the oldest Catholic cathedral west of the Mississippi.

Downtown’s commercial corridor is anchored by **Washington Avenue** — once the nation’s largest concentration of garment manufacturing and now a National Register Historic District of converted lofts, restaurants, and entertainment venues. The corridor stretches from the **Old Post Office** (1884, federal Renaissance Revival) through to the **Central Library** (Carnegie’s last and largest funded library, designed by Cass Gilbert, opened 1912 and restored 2012). Downtown is the site of major corporate headquarters including **Stifel Financial Corp., HOK, and Spire Inc.**, and houses landmark towers like **One Metropolitan Square** (the tallest building in St. Louis at 593 feet, opened 1989) and the historic **Wainwright Building** (Louis Sullivan, 1891 — considered the world’s first true skyscraper). The **Marquette Building** (1913, Chicago School), the Railway Exchange Building, and the Old Post Office round out a downtown rich in Chicago School, Beaux Arts, and Art Deco landmark architecture.

Downtown St. Louis’s modern revitalization began in earnest with the 20-year **“Downtown Now!”** plan launched in 2000 — driving over $4 billion of investment between 1999 and 2006 — and continues today with the **“Design Downtown”** initiative (2020-2030). Residences are predominantly converted lofts, condos, and apartments along Washington Avenue, near Ballpark Village, and in landmark conversions like the Marquette and the Old Post Office. The neighborhood is served by **MetroLink** (with multiple downtown stations including Stadium and Convention Center) and **MetroBus**, with quick access to I-70, I-64, and I-44. With the Gateway Arch as its global symbol, Busch Stadium and Ballpark Village as its sports anchor, Washington Avenue as its loft and entertainment spine, and a growing 24/7 residential community, Downtown is St. Louis’s signature mixed-use neighborhood. The listings on this page draw from the Downtown / Washington Avenue / Ballpark Village professional corridor.

Image: The Gateway Arch and reflecting pool, Downtown St. Louis — the 630-foot stainless-steel weighted-catenary monument designed by Eero Saarinen, the world’s tallest arch and the tallest monument in the Western Hemisphere, opened to the public on June 10, 1967 — Wikimedia Commons / CC BY-SA 3.0 by Daniel Schwen

Downtown St. Louis sits at the western bank of the Mississippi River, where the city follows a classic Midwest four-season pattern. Hot, humid summers with highs in the upper 80s°F bring peak activity to the Gateway Arch grounds, the 91-acre Gateway Arch National Park, Ballpark Village patios, Cardinals home games at Busch Stadium, Kiener Plaza, and the Mississippi River levee. The CityArchRiver project’s 1.5-mile riverfront promenade draws joggers and walkers from spring through fall, while Washington Avenue’s sidewalk cafes and the Locust Central Business District hum with activity through warm-weather months.

Cold winters with periodic snow shift commerce indoors to the coworking and event hubs that define Downtown — STL Fusion on Washington Avenue, Spark Coworking at Ballpark Village (6 Cardinal Way), TechArtista Downtown on Pine Street, T-REX’s tech-incubator hub at 911 Washington, and the climate-controlled Dome at America’s Center and Enterprise Center, which host Blues hockey and major conventions year-round. The Marquette Building, One Metropolitan Square, and the Old Post Office anchor downtown winter office activity, while the 1.5-mile-long network of skywalks and the Gateway Arch museum keep visitors moving even on the coldest January days.

## Downtown West — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/downtown-west

Ranked by 2,394+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  Home of the City Museum, Washington Avenue Historic District & Enterprise Center ✓  Loft District & Garment District legacy ✓  Avg. rating: 4.8/5 across 2,394 reviews

## Downtown West Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Downtown West, St. Louis

Downtown West serves as one of St. Louis’s most active urban revitalization corridors — the bridge between the central business district and Midtown anchored by the Washington Avenue Historic District (the former Garment District nicknamed “Shoe Street, USA”), Bob Cassilly’s legendary City Museum in the former International Shoe building, the Enterprise Center, the Campbell House Museum (1851), Saint Louis City Hall, the Central Library, and the Locust Central Business District — offering coworking, B2B marketing, government services, experiential agencies, dispensaries, historic event spaces, and adaptive-reuse loft tenants across the heart of Downtown St. Louis.

Downtown West is one of St. Louis’s 79 official neighborhoods, located in the central business district at **38.6294° N, 90.2171° W**. Its official boundaries run north along **Cole Street**, south along **Chouteau Avenue**, east along **Tucker Boulevard**, and west along **Jefferson Avenue**. The neighborhood is bordered by **JeffVanderLou** and **Carr Square** to the north, **Downtown** proper to the east (Tucker Boulevard divides Downtown from Downtown West), **LaSalle Park** and **Peabody-Darst-Webbe** to the south across Chouteau, and **Midtown** to the west across Jefferson. The 2020 census recorded the neighborhood’s racial composition as 46.2% White, 41.0% Black, 6.0% Two or More Races, 4.0% Asian, 2.3% Some Other Race, and 0.4% Native American. Important internal streets include **Market Street, Olive Street, Locust Street, Washington Avenue, Dr. Martin Luther King Drive, Delmar Boulevard, 14th Street, 16th Street, 18th Street, Chestnut Street, Pine Street**, and **Interstate 64/40**. The neighborhood is served by two MetroLink stations and the **St. Louis Gateway Transportation Center**.

The neighborhood’s defining historic asset is the **Washington Avenue Historic District**, listed on the National Register of Historic Places in **1987**. The district runs along Washington Avenue bounded by Delmar Boulevard to the north, Locust Street to the south, 8th Street on the east, and 18th Street on the west, with buildings dating from the late 19th century to the early 1920s exhibiting Italianate, Romanesque Revival, Renaissance Revival, Chicago Commercial, and Beaux Arts styles. For more than half a century from the late 1800s through the mid-1900s, this corridor was the epicenter of St. Louis’s **garment-making industry**, lined by clothing factories and warehouses. The avenue earned the nickname **“Shoe Street, USA”** for its concentration of footwear manufacturers, including the **International Shoe Company** — once the largest shoe manufacturer in the United States — whose buildings still anchor the district today.

Beginning in the **1990s**, the Washington Avenue corridor experienced one of the most dramatic urban revitalizations in American urban history. In **1998**, the State of Missouri adopted a tax credit for the redevelopment of historic buildings, making large-scale renovation financially feasible. Local and national developers acquired buildings throughout the district and redeveloped them as loft-style condominiums and apartments, creating the **Washington Avenue Loft District** — now one of the densest and most distinctive residential corridors in the central city. The catalyzing project came when sculptor **Bob Cassilly** (creator of the Dallas Zoo’s 67.5-foot giraffe and the St. Louis Zoo’s 50-foot squid) and his wife **Gail Cassilly** began acquiring the vacant 600,000-square-foot former **International Shoe Company building** at **750 North 16th Street** in 1993, opening **City Museum** there on **October 25, 1997**.

City Museum quickly became one of the most acclaimed urban playgrounds in the world — a 600,000-square-foot, 10-story collection of repurposed industrial and architectural artifacts including school buses, airplane fuselages, fire engines, salvaged bridges, construction cranes, retired smokestacks, and gargoyles from Europe. Built entirely by Cassilly and his 20-member **“Cassilly Crew”** of welders, sculptors, carpenters, and painters, it features the **Enchanted Caves and Shoe Shafts** (opened 2003 with a 1924 Wurlitzer Pipe Organ from New York City’s Rivoli Theater added in 2007), spiral slides from the Shoe Shafts that once moved International Shoe’s product, a 10-story slide from the rooftop, a giant whale in the lobby, an in-house circus (operated by **Circus Harmony**), the Vault Room with two 3,000-pound vault doors from the 1870s First National Bank of St. Louis, and a 3,000-pound praying mantis on the roof. The museum was named one of the “great public spaces” by the Project for Public Spaces and attracted more than 700,000 visitors in 2010. Cassilly served as artistic director until his death in 2011; in **2019**, the museum was purchased by Oklahoma City-based **Premier Parks** but retains its DIY spirit.

Downtown West also contains the **Campbell House Museum** at 1508 Locust Street — built in **1851** and occupied by prominent St. Louis businessman **Robert Campbell** and his family until **1938**, now restored as a period museum following a multi-million-dollar renovation. Other major historic and civic landmarks include **Saint Louis City Hall** at 1200 Market Street (the seat of city government and home to the St. Louis Business Assistance Center), the **Central Library** (an Italian Renaissance-style architectural gem), the **National Blues Museum**, **Union Station**, the historic **Soldiers Memorial Military Museum**, and the **Lucas Avenue Industrial Historic District**. Notable additional National Register districts that touch Downtown West include the **Olive Street Terra Cotta District**, the **Plaza Square Apartments Historic District**, and the **Olive and Locust Historic Business District**.

The modern Downtown West business landscape combines major corporate headquarters — including **Ameren Corporation, Anthem Blue Cross Blue Shield, CCPI Corporation, the Metropolitan Sewer District (MSD)**, and the **Main US Post Office** — with the **Enterprise Center** arena (home of the St. Louis Blues NHL franchise), the **Locust Central Business District** (a Special Business District established by City Ordinance #58728 active since at least 1984, managing the “live-work-play” corridor that connects Downtown to Midtown), and dozens of adaptive-reuse coworking and creative-agency spaces. The neighborhood is the city’s primary transit hub with the **Civic Center MetroLink station**, the **Stadium MetroLink station**, and the **Gateway Transportation Center** serving Amtrak, Greyhound, and regional bus service. Major employers and creative anchors include experiential marketing agency **Momentum Worldwide**, B2B lead-generation powerhouse **Abstrakt Marketing Group**, and a dense corridor of marketing firms, coworking spaces, and boutique professional services along Washington Avenue, Olive Street, Locust Street, and Chestnut Street.

Image: The St. Louis City Museum exterior at 750 North 16th Street — the 600,000-square-foot former International Shoe Company building (a contributing structure to the National Register-listed Washington Avenue Historic District, 1987), transformed by sculptor Bob Cassilly and his Cassilly Crew into one of the world’s most acclaimed artist-built urban playgrounds and opened October 25, 1997 — Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA

Downtown West sits on the central plateau of St. Louis at 38.6294° N, bordered on its eastern edge by Tucker Boulevard (the historic dividing line between Downtown proper and Downtown West) and extending west to Jefferson Avenue. The neighborhood experiences St. Louis’s full humid continental climate: hot, humid summers with frequent 90°F days, cold winters with regular freezing temperatures, and a notably active spring severe weather season including real tornado corridor exposure. Peak outdoor activity falls during spring and fall, when the Washington Avenue Loft District foot traffic peaks, the City Museum’s rooftop opens (typically March through September), the Enterprise Center hosts Blues games and concerts, the National Blues Museum and Campbell House Museum tours draw heaviest visitors, and the Locust Central Business District’s live-music venues and craft bars activate the corridor. 

Cool seasons drive intense indoor activity at the City Museum’s 11 floors of indoor exhibits, the Enterprise Center, the Central Library’s Italian Renaissance reading rooms, Cookies dispensary, Cookies’ downtown corridor restaurants and breweries, STL Fusion and Monkey Building coworking spaces, the Civic Center MetroLink station, and the marketing and B2B agencies along Chestnut and Locust. Winter shifts commerce indoors to the Washington Avenue loft retail, the Enterprise Center event calendar, City Hall and the Business Assistance Center, and the dense corporate office towers including Ameren and Anthem. The neighborhood’s late-19th-century industrial brick warehouses, the early-1920s Chicago Commercial and Beaux Arts garment-district structures, and the 1931 International Shoe Company Annex (now City Museum) have weathered more than 120 years of Missouri climate with the durability typical of St. Louis’s manufacturing-era construction.

## Dutchtown — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/dutchtown

Ranked by 392+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  St. Louis’s historic German-American “Deutsch” neighborhood ✓  Home of St. Anthony of Padua, Marquette Park & the Stork Inn ✓  Avg. rating: 4.5/5 across 392 reviews

## Dutchtown Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Dutchtown, St. Louis

Dutchtown serves as the historic southern center of St. Louis’s German-American (“Deutsch”) settlement — anchored by the twin steeples of St. Anthony of Padua Catholic Church (1910) at 3140 Meramec, the 17-acre Marquette Park (1915), the Anheuser-Busch Stork Inn at Virginia and Taft, Ted Drewes Frozen Custard’s 1931 Grand Boulevard location, and the “Downtown Dutchtown” corridor along Meramec Street — offering commercial printing, community development, microbusiness incubation, fuel distribution, retail, and B2B services across South St. Louis.

Dutchtown is one of St. Louis’s 79 official neighborhoods, located in South St. Louis at **38.5784° N, 90.2536° W** and covering 1.52 square miles. Its official boundaries run north along **Chippewa Street**, east along **Jefferson Avenue to Meramec Street to Compton Avenue**, south along **Walsh Street**, and west along the **Missouri Pacific Railroad**. The neighborhood is bordered by **Tower Grove South** to the north, **Gravois Park** to the northeast, **Marine Villa** to the east, **Carondelet** and **Holly Hills** to the south, **Mount Pleasant** to the northwest, and **Bevo Mill** to the west. The 2020 census recorded 15,356 residents at a density of 10,100 per square mile, making Dutchtown one of the most densely populated neighborhoods in the city. The 2020 racial composition was 50.7% Black, 26.0% White, 7.0% Asian, 8.3% Two or More Races, 7.3% Some Other Race, 0.5% Native American, with 12.1% Hispanic or Latino origin. Important internal streets include **Meramec Street, Virginia Avenue, South Grand Boulevard, Chippewa Street, Compton Avenue, Broadway, Gravois Road, Park Avenue, Osage Street, Gasconade Street, Louisiana Avenue, Minnesota Avenue**, and **South Broadway**. ZIP codes serving the neighborhood are **63111, 63116**, and **63118**.

The name **Dutchtown** comes from *Deutsch* — meaning “German” — reflecting the neighborhood’s identity as the southern center of **German-American settlement** in St. Louis in the early 19th century. As originally surveyed, the portion of present-day Dutchtown south of Chippewa to Bates Street was part of the **Commonfield of Carondelet**, which the City of St. Louis annexed in **1870**. In 1875, the area remained largely rural in character, but rapid development was already underway north of Chippewa and east of Jefferson. Between **1880 and 1920**, Dutchtown grew explosively as German immigrants flocked to the newly opened southeast sections of the expanded city, shedding its rural character as sturdy brick homes and shops popped up around newly built **streetcar lines on Meramec, Chippewa, Gravois, Grand, and Jefferson**. Most of the buildings still standing in Dutchtown today were built during this period, in a mix of late-19th-century Italianate, Foursquare, bungalow, Craftsman, Spanish, Classical Revival, Second Empire, Tudor, and 1-story brick “shotgun” styles distinctive to the South St. Louis brick tradition.

The neighborhood was the original site of **Concordia Seminary** (before its relocation to Clayton, Missouri), **Concordia Publishing House**, **Lutheran Hospital**, the **German Cultural Society** headquarters (still located in the neighborhood), and the historic German-American newspaper **Anzeiger des Westens** (which for a time in the 1840s had the largest circulation of any paper in Missouri). The parish of **St. Anthony of Padua** was established in **1863** by the Franciscan Friars of the Sacred Heart Province (who had arrived from Saxony, Germany in 1858), with parishioners initially gathering for Sunday Mass at the **House of Refuge orphanage** on the land that would later become Marquette Park. After the Union Army confiscated the House of Refuge during the Civil War in 1863, parishioner John Whitnell donated a small frame house that served as a temporary church and residence for the Franciscan friars. The first permanent church building was constructed at the corner of **Meramec and Compton Streets in 1869**; the present church building at **3140 Meramec Street** was completed in **1910**, designed in **Romanesque style borrowed from the German Rhineland** with two square Romanesque towers that today form the symbol of the neighborhood. St. Anthony’s High School was erected in 1922, a monastery in 1931, and a new parochial school in 1962.

Other notable historic landmarks include the **Stork Inn** at Virginia Avenue and Taft Avenue (now an architect’s studio) and **Busch’s Inn** at Grand and Meramec (later The Feasting Fox pub, closed 2020) — two of the three South Side **Anheuser-Busch tied houses** (along with Bevo Mill in the adjacent Bevo neighborhood). **Ted Drewes Frozen Custard** opened its iconic Grand Boulevard location at Grand and Kingsland Court in **1931** (the third Ted Drewes location and the oldest remaining), continuing to serve its famous “concrete” milkshakes today. Other Dutchtown culinary landmarks include **Merb’s Candies**, **Dad’s Cookies**, **Companion Bakery**, **Grbic**, **Urban Eats**, **Maude’s Market**, and **Banh Mi So**. The **1910 former cinema at Virginia and Vermont** still stands today, along with the **Resurrection of Our Lord Catholic Church** at Meramec and Hydraulic (mid-century modern), and **Cleveland High School** (designed by William B. Ittner in 1915, an architectural masterpiece closed since 2006).

The neighborhood’s major park is the **17-acre Marquette Park**, acquired by the city in **1915** from the Board of Children’s Guardians and named after Father Pere Marquette (the Jesuit priest who, with Louis Jolliet, was the first European to explore and map the northern Mississippi River). The park’s historic **Marquette Park Pool** was constructed approximately **1904** with a sand and gravel bottom; an asphalt bottom was added approximately 1930; the pool was renovated and reopened in **2015**. Marquette Park is bisected by South Compton Avenue and bounded by Minnesota, Louisiana, Osage, and Gasconade Streets, and is home to the **Dunn-Marquette Recreation Center** (built 1917 originally as a locker and shower facility for the pool), the **Thomas Dunn Learning Center**, two softball fields, a soccer field, tennis courts, and a playground. Other Dutchtown parks include **Amberg Park** at the west end (designed by landscape architect Robert Goetz in 1963), **Laclede Park** (set aside for park use as early as 1812, near Meramec and Broadway), and **Minnie Wood Memorial Square** to the east.

Major commercial corridors include **Meramec Street, Chippewa Street, South Grand Boulevard, Broadway, Virginia Avenue**, and **Gravois Road**. The neighborhood’s primary commercial district, known as **“Downtown Dutchtown,”** runs along Meramec Street between South Grand Boulevard and Compton Avenue. South of Meramec, Virginia Avenue served historically as the main commercial street. The **Grand Boulevard Park and Grand-Meramec Park subdivisions** were developed on the site of old Hashagen’s Park in the late 1910s; later apartment construction at Spring and Delor followed in the 1960s. Major institutional anchors include the **Alexian Brothers Hospital**, **St. Mary’s High School** (private Catholic), **McMahon Ford** dealership, the **Mel Carnahan School of the Future**, **Scruggs and Meramec public schools**, and the former **St. Thomas Aquinas Catholic School**.

Beginning in the **1990s**, Dutchtown began rapidly diversifying, becoming one of St. Louis’s most demographically diverse neighborhoods. While the influence of the original German settlers remains visible in the brick streetscape and St. Anthony’s steeples, today the neighborhood is home to roughly half Black residents alongside significant **Latino, Asian (including Vietnamese), Arabic-speaking, and Nepali** immigrant communities. **Dutchtown Main Streets** (4219 Virginia Avenue) and **Dutchtown South Community Corporation** (4204 Virginia Avenue, founded over 50 years ago and a HUD grantee) coordinate revitalization, façade grants, multilingual services, and the **Dutchtown Community Improvement District**, while the **Neighborhood Innovation Center** at 3207 Meramec Street operates as a microbusiness incubator, coworking space, farmers market, event venue, and polling location. The 2010 census recorded 15,770 residents (down from 17,222 in 2000), with 8,445 housing units, making Dutchtown briefly the most populous neighborhood in the city.

Image: Private residences in Dutchtown — the locally made St. Louis brick housing stock built during the neighborhood’s rapid German-American development between 1880 and 1920, when streetcar lines along Meramec, Chippewa, Gravois, Grand, and Jefferson transformed the southern Commonfield of Carondelet (annexed 1870) into one of the city’s densest residential districts — Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA

Dutchtown sits at 38.5784° N in South St. Louis, approximately four miles south of Downtown along the Mississippi River corridor, with the intersection of Meramec Street and Virginia Avenue forming a topographical highpoint at the neighborhood’s center. The neighborhood experiences St. Louis’s full humid continental climate: hot, humid summers with frequent 90°F days, cold winters with regular freezing temperatures, and a notably active spring severe weather season including real tornado corridor exposure. 

Peak outdoor activity falls during spring and fall, when Marquette Park’s outdoor pool (open Memorial Day through Labor Day), tennis courts, playground, and Dunn-Marquette Recreation Center programming draw heaviest neighborhood use, the historic **Stork Inn** and **Ted Drewes Frozen Custard** on Grand Boulevard activate the corridor, and the Meramec / Virginia / Grand commercial corridors of “Downtown Dutchtown” host shop-local crawls, “Business After Hours” events, the annual **St. Anthony of Padua Blessing of the Bikes**, the **Trunk-or-Treat at St. Anthony**, and farmers markets at the Neighborhood Innovation Center. Cool seasons drive intense indoor activity at **St. Anthony of Padua Catholic Church**, the **Thomas Dunn Learning Center**, **Resurrection of Our Lord Catholic Church**, **St. Mary’s High School**, **Alexian Brothers Hospital**, **Ted Drewes** (whose drive-up window operates year-round), **Dutchtown Market**, **Merb’s Candies**, **Companion Bakery**, **Dad’s Cookies**, **Grbic**, **Urban Eats**, **Maude’s Market**, and **Banh Mi So**. 

Winter shifts commerce indoors to the dense brick housing stock, the multilingual services (Spanish, Vietnamese, Arabic, Nepali) provided by Dutchtown Main Streets and Dutchtown South Community Corporation, and the Dutchtown Community Improvement District’s safety and beautification programming. The neighborhood’s 1880–1920 Italianate, Foursquare, bungalow, Craftsman, Spanish, Classical Revival, Second Empire, Tudor, and one-story brick “shotgun” homes built from locally fired St. Louis red brick have weathered more than 140 years of Missouri climate with the durability that earned St. Louis the nickname “Brick City.”

## Ellendale — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/ellendale
