## Ellendale Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Ellendale, St. Louis

Ellendale anchors a small, historically industrial stretch of southwest St. Louis built on the former 19th-century farm of James C. Sutton and the steel-and-clay era that defined St. Louis south of Forest Park — anchored today by the Knox Industrial Drive corridor (developed in the 1960s along the River des Peres), the 9-acre Francis R. Slay Park (established 1926 and renamed in 2009 for the 45-year 23rd Ward Democratic Committeeman), the William B. Ittner-era 1927 Wilkinson/Early Childhood Center II Magnet School, and the Manchester Avenue commercial spine of restoration shops, fabrication studios, and creative production agencies that bridge Ellendale to neighboring Maplewood and the Dogtown corridor of St. Louis.

Ellendale sits in southwest St. Louis at **38.6131° N, 90.3226° W** in zip codes 63139 and 63143. Per the City of St. Louis, the neighborhood’s boundaries are **Manchester Avenue** on the North, **Hampton Avenue** on the East, **Interstate 44** on the South, and the **City Limits** on the West. Ellendale is bordered by the Dogtown neighborhoods of **Clayton-Tamm and Franz Park** to the north (across Manchester), **Lindenwood Park and Clifton Heights** to the south (across I-44), **The Hill** to the east (across Hampton), and **Maplewood** and the rest of St. Louis County to the west (across the city limits). The eastern portion of Ellendale is itself considered part of **Dogtown**. The 2020 Census recorded Ellendale as **76.3% White, 10.6% Black, 1.7% Asian, 8.4% Two or More Races, and 4.3% Hispanic/Latino**, with a population of **1,385** — a 12% decline from 2010’s 1,575 residents. Nearly two-thirds of housing is owner-occupied, and the neighborhood historically has carried the **lowest reported crime rate in the city**.

The neighborhood’s name and street grid trace to the **Sutton family’s 19th-century estate**. Much of what is now Ellendale was part of the property of **James C. Sutton**, who died in 1877 and bequeathed his land to his nine children. **Kate Thomas**, one of his daughters, founded Ellendale itself — likely platted in 1884 — while **Mary C. Marshall** sold her share in 1890 to a St. Louis real estate company that platted the neighboring subdivision of Maplewood. **Charles Sutton**, another son, sold land south of the railroad to **Moses Greenwood**, who developed a subdivision with English street names that survive in Ellendale today: **Piccadilly, Commonwealth, Tremont, Oxford, and Cambridge**. McCausland Avenue (named for James McCausland, who opened a tract west of Glades in 1859) bisects the neighborhood. Electric streetcars began running westward from St. Louis along Manchester Avenue in **1896**, opening Ellendale to residential growth — though a gas explosion just before the 1904 World’s Fair destroyed much of the original 1880s-90s housing stock.

The area was first opened to industrial activity in the 1850s with the construction of the **Pacific Railroad** and the mining of clay for fire brick (with small coal deposits also discovered). The neighborhood’s signature industrial landmark for nearly a century was **Scullin Steel Company**, which served the railroad age from the 1880s through World War II and beyond, employing thousands of southwest St. Louis residents. Many of the older surviving houses in Ellendale were originally built for Scullin Steel executives after the 1904 World’s Fair rebuilt the neighborhood. Scullin Steel ceased production in the late 1970s, closing its massive plant on Manchester Avenue on **September 18, 1981** — a casualty of foreign competition that ended a defining chapter of southwest St. Louis industry. The former Scullin Steel site at Manchester and Abbott became the **St. Louis Marketplace**, a strip mall with discount shopping and chain stores; in order to establish the shopping center, railroad tracks had to be diverted.

The neighborhood’s defining topographic feature is the valley of the **River des Peres** — once a surface river, now a drainage works — which emerges from its underground course through Forest Park near Macklind Avenue just south of Manchester, then flows southwesterly before swinging south along the city limits. The river was channelized in the early 1930s by the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, Horner & Shifrin, and the Works Progress Administration, with the project inducted into the **American Society of Civil Engineers’ Historic Civil Engineering Landmarks in 1988**. Because of the river valley and two active railroad lines (Union Pacific and BNSF), most of Ellendale’s residential development sits west of the River des Peres along the city limits. The **Highland Industrial Park** and **Knox Industrial Drive** — both developed in the 1960s along the river — anchor the neighborhood’s modern light-industrial, packaging, and trucking activity.

Ellendale’s housing stock is nearly **70% single-family** — ranging from small starter homes to larger executive dwellings built for Scullin Steel staff — with red brick predominant throughout. Two distinct subdivisions shape the residential streetscape: the original Greenwood subdivision’s English-named streets (Piccadilly, Commonwealth, Tremont, Oxford, Cambridge), and the post-Fair rebuild blocks along Pernod, Scanlan, and Arsenal. The neighborhood’s principal park is **Francis R. Slay Park** (9 acres, formerly Ellendale-Arsenal Park, established 1926 and dedicated in 2009 to Francis R. Slay — a lifelong South St. Louis resident and 45-year Democratic Committeeman of the 23rd Ward, and father of former St. Louis Mayor Francis G. Slay), featuring a playground, two baseball fields, a soccer field, a one-mile walking trail, and a tennis court. The **River des Peres Greenway** connects Ellendale to the citywide trail network. The neighborhood’s commercial center clusters along Manchester Avenue and at Southwest Avenue, with multiple car lots and small fabrication shops mixed with creative agencies and spas.

Public schooling is anchored by **Wilkinson/Early Childhood Center II Magnet School** at 7212 Arsenal Street (built in 1927, in the William B. Ittner era of St. Louis school architecture). Nearby Mallinckrodt Academy of Gifted Instruction (Pernod), Mason School (Southwest Avenue in Clifton Heights), and Gateway STEM High School (McRee Avenue on The Hill) serve neighborhood families. The St. Louis Public Library operates the nearby Marketplace Branch (Manchester Road in Franz Park) and Machacek Branch (Scanlan Avenue in Lindenwood Park). Local institutions also include the **Ellendale Neighborhood Association** (which meets monthly) and the long-standing **Piccadilly at Manhattan** restaurant — founded in 1901 and operated by the same family for five generations, serving classics like fried fish sandwiches and chicken pot pie from one of Ellendale’s signature English-named streets.

Ellendale’s modern business activity reflects its industrial heritage — Knox Industrial Drive houses Independents Graphic & Display and **Midvale Industries** (founded 1901, serving the aerospace and metalcasting industries for over 123 years), while Manchester Avenue hosts **Secure Data Recovery Services** (operator of Missouri’s only Class 10 ISO 4 Cleanroom), the long-running **Servco Equipment Company** (founded 1947 in commercial kitchen fabrication on Jamieson Avenue), the award-winning **Switch** creative production agency, and a growing roster of consultancies and spa businesses. With its proximity to I-44 (one of the most accessible interstate corridors in St. Louis), Forest Park and the St. Louis Science Center to the north, Maplewood and the MetroLink Sunnen station to the west, and Historic Downtown Maplewood and The Hill to the immediate east, Ellendale is a strategic small business corridor for the entire southwest St. Louis service market. The listings on this page draw from this broader Ellendale / Knox Industrial / Manchester Avenue professional corridor.

Image: An intersection in the Ellendale neighborhood, southwest St. Louis — bounded by Manchester Avenue, Hampton Avenue, I-44, and the city limits, with the River des Peres valley and Knox Industrial Drive defining its character — Wikimedia Commons / CC BY 2.0 by Paul Sableman

Ellendale sits along the River des Peres valley in southwest St. Louis, where the city follows a classic Midwest four-season pattern. Hot, humid summers with highs in the upper 80s°F bring peak activity to Francis R. Slay Park’s 9-acre playground and ball fields, the River des Peres Greenway trail network, and the Manchester Avenue corridor that connects Ellendale to Maplewood’s patio scene. Spring and fall draw walkers along the English-named streets (Piccadilly, Commonwealth, Tremont, Oxford, Cambridge), and the corridor between the Knox Industrial Drive light-industrial district and the city limits.

Cold winters with periodic snow shift commerce indoors to Ellendale’s signature service providers — Secure Data Recovery’s climate-controlled Class 10 ISO 4 Cleanroom on Manchester Avenue, Midvale Industries’ 123-year-old industrial supply operations on Knox Industrial Drive, Switch’s indoor experiential marketing studios, and Independents Graphic & Display’s 35,000-square-foot production facility. The neighborhood’s mostly single-family 1880s-through-1950s red-brick housing stock, with its Scullin Steel-era construction, weathers Missouri seasons with the durability that defined the steel-and-clay corridor for nearly a century. Quick I-44 access keeps the southwest St. Louis service market moving year-round.

## Fairground — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/fairground

Ranked by 360+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  10 of 10 verified Fairground / North St. Louis corridor providers ✓  All businesses verified via Google Business ✓  Avg. rating: 4.6/5 across 360 reviews

## Fairground Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Fairground, St. Louis

Fairground anchors a deep-history stretch of North St. Louis where the city held its St. Louis Agricultural and Mechanical Fair from 1856 to 1902 — anchored today by the 131-acre Fairground Park (designed by Louisiana Purchase Exposition landscape architect George Kessler), the medieval-castle-like Bear Pits facade at North Grand and Natural Bridge (the only remaining 1876 structure from St. Louis’s first zoo), the Civil War legacy of Benton Barracks (a 30,000-soldier Union encampment and hospital), the long-running Beaumont High School corridor, and the North Grand Boulevard / Natural Bridge Avenue commercial spine.

Fairground sits in North St. Louis at approximately **38.6665° N, 90.2217° W** in zip code 63107. Per the City of St. Louis Planning & Urban Design Agency, the neighborhood’s boundaries run **West Florissant Avenue to North Florissant Avenue** on the North, southward to **Glasgow Avenue** on the East, westward to **Natural Bridge Avenue** then northward to **North Grand Boulevard**, westward to **Kossuth Avenue** on the South, and northward to **Warne Avenue** on the West back to West Florissant Avenue. Fairground is bordered by **College Hill** to the north (across W Florissant), **Hyde Park** to the east (across Glasgow), **Jeff-Vander-Lou and Greater Ville** to the south (across Natural Bridge and Kossuth, on the far side of Fairground Park), and **O’Fallon** to the west (across Warne). The 2020 Census recorded Fairground as **93.7% Black, 2.8% White, 1.7% Two or More Races, and 0.9% Hispanic/Latino**. The neighborhood is anchored by the **Saint Louis Police Department District 6 North Patrol Division** at 4014 Union, with the nearby **Divoll Branch** of the St. Louis Public Library serving residents.

The neighborhood traces its name and street grid to the **Grand Prairie Commonfield** — a long agricultural tract laid out by French colonial settlers in narrow east-west strips, including U.S. Surveys #422, #458, #658, and #1261, originally owned by names like Richlet Verdon, Jean Marie Cardenal, John Watkins, and Auguste Dodier. By 1850, the larger surveys had been subdivided into tracts held by prominent St. Louis families with familiar North St. Louis street names today: **Lindell, Clay, O’Fallon, Vandeventer, Carter, Shreve, and Hull**. Land that belonged to **John O’Fallon** (nephew of explorer William Clark) would become the site of Fairground Park. Earliest residential subdivisions were platted by 1859 (**White Place** — a portion of riverman J.M. White’s farm bounded by Natural Bridge, White/Newstead, Margaretta, and Fair Avenues), followed by **North Cote Brilliante** (1861), **Lindell’s Additions** (1860), **Chouteau Place** (1875), and **James B. Clay’s Subdivision** (1876). Sections nearest the Fairgrounds developed earliest in the late 1860s; further west development followed in the 1880s and into the early 1900s.

The **St. Louis Agricultural and Mechanical Association** was incorporated on December 7, 1855, and held the first St. Louis Fair on October 13, 1856, on 50 acres purchased from John O’Fallon for $50,000. The fair was a national success, featuring the largest amphitheater in America (seating over 12,000), a Mechanical Hall opened 1876, the Gothic Fine Arts Hall, a three-story “gallinarium” chicken palace, Floral Hall, livestock stalls, and the city’s first **Zoological Gardens in 1876** (with monkey house, carnivore house, and the famous **Bear Pits**). Big Thursday of Fair Week was declared a municipal holiday in 1856, with attendance peaking at over 80,000. The fair hosted Presidents Grant, Cleveland, and Harrison, and an 1860 visit from the Prince of Wales (later King Edward VII) drew 150,000 spectators. During the Civil War, the fairgrounds and adjacent property were turned over to the Union Army as **Benton Barracks** (1861-1865) — a five-building, 740-foot-long, 30,000-soldier training and hospital complex administered by Emily Elizabeth Parsons, the largest Union hospital in the West.

The fair was abolished in 1902 as preparations for the **1904 World’s Fair** drew away attention, and the 1905 Anti-Breeders Act signed by Governor Joseph W. Folk ended horse racing in Missouri. After protracted political debate, the city purchased the abandoned 132-acre fairgrounds in 1908 for $700,000, dedicating **Fairground Park on October 9, 1909**. Renowned landscape architect **George Kessler** — who designed the 1904 Louisiana Purchase Exposition grounds at Forest Park — designed Fairground Park. Most fair structures were removed except the Bear Pits facade and the amphitheater (the latter removed in 1911). In 1912, the city’s first municipal swimming pool was built on the amphitheater site — at five acres, then the world’s largest. In June 1949, the segregated pool became the site of the **Fairground Park Riot** when it was forced to integrate; the pool was closed in 1950 and replaced by a smaller pool in 1957-1958.

**Fairground Park today** is the city’s fourth-largest park at 131.46 acres, featuring a 9-acre stocked fishing lake with casting dock, an outdoor swimming pool, a seasonal skating rink, two field houses, eight lighted tennis courts, five softball fields, five baseball fields (two lighted), two lighted soccer fields, two basketball courts, three football fields, five comfort stations, a one-mile walking and biking trail, and a unique monument to **Louis Kossuth** — the Hungarian freedom fighter known as the “Father of Hungarian Democracy” and only the second foreign statesman invited to address a joint session of Congress (1852). The lake edge features river birch and bald cypress; maples and oaks shade the sports fields. The medieval Bear Pits castle facade at the corner of Natural Bridge and North Grand remains the park’s signature historic landmark.

Fairground’s residential streetscape mixes 120-year-old brick duplexes, ornate-mantle-and-stained-glass split-levels, and post-WWII single-family homes across the gridded blocks between Florissant and Fairground Park. The neighborhood is zoned within the **St. Louis Public Schools district**; students attend Bryan Hill Elementary, Yeatman-Liddell Middle School, and **Vashon High School** (founded 1927 as St. Louis’s second Black high school, now on Cass Avenue with a modern library, 13 computer labs, and a 25-meter indoor pool). The **Divoll Branch** of the St. Louis Public Library serves the area. After decades of disinvestment, a 2022 Aldi opened on Natural Bridge Avenue, and the **Fairground Neighborhood Revitalization Organization (FNRO)** is opening a community-owned grocery at Florissant and Warne. Local takeout institutions include **Canton House Chop Suey** (famous for St. Paul sandwiches), and Ashland Elementary hosts a Shop & Grow Market with free milk, eggs, and produce.

Fairground’s modern business activity reflects North St. Louis’s mix of legacy industrial corridors and community-revitalization service providers. The North Grand Boulevard / Natural Bridge Avenue commercial spine — a major historic streetcar transit corridor from the Fair era onward — connects Fairground to downtown St. Louis and Lambert International Airport. The listings on this page draw from the broader Fairground / North Grand / Natural Bridge professional corridor, with anchor institutions like **Mission: St Louis** (a community-focused non-profit on North Grand serving Fairground residents with workforce development, second-chance hiring, home repairs, and education) and a mix of chamber-of-commerce membership organizations, industrial suppliers, creative agencies, and event venues serving the broader St. Louis region from positions accessible to North Grand and the I-70 corridor.

Image: The Bear Pits facade at Fairground Park — the 1876 castle-wall remnant of St. Louis’s first zoological garden, at the corner of North Grand Boulevard and Natural Bridge Avenue — Wikimedia Commons / Creative Commons

Fairground sits in North St. Louis between Fairground Park and the West Florissant corridor, where the city follows a classic Midwest four-season pattern. Hot, humid summers with highs in the upper 80s°F bring peak activity to Fairground Park’s 9-acre stocked fishing lake, the outdoor swimming pool and seasonal skating rink, the one-mile walking trail, and the eight lighted tennis courts plus baseball, soccer, and football fields. Spring and fall draw walkers to the Bear Pits facade at North Grand and Natural Bridge, and to the Louis Kossuth monument, while the North Grand corridor hosts community-organization events year-round.

Cold winters with periodic snow shift commerce indoors to Fairground’s anchor providers — Mission: St Louis’s North Grand workforce-development and home-repair operations, the Divoll Branch library and its family programs, and the indoor service operations of the broader North Grand and Natural Bridge professional corridor. The neighborhood’s mostly 1880s-through-1950s red-brick housing stock weathers Missouri seasons with the durability that defined North St. Louis for over a century. Quick I-70 access to the north and the Mark Twain Expressway keeps the Fairground service market moving year-round and connects directly to Lambert International Airport and downtown.

## Forest Park Southeast — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/forest-park-southeast

Ranked by 34,297+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  Home of The Grove, Manchester Avenue & Forest Park ✓  Anchored by Park Central Development, Restoration St. Louis & the Grove CID ✓  Avg. rating: 4.9/5 across 34,297 reviews

## Forest Park Southeast Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Forest Park Southeast, St. Louis

Forest Park Southeast (FPSE) serves as one of St. Louis’s most dramatically transformed neighborhoods — bisected by “The Grove” entertainment district along Manchester Avenue (marked by its iconic 24-foot neon entry sign), anchored by the 1,326-acre Forest Park to the west, the Washington University Medical Center / BJC corridor to the north, the Cortex Innovation Community, and the NRHP-listed Forest Park Southeast Historic District (2001) — offering community development, general contracting, commercial printing, custom metal fabrication, marketing, and B2B services across the Central Corridor.

Forest Park Southeast is one of St. Louis’s 79 official neighborhoods, located at **38.6257° N, 90.2649° W** and covering **0.64 square miles**. Its boundaries run **Interstate 64 (U.S. Route 40)** to the north, **Vandeventer Avenue** to the east, **Interstate 44** to the south, and **Kingshighway Boulevard and Forest Park** to the west. Adjoining neighborhoods include **Kings Oak** and **The Hill** to the west, **Southwest Garden** to the south, **Botanical Heights** to the southeast, **Midtown** to the east, and the **Central West End** to the north. The 2020 census recorded **3,458 residents** at a density of 5,400 per square mile, with a racial composition of 46% White, 36% Black, 7% Asian, and 4.7% Hispanic/Latino origin. FPSE comprises four historic sub-neighborhoods: **Adams Grove, Gibson Heights, New Boyle**, and **Ranken East**. The neighborhood serves **ZIP code 63110** and falls within Ward 9 under Alderman Michael Browning.

Prior to the founding of St. Louis, present-day Forest Park Southeast was part of a region of communal French farms known as **Prairie des Noyers** (“Meadows of the Walnut Trees”). Following the **Louisiana Purchase**, the area’s complex land titles were gradually assigned to private owners. By the 1820s, the area was part of a large tract owned by **Samuel McRee** (namesake of McRee Town, today Botanical Heights), and a portion containing modern-day FPSE was sold to **Henry Shaw** in the 1840s. The **Missouri Pacific Railroad** purchased its right-of-way at the southern edge of the neighborhood in **1850**, but the area remained largely undeveloped through the American Civil War. In the 19th century, the area was known as **Rock Spring**, a wooded place St. Louisans visited to relax and unwind near the waters of **Mill Creek**.

The area was **annexed by the City of St. Louis in 1876**, when the city limits shifted westward from Grand Boulevard to Skinker Boulevard — the same year that adjacent **Forest Park** opened as the city’s first major public park. In the following decade, multiple **streetcar lines** were extended westward into the neighborhood, spurring its development as a streetcar suburb. **Gibson Heights**, in the northwestern quadrant, became the area’s first residential development in **1880**. Between 1880 and 1900, the neighborhood grew rapidly, and a vibrant commercial district developed along **Manchester Avenue** — later officially designated **Missouri Route 100**, which connects St. Louis to Jefferson City and beyond. While its proximity to industry and railroads made it less desirable than the affluent Central West End to the north, FPSE was ideally situated to house a diverse working-class population of merchants, tradesmen, and laborers. Forest Park Southeast was largely built out by 1910, with a final wave of construction in the mid-1920s.

Beginning in the **1960s**, deindustrialization and suburbanization resulted in severe disinvestment in and depopulation of the neighborhood, with the area classified as a “reconstruction” zone by 1953. By the 1990s, a third of the population fell under the federal poverty line. The **Manchester streetcar line**, which had served the corridor for over 50 years, closed in **1949**. Gentrification began in the 1990s with the arrival of **LGBT-oriented bars and clubs** along Manchester Avenue, establishing the corridor as a gay village. These nightlife establishments revitalized the largely abandoned retail district, which diversified into daytime restaurants and stores during the 2000s. The **Washington University Medical Center Redevelopment Corporation (WUMCRC)** — a partnership between BJC HealthCare and Washington University School of Medicine — has invested heavily in the neighborhood since the 1990s, purchasing and redeveloping over **500 vacant or abandoned properties**. Husband-and-wife developers **Amy and Amrit Gill** of **Restoration St. Louis** have rehabilitated more than 60 formerly vacant buildings into affordable apartments and pioneered the “**urban husbandry**” movement.

By the end of the 2000s, local business owners began branding the Manchester Avenue retail district as **“The Grove”** — a reference to the historic **Adams Grove** residential development in the southern portion of the neighborhood. The branding was formalized in **2009** with the creation of **The Grove Community Improvement District (CID)**, which levies a special sales tax to fund public improvements. The name was developed by property and business owners **Chip Schloss** (Atomic Cowboy), **Guy Slay** (Mangrove Properties), and **Restoration St. Louis**. In **October 2010**, The Grove became the first area in St. Louis to install an overhanging neon sign to mark its entry — a 24-foot by 11-foot, double-sided neon marker suspended 30 feet above Manchester Avenue just west of Vandeventer, completed as a two-year, $60,000 project funded by the **Forest Park South Business Association**, **The Grove CID**, **Tarlton Corporation**, and **WUMCRC**. The sign was designed by St. Louis firm **Ten 8** and fabricated by **Design Communication LTD** of Boston.

The **Forest Park Southeast Historic District** was added to the National Register of Historic Places in **2001**, deemed significant in both community planning and development and in architecture, with **612 contributing buildings**. The district provides **20% federal tax credits** for eligible renovations (plus an additional **25% Missouri state credit**), and the area is sometimes informally known as **Ranken** for its working-class homes built between 1890 and the 1920s. The district has expanded four times since 2001 (in 2005, 2007, 2009, and 2012), each time bringing more buildings under tax-credit protection. Architecture in the district includes Italianate, Romanesque Revival, Late Victorian, and the distinctive St. Louis **“Shaped Parapet Single Family Houses”** (brick one-story houses with shaped front parapets, decorative glazed bricks, and recessed arched entries, popular 1900–1920). Notable named landmarks within the district include the **Lambskin Temple** and the **Rock Spring School** — both also on the National Register.

Today, Forest Park Southeast is anchored by **The Grove’s** nearly one-mile entertainment corridor (Atomic Cowboy, The Gramophone, Just John, Urban Chestnut Brewing Co., Sanctuaria, Taha’a Twisted Tiki, HandleBar, Grace Meat + Three, the Ready Room concert venue, Tower Classic Tattooing, Prism, Rehab, and dozens of other independent venues); the **Transgender Memorial Garden** a few blocks south of Manchester (believed to be the first of its kind in the nation); **Adams Park** with the Cardinals Care “Jim Edmonds Field” baseball diamond; the new **Chouteau Park** south of I-64; **Adams Elementary School** (opened 1878 with modern expansion); the **Boys and Girls Club of Greater St. Louis** Adams Park location; the **1908 Forest Park Hall** (restored 2008 with state and federal historic tax credits); major Green Street developments including **Chroma** (235 apartments at Manchester and Chouteau), **Hue** (at Sarah and Papin), **Terra**, and **Union at the Grove** (160+ workforce-targeted units); the **Woodward Lofts** (164 units on Tower Grove Avenue); the annual **Grove Fest** (every October since 2005, hosted by Park Central Development); the **Tour de Grove** bicycle race; the **Flyover Comedy Festival**; and proximity to the **Cortex MetroLink station** and **Central West End MetroLink station**.

Image: Forest Park, St. Louis — the 1,326-acre namesake park that forms Forest Park Southeast’s western boundary along Kingshighway Boulevard. Opened in 1876 (the same year the FPSE area was annexed), Forest Park houses the Saint Louis Art Museum, Missouri History Museum, Saint Louis Science Center, the Muny, the Jewel Box, and the Saint Louis Zoo — Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA

Forest Park Southeast sits four miles west of Downtown St. Louis at 38.6257° N, sandwiched between Interstate 64 to the north and Interstate 44 to the south — one of the most centrally located neighborhoods in the city, with ten-minute access to Downtown and immediate adjacency to Barnes-Jewish Hospital (the largest hospital in Missouri and the largest private employer in the metropolitan area), St. Louis Children’s Hospital, the Washington University School of Medicine, and the Cortex Innovation Community. 

The neighborhood experiences St. Louis’s full humid continental climate: hot, humid summers with frequent 90°F days, cold winters with regular freezing temperatures, and a notably active spring severe weather season including real tornado corridor exposure. Peak outdoor activity falls during spring and fall, when **Forest Park’s** 1,326 acres immediately west of Kingshighway draw heaviest visitor use, The Grove’s patios and rooftops along Manchester activate, the annual **Grove Fest** (first Saturday in October) closes Manchester Avenue for live music and food vendors, the **Tour de Grove** cycling race occupies the corridor, **HandleBar’s World Naked Bike Race** rolls every July, and Adams Park hosts Cardinals Care youth baseball at Jim Edmonds Field. 

Cool seasons drive intense indoor activity at **Urban Chestnut Brewing Co.’s** beer hall, **The Gramophone**, the **Atomic Cowboy**, **Just John**, **Sanctuaria**, **Taha’a Twisted Tiki**, **Grace Meat + Three**, the **Ready Room** concert venue, **Tower Classic Tattooing**, **Park Central Development** at 4512 Manchester, **Posted Social** on Tower Grove Avenue, **Restoration St. Louis** on Manchester, **Advertisers Printing** on South Vandeventer, and **Foreman Fabricators** on McRee Avenue. The neighborhood’s 1880–1925 brick housing stock — including the distinctive **Shaped Parapet Single Family Houses** with their decorative glazed bricks, the **1908 Forest Park Hall**, the modern **Chroma**, **Hue**, **Terra**, **Union at the Grove**, and **Woodward Lofts** developments — have weathered more than 130 years of Missouri climate with the durability of the St. Louis Central Corridor’s brick construction tradition.

## Fountain Park — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/fountain-park

Neighborhood

Fountain Park

Coordinates

38.6572° N, 90.2648° W

Ranked by 4,275+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  10 of 10 verified Fountain Park / Central West End corridor providers ✓  All businesses verified via Google Business ✓  Avg. rating: 4.7/5 across 4,275 reviews

## Fountain Park Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Fountain Park, St. Louis

Fountain Park anchors a 1.5-acre oval green space and the surrounding north St. Louis neighborhood that traces its roots to the 1857 Aubert Place subdivision by John Lay — anchored today by the multi-tiered iron fountain donated by the St. Louis Merchants Exchange (the centerpiece of the city’s oldest public open space), the 11-foot bronze Martin Luther King Jr. statue by St. Louis sculptor Rudolph Torrini (the only MLK statue in Missouri), the 1896 Italian Romanesque Revival church anchoring the southwest corner, the National Register-listed Fountain Park Historic District, and the Delmar Boulevard / Dr. Martin Luther King Drive commercial spine just two blocks north of the Central West End.

Fountain Park sits in north St. Louis at approximately **38.6572° N, 90.2648° W** in zip code 63113. Per the City of St. Louis Planning & Urban Design Agency, the neighborhood’s boundaries run **Dr. Martin Luther King Drive** on the North, **Walton Avenue** on the East, **Delmar Boulevard** on the South, and **North Kingshighway Boulevard** on the West. Fountain Park is bordered by **Kingsway East** to the north (across MLK Drive), **Lewis Place** to the east (across Walton), the **Central West End** to the south (across Delmar Boulevard — the historic Delmar Divide), and **Academy** to the west (across Kingshighway). Per the 2010 Census, the neighborhood counted 1,484 residents — 99% Black with strong roots dating to mid-20th century demographic shifts. The **Washington Montessori Elementary School** magnet program (focused on General Academics with a Gifted Enrichment program) sits on North Euclid within the neighborhood, and the **Cabanne Branch** of the St. Louis Public Library — built in 1906 with a gift from Andrew Carnegie — serves nearby Academy.

The neighborhood was originally known as **Aubert Place**, laid out by John Lay in 1857 as eight-block lots surrounding an oval central park planted with oak trees and traversed by brick footpaths. In 1889, Lay donated the 1.5-acre oval green space to the City of St. Louis — **the city’s oldest public open space**. The multi-tiered iron water fountain at the center, gifted by the **St. Louis Merchants Exchange** in the late nineteenth century, gives the park (and the neighborhood) its name. In its 19th-century vision, the neighborhood was planned as a cohesive community to include two schools, a firehouse, a hotel, and two religious edifices. The **Italian Romanesque Revival church** on the southwest side of the park was built in 1896 for the Third Congregational Church, and the homes that hug the oval frame a stately mid-19th-century streetscape with old-growth trees on the perimeter.

In **1978**, St. Louis sculptor **Rudolph Torrini**’s 11-foot bronze statue of Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. was placed in the park — **the only Martin Luther King Jr. statue in Missouri**. The Fountain Park Historic District was placed on the **National Register of Historic Places in October 1982**. Before the 1960s, Fountain Park was part of the broader West End, filled with grand mansions and private streets occupied by St. Louis’s upper middle class and wealthy elite. Segregation drew an arbitrary line at Delmar Boulevard — the well-documented **“Delmar Divide”** — and the resulting split changed the destiny of the neighborhood for the next fifty years. As early as 1944, Black residents began moving in; within a few years the neighborhood transitioned from mostly white to mostly Black, a shift that paralleled patterns across northeast St. Louis.

The neighborhood is anchored architecturally by **large stately houses dating to the 1880s-1920s**, numerous historic Black churches, and the oval Fountain Park itself, which remains the most stable section of the neighborhood. The 1980s and 1990s saw the renovation of major buildings including **The Vault** — a former bank along Delmar that became a $3 million-renovated premier nightclub with the largest dance floor in the city, complete with a VIP room set inside the original bank vault. Recent decades have brought new institutional partnerships: **LaunchCode** arrived along Delmar with technology-workforce training; the **Fountain Park Lewis Place Community Development Committee** formed in 2018 to coordinate redevelopment; and projects like **Elevation Workspace** (rehabilitating the historic USEDC building at 4741 Delmar) and the larger Kingsway Development Initiative have begun to bring commercial revitalization to the Delmar corridor.

The neighborhood is organized by active community groups including **The Enright Neighbors** and the **Fountain Park Block Unit**. Local residents have repeatedly defended the integrity of the historic park, including a successful 2011 effort to keep the MLK statue in place after rumors that it might be moved elsewhere in the city. The neighborhood is served by **Sherman Park** immediately to the south of Delmar (in the Central West End), and connects easily to Forest Park and downtown via Kingshighway, Delmar, and the MetroLink Skinker / Union station to the southwest.

On **May 16, 2025**, an EF-3 tornado tore through north and west-central St. Louis, devastating Fountain Park and damaging dozens of historic buildings, including the 1978 MLK statue, which was toppled from its base. The community immediately rallied to restore the statue, which was rededicated on **Martin Luther King Jr. Day weekend in January 2026** in the original location at the heart of the park — a symbol of resilience that drew citywide attention. The tornado damage spurred the formation of new revitalization initiatives, and Fountain Park has become a focal point for citywide conversation about historic preservation and equitable rebuilding in north St. Louis.

Fountain Park’s service economy reflects its position bridging north St. Louis and the Central West End. The neighborhood is anchored along Delmar Boulevard by commercial revitalization projects (**Elevation Workspace** at 4741 Delmar), specialized B2B marketing firms (**Gorilla 76** at 4818 Washington Avenue — a corridor running parallel to Delmar), professional property management (**J&A Properties** on Enright), and coworking ecosystem providers (**TechArtista CWE** just south of Delmar on Washington Avenue). The listings on this page draw from the broader Fountain Park / Delmar / Central West End professional corridor, with anchor institutions like **Bowood Farms** (Olive Street urban garden center and dining destination), **The Fountain on Locust** (the historic Locust Street Art Deco soda fountain and speakeasy), and a curated set of artisan boutiques and electronics providers serving the wider Gateway City region from positions accessible to Kingshighway, Delmar, and the MetroLink corridor.

Image: The 1.5-acre Fountain Park oval — the city’s oldest public open space, donated by John Lay in 1889 and named for the multi-tiered iron Merchants Exchange fountain at its center — Wikimedia Commons / CC BY 2.0 by Paul Sableman

Fountain Park sits at the intersection of Kingshighway and Delmar, where north St. Louis follows a classic Midwest four-season pattern. Hot, humid summers with highs in the upper 80s°F bring peak activity to the 1.5-acre oval Fountain Park itself — the multi-tiered Merchants Exchange fountain, brick footpaths under old-growth oaks, and the 11-foot MLK statue draw walkers and visitors. Spring and fall fill the Delmar / North Euclid corridor with foot traffic to Central West End businesses, Bowood Farms, and the Cabanne Branch library.

Cold winters with periodic snow — and the residual impact of the May 16, 2025 EF-3 tornado — shift commerce indoors to Fountain Park’s revitalization anchors: TechArtista CWE’s coworking gym and cafe, Elevation Workspace’s rehabilitated USEDC building at 4741 Delmar, Gorilla 76’s B2B marketing studios on Washington Avenue, and The Fountain on Locust’s Art Deco dining rooms south of the Delmar Divide. Quick Kingshighway and Delmar access keeps the service market connected year-round to Forest Park, the Central West End, and the MetroLink corridor.

## Fox Park — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/fox-park

Ranked by 4,371+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  Anchored by St. Francis de Sales Oratory — the “Cathedral of South St. Louis” ✓  Home to the Fox Park Historic District (NRHP, 350+ contributing buildings) ✓  Avg. rating: 4.8/5 across 4,371 reviews

## Fox Park Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Fox Park, St. Louis

Fox Park serves as one of South St. Louis’s most architecturally distinctive German-craftsman neighborhoods — settled by German immigrants in the early 1880s, anchored by the 300-foot Gothic Revival steeple of St. Francis de Sales Oratory (the “Cathedral of South St. Louis”), the namesake Fox Park between Shenandoah and Victor, and the NRHP-listed Fox Park Historic District (1985) — offering residential renovation, custom woodworking, specialty coffee, fine dining, coworking, and wellness services across South Jefferson Avenue.

Fox Park is one of St. Louis’s 79 official neighborhoods, located at **38.6079° N, 90.2310° W** in the city’s **7th Ward**. The trapezoidal neighborhood is bounded by **Interstate 44** to the north, **Gravois Avenue (U.S. Route 30)** to the south, **Nebraska Avenue** to the west, and **South Jefferson Avenue** to the east. Surrounding neighborhoods include the **Gate District** to the north, **Compton Heights** and **Tower Grove East** to the west, **Benton Park West** to the south, and **McKinley Heights** to the east. The 2020 census recorded a population that was 48.9% Black, 41.4% White, 4.8% Hispanic or Latino, 5.4% Two or More Races, 2.9% Some Other Race, 1.3% Asian, and 0.1% Native American/Alaska Native — making it one of the more demographically balanced neighborhoods in South City. Fox Park spans **ZIP codes 63104 and 63118** and sits roughly three miles south of Downtown St. Louis.

Fox Park was **settled by German immigrants in the early 1880s**, many of them first- and second-generation craftsmen who built the brick row houses, two-flats, three-flats, and four-flats that still define the neighborhood today. These German-American settlers — many drawn from the dairying community of nearby **Soulard** — left behind a working- and middle-class district rich in architectural detail. The neighborhood takes its name from **Fox Park** itself, the small public park stretching between Shenandoah and Victor avenues, which has anchored the community since the 19th century. But its spiritual and visual identity belongs to **St. Francis de Sales Oratory**, the only German Gothic Revival church in St. Louis, whose **300-foot steeple** is visible for miles and earned it the long-standing nickname “the **Cathedral of South St. Louis**.”

The story of St. Francis de Sales begins in **1867**, when seven German dairymen, then parishioners of **Saints Peter and Paul** in Soulard, purchased a tract at the corner of **Gravois and Ohio** for $12,850 to build a new church serving the growing German Catholic population of South St. Louis. The first Mass was celebrated at Christmas 1867; the original church was dedicated in May 1868. In 1894 the parish planned a massive 350-foot church of cut stone, but cost forced them to build only the basement of the new church — and then the **Great St. Louis Tornado of May 1896** destroyed the original church entirely. The new pastor opted for brick and terracotta over stone, and the present church — designed by the distinguished German-American architect **Viktor Klutho** of the firm **Klutho and Ranft** — was built between 1907 and 1908 and dedicated in **November 1908**. The parish newsletter proudly declared the front portal an exact copy of that of the **Cathedral of Munich**. The hall church nave is modeled on German medieval prototypes, and its highly distinctive stained-glass windows were created by **Emil Frei, Sr.**, St. Louis’s premier stained-glass artist (recently arrived from Munich). The interior frescoes were painted in **1916** by another German immigrant, **Fridolin Fuchs**, with the **Winkle Terra Cotta Company** producing much of the window tracery.

The St. Francis de Sales complex was designated a **City Landmark in 1971** and added to the **National Register of Historic Places in February 1978**. The parish grew through the 1950s, adding a high school in 1939 and a grade school during the city’s 1930s–1940s growth years. After 1950, outward migration to the suburbs gradually depopulated the parish; the St. Francis de Sales High School closed in 1974. In **July 2005**, the church was closed as a parish and re-erected as an **Oratory** — entrusted by then-Archbishop **Raymond Burke** to the **Institute of Christ the King Sovereign Priest**, a Roman Catholic priestly community of Pontifical right dedicated to the traditional Latin liturgy. The first rector was **Rev. Canon Karl W. Lenhardt**; the Oratory today is the second largest church in the Archdiocese of St. Louis after the Cathedral Basilica of Saint Louis. In 2019, the Oratory acquired **Karl Wilhelm Opus 123**, a three-manual, 58-rank freestanding mechanical-action organ with 2,670 pipes originally built for a church in Syracuse, New York — more than doubling the size of the existing 1924 Wicks gallery organ. The adjacent former high school building is leased by a **KIPP charter school**, and the original grade school provides facilities for the Oratory’s community of approximately 100 home-schooled children.

The neighborhood’s architectural heritage earned the entire area **Historic District designation in 1985**. The **Fox Park Historic District** — listed on the National Register of Historic Places and designated a City of St. Louis historic district — currently includes approximately **350 contributing buildings**, of which about two-thirds are two- to six-family buildings and a little less than one-third are single-family houses. The architectural styles encompass **Italianate/Mansard**, **Second Empire**, **Eclectic Revivals**, **Arts & Crafts**, and sparsely detailed 20th-century flats, with good representative examples of small-scale **corner stores** dating from the late 19th century into the third decade of the 20th century. The period of significance spans **1883 to 1935**, but most of the buildings are expressions of the evolution between **1885 and 1915** of St. Louis working- and middle-class housing — meaning 73.4% of the residential real estate here was built in 1939 or earlier (a greater concentration of historic homes than 99.1% of U.S. neighborhoods).

The namesake **Fox Park** stretches between **Shenandoah and Victor** in the center of the neighborhood and is one of **111 parks** maintained by the City of St. Louis. Park features include a **lighted softball field (Fox Field 01S)** on Shenandoah at 2716, a **half-court basketball court**, a **playground**, a **splash pad**, a **dog park**, and the neighborhood’s two community gardens — **Fox Park Farm** and the **DeSales Community Garden**. The St. Louis Public School District serves the neighborhood, though no SLPS school lies within its boundaries; students typically attend **Shenandoah Elementary School**, the **Academy of Entrepreneurship Studies Middle (AESM) at L’Ouverture**, and **Roosevelt High School**. In adjacent McKinley Heights are the **McKinley Classical Junior Academy** and **McKinley Classical Leadership Academy** (both SLPS Magnet Schools), and the neighborhood’s public charter options include the **KIPP Inspire Academy** (a 5th–8th-grade middle school located in the former St. Francis de Sales school), **KIPP Wisdom Academy**, and **EAGLE College Prep**.

Today, Fox Park has emerged as one of South St. Louis’s most rapidly revitalizing neighborhoods — in February 2017, the **Riverfront Times** reported that **Redfin had named Fox Park the hottest neighborhood in the St. Louis metropolitan area** for that year. Anchors today include **Lona’s Lil Eats** (the iconic Asian-fusion eatery on California Avenue famous for its giant rice paper wraps); **Little Fox** on Shenandoah (the acclaimed New American small-plates restaurant opened in 2020); **Coffeestamp** on South Jefferson (a brothers-owned micro-roastery founded in 2018); **Fritanga Nicaraguan Cuisine** on South Jefferson (Central American fare for 15+ years); **Adventures Of Intrigue** on South Jefferson (the KultureCity-certified open-concept puzzle adventure venue, the largest of its kind in the Midwest); **The Dreamer’s Loft** on Lafayette (St. Louis’s first creative coworking space); **TPC Construction** on Sidney; **Wood Innovations** on Victor (the USDA Wood Innovations Grant recipient); **Love Goddess Healing** on Sidney; the **Fox Park Neighborhood Association**; and access to nearby **Tower Grove Park** (289 acres), the **Missouri Botanical Garden**, **Lafayette Park** (the oldest urban park west of the Mississippi, dedicated 1851), the **South Grand** shopping district, the **Mississippi Greenway** trail, and **Downtown St. Louis**.

Image: St. Francis de Sales Oratory at Gravois and Ohio — the German Gothic Revival church dedicated November 1908, designed by Viktor Klutho, with its 300-foot steeple earning it the nickname “Cathedral of South St. Louis.” NRHP-listed 1978, City Landmark 1971 — Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA

Fox Park sits three miles south of Downtown St. Louis at 38.6079° N, sandwiched between Interstate 44 to the north and Gravois Avenue to the south — a centrally located South City position with ten-minute access to Downtown, the Central West End, Cortex, and Saint Louis University. The neighborhood experiences St. Louis’s full humid continental climate: hot, humid summers with frequent 90°F days; cold winters with regular freezing temperatures; and an active spring severe weather season, real tornado corridor exposure (the original 1868 church was destroyed by the Great St. Louis Tornado of 1896). 

Peak outdoor activity falls during spring and fall, when **Fox Park’s** playground, splash pad, basketball court, and lighted softball field (**Fox Field 01S**) draw heaviest neighborhood use, the **dog park** activates, the **Fox Park Farm** and **DeSales Community Garden** hit their growing season, **Little Fox’s** patio on Shenandoah fills with diners, and nearby **Tower Grove Park’s** 289 acres, the **Missouri Botanical Garden**, and 30-acre **Lafayette Park** draw weekend foot traffic. 

Cool seasons drive intense indoor activity at **Lona’s Lil Eats** on California Avenue, **Little Fox** on Shenandoah, **Coffeestamp** on South Jefferson, **Fritanga Nicaraguan Cuisine** on South Jefferson, **Adventures Of Intrigue**, **The Dreamer’s Loft** on Lafayette, **TPC Construction** on Sidney, **Wood Innovations** on Victor, **Love Goddess Healing** on Sidney, and the Sunday Masses at **St. Francis de Sales Oratory**. The neighborhood’s 1883–1935 brick housing stock — including its Italianate/Mansard, Second Empire, Eclectic Revival, and Arts & Crafts buildings, the German Gothic Revival Oratory, and the small-scale late-19th-century corner stores — have weathered more than 140 years of Missouri climate (including that 1896 tornado) with the durability of the German craftsmen who built them.

## Franz Park — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/franz-park

Ranked by 1,355+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  10 of 10 verified Franz Park / Dogtown corridor providers ✓  All businesses verified via Google Business ✓  Avg. rating: 4.7/5 across 1,355 reviews

## Franz Park Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Franz Park, St. Louis

Franz Park anchors one of the three (formally) or five (per the Dogtown Historical Society) neighborhoods that comprise the historic St. Louis area known as Dogtown — anchored today by the 4.67-acre Franz Park city park (donated 1915 by E.D. Franz), the McCausland Avenue antique-and-vacuum-shop commercial corridor, the Manchester Avenue brick-and-fire-clay industrial legacy, the long-running Failoni’s Restaurant (purchased 1916 from the Lemp Brewery by Alessandro Failoni to serve nearby Scullin Steel workers), and the Wilkinson Early Childhood Center magnet school in the historic 1921 Roe School building.

Franz Park sits in southwest St. Louis at approximately **38.6212° N, 90.3044° W** in zip code 63139. Per the City of St. Louis Planning & Urban Design Agency, the neighborhood’s boundaries run **Dale Avenue** on the North and East, **Manchester Avenue** on the South, and the **City Limits** on the West. Franz Park is bordered by the **Hi-Pointe** neighborhood to the north (across Dale), **Clayton-Tamm** to the east (across Dale on the eastern leg), **Ellendale** directly to the south (across Manchester), and **St. Louis County / the cities of Maplewood and Richmond Heights** to the west (across the city limits at McCausland). The 2020 Census recorded Franz Park as **80.4% White, 7.7% Two or More Races, 6.0% Black, 5.0% Hispanic/Latino, 2.9% Asian, and 0.2% Native American** — with 2,260 residents in 1.1 square kilometers (5,400 per square mile). The St. Louis Public Schools district serves the neighborhood, and the **Marketplace Branch** of the St. Louis Public Library is located on Manchester Avenue.

The neighborhood traces its name to **E.D. Franz**, whose 1915 land donation created the Franz Park city park at the heart of the neighborhood. **Franz Park itself today is a 4.67-acre green space** featuring playground equipment, picnic areas, two lighted tennis courts, a soccer field, two softball fields, horseshoe courts, restrooms, and water fountains — all available on a first-come, first-served basis with no reservation. The neighborhood is part of the broader **Dogtown** area, which got its name as a small mining community in the mid-1800s — an article published **August 14, 1889** in the Missouri Republican is the earliest known reference to the name “Dogtown,” describing a lost 5-year-old boy who lived in “the classic precincts of Dogtown, near Cheltenham.” Dogtown was widely used in the 1800s by miners to describe a group of small shelters around mines.

Franz Park grew with the other Dogtown neighborhoods in the mid-1800s, when **clay mining** moved into the area following the early 1850s extension of the **Pacific Railway** five miles into the countryside (its original station was near today’s Hampton viaduct bridge on Manchester). The area sat atop high-quality fire clay deposits that supported St. Louis’s emerging brick and ceramics industry, and clay extraction intensified through the 1850s onward. Many immigrants from **Ireland, Italy, Germany, and Poland** came to work in the mines. St. Louis fire brick was a growing industry that employed most of the residents, who built homes around the industrial locations. By the end of World War II, the mines had closed, and subdivisions had been built upon the old sites.

The first settler in the area was actually **Charles Gratiot**, a Swiss immigrant from St. Louis credited with opening the area to development more than 200 years ago. After arriving in 1781 and becoming a successful fur trader in the Illinois territory, Gratiot petitioned Spanish authorities for the **“Gratiot League Square”** — an enormous 5,700-acre tract that encompassed many present-day city neighborhoods. He built a mansion near the present-day intersection of Macklind and Manchester (and a farm, a mill, and a distillery), and sold plots of his land to other farmers. The area became a rural farming community until the railroad arrived. With the **1904 World’s Fair**, even more people moved into the neighborhood, as did the **Sulphur Springs Spa** for a few years.

Due to the erratic way in which the houses in the neighborhood were built around individual mining locations, a **variety of architectural styles** exist within Franz Park. Visitors will find 19th-century Queen Anne farmhouses, 1920s bungalows, post-WWII small frame homes, ornate Dutch Colonials, large wood-framed-and-sided homes, even stone homes that look like they belong in Elephant Rock State Park. The neighborhood’s spots near Fairmount and Mitchell sit on some of the highest points in St. Louis, with gorgeous views of Dogtown to the northeast and even the Central West End and Downtown. Franz Park extends a block or so west of McCausland and abuts the suburbs at Richmond Heights, MO — the homes, churches, and retail buildings along McCausland and the homes just to the west on the St. Louis border are particularly distinctive.

Recent decades have brought generational turnover and noticeable investment to the neighborhood — new construction homes are starting around $500,000 while well-maintained older two-bedroom bungalows remain in the $200,000s. Anchor institutions and businesses include **St. James the Greater Catholic Church** (the 1928 English Gothic Revival parish built during the Great Depression entirely by parishioners — the anchor of Dogtown’s Irish Catholic identity and host of the **Ancient Order of Hibernians St. Patrick’s Day Parade** since 1984), the 1922-built **Hi-Pointe Theater** on McCausland Avenue (the oldest continuously operating single-screen movie theater in the St. Louis metro), and the **World’s Largest Amoco Sign** at the intersection of Skinker, Clayton Road, and Oakland — a 44-ton-of-steel, 5,800-bulb landmark that originated as a Standard Oil sign in the 1920s.

Franz Park’s modern business activity reflects its position as a Dogtown service corridor connecting the south-side neighborhoods to Forest Park, the Saint Louis Zoo, and the I-64 / I-44 highway gateway. Commercial activity concentrates along Manchester Avenue (**Failoni’s Restaurant** at 6715 Manchester since 1916, Olympia Kebob House & Taverna on McCausland, El Potro Mexican restaurant, Sunny’s Cantina, Cham Bakery) and along McCausland (the antique-shop corridor, Discount Vac). The listings on this page draw from the broader Franz Park / Dogtown / Manchester Avenue professional corridor, with anchor institutions like Failoni’s, plus a mix of specialty contractors, B2B marketing agencies, political consultancies, and industrial suppliers serving the broader St. Louis region from positions accessible to Forest Park, the Maplewood and Richmond Heights border, and the I-64 corridor.

Image: The annual Ancient Order of Hibernians St. Patrick’s Day Parade in Dogtown, anchored by St. James the Greater Catholic Church — the cultural heart of the five Dogtown neighborhoods, of which Franz Park is one — Wikimedia Commons / CC BY-SA 2.5 by Agent-88

Franz Park sits in southwest St. Louis between Manchester Avenue and Dale, where the city follows a classic Midwest four-season pattern. Hot, humid summers with highs in the upper 80s°F bring peak activity to the 4.67-acre Franz Park itself — the playground, lighted tennis courts, soccer field, two softball fields, and horseshoe courts draw families and casual sports leagues. Spring and fall fill the Manchester and McCausland commercial corridors with foot traffic to Failoni’s Restaurant patio (live music Thursday-Saturday), Olympia Kebob House gyros, the McCausland antique-shop corridor, and the Marketplace Branch library.

Cold winters with periodic snow shift commerce indoors to Franz Park’s steady service-corridor providers — the historic Failoni’s dining rooms (brick-oven pizza and Italian American specialties under the oldest still-in-use cash register in greater St. Louis), the Hi-Pointe Theater’s aquamarine-seated single screen, and the indoor manufacturing and B2B operations of the broader Manchester and Knox Industrial corridors. **St. Patrick’s Day** (March 17) brings thousands to the Ancient Order of Hibernians Parade along Tamm Avenue. Quick I-64 and I-44 access at the McCausland and Hampton gateways keeps the Franz Park service market moving year-round and connects directly to Forest Park, the Saint Louis Zoo, and downtown.

## Why Franz Park Businesses Choose St Louis Near Me Directory

## Gravois Park — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/gravois-park

# Gravois Park Neighborhood Guide – South St. Louis History & Businesses

## Explore Gravois Park’s Historic Streets, Cherokee Street Culture, and Local Services

Updated May 2026.

## Gravois Park Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

Gravois Park, St. Louis

Gravois Park is the densely-built southeast St. Louis neighborhood named for the namesake park whose land was granted by the US government in 1812 as one of the earliest public commons in the city. Originally settled by German and Eastern European immigrants and developed during the turn-of-the-20th-century streetcar suburb era, the neighborhood sits within the 4,642-property Gravois-Jefferson Streetcar Suburb Historic District and is the densest neighborhood in the City of St. Louis (2010 census). The corridor offers boutique retail, restaurants, art galleries, music venues, family services, and creative work along Cherokee Street, Grand Boulevard, and Chippewa.

Gravois Park is one of St. Louis’s 79 official neighborhoods, located in southeast St. Louis at **38.5915° N, 90.2358° W**. Its boundaries run north to **Cherokee Street**, south to **Chippewa Street**, east to **Jefferson Avenue**, and west to **Grand Boulevard**. The neighborhood is bordered by **Benton Park West** to the north (across Cherokee Street), **Marine Villa** to the east (across Jefferson Avenue), **Dutchtown** to the south (across Chippewa Street), and **Tower Grove South** to the west (across Grand Boulevard). At the 2020 census, the neighborhood had 4,683 residents on its 0.42 square miles. The racial makeup was 60.7% Black, 20.6% White, 0.5% Native American, 2.7% Asian, 8.0% Two or More Races, and 7.5% Some Other Race; 10.9% of the population was of Hispanic or Latino origin. As of the 2010 census, **Gravois Park was the densest neighborhood in the City of St. Louis**.

The history of Gravois Park begins with one of the oldest public spaces in St. Louis. The namesake park itself dates from **1812**, when the land was granted by the US government as one of the earliest public commons in the city — making Gravois Park’s anchor green space older than the neighborhood, older than the streetcar lines that built up around it, and older than most of the City of St. Louis itself. Praised for its beauty already in the 19th century, the park was featured in **1914** as part of a walking tour of the city meant to showcase the City Plan Commission’s “idealized view of the shape of the city.” The neighborhood that grew up around it shares its origins with Dutchtown (to the south) and Benton Park West (to the north) — Gravois Park was considered part of both at varying times until fairly recently. Its first settlers were German and Eastern European immigrants who came to St. Louis in massive numbers throughout the 1800s.

Gravois Park is now part of one of the largest historic districts in St. Louis: the **Gravois-Jefferson Streetcar Suburb Historic District**, listed on the National Register of Historic Places, which includes **4,642 historic properties** built during the late 19th and early 20th centuries across Gravois Park, Dutchtown, and adjacent streetcar-era neighborhoods. The neighborhood’s northern boundary, Cherokee Street, also falls within the **Cherokee-Lemp Historic District**, where the famous Cherokee Street Antique Row runs as a corridor of antique shops, boutiques, bakeries, restaurants, art galleries, and music venues. A portion of Cherokee Street’s antique row forms the historic edge of the neighborhood and remains the cultural anchor of the entire corridor.

Several St. Louis landmarks anchor the neighborhood’s German-immigrant heritage. **Holy Cross Lutheran Church** — construction begun in 1867 and completed in 1889 — was originally called “*Die Kirche zum Heiligen Kreuz*” by the large German population that filled these blocks. The **Concordia Lutheran College and Seminary** was dedicated in 1850 and originally occupied a building on the west side of Jefferson Avenue at Winnebago Street before it was demolished and replaced by a large Gothic structure. **Froebel Literacy Academy Elementary School**, opened in 1895 on Nebraska Avenue, has continuously served the neighborhood for over 130 years and remains the neighborhood’s St. Louis Public Schools campus, serving Preschool through 5th grade. Another notable architectural survivor in the neighborhood is a former **Turnverein Hall** (the German-American gymnastic and cultural society building), still identifiable by an engraved stone set into the wall above the foundation on the south side of the building.

Gravois Park sits on one of the most regular, untouched **rectilinear street grids** in St. Louis — the kind of dense, walkable, two-story brick residential pattern that made the neighborhood the densest in the city as of the 2010 census. The neighborhood’s commercial life is shaped by an unusual circumstance: it is **lined with significant commercial corridors on all four sides**. Cherokee Street to the north (antique row, art galleries, music venues, restaurants), Jefferson Avenue to the east, Grand Boulevard to the west, and Chippewa Street to the south — each of these is one of the most historically significant commercial corridors in South City. This four-corridor framing has ensured a steady population and steady street life for the neighborhood, even through the well-known 1970s-onward period of disinvestment and white flight that affected much of south city.

In the 21st century, Cherokee Street has driven a clear renaissance for the entire neighborhood. The streets and sidewalks underwent a major redo with new streetlights, trees, and curb cuts, and the corridor has become widely recognized as one of the city’s most distinctive cultural districts — particularly famous for its concentration of **al fresco Mexican food, Hispanic markets, locally-made delicacies, and rare imported goods**, along with art galleries like **Firecracker Press**, music venues like **The Golden Record**, **2720 Cherokee**, **Off Broadway**, and **The Luminary**, fine art venues like **Untitled Fine Art**, and craft breweries like **Earthbound Beer**. Neighborhood favorites include **The Taco & Ice Cream Joint**, the 24-hour **Buttery Restaurant**, and **Irish Corner Pub** with live music and open mic nights. A new park, **Love Bank Park at Nebraska and Cherokee**, has been planned for the neighborhood as well.

The **Gravois Park Neighborhood Association** — founded in 1999 by original president Rita Ford and meeting at the **Rita Ford Community Center** — remains one of the most active in south city, hosting Easter Eggstravaganza, clothing swaps, holiday parties, December caroling, and seasonal events throughout the year, along with alley cleanups and a Neighborhood Accountability Board for restorative justice with local youth. The association also manages multiple community gardens including the **Pride of the Potomac Garden**, which grows vegetables and flowers. In recent years the neighborhood has gained recognition for having **some of the most comprehensive traffic calming in any of the 79 St. Louis neighborhoods** — speed humps have been strategically installed on most residential streets — and **Chippewa Street has seen significant investment**, looking better than it has in years. The 2020 census recorded the Hispanic/Latino population at nearly 11% of the neighborhood (up from 5% in 2010), reflecting Cherokee Street’s deep ties to St. Louis’s growing Mexican-American community.

Gravois Park is conveniently positioned for the rest of the city. **Cherokee Street** serves as the key east-west artery just north of the neighborhood, connecting to major commercial areas and public transit. **Grand Boulevard** borders the west side and provides access to downtown and interstate connections. **Chippewa Street** and **Jefferson Avenue** frame the southern and eastern edges, respectively, linking local traffic to schools, shops, and adjacent neighborhoods. Metro buses run along all four corridors, connecting Gravois Park residents to downtown St. Louis, Tower Grove Park, the SLU and BJC medical campuses, and the broader regional system. The neighborhood is also home to **St. Alexius Hospital** (the former Lutheran Hospital, also known as the St. Alexius Jefferson Campus), which has historically served Gravois Park and surrounding south city neighborhoods.

Image: Homes across from Gravois Park in the neighborhood of the same name — the densest neighborhood in the City of St. Louis (2010 census), built on land granted as a public commons by the US government in 1812 — Wikimedia Commons / CC BY-SA 3.0 by Onegentlemanofverona

Gravois Park sits on the gently rolling near-southeast terrain of St. Louis at 38.5915° N, a few miles south of downtown on the same broad upland that the French settlers worked into common fields in the late 1700s. The neighborhood experiences St. Louis’s full humid continental weather range: hot, humid summers with frequent 90°F days, cold winters with regular freezing temperatures, and a notably active spring severe weather season that includes real tornado corridor exposure. Peak outdoor activity falls during spring and fall, when the namesake Gravois Park’s playgrounds, gazebo, pavilion, and open lawns draw the heaviest neighborhood use alongside the Cherokee Street Antique Row, the Pride of the Potomac community garden, and the Gravois Park Neighborhood Association’s seasonal events. Cool seasons drive intense indoor activity at Cherokee Street’s music venues (The Golden Record, 2720 Cherokee, Off Broadway), art galleries (Firecracker Press, The Luminary, Untitled Fine Art), and Hispanic markets, along with neighborhood anchors like Holy Cross Lutheran Church (1867-1889), Froebel Literacy Academy Elementary School (1895), and St. Alexius Hospital. Winter shifts commerce indoors to Earthbound Beer, the 24-hour Buttery Restaurant, The Taco & Ice Cream Joint, Irish Corner Pub, and the corridor of dining and retail along Cherokee, Grand, Jefferson, and Chippewa. The neighborhood’s 1880s-1920s brick construction — the densest residential stock in the city — has weathered well over a century of Missouri climate with the consistency typical of South City’s German-built two-story brick housing.

## Hamilton Heights — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/hamilton-heights

Neighborhood

Hamilton Heights

Coordinates

38.6683° N, 90.2891° W

Ranked by 131+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  10 of 10 verified Hamilton Heights / MLK Drive corridor providers ✓  All businesses verified via Google Business ✓  Avg. rating: 4.8/5 across 131 reviews

## Hamilton Heights Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Hamilton Heights, St. Louis

Hamilton Heights anchors a deep-history rectangular stretch of northwest St. Louis named for Civil War Missouri governor Hamilton R. Gamble — once part of a Spanish colonial land grant on the historic St. Charles Road route to St. Louis, today defined by the Dr. Martin Luther King Drive commercial corridor, the Wellston Loop streetcar legacy, the 1903 Hamilton Hotel (956 Hamilton Avenue, built for the 1904 World’s Fair, now on the National Register of Historic Places), and a 1890s-1920s brick residential streetscape with two William B. Ittner-designed schools (Hempstead 1906, on the NRHP).

Hamilton Heights sits in northwest St. Louis at approximately **38.6683° N, 90.2891° W** in zip codes 63112 and 63133. Per the City of St. Louis Planning & Urban Design Agency, the neighborhood’s boundaries run **Dr. Martin Luther King Drive** (co-signed with Missouri Route 180) on the Northeast, southward to **Union Boulevard** on the East, westward to **Page Avenue** on the South, northward along **Cockrill Street / the City Limits** on the Western edge (bordering Wellston, MO) back to Dr. Martin Luther King Drive. Hamilton Heights is bordered by **Wells-Goodfellow** to the north (across MLK Drive), **Academy** to the east (across Union Boulevard), the **West End** to the south (across Page Avenue), and **St. Louis County and the City of Wellston** to the west. The neighborhood today is approximately **98% Black**, with a population that declined significantly from the 1990s to 2020 Census amid the broader patterns of north St. Louis disinvestment.

The land that became Hamilton Heights was once part of a **Spanish colonial land grant** known as Survey 3033, originally the property of Baptiste La Fleur. During colonial times, travelers passed through the area between St. Louis and St. Charles along **St. Charles Road** — the historic Mississippi River-to-Missouri River route, now Dr. Martin Luther King Drive. By the mid-1850s, the land was subdivided and sold as smaller tracts. One such tract became the property and estate of **Hamilton R. Gamble**, who served as governor of Missouri during the Civil War and is the namesake for the neighborhood. The early settlement of **Rinkelville** grew up around the Six Mile House operated by George Rinkel Jr. on the St. Charles Road.

In 1868, **Erastus Wells** — a St. Louis transit magnate — purchased a tract west of Rinkelville and developed a fine country estate. To provide access to the area and stimulate development, Wells and others constructed and opened the **West End Narrow Gauge Railway** in 1878. A station was established at the line’s intersection with St. Charles Road near the Wells estate and bore the name of **Wellston**. The Citizen’s Railway Company simultaneously operated a horse car line along St. Charles Road. The **Wellston Loop** became one of the major streetcar transit hubs in St. Louis — later anchored by the Hodiamont streetcar line — and the surrounding commercial district at the Wellston Loop became a thriving regional shopping destination through the early to mid-20th century.

In 1903, the **Hamilton Hotel** was built at 956 Hamilton Avenue to accommodate visitors to the **1904 Louisiana Purchase Exposition** (the St. Louis World’s Fair). The hotel later became part of the World’s Fair lodging network and remains a National Register of Historic Places landmark (listed April 29, 2011) — today operated as Hamilton Place Apartments, a 98-unit affordable-housing community. By 1906, the St. Louis Public Schools commissioned famed architect **William B. Ittner** to design **Hempstead Elementary School** at 5872 Minerva Avenue (NRHP-listed July 3, 2007), one of eight Ittner schools approved that year. Hempstead closed in 2004 and suffered fire damage in 2014; a second Ittner-designed school (the former Hempstead annex / Roe School) also served the neighborhood.

The **1950s and 1960s** marked a period of demographic transition as white residents moved out and African American residents moved in — many newcomers were part of a large wave of African Americans migrating to St. Louis from the rural South following World War II. Many moved directly into the neighborhood, creating a close-knit community anchored by the vital **Wellston shopping district**. As the children in these families became adults, they moved elsewhere. This generational shift, combined with the **decline of the Wellston shopping district beginning in the early 1970s**, accelerated the neighborhood’s economic decline through the late 20th century. Hamilton Heights lost 30% of its population from 1990 to 2000 and continued declining through 2020.

Hamilton Heights today is anchored by **Hamilton Park** (corner of Dr. Martin Luther King Drive and Clara Avenue) with playground equipment and softball/baseball fields including a **Cardinals Care ballfield** donated by the St. Louis Cardinals charity. The neighborhood is noteworthy for its **numerous religious institutions**, including remaining African American churches and signs of former Jewish places of worship. **Ford Elementary Community Education Center** (built 1964) and **Emerson Elementary** still serve the neighborhood, with magnet/choice school access throughout the St. Louis Public Schools district. The 1907 Minerva Apartments (an early 20th-century brick beauty) anchor the residential character, alongside two-to-four-family flats and larger single-family brick homes built between 1890 and 1920.

Hamilton Heights’s modern business activity reflects its position along the **Dr. Martin Luther King Drive Great Streets corridor** — a 2016 city / East-West Gateway Coordinating Council initiative focused on revitalization from Union Boulevard west to the city limits. The **Wellston Loop Community Development Corporation** coordinates with neighborhood organizations including **Union West Community Corporation** (the local neighborhood association) and **Hamilton Heights Neighborhood Organization, Inc.**, on revitalization. The listings on this page draw from the broader Hamilton Heights / MLK Drive / Union Boulevard professional corridor — with anchor providers along MLK Drive (Go Getter STL at 5736 MLK), Private Duty Services on Clemens Ave, Ade Consulting on Vernon Ave, Majah Services on Union Blvd, Speedy Mobile Market on Maple Ave, and Warehouse West on Hamilton Avenue, plus chamber, association, and business-district partners serving the broader St. Louis region.

Image: The 1903 Hamilton Hotel at 956 Hamilton Avenue — built for the 1904 St. Louis World’s Fair, listed on the National Register of Historic Places April 29, 2011, now operating as Hamilton Place Apartments — Wikimedia Commons / Creative Commons

Hamilton Heights sits in northwest St. Louis along the historic St. Charles Road / Dr. Martin Luther King Drive corridor, where the city follows a classic Midwest four-season pattern. Hot, humid summers with highs in the upper 80s°F bring peak activity to Hamilton Park’s playground, softball and baseball fields (including the Cardinals Care ballfield), and the family programs hosted by Ford Elementary Community Education Center. Spring and fall fill the MLK Drive and Union Boulevard commercial corridors with foot traffic to Go Getter STL, Warehouse West on Hamilton Avenue, and the Wellston Loop revitalization activities along the historic shopping district.

Cold winters with periodic snow shift commerce indoors to Hamilton Heights’ service-corridor providers — Private Duty Services’ structured care planning, Ade Consulting’s Vernon Avenue management services, Majah Services on Union Blvd, and the indoor administrative operations of the Heartland St. Louis Black Chamber of Commerce on North Spring Avenue. The 1890s-1920s brick residential streetscape, the 1903 Hamilton Hotel / Hamilton Place Apartments, and the William B. Ittner-designed schools weather Missouri seasons with the architectural durability of the World’s Fair era. Quick I-70 / Goodfellow Boulevard access keeps the Hamilton Heights service market connected year-round to downtown St. Louis and Lambert International Airport.
