## Hi Pointe — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/hi-pointe

Coordinates

38.6290° N, 90.3079° W

Ranked by 6,807+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  10 of 10 verified Hi-Pointe / Dogtown corridor providers ✓  All businesses verified via Google Business ✓  Avg. rating: 4.5/5 across 6,807 reviews

## Hi-Pointe Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Hi-Pointe, St. Louis

Hi-Pointe takes its name from sitting at one of the highest elevations in the City of St. Louis, anchoring a roughly rectangular section of the city’s southwest edge that locals also call one of the three official Dogtown neighborhoods alongside Clayton-Tamm and Franz Park — defined today by the storied Skinker-Clayton-McCausland gateway, the 1922 Hi-Pointe Theatre, and the World’s Largest Amoco Sign.

Hi-Pointe sits in southwest St. Louis at approximately **38.6290° N, 90.3079° W** in zip code 63117. Per the City of St. Louis, the neighborhood is bounded by **Clayton Avenue and Oakland Avenue** on the North, the **City Limits** (where St. Louis meets the inner-ring suburb of Maplewood) on the West, **Dale Avenue** on the South, and **Louisville Avenue** on the East. Inside that compact footprint, Hi-Pointe centers on the heavily-traveled corner where **Skinker Boulevard, Clayton Road, McCausland Avenue, and Interstate 64/U.S. Highway 40** all converge — sitting directly at the southwest entrance to Forest Park. Hi-Pointe is bordered by **Wydown Skinker** to the north, **Clayton-Tamm** to the east, **Franz Park** to the south (across Dale), and **St. Louis County / Richmond Heights and Maplewood** to the west.

Hi-Pointe’s early industrial story is the same one shared by the rest of **Dogtown**. After the **Pacific Railway** laid five miles of tracks west from St. Louis through Cheltenham in the early 1850s, clay and coal mining became the economic engine of the area, and **St. James the Greater Parish** was founded at Tamm and Wade in 1860 to anchor the immigrant community of Irish, German, French, and Balkan miners and brick-makers who built the neighborhood. Hi-Pointe’s own century includes farming, a goat farm, fire-brick clay mining, and even a small airport landing field within living memory. The name “Dogtown” dates to the mid-1800s mining era — the earliest known reference appears in an August 14, 1889 Missouri Republican article.

The **1904 Louisiana Purchase Exposition** (the World’s Fair) on the western edge of Forest Park — immediately north of Hi-Pointe’s modern boundary — transformed the neighborhood. Locals will tell you some of the leftover Fair building materials made their way into the homes built in Hi-Pointe afterward, and the Fair itself sped up settlement of what had been one of the city’s last loosely-developed corners. Today, Hi-Pointe’s housing stock blends modest brick, stone, and frame homes built between roughly the 1900s and the 1920s with newer infill construction, lofts, and modern apartment buildings — a mix locals praise as some of the best-maintained in all of Dogtown.

The Hi-Pointe corner is home to two of the most-photographed landmarks in St. Louis. The **Hi-Pointe Theatre** at 1005 McCausland Avenue opened in 1922 and is the oldest continuously-operating single-screen movie theater in the St. Louis metropolitan area, acquired by the nonprofit **Cinema St. Louis** in January 2023 as its permanent base of operations. A block away, atop Stevenson’s Hi-Pointe Amoco at the Skinker-Clayton-Oakland triangle, stands the **World’s Largest Amoco Sign** — a 44-ton steel descendant of the original 1932 Standard Red Crown sign, with the current 40-foot-by-60-foot Amoco lettering refreshed in 2019.

The southwest edge of the neighborhood, around 6300 Clayton Road, holds another long-standing institution: **The Cheshire** (originally the Cheshire Inn), a Tudor-styled hotel and restaurant complex whose origins date to the 1920s. The hotel sits partially in Hi-Pointe and partially in adjacent Richmond Heights, and underwent a multi-million-dollar renovation in 2011 that preserved the original Fox & Hounds Tavern fireplace and the British-inn character that made it a generational St. Louis date-night fixture.

Despite its small footprint, Hi-Pointe’s commercial mix is genuinely diverse. The **McCausland Avenue corridor** delivers entertainment, hospitality, and licensed retail. South Hanley Road and Clayton Avenue host professional consultancies and shipping logistics. Oakland Avenue serves the office and coworking community. Dale Avenue is home to a residential architecture firm, and McCausland holds a forensic-engineering specialist with national reach. **Hi-Pointe Drive-In** at 1033 McCausland (a smashburger restaurant that opened in 2017 on the site of a 1980s drive-in) draws regional foot traffic that spills into every adjacent business.

For visitors and St. Louis newcomers, Hi-Pointe is the easiest neighborhood in the city to find: take Highway 40/I-64 west, look for the giant Amoco sign towering over the Skinker-Clayton-Oakland triangle, and you’re at the front door of one of St. Louis’s highest-elevation, most-recognizable, and most-traveled corners — a tight one-square-mile district where Dogtown’s Irish-immigrant brick-making heritage meets Forest Park’s southwest gateway and a century of continuously-operating film, food, and fuel landmarks. The listings on this page draw from the broader Hi-Pointe / McCausland / Dogtown professional corridor, with anchor businesses serving the neighborhood and the wider St. Louis region.

Image: The Grand Basin in Forest Park — the historic 1904 World’s Fair centerpiece sitting immediately north of Hi-Pointe’s Clayton/Oakland boundary, at the southwest gateway to the park — Wikimedia Commons / Creative Commons

Hi-Pointe sits at one of the highest natural elevations inside the City of St. Louis — the very feature that gave the neighborhood its name. The corner of Skinker, Clayton, and Oakland (where the giant Amoco sign tops out roughly 40 feet above a service-station roofline) is visible from up to two miles away on Interstate 64. St. Louis weather brings hot, humid summers in the upper 80s°F and cold, snowy winters with overnight lows that regularly dip into the teens, and Hi-Pointe’s elevated, exposed intersection feels every degree of that range.

Spring and fall drive peak foot traffic to the McCausland Avenue corridor — the Hi-Pointe Theatre, Hi-Pointe Drive-In, Blue Sage Cannabis Deli, and DHL Express all benefit from the mild months and easy walking weather. Summer humidity pushes diners and moviegoers indoors after sundown, where the Hi-Pointe Theatre’s vintage “It’s Cool Inside” neon still applies. Winter ice and overnight snow shift activity to indoor professional services: Hi Pointe Business Center on Oakland, CenterPoint Technologies on South Hanley, Marino Engineering on McCausland, Schaub Projects on Dale, and the St Louis Near Me Directory team in Maryland Heights all run year-round indoor operations.

## Holly Hills — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/holly-hills

Ranked by 3,979+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  Anchored by 180-acre Carondelet Park & 1920s “Hollywood elegance” mansions ✓  AHERA-certified environmental testing & 35-year custom millwork shops ✓  Avg. rating: 4.9/5 across 3,979 reviews

## Holly Hills Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Holly Hills, St. Louis

Holly Hills serves as South St. Louis’s most architecturally elegant 1920s residential showplace — designed as the city’s answer to Hollywood’s grace, anchored by the 180-acre Carondelet Park (dedicated July 4, 1876), the historic 1842 Lyle Mansion, the Carondelet YMCA Rec Plex, and Holly Hills Boulevard’s grand stretch of mansions, German-style castles, and Arts & Crafts bungalows from Arendes to Leona — offering environmental testing, custom millwork, branding, coffee, and chamber services across South Grand and Holly Hills Boulevard.

Holly Hills is one of St. Louis’s 79 official neighborhoods, located at **38.5683° N, 90.2749° W** in southeast St. Louis City. The neighborhood is bounded by **Grand Boulevard** on the east, **Holly Hills Boulevard and Carondelet Park** on the south, **Leona Street and the Union Pacific (formerly Missouri Pacific) Railroad** on the west, and **Bates Street then Walsh Street** on the north. Surrounding neighborhoods include **Dutchtown** to the north, **Bevo Mill** to the west and north, **Boulevard Heights** to the southwest, and **Carondelet** to the east. The neighborhood spans **ZIP codes 63111 and 63116** and sits approximately seven miles south of Downtown St. Louis at the terminus of South Grand Boulevard. Holly Hills is one of the cleanest, safest, and most tightly-knit residential neighborhoods in the city — a small-town atmosphere where neighbors know one another, many residents have lived here their entire lives, and the perimeter is largely landscaped by volunteer-led seasonal plantings of thousands of bulbs and perennials.

The land that Holly Hills now covers was originally part of the **Commonfields of the independent City of Carondelet** — the French Creole farming village founded in **1767** by **Clement Delor de Treget**, a former French naval officer who had been appointed syndic by the Spanish government to grant lots to settlers. By the time of the Louisiana Purchase in 1803, there were approximately 250 villagers living in 50 houses. The Commonfields stretched from present-day Virginia Avenue in the east to Morganford Road in the west, and from Lafayette Avenue south to Meramec Street — with grazing commons expanded south of River des Peres to a mile beyond Jefferson Barracks. Carondelet was originally called **Louisburg** in honor of King Louis XV of France, then **Prairie a Catalan** after settler Louis Catalan, before being officially renamed **Carondelet** in **1794** in honor of **Baron François Louis Hector de Carondelet**, a Fleming appointed Spanish governor of Louisiana. Informal names included **Delor’s Village** and **Vide Poche** (“empty pocket”), supposedly because Carondelet residents’ gambling skill would send their St. Louis neighbors home with empty pockets.

German immigrants began settling in Carondelet in the late 1840s, changing the character of the village from French Creole to German-American. Carondelet was **incorporated as an independent city on March 1, 1851**, and then **annexed by the City of St. Louis on April 4, 1870**. With annexation came the St. Louis park and library systems — in **1874**, the City purchased 180 acres of the old Carondelet Commons (plus part of the **Lyle estate**) for $143,000, establishing **Carondelet Park**. The park officially opened in 1875 and was **dedicated on July 4, 1876**, the centennial anniversary of the signing of the Declaration of Independence. The land had previously belonged to **Alexander Lacey Lyle**, whose family built the **Lyle Mansion** on the property in **1842** (or 1850, by some accounts) — a Southern-sympathizing family who left St. Louis at the start of the Civil War. The Lyle House still stands in the park today and is a designated **St. Louis City Landmark**. The same era produced two other Carondelet firsts: **Carondelet native Susan Blow**, daughter of U.S. Representative **Henry Taylor Blow**, founded what is regarded as the **first continuous public-school kindergarten in the United States** at the **Des Peres School** (6303 Michigan Avenue) in **September 1873**, modeled on Friedrich Fröbel’s German concept.

In the **early 1920s**, developers **William A. Federer**, **Gus Arendes**, and **Don Livingston Sr.** purchased a large plot of land from the **Missouri Pacific Railroad** and developed the area north of Carondelet Park as the Holly Hills neighborhood. The name **“Holly Hills”** was derived from **Hollywood** and intended to reflect a similar level of grace and elegance — an aspirational California-style suburban vision transplanted to South St. Louis. An essential element of Federer’s plan was to construct homes of many different styles, sizes, and characters: from grand **German-style castles** and Tudor mansions to cozy **Arts & Crafts bungalows**. The **first building permit** in the community was issued in **1926** for a home on Holly Hills Boulevard across from Carondelet Park. The Great Depression scaled back many of the later homes from Federer’s original vision, but the showcase “Hollywood elegance” mansions along Holly Hills Boulevard from **Arendes Drive to Leona Street** remained the architectural calling card. Federer’s own residence has remained in his family and is still lovingly maintained by his daughter and her family. Street names within Holly Hills honor the developers (**Arendes Drive**, plus streets named for **William Federer** and **Don Livingston**) and other historic figures: **Adkins Avenue** (after steamboat captain Henry Adkins), **Alexander Street** (after Alexander Lacey Lyle), **Bates Street** (after Frederick Bates, the second governor of Missouri), **Bellerive Boulevard** (after Louis St. Ange de Bellerive, the pre-Spanish commandant of St. Louis), **Bowen Street** (after Confederate General John Bowen, a former St. Louis neighbor of Ulysses S. Grant), and **Burgen Avenue** (after the Burgenland province in eastern Austria, from the 1909 Burgen Place subdivision).

Carondelet Park, which borders the southern edge of Holly Hills along Holly Hills Boulevard, is the **third-largest park in St. Louis** at approximately 180 acres. It is bounded by **Holly Hills Boulevard** on the north, **Loughborough Avenue** on the south, **Interstate 55** on the east, and **Leona Street** on the west. The park’s undulating topography — shaped by underground caves and sinkholes — creates a Picturesque effect; nine sinkholes were joined in **1913** to form **Horseshoe Lake**, now one of two annually stocked fishing lakes. Scenic drives along the ridge lines were introduced in 1890. A bridge entering the park’s north side was constructed as part of an effort overseen by famed landscape architect **George Kessler** from 1902 to 1909 to link major parks and cemeteries along a singular artery. All historic amenities — the Lyle House, Boathouse, Music Pavilion, Old Stable, and Music Stand — were built prior to 1920. Carondelet Park is also unique as the **only original landscape in the St. Louis park system** — an unaltered Karst landscape that paints a picture of what the entire city could have looked like had it not been developed. The **Carondelet YMCA Rec Plex** sits at the east end of the park adjacent to I-55, and the one-mile **Carondelet Connector trail**, opened by **Great Rivers Greenway** in 2014, links the park’s trail system to the nine-mile **River des Peres Greenway**.

Today, Holly Hills is one of South St. Louis’s most desirable neighborhoods. It is a small, residential, tightly-knit community with very few intra-neighborhood businesses — commerce tends to cluster in adjacent Carondelet, Dutchtown, Bevo Mill, and Boulevard Heights. The neighborhood’s key business anchors today include **St. John Environmental Consulting, LLC** on Grace Avenue (the veteran-owned, BBB A+ accredited, AHERA-certified asbestos / mold / lead inspector with 25 years of experience); **Silver Fox Marketing** on Wilmington Avenue (15-year sign, vinyl-wrap, and brand identity shop); **Speakeasy Café** on Holly Hills Avenue (the veteran-owned two-story specialty-coffee shop); **St Louis Sash Corp.** on South 38th Street (the 35-year father-and-son custom wood door / window / millwork manufacturer for historic homes); **St Louis Laser** on South Broadway (industrial engraving); the **Heartland St. Louis Black Chamber of Commerce**; the **Southwest Area Chamber of Commerce**; **Andre’s Banquets & Catering** at Sunset Hills; the privately-run Catholic **St. Stephen Protomartyr School** (preschool through 8th grade); and easy access to the **Loughborough Commons** retail center (Lowes, Schnucks, Starbucks). Holly Hills sits within walking distance of the **Carondelet Branch Library** (completed 1908, designed by Prague-born architect Ernst Preisler in Neoclassical style with Maine granite, funded by Andrew Carnegie as the third Carnegie branch in St. Louis, fully renovated in 2012) and the historic **St. Anthony of Padua Church** (1910, twin steeples) in adjacent Dutchtown.

Image: The Lyle House in Carondelet Park — the 1842 wood-framed Southern home of Alexander Lacey Lyle, whose 180-acre estate became Carondelet Park (purchased by the City in 1874, dedicated July 4, 1876). The Lyle House is a designated St. Louis City Landmark and the historic anchor of the park that borders Holly Hills along its southern edge — Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA

Holly Hills sits seven miles south of Downtown St. Louis at 38.5683° N at the terminus of South Grand Boulevard, with seamless access to Interstate 55 (one mile east), South Broadway, the Carondelet historic district, and the Mississippi River bluffs at Bellerive Park. The neighborhood experiences St. Louis’s full humid continental climate: hot, humid summers with frequent 90°F days, cold winters with regular freezing temperatures, and an active spring severe weather season. 

Peak outdoor activity falls during spring, summer, and fall, when **Carondelet Park’s** 180 acres of rolling Karst topography along Holly Hills Boulevard draw heaviest visitor use at the two annually-stocked fishing lakes (including the 1913 Horseshoe Lake), the boathouse, the tennis courts, the softball / baseball / soccer fields, the horseshoe pits, the playgrounds, the picnic pavilions, the music pavilion, and the 1.6-mile bicycle path that connects to the **River des Peres Greenway** via the 2014 **Carondelet Connector**; when the **Carondelet YMCA Rec Plex** at the east end of the park is humming with indoor aquatic, gymnasium, and weight-room activity; and when the residential streets of **Holly Hills Boulevard** from Arendes to Leona host the volunteer-led seasonal bulb and perennial plantings that have made this neighborhood a perimeter showplace. 

Cool seasons drive intense indoor activity at **Speakeasy Café** at 527 Holly Hills Avenue (the veteran-owned two-story specialty coffee shop open seven days a week), **St. John Environmental Consulting** on Grace Avenue (year-round demand from homeowners of the 1920s brick housing stock for asbestos, lead, mold, and indoor air quality inspections), **Silver Fox Marketing** on Wilmington Avenue, **St Louis Sash Corp.** on South 38th Street (the 35-year custom millwork shop that supplies precision wood doors, windows, and architectural details for historic-home renovators), **St. Stephen Protomartyr School**, the **Carondelet Branch Library** on Michigan Avenue (renovated 2012), the **Loughborough Commons** retail center, and the indoor venues of nearby Bevo Mill, Dutchtown, Carondelet, and Boulevard Heights. The neighborhood’s 1926–1940s brick housing stock — including its Holly Hills Boulevard mansions, German-style castles, Tudor revivals, and Arts & Crafts bungalows — have weathered roughly a century of Missouri climate with the durability of the depression-era German-American masons who built them.

## Hyde Park — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/hyde-park

38.6615° N, 90.2141° W

## Hyde Park Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Hyde Park, St. Louis

Hyde Park anchors one of the oldest German-settled stretches of north St. Louis — founded in 1844 as the independent town of New Bremen by German immigrants and named, somewhat ironically, for Hyde Park in London — today defined by its National Register historic district, the twin 215-foot spires of Most Holy Trinity Catholic Church, the circa-1823 Bissell Mansion (the oldest surviving brick house in the city), the 14.5-acre Hyde Park itself, and a brick-and-flounder-house streetscape that recalls a turn-of-the-century European working-class neighborhood.

Hyde Park sits in north St. Louis at approximately **38.6615° N, 90.2141° W** in zip code 63107. Per the City of St. Louis, the neighborhood is bounded by **Ferry Street** on the North, **Interstate 70** on the East, **Palm Street and Natural Bridge Avenue** on the South, and **Glasgow Avenue** on the West — with Bremen, Salisbury, North Florissant, 20th, Blair, and Broadway among its key streets. Hyde Park is bordered by the **College Hill** and **Fairground** neighborhoods to the northwest, **St. Louis Place** and **Old North St. Louis** to the south, and the **Near North Riverfront** to the east. The neighborhood is a designated **National Register Historic District** and remains a predominantly Black community after decades of population decline.

The area first shifted from rural to urban in the **1840s**, when German immigrants settled the affordable, river-overlooking land along old **Bellefontaine Road** (now Broadway) — then a produce route running north from downtown. Many of these families came from the German city of **Bremen**, and so the settlement was named **New Bremen**. A survey of the town was executed by Edward Hutawa in 1844 at the direction of four principal landowners — **George Buchanan, E.C. Angelrodt, N.N. Destrehan, and Emil Mallinckrodt** — who incorporated the town of Bremen in 1850 and gave their names to four of its east-west streets. Broadway was dedicated as a public highway on May 10, 1852.

The center and namesake of the neighborhood, **Hyde Park** itself, resulted from the sale of **14.5 acres to the city by the Farrar family estate in 1854**. Bernard G. Farrar, an early resident, had lost his life during the 1849 cholera epidemic while saving other victims; he had also donated the land at 14th and Mallinckrodt for the Most Holy Trinity Catholic Church. By 1856, this thriving new center of commercial and industrial activity was annexed by St. Louis. The park saw history unfold on July 4, 1863, when a Civil War-era riot erupted out of wartime tensions, killing two fairgoers and a number of Union soldiers.

The neighborhood’s defining landmark is **Most Holy Trinity Catholic Church** at 3519 North 14th Street. The Holy Trinity congregation was founded in 1848 as the mother church for German Catholics in north St. Louis; the present limestone edifice — a late French Gothic Revival design by architect **Joseph Conradi**, completed in 1898 — features a cruciform plan 155 feet long and twin spires rising **215 feet**, visible for miles, including from I-70. The 1927 tornado severely damaged the rear of the church, destroying the upper choir and apse, which were later restored. Most Holy Trinity became a City Landmark in 1979 and today is part of **St. Josephine Bakhita Parish**.

Just east of the park, the **Bissell Mansion** at 4426 Randall Place is believed to be the oldest surviving brick house in the City of St. Louis. Built around **1823–1831** by Captain Lewis Bissell on a bluff overlooking a bend in the Mississippi, the Greek Revival home stayed in the Bissell family until 1882, became a rooming house in the 1920s, was nearly demolished for the construction of I-70 in the 1950s — a threat that helped spur the founding of the Landmarks Association of St. Louis — and became a City Landmark in 1971. It operates today as a restaurant and murder-mystery dinner theatre.

Hyde Park’s architecture spans nearly every decade since the 1840s — row houses and mansions, brick two- and four-family flats, and the distinctive St. Louis **“flounder” house** (a narrow, side-entry working-class form found mainly in the city’s oldest neighborhoods). Other landmarks include the **1909 Divoll Branch Library** (one of five surviving Carnegie libraries in St. Louis, named “Best Old Building” by the Riverfront Times in 2019), the **1905 Henry Clay School** (designed by William B. Ittner), the 1895 Bethlehem Evangelical Lutheran Church, and remnants of the historic **Hyde Park Brewery**. The neighborhood lost roughly 30% of its population per decade from 1990 through 2010.

Hyde Park’s modern business activity reflects its deep **industrial and manufacturing heritage** along the Blair Avenue and Salisbury Street corridors, balanced by a strong network of community-development and revitalization organizations. The listings on this page draw from the broader Hyde Park / Bremen / North Florissant professional corridor — with anchor manufacturers like Launer Voss Steel Fabricators on Buchanan and the Essmueller Machine Co on Blair (a St. Louis material-handling-equipment maker founded in 1878), the 80-year-old Piekutowski’s European-style sausage shop on North Florissant, Saint Louis Mat and Linen on Salisbury, plus development groups, a youth-services nonprofit, and chamber and web-services partners serving the broader St. Louis region.

Image: The historic Bissell Street District in Hyde Park — part of the 19th-century German-built brick streetscape that defines this north St. Louis National Register neighborhood — Wikimedia Commons / CC BY-SA 3.0

Hyde Park sits on high ground in north St. Louis overlooking the Mississippi River, where the city follows a classic Midwest four-season pattern. Hot, humid summers with highs in the upper 80s°F bring peak activity to the 14.5-acre Hyde Park and nearby Windsor Park — with their one-acre fishing lake, picnic pavilions, playgrounds, basketball and tennis courts, horseshoe pits, and softball fields — along with LinkSTL’s Summer Youth programs and the neighborhood’s community festivals. Spring and fall fill the Blair Avenue and North Florissant corridors with foot traffic to Piekutowski’s sausage counter and the historic business district around the park.

Cold winters with periodic snow shift commerce indoors to Hyde Park’s steady industrial and service providers — the welding and burning operations at Launer Voss Steel Fabricators on Buchanan, the material-handling manufacturing of the Essmueller Machine Co on Blair, the parts inventory at Motion, and the linen-and-mat rotations of Saint Louis Mat and Linen on Salisbury. The 1898 Most Holy Trinity Church, the circa-1823 Bissell Mansion, the 1909 Divoll Library, and the brick-and-flounder streetscape weather Missouri seasons with the durability of their 19th-century construction. Quick I-70 access keeps the Hyde Park market connected year-round to downtown St. Louis and the riverfront.

## Jeff Vander Lou — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/jeff-vander-lou

Ranked by 324+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  10 of 10 verified Jeff-Vander-Lou / North St. Louis providers ✓  All businesses verified via Google Business ✓  Avg. rating: 4.6/5 across 324 reviews

## Jeff-Vander-Lou Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Jeff-Vander-Lou, St. Louis

Jeff-Vander-Lou — known throughout the city simply as “JVL” — is a north St. Louis neighborhood whose name was coined in 1966 by housing activist Macler C. Shepard, drawn from the three major thoroughfares that frame it: Jefferson, Vandeventer, and St. Louis Avenue. Historically the Yeatman district, it was one of the first areas in St. Louis where African Americans were allowed to own real estate, and it remains anchored today by Grand Avenue, the historic Sportsman’s Park site, and a deep legacy of grassroots community development.

Jeff-Vander-Lou sits in north St. Louis at approximately **38.6504° N, 90.2280° W**, spanning zip codes 63106, 63107, 63108, and 63113. Per the City of St. Louis, the neighborhood is situated between **North Vandeventer Avenue** on the northwest, **Natural Bridge Avenue** on the northeast, **North Jefferson Avenue** on the east, **Delmar Boulevard** on the south, and **North Compton Avenue and Dr. Martin Luther King Drive** on the southwest. **Grand Avenue** serves as the central commercial corridor, giving residents and visitors easy access to Interstates 70 and 64. JVL borders Covenant Blu-Grand Center, the Ville, St. Louis Place, and Old North St. Louis.

Historically, most of JVL was part of the **Yeatman area**, named for **James E. Yeatman**, a prominent Tennessee native who relocated to St. Louis in 1842, co-founded the Mercantile Library Association, served as president of the Merchants Bank, and directed the Western Sanitary Commission during the Civil War. Yeatman’s historic boundaries ran Grand on the west, Delmar on the south, Jefferson on the east, and St. Louis Avenue on the north. The neighborhood’s two national historic districts — **Yeatman Square** and the **Lindell Park Subdivision** — testify to its historic significance, and a sub-neighborhood called Lindell Park (just east of Grand and Dodier) still features beautiful homes.

In a time of racial segregation following the end of slavery, JVL was originally designated as the city’s “Negro District” under a controversial 1915 segregation ordinance — and was one of the only places where African Americans were permitted to own land. It grew into a vibrant African American community whose contributions are still felt across St. Louis. By the second half of the 20th century, however, housing conditions had declined sharply, and federal “urban renewal” programs threatened to demolish large sections of the neighborhood.

The neighborhood’s defining story is the work of **Macler C. Shepard (1917–2005)**, an Arkansas-born upholsterer and housing activist who had twice been displaced by downtown clearance — including the construction of the infamous Pruitt-Igoe complex. When the city proposed a 1966 bond issue to carve a highway through the near-north side, Shepard and his neighbors fought back, and that year he founded **Jeff-Vander-Lou, Inc.** Between 1968 and 1978, the organization acquired and rehabilitated dozens of buildings, building or renovating more than 600 units of housing and attracting a shoe factory and millions in private investment — work that earned Shepard a Rockefeller Public Service Award in 1979.

JVL’s most famous lost landmark is **Sportsman’s Park** at Grand and Dodier — built in 1881 and, for decades, home to both the St. Louis Browns and the St. Louis Cardinals. More Major League games were played there than at any other ballpark, and it hosted ten World Series, three All-Star Games, and hometown greats like Stan Musial, Bob Gibson, and Lou Brock before baseball moved downtown in 1966. The site is now home to the **Herbert Hoover Boys & Girls Club**. Towering over the neighborhood’s western edge is the **St. Alphonsus Liguori “Rock” Church** (1118 N. Grand), a Gothic Revival landmark built of rough-cut stone in 1872 with a 237-foot steeple, and one of the city’s most historic Black Catholic parishes.

Architecturally, JVL preserves some of the largest concentrations of **Second Empire and limestone-faced row housing** in St. Louis outside Lafayette Square, particularly south of Cass Avenue. Anchor institutions today include **St. Louis Community College**, the **St. Louis Housing Authority**, Vashon High School, Columbia Community School, and the only U.S. branch of the worldwide corporation Sensient Colors. The neighborhood is roughly 97% African American and, after decades of population loss, is the focus of intensive place-based redevelopment.

JVL’s modern business activity reflects its position as a hub for **economic-empowerment and professional services** along the Grand Avenue and North Jefferson Avenue corridors, fueled by place-based investment and redevelopment initiatives that aim to transform vacant lots into viable commercial and residential space. The listings on this page draw from the broader Jeff-Vander-Lou / Yeatman / Grand Avenue corridor — with anchor providers including the Northside Economic Empowerment Center on Cass Avenue, the Heartland St. Louis Black Chamber of Commerce, the St. Louis Record Center on Glasgow, Liberty Tax on Page, Doorways’ Jefferson Campus, and the Jeff-Vander-Lou Neighborhood Association, plus chamber and directory partners serving the broader St. Louis region.

Image: The St. Alphonsus Liguori “Rock” Church (1872) on North Grand Boulevard — the Gothic Revival stone landmark and historic Black Catholic parish that anchors Jeff-Vander-Lou’s western edge — Wikimedia Commons / CC BY-SA by Farragutful

Jeff-Vander-Lou sits on the near-north side of St. Louis along the Grand Avenue corridor, where the city follows a classic Midwest four-season pattern. Hot, humid summers with highs in the upper 80s°F bring peak activity to the Herbert Hoover Boys & Girls Club at the historic Sportsman’s Park site, neighborhood association events, and the youth and workforce programming run out of the Northside Economic Empowerment Center. Spring and fall fill the Grand Avenue and North Jefferson commercial corridors with foot traffic to local consultants, chambers, and service providers across the developing district.

Cold winters with periodic snow shift commerce indoors to JVL’s steady professional and community-service providers — Liberty Tax’s extended-hours tax preparation on Page Boulevard, the secure archiving and shredding operations at the St. Louis Record Center on Glasgow, the year-round housing and support services at the Doorways Jefferson Campus, and the in-person training and technical assistance offered by the Northside Economic Empowerment Center. The Gothic stone of the St. Alphonsus “Rock” Church and the Second Empire row houses south of Cass weather Missouri seasons with 19th-century durability. Quick I-70 and I-64 access via Grand keeps the JVL market connected year-round to downtown St. Louis.

## Kings Oak Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Kings Oak, St. Louis

Kings Oak is one of the smallest neighborhoods in St. Louis — a compact, highly visible enclave just south of Forest Park and Interstate 64, named for two of the streets that frame it: Kingshighway Boulevard and Oakland Avenue. With fewer than 80 homes tucked among major institutions like Saint Louis University High School and the Saint Louis Science Center, it’s a quiet, walkable pocket of early-1900s homes anchored by an industrial-and-hospitality corridor that has increasingly turned former garages and warehouses into breweries, coworking spaces, and event venues.

Kings Oak sits in the central-south part of St. Louis at approximately **38.6268° N, 90.2726° W** in zip code 63110. Per the City of St. Louis, the neighborhood is bounded by **Oakland Avenue** on the North, **Manchester Avenue** on the South, **Kingshighway Boulevard** on the East, and **Macklind Avenue** on the West. The neighborhood’s name comes from the first syllables of two of those boundary streets — **“Kings”** from Kingshighway and **“Oak”** from Oakland. It is highly visible from I-64, sitting directly across from Forest Park’s baseball fields and horse stables.

The houses in Kings Oak were nearly all built in the **early 1900s**, around the time of the **1904 St. Louis World’s Fair** held in adjacent Forest Park. With fewer than 80 homes — most clustered along the neighborhood’s eastern edge near Kingshighway, on streets like Lawn Place, Berthold, and Wise — Kings Oak is one of the smallest and least-populous neighborhoods in the city. Rows of densely arranged, well-kept centennial homes line tree-shaded sidewalks, giving the residential core a quiet, peaceful character despite its position between several heavily trafficked corridors.

Several recognizable institutions anchor the small neighborhood and account for much of its land. The **Saint Louis Science Center** at 5050 Oakland — with its James S. McDonnell Planetarium, OMNIMAX theater, and 700-plus interactive exhibits — occupies the former **Falstaff Brewing Corporation** headquarters site (earlier the location of St. Louis University’s Walsh Stadium). **Saint Louis University High School (SLUH)** and the **Compton-Drew Investigative Learning Center** middle school (built where the St. Louis Merchants Exchange once stood) round out the institutional anchors.

Kings Oak’s history traces to the broader Oakland area within the old Gratiot League Square. The Manchester Avenue corridor along the southern edge developed as a light-industrial district, spurred by the arrival of the Pacific Railroad in the 1850s and the excellent clay deposits that brought fire-brick works to the vicinity. A notable surviving landmark is the **Swedish National Society building** (erected 1913) along Kingshighway just north of Manchester — one of several historic structures reflecting the area’s layered industrial and immigrant past.

Today the neighborhood is best known for the **adaptive reuse** of its industrial spaces. Former garages and warehouses along Oakland, Macklind, and Manchester have become modern coworking and co-warehousing facilities, microbreweries, and event venues — with **Modern Brewery** on Oakland a standout, occupying a transformed café space. The neighborhood also hosts the **Carol Gates Throop pet memorial park** along Macklind, across from the Humane Society, and is connected to Forest Park via a pedestrian tunnel under I-64.

Kings Oak’s position is its defining business asset: it sits at the convergence of Forest Park, the Cortex Innovation Community, The Grove entertainment district, and the Central West End, with Barnes-Jewish Hospital just across I-64 and downtown St. Louis a five-minute drive east. That central, high-visibility location draws a steady mix of professionals, students, museum visitors, and diners from across the metro into its small footprint.

Kings Oak’s modern business activity reflects this blend of **light industry, professional services, and destination hospitality** along the South Kingshighway, Oakland, Manchester, and Macklind corridors. The listings on this page draw from the broader Kings Oak / Oakland-Kingshighway corridor and the affiliated St. Louis-region hospitality group based here — with anchor providers including the Gladon Company manufacturer and ISC Contracting on South Kingshighway, Essential Audio Visual, Elevator Co-Warehousing on Macklind, Modern Brewery on Oakland, the Ten by Three nonprofit gift shop, plus chamber and award-winning restaurant partners serving the broader St. Louis region.

Image: A residential streetscape in Kings Oak — one of St. Louis’s smallest neighborhoods, with early-1900s homes near Forest Park and the Saint Louis Science Center — Wikimedia Commons / CC BY-SA 2.0 by Paul Sableman

Kings Oak sits on high, central ground in St. Louis just south of Forest Park, where the city follows a classic Midwest four-season pattern. Hot, humid summers with highs in the upper 80s°F bring peak activity to Modern Brewery’s large patio, the Saint Louis Science Center’s outdoor GROW pavilion, and the Forest Park fields and trails just across the I-64 pedestrian tunnel. Spring and fall draw steady foot traffic to the neighborhood’s breweries, coworking events, and the destination restaurants of the affiliated hospitality group during the height of St. Louis’s festival and Cardinals seasons.

Cold winters with periodic snow shift activity indoors to Kings Oak’s steady professional and industrial providers — the manufacturing operations of the Gladon Company, the year-round commercial build-outs handled by the 42-year-old ISC Contracting, the climate-controlled co-warehousing and event space at Elevator Co-Warehousing on Macklind, and the 24/7 technical support of Essential Audio Visual. The early-1900s brick homes along the eastern edge weather Missouri seasons with the durability of their World’s-Fair-era construction. Quick I-64 access keeps the compact Kings Oak market connected year-round to downtown St. Louis, the Central West End, and the Cortex corridor.

## Kingsway East — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/kingsway-east

Neighborhood

Kingsway East

Coordinates

38.6683° N, 90.2526° W

Ranked by 2,156+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  10 of 10 verified Kingsway East / Natural Bridge corridor providers ✓  All businesses verified via Google Business ✓  Avg. rating: 4.6/5 across 2,156 reviews

## Kingsway East Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Kingsway East, St. Louis

Kingsway East is a compact north St. Louis neighborhood within the historic Arlington section, bounded by the Natural Bridge Avenue commercial corridor and anchored by W.C. Handy Park — named for the “Father of the Blues,” composer of the “St. Louis Blues.” Defined by brick bungalows, frame cottages, and multi-unit flats built largely between 1900 and 1920, and ringed by some of the most beautifully landscaped stretches of North Kingshighway in the city, Kingsway East is a close-knit, predominantly Black community holding tightly to its history.

Kingsway East sits in north St. Louis at approximately **38.6683° N, 90.2526° W**, spanning parts of zip codes 63113 and 63115 across roughly 0.49 square miles. Per the City of St. Louis, the neighborhood is defined by **Natural Bridge Avenue** on the North, **Dr. Martin Luther King Drive** on the South, **Marcus Avenue** on the East, and **Kingshighway Boulevard** on the West. It is bordered by **The Greater Ville, Penrose, Kingsway West, and Fountain Park**, and falls within the city’s 12th Ward. The 2020 census counted 2,502 residents — roughly 93% Black — reflecting a steady population decline over recent decades.

The neighborhood lies within an historic section known as **Arlington**, which takes its name from John W. Burd’s **Arlington Grove subdivision of 1868**. A memorable disaster in the area’s history occurred in **October 1916**, when the Christian Brothers College building was destroyed by fire — one of the worst in the city’s history, taking ten lives. Industrial activity in the area owes its development to the construction of the **Terminal Railroad belt line around 1900**, and the housing stock that followed gave Kingsway East its enduring character of early-20th-century brick residences.

The neighborhood’s signature green space is **W.C. Handy Park**, an 11.96-acre park established in 1941 at Euclid and Ashland. It is named for **William Christopher Handy (1873–1958)**, widely known as the “Father of the Blues,” who spent time in St. Louis around 1893 — an experience that inspired one of his best-known works, the “St. Louis Blues” (published 1914), one of the first blues songs to succeed as a pop standard and the inspiration for the foxtrot dance step. The park offers four baseball fields, a playground, a three-quarter-mile asphalt walking trail, four horseshoe pits, a full basketball court, and multi-purpose athletic fields.

Historically, **Natural Bridge Avenue** was a major St. Louis commercial corridor, lined with notable buildings constructed in the late 1920s and early 1930s — many of which still stand and frame the neighborhood’s northern edge today. The housing stock throughout Kingsway East is varied, blending shotgun houses, four-squares, and bungalows, with several beautifully maintained streets including **Wabada Place, Northland Place, and Maffitt Avenue**, plus a distinctive stretch of mid-century homes unlike any elsewhere in the city.

Community institutions remain central to the neighborhood. **Hickey Elementary School** is the sole school within Kingsway East’s boundaries, with Sumner High School, Ashland Elementary, and others nearby. The **Kingsway Housing Corporation** works alongside residents to preserve and continue the neighborhood’s revitalization, while **ECHO (Emergency Children’s Home)** provides social, emotional, and residential services to area youth. Neighboring churches — especially Immanuel Lutheran and Cote Brilliante Presbyterian — offer a range of activity programs for the community.

The stretch of **North Kingshighway** bordering the neighborhood is among the most thoughtfully designed in the city, with landscaped gardens, mature trees, and narrowed lanes that create an attractive, calming gateway between Kingsway East and the rest of St. Louis. Quick connections to Interstates 70 and 64 via the Kingshighway and Natural Bridge corridors keep the neighborhood linked to downtown, the Cortex Innovation Community, and the broader metro.

Kingsway East’s modern business activity is anchored by the **Natural Bridge Avenue and Kingshighway Boulevard corridors**, with a notable concentration of beauty and personal-care retailers alongside established general contractors who handle the seasonal demands of Missouri weather. The listings on this page draw from the broader Kingsway East / Natural Bridge / Arlington corridor — with anchor providers including the high-volume Hollywood Beauty Supply on Kingshighway, the artisan oils of The Prince Collection on Natural Bridge, Global Bundles on Dr. Martin Luther King Drive, the 25-year, BBB A+ / MBE-certified Tough Enough Construction, World Electronics, plus IT, commercial-real-estate, and directory partners serving the broader St. Louis region.

Image: A residential streetscape along Wabada Avenue in Kingsway East — one of the neighborhood’s well-maintained streets of early-20th-century brick homes — Wikimedia Commons / CC BY 2.0 by Paul Sableman

Kingsway East sits on the near-north side of St. Louis between the Natural Bridge and Kingshighway corridors, where the city follows a classic Midwest four-season pattern. Hot, humid summers with highs in the upper 80s°F bring peak activity to the 11.96-acre W.C. Handy Park — its ballfields, walking trail, basketball court, and playground — along with the high foot traffic at Hollywood Beauty Supply and the personal-care shops along Kingshighway and Natural Bridge. Spring and fall draw shoppers to The Prince Collection’s botanical oils and Global Bundles on Dr. Martin Luther King Drive.

Cold winters with periodic snow and Missouri’s storm seasons drive demand for the neighborhood’s contractors and service providers — Tough Enough Construction’s 24/7 emergency roof-leak and masonry work for the area’s century-old brick homes, the tech support and parts at World Electronics, and the managed-IT and commercial-space partners serving the corridor. The early-20th-century brick bungalows and the late-1920s Natural Bridge storefronts weather Missouri seasons with the durability of their original construction, while the landscaped North Kingshighway gateway keeps Kingsway East connected year-round to downtown St. Louis and the I-70 / I-64 corridors.

## Kingsway West Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Kingsway West, St. Louis

Kingsway West is a north St. Louis neighborhood within the historic Arlington section, framed by the Natural Bridge Avenue commercial corridor and a tight residential grid of early-1900s brick homes. Bordered by Union Boulevard, Dr. Martin Luther King Drive, Natural Bridge Avenue, and North Kingshighway, it sits at a geographically central, well-connected point in the city — close to Interstate 70, anchored by Sherman Park and the Wohl Recreation Center, and home to a mix of long-standing community institutions, logistics and industrial firms, and neighbor-led organizations working on revitalization.

Kingsway West sits in northwest St. Louis at approximately **38.6712° N, 90.2701° W**, spanning parts of zip codes 63113 and 63115. Per the City of St. Louis, the neighborhood is defined by **Union Boulevard** on the Northwest, **Dr. Martin Luther King Drive** on the Southwest, **Natural Bridge Avenue** on the Northeast, and **North Kingshighway** on the Southeast. It is bordered by the Kingsway East, Academy, Wells/Goodfellow, and Mark Twain/I-70 Industrial neighborhoods, and falls within the city’s 12th Ward. The compact, roughly 0.42-square-mile neighborhood is home to about 2,600 residents.

The neighborhood lies within an **historic section known as Arlington**, which takes its name from John W. Burd’s **Arlington Grove subdivision of 1868**. A memorable disaster in the area’s history occurred in **October 1916**, when the Christian Brothers College building was destroyed by a fire — one of the worst in the city’s history, taking ten lives. Like neighboring Kingsway East, the area shares the layered residential-and-industrial story of the broader Arlington district along the Kingshighway corridor.

Industrial activity in Kingsway West owes its development to the construction of the **Terminal Railroad belt line around 1900**. The convenience of rail access, combined with plenty of adjacent land, brought numerous industrial plants and logistics operations to the area — a legacy still visible today in the warehousing, distribution, and rigging firms clustered near the I-70 corridor and along Union Boulevard. A stretch of businesses and commercial uses runs along **Natural Bridge Avenue** through the neighborhood.

The neighborhood’s housing reflects its early-1900s origins, with a tight grid of brick residences alongside newer development. The **Academy**-adjacent edges feature large single-family homes and two- and four-family structures, while **Kings Heights** is a newer development of condominiums and attached townhomes in a private setting, and new residential construction has appeared on **Norwood Square**. Several historic churches anchor the community, including Cote Brilliante Presbyterian, Immanuel Lutheran, Most Blessed Sacrament Catholic, and Memorial Boulevard Christian Church.

Civic life in Kingsway West is anchored by **Sherman Park** and the **Wohl Recreation Center** on Kingshighway, which offers an indoor swimming pool, fitness and dance classes, cooking courses, and homework help for students. **Lexington Elementary School** (built 1995) serves the neighborhood’s youngest students, and the nearby Julia Davis and Cabanne branch libraries provide additional resources. Covenant House Missouri, along Kingshighway, supports homeless, runaway, and at-risk youth in their transition to independence.

Today, neighbor-led organizations like the **Kingsway West Neighborhood Association** — a 501(c)(3) nonprofit formed in 2018 — work on neighborhood improvement, advocacy, and community engagement, reflecting a broader sense of pride and a stable, forward-looking trajectory. Observers describe Kingsway West as “holding on” and steadily stabilizing, with proud long-time residents, well-kept properties, and active churches and community groups invested in its future.

Kingsway West’s modern business activity reflects this blend of **logistics, professional services, and community institutions** along the Natural Bridge, Union Boulevard, and North Kingshighway corridors. The listings on this page draw from the broader Kingsway West / Natural Bridge corridor and the surrounding St. Louis region — with providers spanning the neighbor-led Kingsway West Neighborhood Association, third-party logistics and industrial rigging firms, B2B and answering-service agencies, business brokerages, the Wohl Recreation Center, and the regional directory and chamber partners that serve businesses across the metro.

Image: Buildings along Union Boulevard in Kingsway West — the corridor forming the neighborhood’s northwest boundary — Wikimedia Commons / CC BY 2.0 by Paul Sableman

Kingsway West sits on the high ground of north St. Louis, where the city follows a classic four-season Midwest pattern. Hot, humid summers with highs in the upper 80s°F bring activity to Sherman Park and the Wohl Recreation Center’s outdoor pool, ball fields, and tennis courts, and to the community events organized by the Kingsway West Neighborhood Association. Spring and fall draw steady traffic to the businesses along the Natural Bridge corridor, while local logistics and distribution firms like Clark Logistic Services stay busy year-round moving regional freight through the I-70 gateway.

Cold, snowy St. Louis winters shift activity indoors and keep the neighborhood’s service providers in demand — the 24/7 HIPAA-compliant answering services of Business Centers of Missouri, the secure indoor warehousing of Clark Logistic Services, and the heavy-lift industrial relocation handled by HWP Rigging. The early-1900s brick homes along the residential grid weather Missouri seasons with the durability of their Arlington-era construction. Quick access to Interstate 70 keeps the centrally located Kingsway West market connected year-round to downtown St. Louis, the airport, and distribution routes across the Midwest, while neighbor-led groups like the Kingsway West Neighborhood Association keep residents engaged through every season.

## Kosciusko — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/kosciusko

Coordinates

38.6023° N, 90.2212° W

Ranked by 1,904+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  10 of 10 verified Kosciusko / South Broadway industrial corridor providers ✓  All businesses verified via Google Business ✓  Avg. rating: 4.6/5 across 1,904 reviews

## Kosciusko Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Kosciusko, St. Louis

Kosciusko is a riverfront industrial neighborhood on the near-south side of St. Louis, tucked between downtown, Soulard, and the Mississippi River just south of the Gateway Arch. Once a dense neighborhood of 19th-century rowhouses, it was largely cleared during mid-20th-century urban renewal and rebuilt as an industrial-and-logistics district. Today it’s defined by heavy manufacturing, distribution, and engineering firms — alongside two enduring landmarks: the Anheuser-Busch brewery and the 1.9-mile Mississippi River Floodwall, the “Mural Mile” that hosts the annual Paint Louis event.

Kosciusko sits in the near-south part of St. Louis at approximately **38.6023° N, 90.2212° W**, within zip code 63104 (with the brewery edge in 63118). The neighborhood is named in honor of **Tadeusz Kościuszko**, the Polish-born general of the American Revolutionary War. Locals typically pronounce it *kozz-ee-OSS-co*. Its rough borders are Interstate 55 on the north, the Mississippi River on the east, Seventh Street on the west, and St. George and Dorcas Streets on the south. The northern edge of the neighborhood includes an area historically known as **“Chouteau’s Landing.”**

Industrial activity here dates to the mid-1850s, when the St. Louis and Iron Mountain Railroad (later the Missouri Pacific) was built along the riverfront, bringing plants that produced cotton, matches, tobacco products, and stoneware. The riverfront location offered shipping by steamboat and rail, and the surrounding immigrant population — Eastern European Jews, Croats, Germans, and others — furnished a ready labor source. Before its clearance, Kosciusko was part of the greater Soulard area, with red-brick rowhouses, a lively South Broadway commercial wall, and churches serving its diverse community.

The neighborhood’s modern form was shaped by the **Kosciusko Urban Renewal Area** of the late 1950s and 1960s. Following the city’s 1947 Comprehensive Plan, the Land Clearance for Redevelopment Authority finalized a plan in 1958 to clear 221 acres across roughly 70 blocks for industrial expansion. The 19th-century rowhouses and businesses — including the John J. Roe & Co. pork-packing plant (built 1859) that supplied Union soldiers — were demolished. The result is the heavily industrial district seen today, though the change came at the cost of a once-vibrant residential community.

A handful of significant buildings survived the clearance. Chief among them is the historic **St. Mary of Victories Church** (1843), the second Catholic church built in the city and now a St. Louis Landmark and National Register property, long known as the “Hungarian Church.” The **C. Hager & Sons Hinge Company Building** on Victor Street is also National Register–listed. These landmarks anchor the neighborhood’s connection to its pre-urban-renewal past along the riverfront.

Kosciusko’s best-known modern feature is the **Mississippi River Floodwall — the “Mural Mile,”** the longest continuous graffiti wall, running roughly 1.9 miles between Victor and Chouteau Avenues. Started in 1997 and organized by the nonprofit Paint Louis, the wall draws hundreds of graffiti artists from across the country every Labor Day weekend. A trailhead at the north end connects to the Riverfront Trail, which runs all the way to the Chain of Rocks Bridge at the northern tip of the city.

The neighborhood’s anchor employer is the **Anheuser-Busch brewery**, a cornerstone of the Soulard and Kosciusko area since the 1850s and home to the Budweiser brand and the famous Clydesdales. Heavy industry remains the defining economic activity, led by firms like the long-standing **Nooter** industrial-construction operation. Kosciusko is also home to Lift for Life Academy, the first charter school in St. Louis, housed in a former Mercantile Bank building.

Kosciusko’s modern business activity reflects this blend of **heavy manufacturing, logistics, and specialized professional services** along the South Broadway and riverfront industrial corridor. The listings on this page draw from the broader Kosciusko district and the surrounding St. Louis region — with providers spanning welded-plate manufacturing, FDA-approved 3PL warehousing, LEED-accredited engineering, heavy industrial construction, the historic Anheuser-Busch brewery, plus the regional directory, chamber, and SEO partners that serve businesses across the metro.

Image: St. Mary of Victories Church (1843) in Kosciusko — the second Catholic church built in St. Louis and a surviving landmark of the neighborhood’s pre-urban-renewal past — Wikimedia Commons / CC BY-SA 4.0 by Nheyob

Kosciusko sits low along the Mississippi River on the near-south side of St. Louis, where the city follows a classic four-season Midwest pattern. Hot, humid summers with highs in the upper 80s°F bring peak activity to the riverfront — the Mural Mile floodwall, the Riverfront Trail, and the Anheuser-Busch brewery tours and Biergarten — and the Labor Day weekend Paint Louis event draws artists from across the country. Spring and fall keep steady traffic moving through the brewery campus and the South Broadway corridor that links the district to neighboring Soulard.

Because Kosciusko is overwhelmingly industrial, its core businesses run year-round regardless of weather. Cold, wet St. Louis winters keep the welded-plate fabrication at Tishler Industries, the FDA-approved warehousing and just-in-time delivery at Buske Logistics, the LEED-accredited systems design at Custom Engineering, and the multi-craft industrial construction and storm-restoration crews at Nooter fully active. River-level humidity and seasonal flooding risk are constants the floodwall was built to manage. Convergence of Interstates 55, 44, and 64 at the neighborhood’s edge keeps the district connected year-round to downtown, Soulard, the wider metro, and the Illinois side via the Poplar Street Bridge for freight and commuters alike.

## Lafayette Square — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/lafayette-square

Ranked by 3,984+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  St. Louis’s first historic district (1972) & NRHP-listed Victorian “Painted Ladies” ✓  Anchored by 30-acre Lafayette Park — oldest urban park west of the Mississippi (1851) ✓  Avg. rating: 4.9/5 across 3,984 reviews

## Lafayette Square Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Lafayette Square, St. Louis

Lafayette Square serves as St. Louis’s first historic district and most architecturally celebrated Victorian neighborhood — dedicated November 12, 1851 by City Ordinance 2741, anchored by the 30-acre Lafayette Park (the oldest urban park west of the Mississippi River), Benton Place (Montgomery Blair’s 1866 private street designed by Julius Pitzman), and the Second Empire “Painted Ladies” mansions — offering historic restoration, fine dining, wine bars, wellness, coworking, and artisanal markets across the South Jefferson and Park Avenue corridors.

Lafayette Square is one of St. Louis’s 79 official neighborhoods, located at **38.6173° N, 90.2200° W** approximately one mile south of Downtown St. Louis. The neighborhood is bounded by **Chouteau Avenue** to the north, **Interstate 44** to the south, **Truman Parkway** to the east, and **South Jefferson Avenue** to the west. Surrounding neighborhoods include **Downtown West** to the north, **LaSalle Park** and **Peabody-Darst-Webbe** to the east, **Soulard** to the southeast, and **Gate District** / **Tiffany** to the west. The neighborhood lies within **ZIP code 63104** and surrounds the 30-acre **Lafayette Park**, the oldest public park in the City of St. Louis and the oldest urban park west of the Mississippi River. Today Lafayette Square is one of the most architecturally and historically significant neighborhoods in St. Louis — a meticulously preserved district of Second Empire Victorian town houses, Italianate mansions, and Romanesque Revival homes that earned the title of **St. Louis’s first historic district by City Ordinance in 1972** and was added to the **National Register of Historic Places in 1973** (with additional NRHP listings in 1986).

The story of Lafayette Square begins with the founding of the French village of St. Louis in **1764**. The land that became the neighborhood was originally part of the **St. Louis Commons** — a European-style common field system in which land was held in common by village settlers for agricultural use and pasture for livestock. Lafayette Park, at the center of the neighborhood, has the distinction of being the **only land in the city which has never been privately owned**. In **1835**, under American rule, Mayor John Darby gained permission from the state legislature to begin selling the Commons to drive out the bands of criminals who had been using the unfenced commons as encampments to attack travelers. When the city began to sell the common pasture, the **Board of Aldermen set aside approximately 30 acres for community recreation** — making it the **first such preserve within the area of the Louisiana Purchase**. The site was officially designated a public square in **1836**, and the surrounding streets were laid out with the southern street named **Lafayette Avenue** in honor of **Marquis de Lafayette** (Marie-Joseph-Paul-Roch-Yves-Gilbert du Motier), the French statesman and Revolutionary War general who had volunteered under George Washington and who had visited St. Louis during his famous **1824–1825 tour of the United States**.

In **1837**, a real estate panic forced many who had bought land surrounding the Square to cease their payments, causing the land to revert to the City. In the early 1850s, after courts had adjudicated the ownership of these properties, several prominent St. Louisans bought most of the land bordering the southern end of the park, built expensive homes along **Lafayette Avenue**, and secured state legislation preventing “any nuisance within a distance of 600 feet from the Park.” The 30-acre tract was separated from the Commons in **1844**. On **November 12, 1851**, the park was formally dedicated as **“Lafayette Square”** by **City Ordinance 2741** — the name that became associated with the neighborhood that grew up around the park. With the dedication ordinance, funds were raised to build a wood fence around the park and to plant trees and shrubs. The park was renamed **Lafayette Park** in **1854**. By **1856**, real estate developers had begun to sell lots on the western edge of the park along **Missouri Avenue**, and by **1858** lots on the east side along **Mississippi Avenue** were being sold. The athletic field at the park’s northwest corner was used in **1858** as a training site for local militias who referred to it as the “parade grounds.” On the north side of the park, **Benton Place** was platted by **Montgomery Blair** in **1866** and designed by **Julius Pitzman** — one of the earliest **private streets** in the nation. The lots on Park Avenue along the north edge of the Square were developed by the 1870s.

The **1870s through early 1890s** were Lafayette Square’s heyday. Lafayette Park became one of the most popular gathering places in St. Louis — the park was tended by thirteen gardeners, and free concerts at the music stand on Thursdays and Sundays routinely drew crowds in the **thousands** (sometimes more than 10,000). Victorian visitors strolled the curvilinear paths designed by superintendent **Maximilian Kern**, picnicked under canopy trees, cruised the main lake in **swan boats**, admired the **decorative stone grotto**, and enjoyed the **flower gardens**. The original **iron and stone fence** was erected in **1869** and still encircles the park today. A bronze statue of **Senator Thomas Hart Benton** (1868) and a bronze statue of **George Washington** (1869) — cast from the Houdon original by French sculptor **Jean-Antoine Houdon** — were erected within the park. The Benton statue is inscribed with his famous phrase “*There is the East. There is India*,” reflecting his belief that the Western United States contained all the fabled riches of India and more. A **police substation (Park House)** was constructed in **1867** to maintain order during the park’s busiest gatherings. By **1890**, Lafayette Square had reached the zenith of its development as the most exclusive residential district in St. Louis — home to several **mayors**, **congressmen**, **cabinet members** (including **Montgomery Blair**, U.S. Postmaster General under President Abraham Lincoln, and co-counsel for Dred Scott in *Dred Scott v. Sandford*), a **Supreme Court Justice** (**Louis Brandeis**), the **president of the American Bar Association**, and famous steamboat pilots **Horace Bixby** and **Joseph LaBarge**.

Everything changed on **May 27, 1896**. The **Great St. Louis Tornado** tore through the near south side of the city, devastating Lafayette Square in the worst urban tornado disaster in American history. The tornado **uprooted nearly all of the trees in the Park** as well as the trees on Benton Place, damaged the iron fence, destroyed the **bandstand**, destroyed the **Union Club** and the Methodist church at Jefferson and Lafayette Avenues, crippled the **Presbyterian** and **Methodist churches**, tore the roof off the **Unitarian church**, and crippled or destroyed many homes on the Square. The tornado did millions of dollars of damage and killed many. Rebuilding began immediately and the area improved enough by **1904** to earn special commendation from foreign landscape architects visiting the World’s Fair in Forest Park. But Lafayette Square never recovered its former grandeur. Improved transportation and the rise of the much larger Forest Park caused many wealthy residents to move west rather than rebuild. The original **1876 Music Stand** was demolished in **1951**. The cannons in the park today are from the **British warship HMS Acteon**, which sank attacking Fort Moultrie at the **Battle of Sullivan’s Island in June 1776**; they were raised from Charleston Harbor in the late 19th century and donated to Lafayette Park by St. Louis veterans in **1897**.

The City’s **first zoning ordinance in 1918** classified the park’s perimeter as residential, briefly protecting the neighborhood from commercial encroachment. But when the **Missouri Supreme Court declared the zoning law unconstitutional in 1923** (in *City of St. Louis v. Evraiff*), commercial interests began to impinge on the park’s surroundings, speeding the exodus of the remaining Victorian families. Beginning in the Depression, many of the large old homes were converted into **rooming houses**. The post-war decades brought further decline: the **west pond** (today’s Rock Garden) was filled in during the 1950s; **Interstate 44** was cut through the southern edge of the neighborhood, destroying many homes; and by the late 1960s the Park House had fallen into disrepair and was boarded up. By 1970 the neighborhood was nearly leveled for urban renewal, with crime, dilapidated homes, squatters, and abandoned mansions throughout. The turning point came that year when **Ruth Kamphoefner** visited Lafayette Square on a house tour, purchased one of the old Victorian homes, and began renovating it herself. She bought several more homes and renovated those as well — sparking a renaissance.

A group of like-minded preservationists formed the **Lafayette Square Restoration Committee**, and in **1972** their efforts resulted in Lafayette Square becoming **St. Louis’s first historic district by City Ordinance**. In **1973**, the U.S. Congress placed Lafayette Square on the **National Register of Historic Places** (with additional NRHP listings in **1986**). In **1976**, as part of the U.S. Bicentennial, the park received a gift from the people of France: a Jean-Antoine Houdon bust of the Marquis de Lafayette, now displayed in the restored Park House. The majority of the houses in the district were built before 1900 — some dating before the Civil War — with the period of greatest construction activity (the two decades after 1865) producing the Square’s signature **Second Empire Victorian town houses**: two full stories topped by a third-floor mansard roof with dormer windows, arched doorways and windows, bay windows, and ornate cornices, with limestone or sandstone facades and brick side and rear walls. The largest examples are on **Benton Place** and **Park Avenue** north of the park. In **2001**, the **Lafayette Park Conservancy (LPC)** was formed on the 150th anniversary of the park’s dedication. A **2004 Master Plan** developed by H3 Studio with the LPC and the City of St. Louis guided park restoration; the **grotto and bridge** were rehabilitated in **2012**, and the **Mediterranean boathouse** (built 1908) was restored in **2014** as the **Kern Lakeside Pavilion**. A **Tree Master Plan** for Lafayette Park was prepared in 2016 by SWT Design.

Today, Lafayette Square is one of St. Louis’s most desirable and architecturally celebrated neighborhoods — a walkable, vibrant Victorian district of restored **“Painted Ladies”** mansions doused in vibrant hues of yellow, salmon, green, blue, and purple. The neighborhood’s key anchor businesses today include the **Lafayette Square Restoration Committee** on Lafayette Avenue (the non-profit that started the 1970s renaissance); **Lafayette Construction** on South Jefferson (the 24-year licensed-and-bonded general contractor specializing in Victorian historic restorations under Ree Winston); **The Dreamer’s Loft** at 2710 Lafayette (St. Louis’s first creative coworking space); **Lafayette Square Chiropractic Centre** at 1013 South 18th Street (Dr. Crystal B. Peairs’s 15-year family-oriented practice); **Vicini Pastaria Cafe & Market** at 1916 Park Avenue (artisanal handmade pasta); **Winnie’s Wine Bar** at 2101 Chouteau (opened 2022 by Paul and Wendy Hamilton); **CBD Kratom Lafayette Square** at 1917 Park Avenue; **HITS** on Chouteau Avenue (the 25-year document scanning and digital record transformation firm with 2+ billion documents scanned); the **Lafayette Park Conservancy** housed in the 1867 Park House; **Sqwires Restaurant** in the former Western Wire Factory; **Square One Brewery** at Park and 18th; and the annual **Lafayette Square Holiday Parlour Tour & Winter Fair**, the **summer free concert series** (June–September), the **summer movie screenings**, the **St. Louis Perfectos vintage baseball club**, and the **Lafayette Park Conservancy events** at the Park House, Betsy Cook Pavilion, and Kern Lakeside Pavilion.

Image: Lafayette Park — the 30-acre urban green space at the center of the Lafayette Square neighborhood, set aside from the St. Louis Commons in 1836 and dedicated by City Ordinance 2741 on November 12, 1851. The oldest public park in St. Louis and the oldest urban park west of the Mississippi River — Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 3.0 by Onegentlemanofverona

Lafayette Square sits one mile south of Downtown St. Louis at 38.6173° N, with seamless access to Interstate 44 along its southern boundary, Truman Parkway to the east, South Jefferson Avenue to the west, and Chouteau Avenue to the north. The neighborhood experiences St. Louis’s full humid continental climate: hot, humid summers, cold winters, and an active spring severe weather season — punctuated historically by the **Great St. Louis Tornado of May 27, 1896**, which destroyed virtually every tree and structure in the park. 

Peak outdoor activity falls during spring, summer, and fall, when **Lafayette Park’s** 30 acres draw heaviest neighborhood and citywide use at the **summer free concert series** on select Saturdays from **June through September**, the **summer movie screenings**, the **St. Louis Perfectos vintage baseball club** on the parade-grounds athletic field, the wedding-photo sessions at the **Betsy Cook Pavilion** and the historic **Rockery** (1866), the **Kern Lakeside Pavilion** (the restored 1908 Mediterranean boathouse), the kids climbing the **three Revolutionary War cannons** from HMS Acteon, and the dawn-to-dusk strolling within the original **1869 iron-and-stone fence**. 

The annual **Holiday Parlour Tour & Winter Fair** draws weekend foot traffic during the holiday season. Cool seasons drive intense indoor activity at **Winnie’s Wine Bar** at 2101 Chouteau, **Vicini Pastaria Cafe & Market** at 1916 Park Avenue, **Sqwires Restaurant** in the former Western Wire Factory, **Square One Brewery** at Park and 18th, **The Dreamer’s Loft** at 2710 Lafayette, **Lafayette Square Chiropractic Centre** at 1013 South 18th Street, **Lafayette Construction** at 3002 South Jefferson, **CBD Kratom Lafayette Square** at 1917 Park Avenue, and the Park House (the 1867 former police substation, now home to the Lafayette Park Conservancy). The neighborhood’s 1850s–1890s Second Empire Victorian town houses — with their mansard roofs, dormer windows, arched doorways, bay windows, ornate cornices, and limestone / sandstone facades — have weathered more than 130 years of Missouri climate (and the 1896 tornado) with the durability of the European-trained craftsmen who built them.

## LaSalle Park — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/lasalle-park

Coordinates

38.6142° N, 90.2015° W

Ranked by 2,987+ Google reviews. Updated June 2026.

✓  A restored red-brick historic district just south of downtown ✓  Home to Nestlé Purina’s world headquarters & Old Frenchtown heritage ✓  Avg. rating: 4.4/5 across 2,987 reviews

## LaSalle Park Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 LaSalle Park, St. Louis

Tucked just south of downtown St. Louis, LaSalle Park is one of the city’s smallest and most quietly remarkable neighborhoods — a tight grid of restored red-brick rowhouses that survived because a hometown company refused to let it disappear. Today it pairs Civil War–era architecture with the world headquarters of a Fortune 500 pet-food giant, all within walking distance of the Arch.

LaSalle Park sits along the southern edge of downtown St. Louis, in ZIP code **63104**. Its boundaries run roughly from Chouteau Avenue on the north to Interstate 55 on the southeast and Tucker (12th Street) on the west, packing a remarkable amount of history into about **0.21 square mile**. The neighborhood is part of the older “Old Frenchtown” area that also includes Lafayette Square and Soulard, and it shares Ward 8 representation in city government.

The land was first settled by French families — the Soulards and Chouteaus among them — whose names still mark the surrounding streets and districts. After the Civil War and the California gold rush, waves of **German, Lebanese, Czech, and African-American** immigrants moved in, and within a few decades the French presence had largely faded; by the early 20th century, the City’s own history notes, only four residents in a hundred were French. That immigrant story is written into the neighborhood’s historic churches, including **St. John Nepomuk**, noted as the first Czech Catholic church in America, and the Romanesque **St. Vincent de Paul**, completed in 1843.

Like much of the near-south side, LaSalle Park declined badly after 1950s highway construction sliced through the old street grid. What saved it was an unusual partnership. **Ralston Purina** — in the neighborhood since 1894 and today known as the **Nestlé Purina PetCare Company** — chose to invest in the streets around its own factory rather than flee them. In March 1969, 137 acres were declared blighted, and the St. Louis Land Clearance for Redevelopment Authority sought a federal planning grant.

Ralston Purina formed a subsidiary, the **LaSalle Park Redevelopment Corporation**, to carry out the work. Federal redevelopment funds were approved in **1971**, and in early **1976** property across the neighborhood was sold to individuals and developers willing to restore the historic homes and businesses or build sympathetic in-fill houses on the gaps. Through that effort LaSalle Park was, in effect, re-founded as a “new” neighborhood, legally distinct from the larger Soulard district from which it was carved.

The payoff is visible on nearly every block. Historic-district plans filed in 1977 and revised through 1982 placed the area on the **National Register of Historic Places**, and a mixture of Victorian and Federalist rowhouses — many dating to the 1860s — was carefully rehabbed. The standout is the **10th Street pedestrian way**, a restored stretch of red-brick rowhouses that has become the neighborhood’s signature image. Anchoring it all, Nestlé Purina still keeps its world headquarters here at **Checkerboard Square**, a reminder that this restoration was as much a corporate-civic experiment as a preservation project.

LaSalle Park remains intimate — the 2020 census counted just **1,088** residents — but its central position is hard to beat. Soulard and the historic Soulard Farmers Market sit immediately to the south and east, downtown and Busch Stadium are minutes north, and the Gateway Arch, the Mississippi riverfront, and the Anheuser-Busch Brewery are all close by. The businesses below — distilleries, breweries, a celebrated live-music venue, trade schools, and more — make the most of that near-downtown setting.

The 10th Street pedestrian streetscape of restored rowhouses in LaSalle Park. Photo: Dan7a7davis, licensed under CC BY-SA 3.0 via Wikimedia Commons.

LaSalle Park shares the humid-subtropical climate of St. Louis, with four distinct seasons. Summers are warm and muggy — July highs average around **89.6°F** — while spring and fall bring milder, pleasant stretches ideal for walking the neighborhood’s brick-paved streets. The region averages roughly **41.7 inches** of rain a year, and because St. Louis sits within Tornado Alley, spring and summer can deliver severe thunderstorms and occasional tornado watches. 

Winters are cold but rarely extreme, with January highs near **40.4°F** and about **16.6 inches** of snow in a typical year. The solid masonry construction of LaSalle Park’s 19th-century rowhouses handles the temperature swings well, which is part of why so many of these Civil War–era buildings are still standing and lived in more than 150 years later.
