## Lewis Place — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/lewis-place

Coordinates

38.6551° N, 90.2564° W

Ranked by 225+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  10 of 10 verified Lewis Place / Central West End corridor providers ✓  All businesses verified via Google Business ✓  Avg. rating: 4.7/5 across 225 reviews

## Lewis Place Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Lewis Place, St. Louis

Lewis Place is a historic north-central St. Louis neighborhood named for the oldest African-American private street in the city — a parkway-lined boulevard whose residents played a pivotal role in the national fight against racially restrictive housing covenants. Laid out in 1890 and listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1980, the street and its surrounding neighborhood sit just north of the Central West End and the Cortex Innovation Community, blending deep civil-rights heritage with an active, resident-led revitalization effort.

Lewis Place sits in north-central St. Louis at approximately **38.6551° N, 90.2564° W**, spanning parts of zip codes 63108 and 63113. Per the City of St. Louis, the neighborhood is defined by **Dr. Martin Luther King Drive** on the North, **Delmar Boulevard** on the South, **Newstead Avenue** on the East, and **Walton Avenue** on the West. It is bordered by Fountain Park, the Central West End, The Greater Ville, The Ville, Kingsway East, and Vandeventer, falling within the city’s 10th and 12th Wards. The roughly 0.33-square-mile neighborhood is home to about 1,360 residents.

The neighborhood takes its name from **Lewis Place**, the oldest African-American private street in St. Louis. It was laid out beginning in **1890** by the family of real-estate developer **William J. Lewis** — a three-block street with a landscaped center parkway, lined on both sides with homes built between **1890 and 1928**. A monumental ornamental gate, designed in 1895 by the celebrated architectural firm **Barnett, Haynes & Barnett**, marks the Taylor Avenue entrance and remains the street’s signature landmark.

Lewis Place holds a singular place in American civil-rights history. In the 1940s, its residents fought back against the racially restrictive covenants used to bar Black families from buying homes — a struggle led locally by figures such as Robert Witherspoon. That fight helped propel the landmark **Shelley v. Kraemer** case, which originated in St. Louis and in **1948** reached the U.S. Supreme Court, where restrictive covenants were struck down across the entire nation, opening the door to modern fair-housing law.

The **Lewis Place Historic District** — 78 homes along the private street — was placed on the National Register of Historic Places on **September 15, 1980**, and is also a designated City Historic District. The housing stock reflects the turn-of-the-century elegance of the nearby Euclid–Central West End area, with refined Victorian and revival-style residences. Several were designed by prominent St. Louis architects, including work tied to the Barnett architectural dynasty, whose name is among the most illustrious in the city’s building history.

By the 1950s, Lewis Place had become a desirable neighborhood for Black professionals, and it remained strong even as surrounding areas declined — residents modernized street lighting, repaved streets through a special levy, and kept the neighborhood association active. After decades of disinvestment in the late 20th century, residents led by Pamela Talley formed **Lewis Place Historic Preservation, Inc.** in 2000, a nonprofit dedicated to preserving the street’s historic homes and improving the surrounding community.

Today Lewis Place sits at the heart of a revitalizing community, within a three-mile radius of Saint Louis University, Washington University, and Ranken Technical College, and immediately adjacent to the booming Central West End and Cortex Innovation Community. Anchors like the **Lewis Place Historical Preservation Garden** — a community garden launched in 2000 that also serves as an educational space — reflect the neighborhood’s ongoing, resident-driven beautification and food-access efforts.

Lewis Place’s business landscape reflects this blend of **heritage preservation, professional services, and proximity to the Central West End innovation corridor**. The listings on this page draw from the broader Lewis Place / Central West End area and the surrounding St. Louis region — with providers spanning business publishing, smart-home installation, community gardening, executive consulting, city business assistance, direct-mail and coworking services, corporate gifting, and the regional directory and commercial partners that serve businesses across the metro.

Image: The Lewis Place Historic District — the oldest African-American private street in St. Louis, listed on the National Register of Historic Places — Wikimedia Commons / CC BY-SA 4.0 by LittleT889

Lewis Place sits on high, central ground in St. Louis, just north of the Central West End, where the city follows a classic four-season Midwest pattern. Hot, humid summers with highs in the upper 80s°F bring activity to the Lewis Place Historical Preservation Garden and the tree-lined center parkway, while nearby coworking hubs like TechArtista CWE stay busy with remote workers and startups year-round. Spring and fall are prime seasons for the neighborhood’s ongoing preservation and beautification work along the historic private street and its 1890–1928 homes.

As the Midwest transitions into winter, demand rises for the home-automation and security installation handled by St. Louis Smart Home and the property-maintenance services that keep the district’s century-old homes in good repair through the cold months. Professional, publishing, consulting, and direct-mail services — from St. Louis Small Business Monthly to Levy Consulting and Specialty Mailing — remain steady through every season. The neighborhood’s central location and quick access to Delmar Boulevard, Dr. Martin Luther King Drive, and the Central West End keep Lewis Place connected year-round to downtown, the Cortex corridor, the region’s major universities, and the wider metro through every season.

## Lindenwood Park — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/lindenwood-park

Lindenwood Park

Coordinates

38.598° N, 90.306° W

Ranked by 3,423+ Google reviews. Updated June 2026.

✓  A brick-bungalow neighborhood in far-southwest St. Louis ✓  Along the Hampton Avenue & Watson Road (Historic Route 66) corridors ✓  Avg. rating: 4.6/5 across 3,423 reviews

## Lindenwood Park Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Lindenwood Park, St. Louis

Lindenwood Park is one of far-southwest St. Louis’s quietest, most settled residential neighborhoods — a tidy grid of brick bungalows and single-family homes wrapped around its namesake city park, bounded by the busy Hampton Avenue and Watson Road (Historic Route 66) corridors.

Tucked into the far southwest corner of the City of St. Louis, Lindenwood Park is a roughly 1.5-square-mile residential neighborhood defined by **Arsenal Street and Interstate 44 to the north, Watson and Chippewa Street to the south, Hampton Avenue on the east, and the city limits to the west**. That western edge runs close to the River Des Peres, and just across Chippewa to the south sits the St. Louis Hills neighborhood. With around 9,400 residents counted in the 2020 census, it ranks among the larger neighborhoods in south city.

The land here has a long history. It was part of a vast Spanish colonial land grant in 1798, then carved into large tracts by about 1850. For decades the area stayed mostly agricultural and undeveloped. Real building only began pushing in along and north of Fyler Avenue, east of the River Des Peres, in the 1880s, as the growing city slowly reached this far southwest corner.

Lindenwood Park took the residential shape you see today during a rapid building boom in the **1920s and 1930s**, with most of its housing finished by 1950. The defining look is brick — rows of sturdy **brick bungalows and single-family homes** from the 1920s through the 1950s, mixed in with a handful of older 19th-century farmhouses, some 1950s “prestige” houses, and a few art-deco apartment buildings. Because so little open land was left once the boom ended, newer construction has been limited, and the neighborhood keeps its early-20th-century feel. As of the early 2000s, about 83% of its homes were single-family detached and largely brick.

The neighborhood is named for its namesake city park, **Lindenwood Park**, which actually came along after most of the surrounding houses were already built. The roughly 12-acre park tract was secured through a settlement with Darst Realty — a firm owned by then-Mayor Joseph Darst — that would otherwise have placed multi-family housing on the land. The park did not take its real shape until after a 1955 bond issue; before that it was a four-block-by-one-block stretch of woods dotted with sinkholes. Today the city park offers walking trails, a soccer field, two softball fields, tennis courts, a picnic pavilion, a playground, and a skating rink.

The neighborhood’s edges do most of its commercial work. **Hampton Avenue**, the eastern boundary, is a major south-city artery lined with shops and services, while the southern boundary along **Chippewa Street and Watson Road carries Historic Route 66** (Missouri Route 366). That Route 66 frontage gives the area a real slice of Americana — the beloved **Donut Drive In** has served classic donuts on Chippewa for generations, and just across Chippewa in neighboring St. Louis Hills you’ll find the famous **Ted Drewes Frozen Custard**, a Route 66 landmark that sits adjacent to (not within) Lindenwood Park.

Historically a white, middle-class neighborhood — more than 99% white as recently as 1990 — Lindenwood Park has grown somewhat more mixed, with the 2020 census recording about 83% White, 6% Black, and smaller Asian and multiracial shares. What hasn’t changed is the appeal: solid brick homes, a central park, easy I-44 access, and a wall of small businesses along Hampton, Watson, and the interior streets of Ivanhoe and Lansdowne. The local shops, bakeries, auto shops, and restaurants profiled below are the everyday backbone of the neighborhood.

Brick homes in Lindenwood Park. Photo: “Lindenwood Park Homes” by Paul Sableman, licensed under CC BY 2.0 via Wikimedia Commons.

Like the rest of St. Louis, Lindenwood Park has a humid subtropical climate (Koppen Cfa) with four full seasons. Summers are warm and humid, with July high temperatures averaging around 89.6°F, while spring and fall bring milder, pleasant stretches that are ideal for walking the park trails or strolling the Hampton and Watson corridors. The area sees about 41.7 inches of rain a year, and it falls within the broader severe-weather region sometimes called Tornado Alley, so spring and summer can bring occasional thunderstorms. 

Winters are cold but rarely extreme, with January high temperatures averaging around 40.4°F and roughly 16.6 inches of snowfall in a typical year. The neighborhood’s sturdy brick housing stock holds up well against the seasonal swings, which is one reason these 1920s-through-1950s homes have aged so gracefully and remain in steady demand among buyers looking for a durable, low-maintenance south-city address.

## Marine Villa — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/marine-villa

Coordinates

38.5866° N, 90.2293° W

Ranked by 703+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  10 of 10 verified Marine Villa / Cherokee Street corridor providers ✓  All businesses verified via Google Business ✓  Avg. rating: 4.7/5 across 703 reviews

## Marine Villa Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Marine Villa, St. Louis

Marine Villa is a historic working-class neighborhood on the south St. Louis riverfront, set along the bluffs of the Mississippi River just south of the Anheuser-Busch Brewery. Named in 1968 for the rivermen who once made their community here, it pairs a German streetcar-suburb heritage of brick flounder houses and rowhomes with the vibrant retail energy of Cherokee Street’s Antique Row along its northern edge — a blend of riverfront industry, neighborhood revitalization, and an eclectic arts-and-vintage commercial scene.

Marine Villa sits in south St. Louis at approximately **38.5866° N, 90.2293° W**, within zip code 63118. Per the City of St. Louis, the neighborhood lies just south of the Anheuser-Busch Brewery along the Mississippi River bluffs, and is defined by **Cherokee Street’s Antique Row** on the North, **Gasconade Street** on the South, **South Broadway and Jefferson Avenue** on the West, and the **Mississippi River** on the East. The roughly 0.65-square-mile neighborhood is home to about 2,530 residents and falls within the city’s 8th Ward.

The neighborhood’s name and boundaries came into existence in **1968**, chosen as a tribute to the large number of **rivermen** who once had their own community in the area. The old **Marine Hospital** — one of several hospitals authorized by an act of Congress in 1837 for the treatment of sick and disabled rivermen — was located at Marine and Winnebago Streets. The riverfront location, just a few miles south of downtown, made the area a natural home for those who lived and worked on the Mississippi.

Originally part of the **St. Louis Commons**, the area was subdivided and gridded in **1855**, with early development consisting of small farms and brickyards. Some of the oldest surviving buildings are small frame and brick farmhouses from the 1860s, many in the form of the distinctive **flounder house** — a simple one-and-a-half-story vernacular home with a slanted roof unique to St. Louis. The **Lemp Brewery** relocated from downtown to the northern edge of Marine Villa in the 1860s, drawing an influx of German immigrants to the area.

When a **streetcar line opened on South Broadway in 1890**, the district developed rapidly into one of St. Louis’s many working- and middle-class **streetcar suburbs**, populated largely by German immigrant families. That heritage is preserved in the **Marine Villa Neighborhood Historic District**, listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 2009, which encompasses ten blocks and 356 buildings — 187 of them contributing to the district’s historic character. The German Cultural Society building on South Jefferson reflects this lasting immigrant legacy.

Today Marine Villa is a neighborhood transforming its industrial bones. The northern half has benefited from the reinvestment along **Cherokee Street**, whose Antique Row and arts district anchor an eclectic mix of vintage shops, galleries, and source-direct retail. Adaptive reuse of historic buildings into coworking spaces, art studios, and professional offices has accelerated, supported by neighborhood developers and an active neighborhood association. Brick townhouses, Second Empire designs, and flounder homes draw buyers and renovators to one of the city’s more affordable historic markets.

Marine Villa’s business landscape reflects this blend of **riverfront industry, specialty retail, and creative revitalization** along the Cherokee Street and South Broadway corridors. The listings on this page draw from the broader Marine Villa / Cherokee Antique Row area and the surrounding St. Louis region — with providers spanning riverfront industrial terminals, fine-art and vintage retail, crystal and jewelry shops, historic-building redevelopment, business chambers and districts, B2B marketing, and the regional directory partner that serves businesses across the metro.

Image: Looking south on Indiana Avenue at Potomac Street in Marine Villa — a historic brick streetscape in this south St. Louis riverfront neighborhood — Wikimedia Commons / CC BY 2.0 by Paul Sableman

Marine Villa sits low along the Mississippi River bluffs on the south side of St. Louis, where the city follows a classic four-season Midwest pattern. Hot, humid summers with highs in the upper 80s°F bring peak foot traffic to Cherokee Street’s Antique Row, drawing vintage hunters and crystal collectors to shops like Retro 101/Cherry Bomb Vintage, STL Rocks, and the LA Marler gallery. Spring and fall are prime seasons for the arts walks, gallery openings, and source-direct retail that define the neighborhood’s creative northern edge along Cherokee Street.

As the Midwest transitions into colder months, activity shifts toward the neighborhood’s steady operators — the riverfront industrial work at the ADM North Terminal, the year-round historic-building redevelopment by South Side Spaces, and the professional, chamber, and marketing services that keep working through every season. The district’s century-old brick flounder houses and rowhomes weather Missouri winters with the durability of their German streetcar-suburb construction, while indoor retail and gallery foot traffic stays steady. Quick access to Interstate 55 and South Broadway keeps Marine Villa connected year-round to downtown St. Louis, the riverfront, the Cherokee Street commercial corridor, and the wider metro just four miles north through every season.

## Mark Twain Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Mark Twain, St. Louis

Mark Twain is a north St. Louis industrial-and-logistics neighborhood named in honor of the iconic American author Samuel Clemens — better known by his pen name Mark Twain — whose Hannibal, Missouri boyhood and Mississippi-River sensibility shaped American literature. The neighborhood is defined by its position along the Interstate 70 expressway and the Natural Bridge / San Francisco / Penrose / Semple / Geraldine Avenue commercial-and-industrial spine, anchored by chemical manufacturers, food-grade and hazmat warehouses, and screen-printing and industrial-coatings firms operating for 29 to 100 years.

Mark Twain sits in north St. Louis at approximately **38.6899° N, 90.2519° W**, within zip code 63115 (with adjacent industrial addresses in 63120 and 63103). The neighborhood is anchored by the **Interstate 70 expressway** along its southern edge, providing rapid transit access to downtown St. Louis, Lambert Airport, and the wider Midwest distribution network. Its industrial-and-logistics spine runs along **Natural Bridge Avenue**, **San Francisco Avenue**, **Penrose Street**, **Semple Avenue**, and **Geraldine Avenue**, with rail-and-truck access drawing a high concentration of specialized service providers to the corridor.

The neighborhoodu2019s name honors **Samuel Langhorne Clemens (1835–1910)**, the iconic American author known worldwide by his pen name **Mark Twain**. Although Clemens spent his boyhood in Hannibal, Missouri — about 110 miles north along the Mississippi River, where his preserved boyhood home stands today as **The Mark Twain Boyhood Home & Museum** — the St. Louis neighborhood adopted his name as part of the city’s civic recognition of Missouri’s most internationally celebrated literary figure. The name is shared with the **Mark Twain National Forest**, the massive 1.5-million-acre managed federal land spanning southern Missouri.

Industrial activity here is defined by the **I-70 freight corridor**, which drives a high concentration of warehousing, chemical manufacturing, and nationwide logistics operations. The geographic advantage of rapid transit to the broader St. Louis metro — combined with expansive industrial square footage and 19th-and-20th-century rail access — supports food-grade and hazmat warehousing, transloading, polyurethane and polyurea coatings manufacturing, screen-printing and digital-imaging supply, and 501(c)(3) inventory-donation operations. The neighborhood is a primary hub for businesses that require rapid transit access to the broader St. Louis market.

Mark Twain’s anchor employers reflect the longevity of the corridor — many operating for three decades or more, with a few approaching their centennial. **Harcros Chemicals Inc.** has spent **100 years** on Geraldine Avenue managing nationwide chemical distribution and transloading. **Lawson Screen & Digital Products** has been **family-owned since 1949** on Penrose Street, supplying the printing industry. **WIN Warehouse** has run a 501(c)(3) inventory-donation center on San Francisco Avenue for **33 years**. **FW Logistics** has spent **32 years** on Semple Avenue building 7 million square feet of warehouse space across nearly 30 nationwide locations. And **Chemline Inc.** has spent **29 years** on Natural Bridge Avenue manufacturing high-performance polyurea coatings.

Local demand is bolstered by long-standing family-owned enterprises and non-profit organizations that use the district’s robust infrastructure to manage regional distribution and technical support services. While the area is heavily focused on business-to-business services, it also supports niche retail and community-focused organizations that draw visitors from across the metropolitan region. Market dynamics are currently influenced by regional infrastructure investments and a steady demand for specialized trades, including screen printing and industrial coatings. Most businesses in this part of St. Louis maintain traditional Monday-through-Friday hours, reflecting the professional and manufacturing nature of the neighborhood.

Mark Twain’s modern business activity reflects this blend of **heavy chemical manufacturing, nationwide warehousing, industrial coatings, and specialized professional services** along the I-70 corridor and the Natural Bridge / San Francisco / Penrose / Semple / Geraldine industrial spine. The listings on this page draw from the broader Mark Twain / I-70 Industrial area and the surrounding St. Louis region — with providers spanning commercial-PACE finance, food-grade and hazmat 3PL warehousing, polyurea coatings manufacturing, 501(c)(3) donation logistics, business-district associations, family-owned screen-printing supply, nationwide chemical distribution, and the regional retail and tourism partners that share the Mark Twain literary name.

Image: New and old buildings standing side-by-side in the Mark Twain neighborhood — a streetscape of the layered industrial and residential heritage along the I-70 corridor in north St. Louis — Wikimedia Commons / CC BY 2.0 by Paul Sableman

Mark Twain sits on high ground along the I-70 corridor in north St. Louis, where the city follows a classic four-season Midwest pattern. Hot, humid summers with highs in the upper 80s°F bring peak activity to the outbound shipping, transloading, and industrial-coatings application at the warehouses and chemical plants along Natural Bridge Avenue, San Francisco Avenue, and Semple Avenue. Spring and fall keep steady traffic moving through the Mark Twain / I-70 Industrial spine and the family-owned screen-printing and digital-imaging supply operations on Penrose Street.

Because Mark Twain is overwhelmingly industrial, its core businesses run year-round regardless of weather. Cold, snowy St. Louis winters keep the food-grade and hazmat warehousing at FW Logistics, the chemical distribution and transloading at Harcros, the polyurea coatings manufacturing at Chemline, the 501(c)(3) donation logistics at WIN Warehouse, and the screen-printing and DTG/DTF/UV-DTF supply at Lawson Screen & Digital Products fully active. The I-70 corridor’s rapid transit access keeps the district connected year-round to downtown St. Louis, Lambert Airport, the Illinois side via the Stan Musial and Poplar Street bridges, and the wider Midwest distribution network through every season.

## Mark Twain I-70 Industrial Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Mark Twain I-70 Industrial, St. Louis

Mark Twain / I-70 Industrial is a critical logistics-and-freight corridor in north St. Louis, defined by its strategic position between Interstate 70 and Natural Bridge Road. Anchored by the Union 70 Center industrial hub, the corridor is currently in the middle of a major revitalization that is expanding its identity from heavy industry alone into a mixed-use district where warehouses, urban farms, nonprofits, greenway-trail networks, and independent retail share the same I-70 freight gateway.

Mark Twain I-70 Industrial sits in north St. Louis at approximately **38.6875° N, 90.2714° W**, within zip code 63120. The corridor is framed by **Interstate 70** on the north and **Natural Bridge Road** on the south, with **North Kingshighway** and **West Florissant Avenue** forming the primary east-west arteries. The district shares its name and freight-corridor identity with the adjacent Mark Twain residential neighborhood, and serves as the industrial-and-logistics backbone connecting north St. Louis to downtown, Lambert Airport, and the Illinois side via the I-70 / I-44 / I-64 gateway.

The corridor’s cornerstone is the **Union 70 Center**, a major industrial hub bounded by Interstate 70 and Natural Bridge Road. As the central component of the area’s commercial infrastructure, the center provides extensive warehouse and distribution space designed for large-scale logistics and manufacturing tenants, with immediate access to I-70 for regional freight movement. Union 70 is a key piece of the city’s strategic plan to revitalize the corridor into one of the region’s leading mixed-use industrial-and-innovation districts.

Beyond the warehouses and distribution facilities, Mark Twain I-70 Industrial is currently being reshaped by a growing layer of **neighborhood-based providers** supporting the surrounding residential pockets and workforce. **Good Life Growing** brings urban-agriculture and sustainable food systems to the corridor. The **American Red Cross of Missouri** anchors community service with blood drives, disaster relief, and CPR/first-aid training. **Great Rivers Greenway** connects north St. Louis neighborhoods to the broader regional trail network. **Garden District STL** and **Maypop Coffee & Garden Shop** bring botanical retail and cafe culture to the district. And the local **Imo’s Pizza** franchise on Natural Bridge serves classic St. Louis-style pizza to residents and workers alike.

Consumers navigating the Mark Twain I-70 Industrial market will find a landscape undergoing significant revitalization aimed at diversifying the local economy. While the area is traditionally dominated by large-scale industrial operations, the growing network of neighborhood-based providers reflects the broader strategic shift toward modernizing existing facilities to attract innovation-focused enterprises. Current market dynamics show a steady demand for warehouse and distribution space alongside increasing investment in urban-agriculture, community-service, retail, and hospitality businesses that round out the corridor’s evolving identity.

Mark Twain I-70 Industrial’s modern business activity reflects this blend of **heavy industry, logistics, and emerging mixed-use commerce** along the I-70 freight corridor and the Natural Bridge / North Kingshighway / West Florissant arteries. The listings on this page draw from the broader I-70 Industrial district and the surrounding north St. Louis region — with providers spanning industrial real estate, urban farming, regional nonprofit services, greenway-trail development, independent botanical and cafe retail, and classic St. Louis-style pizza.

Image: The Mark Twain / I-70 Industrial corridor — north St. Louis’s logistics-and-freight backbone along Interstate 70 and Natural Bridge Road

The Mark Twain I-70 Industrial corridor sits on high ground along the I-70 freight gateway in north St. Louis, where the city follows a classic four-season Midwest pattern. Hot, humid summers with highs in the upper 80s°F bring peak activity to the Union 70 Center distribution operations, the Good Life Growing urban-agriculture work, the outdoor trail-network projects of Great Rivers Greenway, and the patio seating at Maypop Coffee & Garden Shop. Spring and fall draw steady traffic to the corridor’s independent retail and the neighborhood Imo’s Pizza franchise.

Because the corridor is dominated by warehousing and freight logistics, its core operations run year-round regardless of weather. Cold, snowy St. Louis winters keep the Union 70 Center industrial tenants, the American Red Cross of Missouri’s blood-drive and disaster-relief services, and the indoor retail at Garden District STL fully active. The I-70 corridor’s expressway access keeps freight moving through every season, while neighborhood-based providers maintain steady year-round service to residents and workers along the Natural Bridge / North Kingshighway / West Florissant arteries.

## McKinley Heights — St. Louis Neighborhood

Neighborhood

McKinley Heights

Coordinates

38.6098° N, 90.2183° W

Ranked by 1,231+ Google reviews. Updated June 2026.

✓  A historic brick conservation district on the near South Side ✓  In the McKinley-Fox National Register Historic District, between Lafayette Square & Soulard ✓  Avg. rating: 4.6/5 across 1,231 reviews

## McKinley Heights Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 McKinley Heights, St. Louis

McKinley Heights is one of the smallest and most tightly knit neighborhoods on the near South Side of the City of St. Louis — a compact, half-square-mile pocket of historic brick homes and townhouses tucked between Lafayette Square, Soulard, and Benton Park. It is one of the 79 official City neighborhoods, and almost the entire neighborhood sits inside a protected historic district.

The neighborhood is easy to place on a map. It is bounded by **Interstate 44 on the north**, **Jefferson Avenue on the west**, and **Gravois Avenue and Interstate 55 on the east and south**. That puts McKinley Heights within walking distance of Lafayette Square and Lafayette Park to the northwest, the Soulard Farmers Market just east, and Benton Park and Fox Park to the south. Despite covering only about 0.22 square miles, it sits at a genuine crossroads of the near South Side, with the Jefferson Avenue and Gravois Avenue corridors carrying most of its small-business activity.

McKinley Heights grew up as a working-class neighborhood in the second half of the 19th century. The land was originally part of the old Petit Prairie Common Field and was first platted in the 1850s. By 1875 it was filling in with small- to medium-sized single-family brick dwellings that closely resembled the homes going up in neighboring Soulard. Early streetcar service from the Gravois Railway Company ran along Jefferson Avenue, and the first corner stores appeared around 1875 before larger commercial strips took shape along Jefferson and Gravois.

Like much of south St. Louis, McKinley Heights was built by immigrants. **German immigrants settled here first**, followed in later decades by **Serbian and Russian families**. You can read that history directly in the buildings: Second Empire Victorian townhouses from the 1860s through the 1880s, Italianate homes from the late 1880s, and houses of clear Germanic influence after 1890. Most of the surviving brick housing stock was built between roughly 1900 and 1915, though buildings in the historic district date as far back as 1865.

The neighborhood’s churches still carry that immigrant story. The **Russian Orthodox Church of St. Michael** (1927) stands near Ann and Gravois, **St. Marcus U.C.C.** (1914) sits at Russell and McNair, and the **Holy Trinity Serbian Eastern Orthodox Church** anchors the corner of Geyer and McNair. Together they trace the German, Serbian, and Russian families who built and worshiped here, and they remain some of the most recognizable landmarks in a neighborhood made mostly of houses.

McKinley Heights takes its name from **President William McKinley**, a connection echoed in the nearby McKinley School on Russell Avenue and the McKinley Classical Leadership Academy on Ann Avenue. The neighborhood’s historic character earned it formal protection: it was listed as the **McKinley-Fox National Register Historic District in 1984** and became a local historic district in 2008. That status now guides how the area’s brick homes are repaired and restored.

The neighborhood has not always had it easy. The mid-20th-century construction of Interstates 44 and 55 boxed McKinley Heights in on two sides, cutting it off from its surroundings and contributing to a long stretch of population loss and building deterioration. Today the story is one of repair. Owners and rehabbers are steadily bringing the historic brick housing stock back to life, and the small businesses along Jefferson and Gravois — a veterinarian, a contractor, photographers, a screen printer, a piano bar, and a couple of well-loved kitchens — make up the working fabric of the neighborhood profiled in this guide.

A historic brick streetscape in McKinley Heights. Photo: “McKinley Heights” by Paul Sableman, licensed under CC BY 2.0 via Wikimedia Commons.

McKinley Heights shares the humid subtropical climate of greater St. Louis, so the year runs through four full seasons. Summers are warm and humid, with July high temperatures averaging around **89.6°F**, while spring and fall bring milder, comfortable stretches that are ideal for walking the neighborhood’s shaded brick blocks. The region averages roughly **41.7 inches of rain** a year, and spring and summer can deliver the severe thunderstorms common to this part of the Midwest, which sits within Tornado Alley. 

Winters are cold but generally manageable, with January high temperatures averaging near **40.4°F** and annual snowfall of about **16.6 inches**. For a neighborhood built almost entirely of brick, this swing from humid summers to freezing winters is exactly why solid masonry and careful upkeep matter so much — the same sturdy brick homes that have stood here since the early 1900s continue to hold up well against St. Louis weather when they are properly maintained.

## Midtown — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/midtown

Coordinates

38.6356° N, 90.2287° W

Ranked by 18,140+ Google reviews. Updated June 2026.

✓  Anchored by Saint Louis University & City Foundry STL at the center of the city ✓  Steps from the Grand Center arts district — the Fox, Powell Hall & the Pulitzer ✓  Avg. rating: 4.6/5 across 18,140 reviews

## Midtown Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Midtown, St. Louis

Sitting at the geographic center of the city around the Saint Louis University campus at Grand and Lindell, Midtown blends one of America’s oldest universities with City Foundry STL’s food hall and shops — and opens onto the adjacent Grand Center arts district just north, home to the Fox, Powell Hall and the Pulitzer.

Midtown is one of the City of St. Louis’s 79 official neighborhoods, covering roughly 1.35 square miles at the heart of the city. Its boundaries run along **Chouteau Avenue on the south, Jefferson Avenue on the east, Vandeventer Avenue on the west, and Delmar Boulevard and Olive Street on the north**. The neighborhood falls within ZIP codes 63103, 63108 and 63110 and sits in Ward 11, about three miles west of the Mississippi riverfront.

Midtown’s story begins with the **Camp Jackson Affair of May 10, 1861** — the only Civil War clash fought in St. Louis — which took place on land that is now part of the Saint Louis University campus. As the city pushed west from the riverfront, the district filled in through the 1870s and became one of the city’s defining western neighborhoods.

**Saint Louis University** has anchored Midtown for more than a century. Founded in 1818, SLU is the oldest university west of the Mississippi River and the second-oldest Jesuit university in the country. The university bought land at Grand and Lindell in 1867 and opened DuBourg Hall on its Midtown campus in 1888, with St. Francis Xavier (College) Church rising at the same intersection between 1884 and 1914. In the 1970s, Father Paul C. Reinert led the urban-renewal work that helped steady the district during a period of decline.

By the 1920s the stretch around Grand Boulevard had become a bustling entertainment district often compared to New York’s Times Square, lined with grand movie palaces and theaters. Much of that legacy lives on just north of SLU in the **adjacent Grand Center arts district** — officially the separate Covenant Blu Grand Center neighborhood — which today is home to the Fox Theatre, the St. Louis Symphony at Powell Hall, the Sheldon, the Grandel, the Pulitzer Arts Foundation and the Contemporary Art Museum St. Louis. The Midtown Historic District was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1979.

Today Midtown is once again on the rise. The 2020 Census counted 6,862 residents, up more than 21 percent from 2010, and the neighborhood’s biggest recent landmark is **City Foundry STL** — a $220 million, 15-acre adaptive reuse of the former Century Electric factory whose food hall opened in August 2021, bringing a movie theater, the Museum of Illusions and dozens of restaurants and shops to the center of the city.

The businesses below sit in Midtown proper or just steps away in the adjacent Grand Center arts district, and each one is labeled honestly so you know exactly where it is. From a Tadao Ando-designed museum to a dine-in cinema at City Foundry, here are the highest-rated places anchoring this central St. Louis neighborhood.

The Fabulous Fox Theatre in the Grand Center arts district just north of Midtown. Photo: “Fabulous Fox Theatre” by Matthew (m shipp22), licensed under CC BY 2.0 via Wikimedia Commons.

St. Louis has a humid subtropical climate (Köppen Cfa), so Midtown sees warm, lively spring and fall seasons and hot, humid summers with July highs around 89.6°F. Those stretches are when Grand Boulevard, City Foundry’s patios and the SLU campus feel busiest, and the city averages about 41.7 inches of rain across the year. 

Winters are cool to cold, with January highs near 40.4°F and about 16.6 inches of snow in a typical year, and the metro sits within the broader Tornado Alley risk zone. The good news for visitors is that so much of Midtown’s draw — City Foundry’s food hall, the Museum of Illusions, the Alamo Drafthouse and the theaters of nearby Grand Center — is indoors and enjoyable in any season.

## Mount Pleasant — St. Louis Neighborhood

Mount Pleasant

38.5723° N, 90.2436° W

✓  10 of 10 verified Mount Pleasant providers ✓  All businesses verified via Google Business ✓  Avg. rating: 5.0/5 across 123 reviews

## Mount Pleasant Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Mount Pleasant, St. Louis

Mount Pleasant serves as one of the city’s most historically layered Near Southside river-bluff neighborhoods — anchored by Sugarloaf Mound (the only surviving Mississippian platform mound in St. Louis, now fully reacquired by the Osage Nation in September 2025), the 1863 St. Anthony of Padua Catholic Church with its twin 175-foot towers, the 1895 Mount Pleasant School on the National Register, the Neighborhood Innovation Center microbusiness incubator at 3207 Meramec Street, and the Gravois Industrial Court precision-manufacturing cluster — offering Black and Hispanic chamber advocacy, ISO-certified precision machining, event venues, microbusiness incubation, and specialized industrial services for residents and businesses across the Greater Dutchtown corridor.

Mount Pleasant sits along the South Side river bluff at **38.5723° N, 90.2436° W** in zip code 63111. The neighborhood’s official boundaries run from **Meramec Street** on the north, south to **Eichelberger Street**, east to the **Mississippi River**, and west to **South Compton Avenue**. The neighborhood’s primary corridors are **South Broadway** — the commercial and industrial spine running along the river bluff — **Meramec Street** (the main east-west commercial corridor), **Virginia Avenue**, **Mount Pleasant Street**, and the interior grid of **Minnesota Avenue**, **Nebraska Avenue**, **Ohio Avenue**, and **Delor Street**. The street grid follows the distinctive South Side convention: east-west streets are named for Native American nations (Meramec, Osceola, Iowa, Itaska), and north-south streets are named for U.S. states (Compton, Virginia, Minnesota, Pennsylvania, Ohio, Indiana). Mount Pleasant is bordered by **Marine Villa** and **Benton Park West** to the north, **Dutchtown** to the west, **Carondelet** to the south, and the Mississippi River to the east. Despite a widely held local belief that Mount Pleasant is part of Dutchtown, the two are distinct neighborhoods — Mount Pleasant occupies the southeastern sector of the broader **Greater Dutchtown** cultural area, a regional identity that derives from *Deutsch*, a colloquial reference to the German Catholic immigrants who settled this part of south St. Louis beginning in the 1840s and 1850s.

The neighborhood’s land has a remarkably deep history. A portion of Mount Pleasant was originally part of the **old St. Louis Commons**, the colonial-era public land tract that the City of St. Louis purchased in 1836 and gradually sold off through the 1860s, retaining only what eventually became Gravois Park, Laclede Park, and **Mount Pleasant Park**. Residential development took place east to west, with most of the area built up by 1910. The 1849 fire destroyed most of the wooden buildings along the riverfront. Single-family brick structures filled in along South Compton north of Delor between 1900 and 1930. The neighborhood’s primary green space is **Mount Pleasant Park** at Michigan and Dakota — a 3-acre city park established by ordinance in 1812, making it among the oldest park reservations within the city limits — featuring a roller hockey rink in some of the best condition in St. Louis and open neighborhood grounds. The **Minnie Wood Memorial Playground** at Broadway and Meramec was acquired as a gift in 1925. Riverfront properties along South Broadway include both 19th-century industrial parcels and a handful of remaining large stately homes converted to elder-care facilities like Edgewater.

Mount Pleasant’s most extraordinary landmark predates European settlement entirely. **Sugarloaf Mound** at **4420 Ohio Avenue** is the only surviving Native American mound within the City of St. Louis — constructed between **600 and 1200 AD** by the Mississippian culture, the same civilization that built Cahokia Mounds across the river. St. Louis was once known as “Mound City” for the approximately 40 to 100 earthen structures that European settlers found in the area at the city’s 1764 French founding; by 1875, nearly all had been leveled for railroads, residences, beer gardens, and the 1904 World’s Fair preparation. Sugarloaf alone survived. The mound was named for its conical shape that resembled the sugar loaves transported in the 18th century. Listed on the **National Register of Historic Places on February 17, 1984**, the mound’s sole-ownership reacquisition by its ancestral stewards is one of the most significant Native land repatriation events in Missouri history. After 17 years of advocacy beginning in 2008, the **Osage Nation Historic Preservation Office** announced on **September 22, 2025** the complete reacquisition of the three-tier mound, in partnership with the St. Louis arts and civic-impact organization **Counterpublic**. On **September 19, 2025**, St. Louis Mayor Cara Spencer declared the day “Sugarloaf Mound Preservation Plan Day” at a public City Hall event — the city’s first public acknowledgment of the sovereignty of the Osage Nation’s rights to the sacred site. The 1928 home at the summit (the Adams House, 4420 Ohio Street mailing address) was razed in September 2017; remaining structures will be carefully demolished and the mound stabilized, with a future **interpretive center** under development.

The neighborhood’s most visible architectural legacy from the German immigrant era is **St. Anthony of Padua Catholic Church** at **3140 Meramec Street**, whose twin **175-foot Romanesque towers** are visible for miles across the South Side. Founded in 1863 specifically for the German Catholic immigrants who were filling Mount Pleasant and the surrounding neighborhoods, the present 1910 Romanesque sanctuary features **Emil Frei art glass windows** of exceptional quality, an organ regularly cited as one of the finest in the city, and ornate paintings and statuary that local guidebooks rank among the most magnificent Catholic interiors in St. Louis. The parish has been served by the **Order of Friars Minor (Franciscans)** for more than 160 years. The **parish food pantry** at 3130 Meramec Street, run by lay volunteer Outreach Ministries, remains one of the most important social-service operations on the South Side. The **St. Anthony parish school** on Meramec was recently sold and is being adapted for new neighborhood use. Nearby, **St. Hedwig Catholic Church** at Compton and Pulaski — founded in 1904 for Polish immigrant families and now operating as **Diverse City Church** — is having its former school building converted into a campus for **Gene Slay’s Girls & Boys Club of St. Louis**, one of the most successful adaptive-reuse projects in the neighborhood. The **Mount Pleasant School** on Nebraska Avenue, a three-story brick structure built in 1895 by the St. Louis Public Schools architectural office, is on the **National Register of Historic Places** and has been converted to apartments.

Mount Pleasant’s 20th-century history was disrupted, like much of south-city St. Louis, by the construction of **Interstate 55** in the mid-20th century, which bisected the neighborhood and cleared blocks of historic housing stock. The mid-century homes built in the wake of the interstate construction now form a distinctive pocket of post-war architecture amid the predominantly Victorian and early-20th-century brick fabric. The **Maryville Academy**, a long-established Catholic girls’ school, moved to the suburbs in 1961, leaving behind a fenced post-office site and the **Maryville Gardens apartments** (1978) with their massive surface parking lot on what was once the academy’s sports field. White flight and suburbanization through the 1960s and 1970s changed the neighborhood’s demographic balance dramatically. The 2000 Census recorded 4,302 residents (59% White, 32% Black, 4% Asian, 2% Hispanic/Latino) in 2,281 housing units, with 85% occupied and a 31%/69% owner-to-renter split — a low owner-occupancy rate for a South Side neighborhood, reflecting Mount Pleasant’s position as a transitional corridor connecting Dutchtown, Marine Villa, Carondelet, and the river-bluff industrial parcels.

The neighborhood’s commercial corridors have followed the city’s usual North-South pattern of disinvestment followed by selective revitalization. **Meramec Street**, with its many former storefronts (the Schaeffer Hobby Store moved to the suburbs years ago) and Virginia Avenue have both seen extended periods of vacancy. The current wave of revitalization is centered on three major institutional efforts: the **Neighborhood Innovation Center (NIC)** at 3207 Meramec Street is a nonprofit microbusiness incubator providing coworking space, digital upskilling programs, community-market and farmers’-market hosting, and high-speed infrastructure access to entrepreneurs who might otherwise lack it. The center also functions as a local polling place. The **Marquette Homes affordable housing project** (182 units, expected completion in 2026) and the **St. Joseph Housing Initiative** home sales program are working to rebuild the residential housing stock, while the **Dutchtown Main Streets façade grant program** is restoring commercial storefronts along Meramec and Virginia. Mount Pleasant is actively engaged in the **PlanSTL Area 7 planning process** (2026–2027), which will set the strategic framework for the broader Greater Dutchtown corridor.

The neighborhood’s industrial economy concentrates along the **Gravois Industrial Court** cluster to the south and the South Broadway corridor, where precision manufacturers like **BEC Machining LLC** (an ISO 9001:2008-registered CNC machining shop with 17 years of experience under Mike Baudrexl, BSME) and specialty operations like **St. Louis Laser** on South Broadway serve the regional industrial economy. The combination of pre-European Native heritage, German Catholic religious institutions, river-bluff industrial fabric, mid-century interstate disruption, and 21st-century microbusiness incubation makes Mount Pleasant one of the most layered and culturally distinctive neighborhoods on the South Side. As Mayor Spencer wrote on September 19, 2025: “St. Louis came much too close to losing all of the more than 100 mounds that were here before European settlers arrived and ultimately tore down all but one” — a sentiment that captures both the fragility and the resilience of Mount Pleasant’s extraordinary heritage.

Image: Sugarloaf Mound at 4420 Ohio Avenue in Mount Pleasant — the last Native American mound in St. Louis (constructed 600–1200 AD), reacquired in full by the Osage Nation in September 2025 after a 17-year preservation effort — Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 3.0 by Parker Botanical

Mount Pleasant occupies the South Side river bluff, with South Broadway running along elevated terrain that drops sharply toward the Mississippi to the east. The neighborhood’s proximity to the river brings slightly more moderate temperatures than inland neighborhoods — cooler summer evenings, slower spring warming — while Interstate 55, which bisected the neighborhood during mid-20th-century construction, provides fast connections north to downtown and south along the river corridor. Hot, humid summers in the upper 80s°F bring peak outdoor activity to Mount Pleasant Park’s roller hockey rink, the Neighborhood Innovation Center’s outdoor community markets and farmers’ markets, and the Meramec Street commercial corridor. Cool springs and falls are the busiest seasons for the Osage Nation’s ongoing preservation work at Sugarloaf Mound, Dutchtown Main Streets façade rehabilitation projects, and the ongoing PlanSTL Area 7 planning process. Cold winters with periodic ice and snow shift commerce indoors to the St. Anthony of Padua sanctuary, Celebrate Event Venue, and the precision-manufacturing operations along Gravois Industrial Court. The historic brick construction of Mount Pleasant’s 1890s and early-20th-century housing stock has weathered more than a century of Missouri climate with remarkable durability.

## Near North Riverfront — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/near-north-riverfront

Neighborhood

Near North Riverfront

Coordinates

38.6595° N, 90.1902° W

Ranked by 2,179+ Google reviews. Updated June 2026.

✓  On the Mississippi riverfront, anchored by the McKinley Bridge & Riverfront Trail ✓  A historic industrial riverfront district just north of downtown ✓  Avg. rating: 4.6/5 across 2,179 reviews

## Near North Riverfront Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Near North Riverfront, St. Louis

Near North Riverfront is one of the most unusual neighborhoods in St. Louis: a large, overwhelmingly industrial stretch of the Mississippi River just north of downtown, where rail yards, freight tracks, cold-storage warehouses, vacant land, and barge traffic vastly outnumber the roughly 395 people who actually live here. It is not a residential neighborhood in the ordinary sense — it is a working riverfront corridor with deep history, anchored by the McKinley Bridge and threaded by the St. Louis Riverfront Trail.

The neighborhood is the City of St. Louis neighborhood **#64**, sitting in north St. Louis along the riverfront **north of Laclede’s Landing**. Its rough boundaries run along **Tucker Boulevard, West Florissant Avenue, and N. Broadway**, with the **Mississippi River** forming the eastern edge. It spans Wards 8 and 14 and covers about 2.17 square miles — a big footprint for such a small population, which is exactly what you would expect from a district built for industry rather than houses. (Note that it is a separate neighborhood from the City’s North Riverfront district, #79, farther upriver.)

In the 19th century this stretch of riverfront was an emblematic cross-section of the Gateway City, a place where the Mississippi, heavy industry, commerce, and (then) dense residential blocks all collided. Early land here traced to former mayor **Bryan Mullanphy** and prominent owner **Peter Lindell**, who ran a stone **quarry** near the river. The **Wiggins Ferry**, a critical crossing to the Illinois side, departed from near here — and that river-crossing role drew the extensive rail connections that would come to define, and ultimately demolish much of, the historic fabric of the district.

Industry flourished on the riverbank. Luther Kennett’s **Shot Tower** used gravity to make lead shot, and the area hosted what was described as one of the grandest **sugar refineries** in America. The skyline of smokestacks, grain elevators, riverboats, and rocky bluffs was distinctive enough that Compton & Dry chose the district for their 1876 *Pictorial St. Louis*. Into the 20th century, large **refrigerated cold-storage warehouses** — several designed by architect **Isaac Taylor** and his competitors — rose north of the Martin Luther King (MLK) Bridge, and a number of those warehouse buildings still stand today.

Over the 20th century the once-populous neighborhood emptied out. What remains is a landscape of vacant lots, surviving historic industrial buildings, active freight rail, barge and tugboat traffic, and open Mississippi River frontage. Population has never been high here — it rose to 648 in 2000, then fell to 553 in 2010 and 395 in 2020 — and the City counts only about 95 housing units in the whole neighborhood. We want to be honest with readers: this is a sparsely populated working industrial and riverfront corridor, not a place with a traditional residential main street.

For visitors and cyclists, the river edge is the draw. The **St. Louis Riverfront Trail (Mississippi Greenway)** runs along this stretch, offering river-meets-industry views — tugboats pushing barges, freight trains on the commodity tracks, and the wide brown sweep of the Mississippi. At Branch Street the trail meets the **McKinley Bridge Bikeway**, where the historic “Iron Horse” Trestle project has been converting an abandoned steel rail trestle into an elevated park reaching toward Old North St. Louis.

The **McKinley Bridge** itself is the neighborhood’s signature landmark: a historic early-1900s span over the Mississippi to Venice, Illinois, now carrying road traffic plus the bikeway that links to trails on the Illinois side. Inland, the neighborhood abuts the historic Mullanphy area and **Old North St. Louis** on its western edge, while the I-70 **Stan Musial Veterans Memorial Bridge** sits just to the south near downtown and Laclede’s Landing. Together these crossings, trails, and surviving warehouses make Near North Riverfront a fascinating place to explore on two wheels, even if few people call it home.

The McKinley Bridge over the Mississippi at the Near North Riverfront. Photo: “McKinley Bridge 2025” by Antony-22, licensed under CC BY-SA 4.0 via Wikimedia Commons.

St. Louis sits in a humid subtropical climate (Köppen Cfa), and the Near North Riverfront feels every season of it out on the open water. Summers are warm and humid, with July average highs around **89.6°F**, and spring and fall bring the most comfortable riding weather along the Riverfront Trail. The region averages about **41.7 inches of rain** a year, and because this is low-lying land right on the Mississippi, the riverfront is especially exposed to high-water and flooding during heavy-rain events. 

Winters are cold but generally manageable, with January average highs near **40.4°F** and roughly **16.6 inches of snow** across a typical year. The St. Louis area also sits in or near Tornado Alley, so spring storms can be severe — worth keeping in mind for anyone working the rail yards, warehouses, and barge operations that define this industrial corridor year-round.

## North Hampton — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/north-hampton

# North Hampton Neighborhood Guide – Southwest St. Louis History and Local Services

## Explore North Hampton’s Tree Lined Streets, Tilles Park, and Local Services

Updated May 2026.

## North Hampton Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

North Hampton, St. Louis

North Hampton is the southwest St. Louis neighborhood made up of two distinct sub-neighborhoods — Tilles Park (Hampton to Macklind) and Kingshighway Hills (Macklind to Kingshighway) — that developed only after the depletion of the area’s coal and clay mines around the turn of the 20th century. Its 29-acre Rosalie Tilles Memorial Park was created by city ordinance 48569 in 1956 using proceeds from the sale of the original 1932 Tilles Park in St. Louis County. The corridor offers boutique retail, restaurants, family services, and neighborhood businesses along Hampton Avenue, Kingshighway, and Chippewa.

North Hampton is one of St. Louis’s 79 official neighborhoods, located in the southwest section of the city at **38.5970° N, 90.2935° W**. Its boundaries run north to **Scanlan, 59th Street, and Connecticut**, south to **Chippewa Street**, east to **South Kingshighway Boulevard**, and west to **Hampton Avenue**. The neighborhood is bordered by **Southwest Garden** to the north, **Tower Grove South** to the east, **Bevo Mill** to the southeast, **Southampton** to the south, **St. Louis Hills** to the southwest, and **Lindenwood Park** to the west. North Hampton is itself made up of two smaller sub-neighborhoods: the **Tilles Park** neighborhood (Hampton Avenue to Macklind Avenue) and the **Kingshighway Hills** neighborhood (Macklind Avenue to South Kingshighway Boulevard). The 2020 census recorded the neighborhood’s population at 71.4% White, 15.9% Black, 4.2% Asian, 6.8% Two or More Races, 1.5% Some Other Race, and 0.3% Native American.

North Hampton holds an unusual distinction in St. Louis development history: **much of the neighborhood was the city’s last open land**. While development had already occurred to the west in Lindenwood Park (driven by interurban rail) and to the north and east with immigrant workers serving the Oak Hill railroad, this stretch of southwest St. Louis was the area of actual **coal and clay mine deposits**. Though the deposits had been discovered as early as the 1830s and were largely depleted by the turn of the 1900s, the **settling of the disturbed land took nearly another half-century**. The earliest residential subdivision in the neighborhood was **Kingshighway Hills in 1929**, platted between South Kingshighway and Macklind Avenue. The area west of Macklind did not develop until **after World War II**, slowed both by the unsettled mining ground and by wartime labor and material shortages.

The streets and surrounding context preserve traces of the mining era. **Chippewa Street** — now North Hampton’s southern boundary — was once called “Mine Road” west of Grand Avenue, because it led to the clay mines owned by Russell, Christy, and Bingham (whose surnames live on in nearby southside streets). According to *The Streets of St. Louis: A History of St. Louis Street Names* by William and Marcella Manan, James Russell and his son-in-law George Parker operated a coal mine at Morganford and Tholozan and a clay mine at Morganford and Utah; **Fyler Avenue** commemorates James D. Fyler, an early landowner whose estate was subdivided in 1866. The streets **Lindenwood, Mardel, and Oleatha** were named in 1888 by Oleatha Didawich, the wife of real estate developer Samuel Thomas Rathell — Oleatha for herself, Lindenwood for the college she attended, and Mardel by reversing the syllables of Delmar (the street she grew up on, named by two landowners from Delaware and Maryland).

North Hampton was connected to the rest of St. Louis by streetcar as early as 1889. The neighborhood was served by two lines: the **#50 Southampton line** (established 1889 as an east-west route traveling between Third & Washington and Macklind & Devonshire, stopping frequently along Kingshighway between Connecticut and Chippewa), and the **#21 Tower Grove line**. These streetcar connections shaped the neighborhood’s commercial corridors along Kingshighway and Hampton long before the residential blocks themselves filled in. North Hampton’s residential blocks reflect a recognizable mix of 1930s-1950s housing stock: brick “gingerbread” Tudors with arched stone entryways, cozy wood bungalows, and mid-century Cape Cods on the northeast side of the neighborhood. A George Sanger-designed 1930 classic on Kingshighway remains a landmark home.

The neighborhood’s defining green space is the 29-acre **Rosalie Tilles Memorial Park** at Hampton Avenue and Fyler, created by St. Louis city **ordinance 48569 in 1956** (placed into ordinance in 1957) and named in honor of **Rosalie Peck Tilles** — the mother of park founder Andrew “Cap” Tilles (1865-1951), a prominent St. Louis businessman who was co-founder and president of an investment syndicate that dominated the early-20th-century US horse racing industry (the “Big Three” partnership that monopolized Midwestern and Southern tracks). There is also a Tilles Park in St. Louis County, and the connection between the two is unusual: the original 1932 Tilles Park in St. Louis County belonged to the City of St. Louis and was developed in 1937 by the City and WPA funds; a 1955 study found that more than 80 percent of the people who used the county park lived in the county. In **March 1955**, the Mayor and Comptroller were authorized to sell the county park, and the proceeds were used to purchase the present 29-acre Rosalie Tilles Memorial Park in North Hampton at Hampton and Fyler — which is why two parks in metro St. Louis share the Tilles name. The park was extensively developed from the 1955 bond issue.

The park is also part of one of the most haunting sub-histories of North Hampton. The **Hampton Gardens apartment complex** — a 510-unit complex on the park’s north side — was built in **1952** on the site of **the City’s last known potter’s field**, a cemetery for indigents in the area bound by Hampton, Fyler, Scanlan, and Sublette. The remains buried there are believed to have been moved to **Mt. Lebanon Cemetery** at Lindbergh and St. Charles Rock Road before Hampton Gardens was built. Today the park offers soccer fields, a tennis complex with pickleball courts, a roller hockey rink, a recently-restored basketball court, a spray fountain, updated playground equipment, exercise stations, a prairie restoration project, and winding internal walking paths designed without cars to create a quiet pedestrian-only experience. The neighborhood has its own **Tilles Park Neighborhood Association**, and the broader area is also served by the **North Hampton Neighbors Association**.

North Hampton also borders one of the most prominent architectural landmarks of southwest St. Louis: the **St. Louis Psychiatric Rehabilitation Center** (originally the **St. Louis State Hospital**, formerly the **St. Louis County Lunatic Asylum**), built in **1869** on the highest point of the city. Located partially in North Hampton and partially in adjacent Southwest Garden along the Scanlan/Connecticut northern boundary, it was outside the city limits at the time it was built, but renamed the St. Louis City Insane Asylum after the 1876 city-county separation made it part of St. Louis. The neighborhood’s public school is the **Kennard Classical Junior Academy** on Potomac Avenue — an SLPS magnet elementary school with a focus on gifted education, serving students Preschool through 5th grade.

North Hampton’s commercial life is concentrated along the Hampton Avenue, Chippewa Street, and Kingshighway corridors. Anchor businesses include **The Golden Hoosier** (a classic restaurant glowing in Art Deco neon on Hampton), **Himalayan Yeti** (Indian and Nepalese cuisine off Kingshighway), **Hampton Village Shopping Center** (anchored by Target and Schnucks Hampton Village right across from each other), and the historic **Silverleaf Tavern**. The northeastern portion of the neighborhood — east of Sublette and north of Fyler — consists of light industry and distribution facilities. The neighborhood notably **gained 4.3% in population from 1990 to 2000**, growing to 8,097 residents at the 2000 census, making it one of the few St. Louis neighborhoods to grow during that decade. North Hampton falls within Ward 5 of the City of St. Louis. The Machacek Library Branch of the St. Louis Public Library is conveniently located just west in the Lindenwood Park neighborhood on Scanlan Avenue.

Image: Historic postcard of the St. Louis Insane Asylum (today the St. Louis Psychiatric Rehabilitation Center) at 5300 Arsenal Street — designed by William Rumbold (architect of the Old Courthouse dome) and opened April 23, 1869 on the highest point of the City of St. Louis along the northern boundary of North Hampton — Wikimedia Commons, public domain

North Hampton sits on the gently rolling southwest terrain of St. Louis at 38.5970° N — just south of the highest point in the City of St. Louis, which is occupied by the 1869 St. Louis Psychiatric Rehabilitation Center grounds straddling the Scanlan/Connecticut northern boundary with Southwest Garden. The neighborhood experiences St. Louis’s full humid continental weather range: hot, humid summers with frequent 90°F days, cold winters with regular freezing temperatures, and a notably active spring severe weather season that includes real tornado corridor exposure. Peak outdoor activity falls during spring and fall, when Rosalie Tilles Memorial Park’s 29 acres of walking paths, soccer fields, tennis complex with pickleball courts, roller hockey rink, prairie restoration, basketball court, spray fountain, and updated playground draw the heaviest neighborhood use. Cool seasons drive intense indoor activity at The Golden Hoosier, Himalayan Yeti, Silverleaf Tavern, Hampton Village Shopping Center, and the Kennard Classical Junior Academy. Winter shifts commerce indoors to Target, Schnucks Hampton Village, the Machacek Library branch just west in Lindenwood Park, and the corridor of restaurants along Kingshighway between Chippewa and Tholozan. The neighborhood’s 1929-onward residential construction (Kingshighway Hills 1929, west of Macklind post-WWII, Hampton Gardens 1952) has weathered more than three-quarters of a century of Missouri climate with the consistency typical of brick Tudor and Cape Cod stock.

## North Pointe — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/north-pointe

Coordinates

38.7150° N, 90.2583° W

Ranked by 74+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  10 of 10 verified service providers ✓  All businesses verified via Google Business ✓  Avg. rating: 5.0/5 across 74 reviews

## North Pointe Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 North Pointe, St. Louis

North Pointe serves as one of the city’s most stable far-north residential neighborhoods — anchored by the 1921 Beck Realty “North-Pointe Addition” bungalow plat, the 1.10-acre Vivian-Astra Park boulevard, the Halls Ferry Circle traffic rotary at Riverview, Goodfellow, and West Florissant, the 77% homeowner-occupied residential blocks, and the new PlanSTL Baden–North Pointe Neighborhood Plan — offering regional development, business and IT consulting, professional services, and entrepreneurial support for residents and businesses across north St. Louis and the North County corridor.

North Pointe sits at the far-north edge of the City of St. Louis at **38.7150° N, 90.2583° W** in zip code 63147. The neighborhood is shaped as an irregular pentagon defined by three boundary corridors: **Goodfellow Boulevard** along the north and northwest (which also serves as the city-limit boundary with Jennings), **West Florissant Avenue** on the southwest, and **Riverview Boulevard** on the east and northeast. The convergence of Riverview, Goodfellow, and Halls Ferry creates the dramatic **Halls Ferry Circle** traffic rotary at the neighborhood’s northern apex. Interior streets including **Orchid Avenue**, **Northcrest Street**, and the tree-lined teardrop boulevard of **Vivian and Astra Avenues** — which forms the 1.10-acre **Vivian-Astra Park** at the southeastern edge near the intersection of Riverview and West Florissant — give North Pointe a distinctly residential character that belies its position at the edge of the city proper. West Florissant Avenue, the neighborhood’s commercial spine, continues north across the city-county line into Jennings and beyond to Ferguson, making North Pointe a natural gateway between the city’s northernmost residential blocks and the North County corridor. North Pointe is bordered by **Walnut Park East** and **Walnut Park West** to the south, **Baden** to the southeast, **Riverview** to the north, and **Calvary Cemetery** on its southeast city side.

North Pointe is genuinely one of the youngest neighborhoods in the City of St. Louis. The 1875 Compton and Dry pictorial atlas shows nothing here but rolling hills and farm fields way out on the city’s edge. The land had originally been part of French and Spanish colonial land grants made in the late 18th century, confirmed by American authorities in the early 1800s, and subdivided into farms in the 1850s. **Columbia Bottoms Road** — today’s Riverview Drive — was laid out around 1830 as a farm-to-market road, carrying traffic from more than 100 square miles of farms in northern St. Louis County south to the St. Louis market. The first inhabitants were largely farmers, with major settlement coinciding with the arrival of German immigrants in the 1840s and 1850s. A few magnificent country houses from this era still survive within the neighborhood’s modern grid. Real residential development did not begin until the 1920s and 1930s, when streetcar lines pushed the city’s growth boundary far enough north to make subdivision profitable.

The neighborhood’s defining moment came on **Friday, May 20, 1921**, when the **Beck Realty Development Company** placed a now-famous advertisement in the St. Louis Star and Times announcing the official sale of lots in the new **“North-Pointe Addition.”** The promotion boasted that “Water, Lights, Sewers, Sidewalks, and Streets Graded All Included in the Price” — the first stirrings of the suburban vocabulary that would soon shape American real estate marketing. Beck Realty’s circular cards offered working and middle-class families their first opportunity to own a home this far north in the city. The bungalows and two-story brick houses that filled the lots between the 1920s and 1930s — with their striped awnings, arched stone doorways, and gingerbread-house brickwork — still define the streetscape today, making North Pointe one of St. Louis’s most intact early-20th-century residential districts. Development of this period also included parallel subdivisions: **Coshocton Heights** (1911), the Finch subdivisions on Florissant Avenue (1920), and **Lilian Terrace** and **Electra Park** (1926). The neighborhood’s near-100% brick construction, dense block pattern, and tree-lined boulevards have been compared to the same-era Bel-Nor and Pasadena Hills developments — though notably here within the city limits rather than as separate municipalities.

The neighborhood’s defining mid-century transformation came in the 1960s, when North Pointe became home to a thriving middle-class African American community at a time when much of the north side was experiencing dramatic disinvestment. That community endures today: the 2020 Census recorded a neighborhood that is 95% Black, 2% Two or More Races, and 2% White, with 3,396 residents in 1,648 housing units — and a remarkable **77% owner-occupancy rate**, among the highest of any north-city neighborhood. The 89% overall occupancy rate and the loss of only 10 housing units between 2010 and 2020 distinguish North Pointe from the population decline curves seen elsewhere on the north side. The neighborhood’s reputation among urban planners and observers reflects this stability: one prominent local writer described North Pointe as “the Southampton of the north,” while another ranked it in the top five most stable and cleanest neighborhoods in the city. The neighborhood’s housing prices remain notably more affordable than comparable south-city neighborhoods — a function of location rather than the quality of the craftsmanship.

A nearly arrow-straight diagonal **railroad corridor** with an old-growth tree canopy bisects the neighborhood from north to south — a quiet reminder of the industrial heritage beneath the residential surface. Public schools anchor each end of the corridor: **Herzog Elementary School** at the southern end and **Nance Elementary School** at Halls Ferry Circle at the northern end. **North Park United Methodist Church** at 1525 Orchid Avenue has served as the neighborhood’s civic anchor for generations — the church is the host site for the May 13, 2026 public adoption hearing for the **PlanSTL Baden–North Pointe Neighborhood Plan**. Commercial activity concentrates along the perimeter arteries, with small commercial districts at West Florissant Avenue, Goodfellow Boulevard, and Halls Ferry Circle — including bike lanes and parking lanes along Riverview Boulevard that have been recognized as exemplary streetscape investments. Speed humps installed in recent years have helped calm the speedway-style traffic that long plagued Riverview.

**North County Inc.**, the regional development association headquartered at 8225 Florissant Road Suite 11 just beyond North Pointe’s commercial edge, has been the primary civic and economic development organization for all 45 North County municipalities since its founding in 1977. As a 501(c)(6) association, North County Inc. represents local businesses, school districts, and elected officials across the Florissant Road and West Florissant Avenue corridor — the same commercial spine that connects North Pointe to the broader north St. Louis County ecosystem including Jennings and Ferguson. The organization is the regional administrator of the **Robert L. Pearce Award**, Missouri’s most prestigious honor for public school superintendents. Established in 1987 by the Missouri Association of School Administrators and named for the founders of the Pearce Corporation architectural firm, the award includes a $1,000 student scholarship and recognizes the kind of long-term, community-embedded leadership that defines the institutions serving North Pointe.

North Pointe is currently the subject of the most comprehensive neighborhood planning effort in its history. The **PlanSTL Baden–North Pointe Neighborhood Plan**, developed by Interboro Partners and published in draft form on March 31, 2026, is a 710-page document addressing housing, West Florissant Avenue economic development, transportation, arts, sustainability, and flood resilience — the latter a critical concern given that the Metropolitan Sewer District has been engaged for years in analyzing recurring flood inundation in the Baden-Riverview area. The plan’s adoption hearing is scheduled for May 13, 2026 at North Park United Methodist Church — a community-driven planning process that reflects the high homeownership rates, near-100% brick construction, and stable residential fabric that have defined North Pointe through decades of north-city change. The challenge for North Pointe going forward, in the words of historian Chris Naffziger, is whether the city’s leadership will remember the hard-working, proud residents who have been investing in their neighborhood and return that investment back up West Florissant Avenue.

Image: The 1921 Beck Realty Development Company circular card that launched the North-Pointe Addition — the original lot sale that built the neighborhood — Wikimedia Commons / Public Domain via DPLA

North Pointe sits at the northern edge of the St. Louis urban plateau, where the city grid begins to give way to the more varied terrain of North County. The neighborhood’s position along the Florissant Road and West Florissant Avenue corridor places it within the full sweep of the four-season Midwest climate, with hot, humid summers in the upper 80s°F, cool springs and falls, and cold winters with periodic ice and snow. Spring and early summer bring the city’s active severe-weather season, and the Baden-Riverview area has experienced recurring flooding in recent years — a primary driver of the flood-resilience focus in the new PlanSTL neighborhood plan. The old-growth tree canopy along the diagonal railroad corridor and the boulevard trees of Vivian-Astra Park provide meaningful seasonal relief within the residential blocks, while peak outdoor activity falls during spring and fall along the West Florissant commercial corridor and at Halls Ferry Circle. The neighborhood’s near-100% brick construction has weathered a full century of Missouri climate with remarkable durability.

## North Riverfront — St. Louis Neighborhood

North Riverfront

38.7044° N, 90.2347° W
