✓  10 of 10 verified industrial-corridor providers ✓  All businesses verified via Google Business ✓  Avg. rating: 4.9/5 across 1,009 reviews

## North Riverfront Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 North Riverfront, St. Louis

North Riverfront serves as the city’s northernmost riverfront district — anchored by the 1929 Old Chain of Rocks Bridge, the 250-acre North Riverfront Park, the western flanks of historic Bellefontaine and Calvary Cemeteries, and the working industrial corridor along North Broadway — offering nonprofit career development, federal sales consulting, water and wastewater engineering, foundry and industrial manufacturing, and economic-development advocacy for residents and businesses across north St. Louis and the Mississippi River corridor.

North Riverfront sits at the northernmost edge of the City of St. Louis at **38.7044° N, 90.2347° W** in zip code 63147. The neighborhood’s official boundaries run from **Adelaide Street** on the south, north to **Maline Creek** on the north, with the **Mississippi River** forming the entire eastern edge and **Hall Street, Calvary Avenue, Bellefontaine Cemetery, and Interstate 70** forming the western edge. The neighborhood’s primary corridors are **North Broadway** — the industrial spine running parallel to the river — along with **Hall Street, Riverview Drive, Thatcher Avenue, and Palm Street**. North Riverfront is distinct from **Near North Riverfront**, the separate neighborhood immediately to the south between Carr Street and Adelaide; together, the two form the city’s long Mississippi industrial corridor. This is not the riverfront of the Gateway Arch, which sits roughly five miles to the south; North Riverfront is a working industrial corridor where scrap yards, foundries, trucking distribution, recycling facilities, and the only publicly owned port facility on the Missouri side of the river have operated since the 19th century.

The neighborhood’s defining geographic feature is the **Chain of Rocks Reach** — a 17-mile series of rock ledges along the Mississippi that begins just north of St. Louis. These submerged rocks made the river extremely difficult and dangerous to navigate, especially during low water, which is why in the late 1940s and early 1950s the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers built an 8.4-mile bypass canal completed in 1953. The area was chosen as the site for the St. Louis waterworks in 1865, with construction beginning in 1887 and the water plant opening in 1894. A 1915 filter plant was the largest in the world at the time of its construction. **Intake Tower #1**, completed in 1894 and designed by architect William Eames in the Richardsonian Romanesque style with a distinctive oval plan and green conical roof, still stands directly in the river off the Missouri shoreline — its silhouette often compared to the Mäuseturm on the Rhine River in Germany.

The neighborhood’s most recognizable landmark is the **Old Chain of Rocks Bridge**, opened in 1929 after privately constructed at a $3 million cost. The bridge has an unusual **22-degree bend at mid-span** — necessary because the bedrock could not support straight piers and because river captains needed extra clearance near the water-intake towers. In 1936 Bypass U.S. Route 66 was rerouted over the bridge, making it the legendary Route 66 crossing into Missouri. The bridge’s bright-red paint was changed to dull green in the early 1940s to make it less visible to aircraft during World War II. After being closed to vehicle traffic in 1970 when I-270 opened, the bridge spent nearly three decades in uncertain status — it even appeared in the 1981 film “Escape From New York.” In 1999 it reopened as the **Old Chain of Rocks Bridge Bikeway**, a one-mile pedestrian and bicycle crossing connecting the St. Louis Riverfront Trail to the MCT Confluence Trail in Illinois. Since 2014 Great Rivers Greenway has maintained the bridge, and it is now one of the longest pedestrian-only bridges in the world. The bridge has been individually listed on the **National Register of Historic Places**. From 1927 to 1978, the riverbank just south of the bridge was home to the **Chain of Rocks Fun Fair Park**, a beloved local amusement park that closed when Six Flags drove it out of business.

The North Riverfront corridor functions as an extension of two of the most historic burial grounds in the American Midwest. **Bellefontaine Cemetery**, forming part of the neighborhood’s western boundary, was established in 1849 on the former 138-acre Hempstead property as the first rural cemetery west of the Mississippi River and one of the nation’s finest examples of the 19th-century Rural Cemetery Movement. The 314-acre cemetery, listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 2014, contains more than 87,000 graves including **William Clark** of the Lewis and Clark Expedition (under a 35-foot granite obelisk near the Broadway entrance), beer barons **Adolphus Busch** (in a Bavarian Gothic mausoleum reportedly costing $250,000 in 1921 to build), **Eberhard Anheuser**, **William Lemp**, and **Joseph Griesedieck**, civil engineer **James B. Eads**, poet **Sara Teasdale**, artist **Thomas Hart Benton**, author **William S. Burroughs**, inventor **William S. Burroughs** (the author’s grandfather and namesake), **Susan Blow** (founder of the first public kindergarten in the United States at Carondelet’s Des Peres School in 1873), and the **Louis Sullivan-designed Wainwright Tomb** (architectural pilgrimage destination). The adjacent **Calvary Cemetery** contains the graves of Tennessee Williams, General William Tecumseh Sherman, and Dred Scott.

The corridor’s industrial economy traces back nearly as deep. **Wiggins Ferry**, a critical pre-bridge connection to the Illinois side of the river, departed from just south of the modern neighborhood. **Roy’s Mill**, a Spanish colonial-era grist mill, anchored the early settlement; a lead shot tower and one of America’s largest sugar refineries followed. By the late 19th century, the corridor had become one of the densest industrial zones in the American interior, linked by the **Terminal Railroad Association network** whose **Merchants Bridge** (1889, $222 million renovation completed 2022) crossed the river just south of the neighborhood. North Broadway and the streets between it and the river filled with cold storage warehouses, brake manufacturers, stamping plants, foundries, and lumber operations. Today the corridor is home to scrap yards, salvage yards, the **Dial Corporation detergent manufacturing plant**, trucking distribution facilities, an auto body shop, a school bus service company, lumber and plywood operations, the **Municipal River Terminal** (the only publicly owned port facility on the Missouri side of the Mississippi), and the **St. Louis Medium Security Prison** on Hall Street.

The neighborhood’s civic infrastructure for residents and visitors is anchored by parks and trails. The **11-mile St. Louis Riverfront Trail** (also called the Mississippi Greenway) runs from the Gateway Arch north through Near North Riverfront and along the entire length of North Riverfront to the Old Chain of Rocks Bridge, ending at North Riverfront’s northern tip. **North Riverfront Park** — the city’s largest and northernmost riverfront park at 250 acres — sits in the heart of the corridor with a stocked fishing lake, picnic shelters, and trail access. **Chain of Rocks Park** (sometimes called Blase Park), at the corridor’s northern terminus, completed a $5 million Great Rivers Greenway renovation in 2024, reopening with a new event lawn, parking, picnic pavilion, food-truck infrastructure, drinking fountains, local artwork by Simiya Sudduth highlighting the Mississippi Flyway, and a 1.5-acre reconstructed prairie. The National Park Service provided a $990,000 grant in 2021 toward the renovation. The 2020 Census recorded a neighborhood population of 154 residents living within the corridor itself — small but stable, with a 2020 racial makeup of 57.8% White, 32.5% Black, 9.7% Hispanic or Latino origin, 0.6% Asian, and 7.8% some other race.

Two active city plans govern North Riverfront’s ongoing revitalization. The **North Riverfront Commerce Corridor Land Use Plan** (adopted January 2013, covering 3,000 acres) sets the framework for industrial redevelopment. The **North Riverfront Open Space & Redevelopment Plan** (adopted December 2015) envisions the corridor from the Eads Bridge to the Stan Musial Bridge — including all of North Riverfront and Near North Riverfront — as a long-term mixed live-work-play destination linking industrial heritage, riverfront recreation, and select residential and commercial reinvestment. The 2024 Chain of Rocks Park renovation, the 2022 Merchants Bridge restoration, and ongoing Great Rivers Greenway trail improvements continue to reshape the corridor’s future while preserving the working industrial character that has defined it since the 1880s.

Image: Old Chain of Rocks Bridge at the northern end of North Riverfront — the 1929 Route 66 crossing of the Mississippi, listed on the National Register of Historic Places and reopened in 1999 as a one-mile pedestrian and bicycle bridge — Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA

North Riverfront sits at river grade along the Mississippi’s west bank — lower elevation than most St. Louis neighborhoods, fully exposed to the river’s weather patterns and seasonal extremes. The Mississippi flows here over the Chain of Rocks Reach’s submerged rock ledges, and the broad floodplain shapes the rhythm of riverfront industry. Spring flooding is a real operational consideration for businesses and infrastructure managers in the corridor; rapid water-level changes during heavy precipitation events regularly affect trail access, riverfront industrial operations, and the Mississippi Flyway migratory bird stopovers that bring 40% of all North American migrating birds through the corridor. Summers are hot and humid with the river amplifying moisture — peak season for the Old Chain of Rocks Bridge Eagle Days, Car Shows, Route 66 tours, and runs and rides organized by Great Rivers Greenway. Winters bring the city’s full continental cold with occasional ice on the trails and bridges, while late winter brings the spectacular spectacle of bald eagles wintering at the Chain of Rocks. The historic industrial buildings along North Broadway and Hall Street have weathered nearly two centuries of riverside Missouri climate with remarkable durability.

## O'Fallon — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/ofallon

Coordinates

38.6749° N, 90.2200° W

Ranked by 2,107+ Google reviews. Updated June 2026.

✓  Anchored by historic O'Fallon Park & its 1913 Kessler boathouse ✓  A north-city neighborhood named for Col. John O'Fallon; park opened 1908 ✓  Avg. rating: 4.4/5 across 2,107 reviews

## O'Fallon Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 O'Fallon, St. Louis

O'Fallon is a historically and predominantly African American neighborhood in north St. Louis City, built around the leafy 126-acre O'Fallon Park and its 1913 Kessler-designed lakeside boathouse. Roughly two-thirds of a square mile of brick and frame homes, it carries a deep streetcar-era past and the name of one of 19th-century St. Louis’s most prominent citizens.

The O'Fallon neighborhood sits in north St. Louis City, framed by **West Florissant Avenue** to the northwest, Adelaide and Harris avenues to the north, Kossuth and Natural Bridge avenues to the south, Newstead Avenue to the west, and Warne Avenue to the east. Covering about **0.59 square miles** within the city’s 11th Ward (historically the 21st), it falls inside ZIP codes 63107 and 63115 and is centered on the green expanse of O'Fallon Park.

The land here was originally part of the French-laid **Grand Prairie Commonfield**, the long farming strips that fed colonial St. Louis. By 1850 it had been subdivided among several prominent St. Louisans, among them **Colonel John O'Fallon** — a War of 1812 veteran, a nephew of the explorer William Clark, and a leading businessman and philanthropist of his era. The neighborhood and its signature park both carry his name.

Residential growth arrived with the **1890s and 1900s electric streetcar buildout**, which opened subdivisions such as O'Fallon Heights and Plymouth Park to working families. In the mid-1870s the City acquired Colonel O'Fallon’s former country estate, and in **1908 it opened O'Fallon Park** on the grounds — a roughly 126-acre municipal park that became part of the city’s Kessler-era park system. Its 5-acre lake gained an Art Deco-influenced **boathouse designed by landscape architect George Kessler and built in 1913**, the landmark restored around 2020 that still anchors the neighborhood today.

Civic and recreational life remains tied to the park. In 2013 the **O'Fallon Park Recreation Complex** opened at 4343 West Florissant, a 76,000-square-foot, city-owned facility run by the Gateway Region YMCA with an indoor pool, an indoor track, and gymnasiums. Nearby, the **Julia Davis Branch Library** — named for a distinguished African-American educator — reflects the heritage of a community that became predominantly African American during the 1950s and 1960s and remains so today (the 2020 Census recorded a population near 4,342, about 95% Black).

Just beyond the neighborhood’s edges sit two of the region’s great historic burial grounds along West Florissant — **Calvary Cemetery**, the Catholic cemetery founded in 1854, and **Bellefontaine Cemetery**, established in 1849. The **West Florissant and Natural Bridge commercial corridors** frame the area and carry most of its everyday shopping, dining, and auto services. A 2025 tornado damaged O'Fallon Park, felling roughly 300 trees, a recent reminder of the severe weather this part of the city contends with.

Housing across O'Fallon is mostly brick and frame single- and two-family homes from the streetcar era. Like much of north St. Louis, the neighborhood faces real disinvestment and vacancy challenges, even as long-tenured residents, the park, and corridor businesses keep daily life rooted. The 10 businesses below — ranked by Google rating and review volume — serve O'Fallon and the streets just around it.

The 1913 Kessler-designed boathouse on O'Fallon Park’s lake — the signature landmark of the O'Fallon neighborhood. Photo: “O'Fallon Park Boat House” by Paul Sableman, licensed under CC BY 2.0 via Wikimedia Commons.

O'Fallon shares St. Louis’s humid subtropical climate (Köppen Cfa), with warm, muggy summers and four distinct seasons. The average July high reaches about 89.6°F, and the area sees roughly 41.7 inches of precipitation a year spread across about 48 thunderstorm days, so summer afternoons in O'Fallon Park often pair sunshine with the chance of a passing storm. 

Winters are cooler but moderate for the Midwest, with an average January high near 40.4°F and roughly 16.6 inches of snow over the season. O'Fallon also lies within Tornado Alley and sees active spring severe weather — the 2025 tornado that felled hundreds of trees in the park is a vivid example — so local homes and businesses plan for storms as a normal part of the year.

## Old North St Louis — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/old-north-st-louis

Ranked by 267+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  4 of 4 are Summit Real Estate Group Managed ✓  All businesses verified via Google Business ✓  Avg. rating: 4.3/5 across 267 reviews

## Old North St. Louis Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Old North St. Louis

Old North St. Louis serves as one of the oldest and most historically layered neighborhoods on the Near North Side — anchored by Crown Candy Kitchen (1913, St. Louis’s oldest soda fountain), Marx Hardware & Paint Co. (1875, in business 150 years), the $35.5 million Crown Square historic rehabilitation, and three National Register Historic Districts — offering commercial real estate, wholesale supply, economic-development advocacy, and historic-corridor retail for residents and businesses across the Near North Side.

Old North St. Louis sits just north and slightly west of downtown at **38.6480° N, 90.2056° W** in zip code 63107. The neighborhood’s official boundaries are **Branch Street** to the north, **Cass Avenue** to the south, **Interstate 70** to the east, and **N. Florissant Avenue**, **Mullanphy Street**, and **N. 13th Street** to the west. The Mississippi River was the original eastern boundary; when I-70 was built through the city, it divided the residential west side from the commercial and manufacturing east side. The neighborhood’s primary commercial corridor is **N. 14th Street**, which has served as the neighborhood’s main street for nearly two centuries, with **St. Louis Avenue**, **Hadley**, **Palm**, and **Blair** completing the network of historic routes. Old North is less than a mile from the Gateway Arch — close enough that the Arch is visible from the Fourteenth Street Mall on a clear day, yet far enough in character and history to feel like an entirely different city.

Old North’s history began with its founding as the independent **Village of North St. Louis in 1816** — making it one of the oldest platted neighborhoods in the entire city. The original 1816 plat included three circular plots designated for a church, a park, and a school — an unusual and remarkably progressive civic design for frontier Missouri. One of those original circular plats, **Jackson Place Park**, still exists today as the second-oldest green space in St. Louis. The village was annexed by the City of St. Louis in 1841 when the city’s limits expanded to the foot of Dock Street, and it grew rapidly through subdivisions by north-city landowners like **William C. Christy, John J. O’Fallon, William C. Carr, and John Mullanphy** through the 1850s. The 1848 Thomas Wright Estate subdivision extended the village westward, and the 1850 Union Addition by O’Fallon platted streets westward to Jefferson Avenue and northward to Hebert Street.

By the 1850s, Old North St. Louis was the most densely populated neighborhood in all of St. Louis, driven by successive waves of immigrants — first German Catholics around 11th and Biddle Streets, then Irish, then Italian, Polish, and Jewish. The neighborhood’s most legendary enclave was the **Kerry Patch**, founded in 1842 when a group of Irish immigrants from County Kerry settled on a stretch of open land near 18th and O’Fallon Streets. The land was owned by the heirs of **John Mullanphy** — said to be the richest man in the Mississippi River Valley when he died in 1833 — whose sympathetic Irish-descended family chose to ignore the squatters. The immigrants built small makeshift shanties along no particular street pattern, and over the next 80 years the Patch produced silent-film star **King Baggot**, **William Marion Reedy** (editor of Reedy’s Mirror, publisher of Tennessee Williams, T. S. Eliot, and Theodore Dreiser), crime bosses and pickpockets, and a series of elected “kings” including Dennis Sheehan, James Cullinane, and Jack Sheehan. By the 1930s, brick houses had replaced most of the Patch’s wooden shacks, and the Patch as a distinct enclave faded.

The neighborhood’s built environment from this immigrant era remains its defining feature. Old North has the most buildings constructed with **limestone window lintels** of any neighborhood in St. Louis. The 1880s and 1890s filled in the northern part of the neighborhood with **Second Empire mansions** — the French-inspired style with sloped, fish-scale mansard roofs — alongside multifamily buildings and brick row houses. Three separate National Register Historic Districts document this legacy: the **Mullanphy**, **Murphy Blair**, and **SS. Cyril & Methodius** districts, all established in the 1980s. **Saints Cyril and Methodius Polish National Catholic Church**, whose 1857 building is individually listed on the National Register, anchors one of the three districts. The **Mullanphy Emigrant Home** at 1609 N. 14th Street — built in 1867 with funds from the estate of **Bryan Mullanphy** (son of John, who bequeathed money to the city through the “Mullanphy Traveller’s Aid Fund” to help newly arrived immigrants) — provided short-term housing to thousands of post-Civil War immigrants and survives today after years of neglect and storm damage, rescued by the Old North St. Louis Restoration Group from demolition.

The neighborhood that endured mid-century decline with the most grace is often the one with the deepest roots — and nothing in Old North illustrates that better than **Crown Candy Kitchen**. Opened in 1913 by Greek and Macedonian immigrants **Harry Karandzieff and Pete Jugaloff** at the corner of St. Louis Avenue and N. 14th Street, Crown Candy is the oldest soda fountain in St. Louis and now in its fourth generation of Karandzieff family ownership under current owner Andy Karandzieff. The interior is an intact Victorian-era confection counter — pressed tin ceilings, vintage bar stools, and glass cases of house-made chocolates — famous for its malts, its BLT sandwiches (the kitchen fries roughly 22 tons of bacon a year), and the legendary **five-malts-in-30-minutes challenge** that has been a neighborhood institution for decades. Customers regularly drive 30 miles for lunch. **Marx Hardware & Paint Co.**, established in 1875 and originally at 14th and Warren Street before moving one block to its current 14th and Benton Streets location in 1891 (when Henry Marx, a 1887 hire, bought the store in 1903), remains in family ownership 150 years later as the oldest continuously operating business in Old North and one of the oldest hardware stores in St. Louis. When the 1974 pedestrian mall experiment along N. 14th Street backfired and accelerated business departures, Crown Candy Kitchen and Marx Hardware were two of only a handful of businesses that stayed. They are still there.

The neighborhood’s revival is anchored by the **Old North St. Louis Restoration Group (ONSLRG)**, founded in 1981, which has been the primary community development engine for more than four decades. ONSLRG’s signature achievement is **Crown Square** — the $35.5 million rehabilitation of 27 historic buildings completed in 2010 in partnership with RISE Community Development and the Regional Housing & Community Development Alliance. Financed through New Markets Tax Credits, Historic Tax Credits, Low-Income Housing Tax Credits, HOME funds, CDBG, and a Surface Transportation Enhancement Grant, the project converted the failed Fourteenth Street Mall into **80 mixed-income rental units and 33,000 square feet of retail space**. It won the **HUD Secretary’s Award for Excellence in Historic Preservation**. ONSLRG also operates the **13th Street Community Garden** (a half-acre volunteer-run garden on a former factory lot opened in 2019 that hosts the North City Farmers’ Market), the **Chess Pocket Park** adjacent to Crown Square (created 2010), and ongoing rehabilitation of a 30-acre area along North Market Street and Monroe Avenue that will eventually include more than 100 new historically sensitive homes alongside dozens of rehabilitated historic buildings.

Beyond Crown Square, Old North’s civic infrastructure includes **Municipal Bath House No. 6** (1937, the last of six city bath houses originally built to serve St. Louis’s tenement neighborhoods), **Jackson Place Park** (the 200-year-old circular plat from the 1816 founding), the **Zuka Arts Guild** (open Friday evenings for music and poetry), **Central Print** (bookbinding and printmaking classes), and a growing roster of art galleries, boutiques, and creative studios along the mural-covered walls of N. 14th Street. **Patrick Henry Downtown Academy** — the neighborhood’s elementary school — won a National Green Ribbon School award for its school garden, and **Vashon High School** (Niche A−) won both boys’ and girls’ state basketball championships in 2023. The 2020 Census recorded a neighborhood that has been steadily diversifying: from 78% Black in 2010 to roughly 50% Black and 41% White by 2015, reflecting the rehab-driven demographic shift that ONSLRG and homeowner-led restoration have brought to the area north of St. Louis Avenue.

The neighborhood remains in active recovery, with empty lots, fallow fields, and crumbling structures still present alongside the restored Victorian row houses and the new construction. Some of the larger institutional projects — including Paul McKee’s NorthSide Regeneration developments and the Bristol Place and Murphy Blair apartment complexes — have drawn criticism for not fitting the neighborhood’s historic fabric. But what defines Old North today is the rare combination of two-century-old civic infrastructure, multi-generational family businesses, citizen-led restoration, and an active artist-and-entrepreneur scene anchored by Crown Square. As Andy Karandzieff of Crown Candy puts it: “This is an area for people who want to live in old, historical buildings and want to restore these buildings.”

Image: Inside Crown Candy Kitchen, Old North St. Louis — open since 1913 and now in its fourth generation of Karandzieff family ownership — photo by Marcus Qwertyus — Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 3.0

Old North sits on gently elevated terrain in north-central St. Louis, just inside the I-70 corridor that forms its eastern edge and less than a mile from the Mississippi River. The four-season Midwest climate shapes the rhythm of the neighborhood’s historic blocks and N. 14th Street commercial corridor. Hot, humid summers with highs in the upper 80s°F bring peak activity to the North City Farmers’ Market at the 13th Street Community Garden, the Crown Square pedestrian zone, and the patios along Crown Candy Kitchen’s block. Cool springs and falls are the busiest seasons for ONSLRG-led historic walking tours, Zuka Arts Guild Friday-evening events, and rehab projects across the three National Register districts. Cold winters with periodic snow shift commerce indoors to Crown Candy’s pressed-tin-ceiling counter, Marx Hardware’s 150-year-old aisles, and the small storefronts of Crown Square. The historic density of brick construction along N. 14th Street and the surrounding residential blocks provides a built environment that has weathered two centuries of Missouri weather with remarkable durability.

## Patch Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Patch, St. Louis

Patch serves as the southernmost neighborhood of the City of St. Louis — the riverfront tip of historic Carondelet anchored by St. Boniface Catholic Church (now the Ivory Theatre), Doering’s Carondelet Bakery (in business since 1875), and three National Register historic districts — offering custom manufacturing, B2B consulting, coworking, event services, and small-business support for residents and businesses across the south St. Louis riverfront.

Patch sits at **38.5715° N, 90.2398° W** in zip code 63111, occupying the southernmost tip of the City of St. Louis along the Mississippi River. The neighborhood’s official boundaries are **Robert Avenue** on the north, the **City Limits** on the south, the **Mississippi River** on the east, and **Alabama Avenue** on the west. City planning descriptions trace the northern boundary across Robert Avenue, Virginia Avenue, South Broadway, and East Nagel Avenue, accounting for Patch’s slightly irregular shape. Covering approximately 0.9 square miles, Patch is one of the smallest of the city’s 79 official neighborhoods, with a 2020 population of 2,842 — a 5% increase from 2010 and one of the few St. Louis neighborhoods to gain residents during that decade. The neighborhood forms the southern tip of historic Carondelet, and many residents and visitors still consider the two as one community.

The land that became Patch was originally part of **Carondelet Village**, founded in 1767 by French settler **Clément Delor de Treget** on a 6,000-acre Spanish land grant. Originally called Louisbourg in honor of King Louis XVI and nicknamed **“Vide Poche”** (folk-translated “empty pocket,” reflecting the friendly poverty of its early inhabitants), the village was renamed Carondelet in 1794 to honor Baron de Carondelet, governor-general of Spanish Louisiana. Patch itself sat within **Carondelet Commons North** — a tract of shared grazing land north of the River des Peres set aside for the use of all villagers. After the Louisiana Purchase brought the village into American hands in 1803, Carondelet was incorporated as a town in 1832, became a city in 1851, and was annexed by St. Louis in 1870. Patch was carved out as a distinct neighborhood within Carondelet’s southern tip and now stands on its own among the 79 official neighborhoods of the City of St. Louis.

German immigration transformed the area beginning in the 1840s. In 1843, German immigrant **Joseph Stein** acquired a lot at what is now the corner of Steins and Reilly Streets and built a large stone house that same year — a structure still standing and listed as a St. Louis City Landmark. Stein was so impressed with the land that he convinced many other Germans to settle in the area, and his surname now graces **Steins Street**, joined by other German-derived street names like **Koeln, Espenschied, Schirmer, and Polk**. The Irish followed in the 1850s and settled in what became known as the Patch, working in the riverfront industries. By the late 19th century, the neighborhood’s identity was deeply tied to its German Catholic and Irish immigrant working-class roots, the riverfront economy, and the Carondelet shipyards.

During the Civil War, Patch’s riverfront position gave it national significance. At the **Union Marine Works shipyards** near the confluence of the Mississippi and River des Peres — owned by engineer **James Buchanan Eads** — 32 ironclad gunboats were built for the Union Army and Navy. Among them was the **USS Carondelet**, launched in 1862, which became one of the most-engaged Union ironclads of the war. Several Civil War-era stone houses still stand in Patch today, including the **Henry Zeiss houses on Vulcan Street** (built in the 1850s by German stonemasons known as “Steins”) and the **Anton Schmitt House** from 1859, since relocated for preservation.

Patch contains **three National Register Historic Districts**, each documenting a different chapter of the neighborhood’s story. The **Steins Street District** (designated 1980) showcases the 1851 Steins Street Row Houses and other early German stone construction. The **St. Boniface District** (designated 2002, bounded by Koeln, Tesson, Broadway, and Alabama) is anchored by **St. Boniface Catholic Church** — a Romanesque Revival church built by German Catholics beginning in the early 1860s and completed in 1890 with the addition of its final tower. The parish closed in 2005, and the restored church now serves as the **Ivory Theatre**, the neighborhood’s primary cultural venue. The **Central Carondelet District** (designated 2006, expanded 2007 and 2009; bounded by Koeln, Loughborough, Broadway, and Alabama) ties together the heart of the old village. Together, these three districts preserve more than 150 years of layered building history.

Patch’s civic and cultural landmarks include the **Joseph Otzenberger House** (1858, at Reilly and Primm Streets), the **Carlin Rathgerber-Krauss House** (1848), **Firehouse No. 34** (1895, one of the oldest fire houses in St. Louis), and the **Carondelet School** (1877, restored and now home to Grace Hill Head Start). **Doering’s Carondelet Bakery** has operated continuously on Schirmer Street since 1875 — one of the oldest continuously operating bakeries in St. Louis. **Ivory Triangle Park**, the small green wedge where horses were once tied while their owners shopped at Ivory and Schirmer Street stores, now features a bronze bust of longtime Alderman **Albert “Red” Villa** sculpted by **Bob Cassilly** (founder of the City Museum), and an old horse watering trough has been converted into a fountain. **South Public Park** and **Patch Park** provide neighborhood green space, and the **Sisters of St. Joseph of Carondelet** — whose 1836 founding in Carondelet seeded institutions including Fontbonne University, St. Joseph Institute for the Deaf, and St. Joseph’s Academy — still maintain their international headquarters at the historic Carondelet motherhouse just to the north.

Beyond its boundary streets, the Patch street network includes familiar south St. Louis routes such as **South Broadway, Minnesota, Pennsylvania, Steins, Davis, Marceau, Vulcan, Reilly, Schirmer, and Primm**. Most of the neighborhood’s housing dates from the mid-19th to early 20th centuries: an eclectic mix of stone houses, brick rowhomes, modest bungalows, and occasional larger residences laid out in a traditional grid pattern. The neighborhood divides naturally into a residential western half and a heavily industrial eastern half along the Mississippi River, where the former **Carondelet Coke plant** site has been repurposed as the **River City Business Park**, and Anheuser-Busch beer kegs have historically been moved through the riverfront industrial corridor.

Today, Patch retains a strong south-side identity even as it slowly reinvests. Public neighborhood guides describe a mix of homes roughly a century old alongside signs of renovation and newer business activity in recent years. Long-time residents remain a defining part of the neighborhood’s character while newer homeowners and small businesses gradually reshape its blocks. Patch’s riverfront position, three historic districts, German-stone-masonry heritage, and Civil War shipbuilding history together give it one of the deepest and most distinctive identities of any St. Louis neighborhood — one whose name remains uniquely its own among the city’s 79 official neighborhoods.

Image: Streetscape view in the Patch neighborhood, south St. Louis — photo by Onegentlemanofverona — Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 3.0

Patch sits at the southernmost edge of St. Louis where the Mississippi River meets the city limits, with the four-season Midwest climate shaping the rhythm of the neighborhood’s residential blocks and riverfront industry. Hot, humid summers with highs in the upper 80s°F bring peak activity to Ivory Triangle Park, South Public Park, and the patios along South Broadway. Cool springs and falls are the busiest seasons for Ivory Theatre performances, Doering’s Carondelet Bakery’s 150-year holiday baking tradition, and the historic-district walking tours that connect Patch with greater Carondelet. Cold winters with periodic snow shift commerce indoors to the bakery, the Ivory Theatre, and the small storefronts along South Broadway. Because the neighborhood hugs the Mississippi River, residents and businesses also remain attentive to spring high-water levels, with portions of the riverfront industrial corridor occasionally affected during major flood years like 2019.

While the organization has undergone structural changes and mergers in recent years, it remains a significant name in the city’s professional history. It is a strong fit for established businesses looking to engage in regional advocacy and large-scale economic initiatives.

**Years in Business:** Legacy organization (merged 2021)

**Service Radius:** St. Louis Region

**Licensed & Insured:** Yes, non-profit association

## Peabody Darst Webbe — St. Louis Neighborhood

Ranked by 484+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

## Peabody Darst Webbe Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Peabody Darst Webbe, St. Louis

Peabody Darst Webbe serves as a compact “Near Southside” neighborhood of St. Louis — anchored by The Georgian Condominiums at the historic City Hospital site, the A.T. Still University Missouri School of Dentistry & Oral Health, and the 350-unit Clinton-Peabody mixed-income redevelopment — offering B2B marketing, beverage distribution, historic condominium living, affordable housing, and professional services for residents and businesses across the south St. Louis corridor.

Peabody Darst Webbe sits just south of downtown St. Louis at **38.6065° N, 90.2073° W** in zip code 63104. The neighborhood’s official boundaries are **Chouteau Avenue** on the north, **South Tucker Boulevard / 12th Street** on the east, the **I-55 / I-44 interchange** on the south, and **Dolman Street** on the west. Also known historically as the **Near Southside**, the neighborhood is hemmed in by Lafayette Square to the west, LaSalle Park to the east, Downtown West to the north, and Soulard and McKinley Heights to the south. As of the 2020 Census, the neighborhood’s racial makeup was 79.7% Black, 15.3% White, 3.6% two or more races, 0.8% Asian, and 1.6% Hispanic or Latino — reflecting a 65-resident gain and a substantial 52-unit drop in vacancy since 2010, when the population had already grown 61 percent from the 2000 count.

The neighborhood’s identity is inseparable from three mid-20th-century public housing projects whose names it still bears. **Clinton-Peabody**, built in 1942 under the New Deal Housing Act of 1937, was the first generation of St. Louis public housing — a low-rise development that opened as the segregated white-tenant sibling to **Carr Square Village** on the Near North Side, which was built for Black families. The **Joseph M. Darst Apartments** followed in 1956 (four nine-story towers, 645 units), named for Mayor Joseph Darst (1949–1953), the same St. Louis mayor whose urban-renewal vision also produced the infamous Pruitt-Igoe. The **Anthony M. Webbe Apartments** opened in 1961 (two nine-story towers, one twelve-story, one eight-story, 578 units). Designed by the famed firm of **Leinweber, Yamasaki & Hellmuth** — led by architect Minoru Yamasaki, who would later design Pruitt-Igoe and the original World Trade Center — the Darst-Webbe complex was built on the grounds of a 1940s-cleared near-south neighborhood between Soulard and Lafayette Square.

Like Pruitt-Igoe, Darst-Webbe declined quickly under chronic underfunding by the St. Louis Housing Authority — elevators failed, sanitation lapsed, and by the 1990s the high-rises were widely regarded as failures of mid-century housing policy. **The Darst-Webbe apartments were demolished in 1999**, and the site was replaced with the current two-story, mixed-income brick properties — designed to echo the older brick rowhomes of Soulard and Lafayette Square — that define the neighborhood’s residential streets today. The neighborhood also contains a small number of Italianate-style rowhomes (some dating to the turn of the 20th century, others recent infill) plus a corridor along Park Avenue that has seen new construction in the years since the demolitions.

The redevelopment story continues today with the **Clinton-Peabody Apartments** — St. Louis City’s oldest public housing development, built in 1942. In 2023, residents voted overwhelmingly to demolish the existing buildings and replace them with a **$150 million POAH-led redevelopment** creating 350 new mixed-income apartments and affordable homes, centered around a reconfigured street grid, a renovated community center, and a new central park. Demolition began in 2025; current residents were relocated to refreshed on-site units in 2023 and 2024; and the first wave of new units broke ground in early 2026. The 10-year project is expected to fundamentally reshape the western half of the neighborhood through the early 2030s.

Anchoring the eastern side of the neighborhood is the **City Hospital Historic District**, listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 2001. The site has served continuously as a public-health landmark since the city built its first hospital here in 1846, in response to a cholera outbreak. The original wooden complex was destroyed by fire in 1856; rebuilt the next year; then leveled by the “Great Cyclone” tornado of 1896 — the third-deadliest tornado in U.S. history. The current **Georgian Revival complex**, designed by famed St. Louis architect **Albert B. Groves** of Grable, Weber & Groves, was completed in 1912 on the same 10-acre site at 1515 Lafayette Avenue. Under Dr. Max C. Starkloff — the St. Louis City Health Commissioner whose 1918–19 Spanish flu protocols are still studied today — the hospital became a state-of-the-art institution and was renamed the Max Starkloff Memorial Hospital after his death. The complex grew to 18 buildings, included a power plant and laundry building, and operated continuously until it closed in 1985.

After two decades of abandonment, the City Hospital site was successfully redeveloped. In 2006, the 1912 Administration Building reopened as **The Georgian Condominiums** — 102 loft-style residences with high ceilings, hardwood floors, gas fireplaces, walk-in closets, and 360-degree views of downtown St. Louis. The old **Power Plant** became Climb So iLL, an indoor climbing gym; the **Laundry Building** (a 1940 Public Works Administration project) became Palladium St. Louis, a private event space. In 2014, **A.T. Still University’s Missouri School of Dentistry & Oral Health** broke ground on the former Malcolm Bliss Psychopathological Institute site — a 79,000-square-foot dental school that opened in 2013 and now serves as ATSU’s third campus after Kirksville, Missouri, and Mesa, Arizona. By 2018, redevelopment had moved on to the remaining vacant buildings — the Commissioner’s Building (1907), Commissary (1940), Ambulance Garage (1921), and Clinic Building (1921) — under Pearl Companies of Indianapolis with design by Trivers Associates.

The neighborhood’s civic infrastructure includes **Peabody Academy** (built 1957 on 14th Street, the St. Louis Public Schools system’s only school in the neighborhood, serving Kindergarten through 8th grade), the **Al Chappelle Community Center** (a St. Louis Housing Authority operation with public meeting rooms), **Mestres Park** (a 3-acre linear park parallel to the neighborhood), and the adjacent **Ray Leisure Park** with its **12th & Park Recreation Center** (gymnasium, multipurpose courts, and indoor pool). The neighborhood’s most visible amenity remains the proximity to **Lafayette Park** — established in 1851 as the city’s first public park — just to the west, with its biweekly summer concert series, fall festival, and historic Victorian-era surroundings.

Today Peabody Darst Webbe sits at one of the most strategic transit positions in St. Louis: surrounded by I-44, I-55, and I-64, two miles east of SSM Health Saint Louis University Hospital, and 16 miles south of Lambert St. Louis International Airport. The neighborhood’s mix of historic condominium living, ongoing affordable-housing redevelopment, brick infill homes, and B2B professional services makes it a uniquely transitional district — one whose blocks reflect every major chapter of 20th- and 21st-century St. Louis housing policy, from the New Deal’s first public housing experiments to today’s mixed-income reinvestment.

Image: City Hospital Historic District (now The Georgian Condominiums), 1515 Lafayette Avenue, Peabody Darst Webbe, St. Louis — designed by Albert B. Groves, completed 1912 — Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 3.0

## Penrose — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/penrose

Coordinates

38.6815° N, 90.2578° W

Ranked by 164+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  10 of 10 are North Spring Empowerment Center Partner ✓  All businesses verified via Google Business ✓  Avg. rating: 4.9/5 across 164 reviews

## Penrose Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Penrose, St. Louis

Penrose serves as a stable residential and small-business neighborhood on the Near North Side of St. Louis — anchored by the 51.22-acre Penrose Park, the historic Penrose Park Velodrome (one of only 27 in the United States), and the Heartland St. Louis Black Chamber of Commerce at the North Spring Empowerment Center — offering chamber advocacy, telecom infrastructure, general contracting, mold remediation, screen printing manufacturing, industrial packaging, chemical distribution, and wholesale tire supply for residents, contractors, and Near North Side businesses across the metro.

Penrose sits on the north side of St. Louis at **38.6815° N, 90.2578° W** in zip code 63115, just south of I-70 and west of O’Fallon Park. The neighborhood’s official boundaries are **Florissant Avenue and I-70** on the north, **Natural Bridge Avenue** on the south, **North Newstead and Pope Avenues** on the east, and **Kingshighway Boulevard** on the west. The neighborhood is split between the city’s 21st Ward and 1st Ward, and as of the 2020 Census, Penrose had 5,243 residents — 95.3% Black, 0.9% White, 2.8% two or more races, with 0.8% Hispanic or Latino origin. Familiar residential streets like **Penrose, Euclid, Shreve, Lee, Margaretta, and Steinlage Drive** tie the area together, with Natural Bridge, West Florissant, and Kingshighway carrying most of the commercial activity.

Penrose’s name comes not from a settler but from a speculator: in the early 19th century, the area was characterized more by land speculation than actual settlement. The neighborhood takes its name from **Clement B. Penrose**, whom **Thomas Jefferson** appointed land commissioner in 1805 to settle disputed Louisiana Purchase land claims. Penrose lived on a nearby estate and was one of the region’s prime land investors. Another early landowner was the Kentucky statesman **Henry Clay**, a shaper of the Missouri Compromise of 1820 — making the Penrose ground one of the few St. Louis neighborhoods to claim a direct connection to two major figures of the early American republic.

Penrose did not take on the contours of a community until the late 1880s, when **farmers and dairymen, most of German heritage**, moved their families into the area. By 1900, the community — though still rural — was sufficiently settled to boast two German churches: **St. Engelbert’s Catholic Church** and **Salem German Evangelical Church**. The 1920s brought commercial development and transit lines, raising land values around the northern edges of the city, and most of Penrose’s subdivisions date from that era when single-family brick homes sprang up along Euclid, Shreve, and Lee Avenues. Some subdivisions were the work of outside investors; others were developed by long-time residents — a prime example being the area around **Steinlage Drive**, a street named for a local German family in the dairy business whose properties were subdivided for housing.

The early 1960s were years of transition for Penrose. As older residents moved out, **middle- and upper-income African-American families** moved in. The well-constructed brick houses were ideal for moderate-sized families, offering many their first opportunity for home ownership. Teachers, nurses, and city and government employees made Penrose their new home — a significant number coming from **The Ville**, located to the southeast and long the city’s premier African-American neighborhood. As Penrose’s African-American population grew from 33 percent in 1960 to 95 percent in 1970, African-American institutions helped solidify the neighborhood: **St. Peter’s A.M.E. Church**, a historic African Methodist Episcopal congregation, relocated to the corner of Shreve and Margaretta in 1962, and in 1974 the **Julia Davis Branch Library** — named to honor the well-known St. Louis educator — opened on Natural Bridge Avenue. Penrose developed into a stable African-American middle-class neighborhood, though residents were long subjected to **redlining** by financial institutions because of the neighborhood’s location north of Delmar.

One of the standout features of Penrose is the **51.22-acre Penrose Park**, proposed in the city’s landmark 1907 comprehensive plan by the “Inner and Outer Parks Committee” — whose members included George Kessler and Julius Pitzman, the same Forest Park master planner who would later plat the Central West End’s Westmoreland and Portland Places. Established in 1910, the park is bisected by North Bircher and Kingshighway Boulevards and east-west oriented train tracks. Its amenities include ball fields, basketball and handball courts, a softball field, tennis court, playground, pavilions with BBQ grills, a jogging path with fitness stations — and the centerpiece, the **Penrose Park Velodrome**: a banked outdoor cycling track measuring 1/5 of a mile long with a 28-degree bank, one of only 27 velodromes in the United States and the only one in Missouri.

The Penrose Park Velodrome has a remarkable origin story. When highway officials announced in 1958 that they would expand U.S. Highway 40 along the southern edge of Forest Park — razing the city’s 1930s-built Forest Park bicycle track in the process — the City of St. Louis donated land adjacent to Penrose Park for a replacement velodrome. Built in 1962 and designed by **Olympic cyclist and Schwinn Bicycle Company designer Frank Burlando**, the new Penrose Park Velodrome hosted the prestigious **U.S. National Track Cycling Championships** in its inaugural year. A sewer collapse in the 1970s left the track with the affectionate nickname “**Mr. Bumpy Face**”; resurfacings in 1984 and 2005 followed, and in 2019 the track was rebuilt with a brand-new concrete surface that restored it to championship caliber. Open dawn to dusk, 365 days a year, the velodrome remains a regional cycling destination and a defining feature of Penrose.

Beyond Penrose Park, the neighborhood’s civic infrastructure includes the **St. Elizabeth Mother of John the Baptist Catholic Church** on Shreve Avenue (operated by the Roman Catholic Archdiocese of St. Louis), the historic **St. Peter’s A.M.E. Church** on Margaretta Avenue, the **Julia Davis Branch Library** on Natural Bridge, and the small neighborhood park **Eugene “Tink” Bradley Park** (3.18 acres, established 1958). The neighborhood’s housing stock remains predominantly classic North St. Louis brick — many homes built during the 1920s boom on Euclid, Shreve, Lee, and surrounding side streets — with deep front yards, front porches, and mature street trees that give Penrose a traditional St. Louis feel. Economically, Penrose is largely residential but closely tied to the broader north-city business network along I-70, Natural Bridge, and West Florissant, giving many residents short commutes to industrial, logistics, health care, and public sector jobs.

Image: Residential Modern Infill, North Newstead Avenue, St. Louis — photo by Paul Sableman — Wikimedia Commons, CC BY 2.0

Penrose sits on the level upland of the Near North Side, where the four-season Midwest climate shapes the rhythm of the neighborhood’s residential blocks and small commercial corridors. Hot, humid summers with highs in the upper 80s°F bring peak activity to Penrose Park — the velodrome’s Thursday-night racing series runs from May through August, and the ball fields, handball courts, and pavilions stay busy with church picnics, family reunions, and youth leagues. Cool springs and falls are the best seasons for biking, fitness-station walking, and the neighborhood-organization meetings that have defined Penrose’s civic life since the early 1970s. Winters are chilly with occasional light snow that dusts the brick rooftops and the velodrome berms, shifting commerce indoors to the chambers of commerce, churches, and small storefronts along Natural Bridge, West Florissant, and Kingshighway. Residents are used to quick weather swings, from pop-up thunderstorms in growing season to the occasional snow squall.

## Princeton Heights — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/princeton-heights

Neighborhood

Princeton Heights

Coordinates

38.5746° N, 90.2961° W

Ranked by 153+ Google reviews. Updated June 2026.

✓  Anchored by the historic Macklind Avenue Business District ✓  1920s brick-bungalow streetcar suburb, platted as Princeton Place in 1906 ✓  Avg. rating: 4.9/5 across 153 reviews

## Princeton Heights Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Princeton Heights, St. Louis

Princeton Heights is a quiet, walkable south St. Louis neighborhood of early-1900s brick bungalows, anchored by the lively Macklind Avenue Business District and bordered by Hampton, Eichelberger, Christy, and Gravois.

Princeton Heights sits in south St. Louis, bounded by **Hampton Avenue** on the west, **Eichelberger Street** on the north, **Christy Boulevard** on the east, and **Gravois Avenue** on the south. It covers roughly **0.83 square miles** and falls within the city’s Wards 2 and 5.

Long before the brick streets arrived, this was rural farmland known as **Gardenville**, and one 1860s Moellenhoff-family farmhouse still stands on Loughborough as a reminder of those days. The neighborhood name has two roots: the historic **Princeton Creamery** that once operated on Kingshighway near Gravois, and the **“Princeton Place” subdivision platted in 1906** — the “Heights” nodding to the gentle rise up from the River Des Peres.

**Gravois Road was the first road paved by the State of Missouri**, and streetcar lines soon followed it south. By around 1920, nearly all of the old farms had been developed, with the main buildout coming in the **1920s and 1930s**. Many of the earliest homeowners were **German** families, and a number of street names were later Anglicized after the World Wars.

The housing stock is the defining feature of Princeton Heights. Block after block is lined with **1920s and 1930s brick Craftsman bungalows, “storybook” gingerbread houses, and American Foursquares** — the kind of sturdy, gabled brick homes that give south city its character. The resident-run **Princeton Heights Neighborhood Association (PHNA)**, a 501(c)(3) **incorporated in 1976**, helps look after those streets, organizes the neighborhood, and keeps residents connected.

Daily life centers on the **Macklind Avenue Business District**, a compact, walkable strip of locally owned restaurants, cafés, and shops that promotes the Southampton and Princeton Heights area together. Just off the southeast edge sits **Christy Park**, a roughly 16-acre city park founded in 1910 and named for brick-industry businessman William Tandy Christy; its **Christy Greenway** connects toward Carondelet Park. Along the south and southwest edge, the **River Des Peres Greenway and Christy Greenway** (part of Great Rivers Greenway) offer adjacent trails for walking and biking.

A few landmarks border the neighborhood without sitting squarely inside it. **Our Lady of Sorrows** parish, at the Kingshighway edge, was founded in 1907 with a school that opened in 1908 — a longtime heritage anchor, though the **parish closed in 2023**. Just northwest, across Hampton Avenue, lie **St. Louis Hills and Francis Park**, popular adjacent destinations that many Princeton Heights residents treat as part of their extended backyard.

Princeton Heights is known for its early-1900s brick ‘gabled’ bungalows on quiet, tree-lined streets. Photo: “Princeton Heights” by Paul Sableman, licensed under CC BY 2.0 via Wikimedia Commons.

Princeton Heights shares St. Louis’s humid subtropical climate (Köppen Cfa), which means warm, sticky summers and cool, changeable winters. The average July high runs about 89.6°F while the average January high sits near 40.4°F, and the area sees roughly 41.7 inches of rain and about 16.6 inches of snow in a typical year. With around 48 thunderstorm days annually and a spot inside Tornado Alley, spring can bring active severe weather, so local homes and businesses keep an eye on the forecast. 

What residents notice day to day is the mature tree canopy arching over the brick-lined streets, which softens the summer heat and turns the neighborhood gold and red each fall. Those big shade trees, paired with the solid brick housing stock, give Princeton Heights a comfortable, lived-in feel through every season — from leafy summer evenings on the porch to crisp, quiet winter mornings.

## Riverview — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/riverview

Coordinates

38.7600° N, 90.1980° W

Ranked by 1,932+ Google reviews. Updated June 2026.

✓  Anchored by the Old Chain of Rocks Bridge & Mississippi riverfront ✓  Riverview Drive dates to the 1830s Columbia Bottoms road ✓  Avg. rating: 4.3/5 across 1,932 reviews

## Riverview Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Riverview, St. Louis

Riverview is a far-north St. Louis riverfront area strung along Riverview Drive at the Chain of Rocks, where the Mississippi bends past historic water-intake towers and the Old Chain of Rocks Bridge — a stretch of the city defined by its river, its 19th-century waterworks, and a working corridor of independent businesses.

This guide covers primarily the City of St. Louis “Riverview” neighborhood, which sits at the far-northern edge of the city along Riverview Drive near the Chain of Rocks. For accuracy, note that there is also a separately incorporated Village of Riverview just to the north in St. Louis County — a distinct community of roughly 2,397 residents that was incorporated in 1950 and voted in November 2023 to become a fourth-class city. This is not the same as Riverview Gardens, a different area nearby. The city neighborhood itself is small, with about 242 residents recorded in the 2020 census and a population that is roughly 70 percent Black.

The story of the area begins with **Riverview Drive** itself. Around 1830 it took shape as the “Columbia Bottoms road,” a farm-to-market route that ran through north St. Louis County and down through Baden to the city market. Residential building along its southern end began in the early 1870s and picked up again in the early 1890s, a wave that coincided with construction of the new Chain of Rocks waterworks. That road, now lined with shops, distributors, and small industrial firms, is still the spine of the neighborhood today.

Water shaped Riverview as much as the road did. The **Chain of Rocks site** was chosen for the St. Louis waterworks in 1865; construction began in 1887, and the plant went into service in 1894. A 1915 filter-plant addition was, at the time, the largest in the world, and the facility is still operational today — recognized as a historic civil-engineering landmark by the American Society of Civil Engineers. Out in the Mississippi, the castle-like **Chain of Rocks water intake towers** still stand; Tower #1, built in 1894 in a Romanesque Revival style, remains in use by the St. Louis Water Works. The connected **Chain of Rocks Water Treatment Plant**, established in 1894, is known for award-winning tap water drawn from the river.

The neighborhood’s most famous landmark is the **Old Chain of Rocks Bridge**. Opened in 1929 and stretching about 5,353 feet across the Mississippi, it once carried Bypass Route 66 and is known for the dramatic 22-degree bend at its middle, where the span turns mid-river. It closed to car traffic on February 25, 1970, and reopened in 1998 as a pedestrian and bicycle bridge. Today it serves as the northern point of the **Mississippi Greenway / Riverfront Trail**, a riverside path that runs about 12 miles toward downtown St. Louis.

The riverfront has seen new investment as well. **Chain of Rocks Park**, a 16-acre park at roughly 10840 Riverview Drive, reopened in April 2024 through Great Rivers Greenway. It offers a river overlook with access to the bridge, a pavilion, and a restored prairie, giving residents and visitors a place to take in the Mississippi up close. The area also carries a lighter slice of local history: the former **Chain of Rocks Amusement Park** operated nearby from 1927 to 1978 and, though long gone, is still remembered as part of the neighborhood’s recreational past.

Taken together, these landmarks — the 1830s farm-to-market road, the historic bridge, the river intake towers, the working treatment plant, the new riverfront park, the long greenway trail, and the memory of the old amusement park — give Riverview a character rooted firmly in the Mississippi. The businesses listed below operate along this corridor, serving both the local residents and the regional clients who pass through this far-north stretch of St. Louis.

The historic Old Chain of Rocks Bridge spans the Mississippi at the northern edge of St. Louis, beside Riverview. Photo: “Chain of Rocks Bridge” by Marcin Wichary, licensed under CC BY 2.0 via Wikimedia Commons.

St. Louis sits in a humid climate zone (Köppen Cfa), and Riverview feels it fully. The average July high runs near 89.6°F while the average January high is about 40.4°F, with an annual average around 57°F. The metro typically sees roughly 41.7 inches of precipitation and about 16.6 inches of snow each year, along with some 48 thunderstorm days. Because Riverview sits directly on the Mississippi, summer humidity is amplified near the water, and the low riverfront ground faces periodic flooding — the Great Flood of 1993 remains the local benchmark for how high the river can rise. 

The wider region lies in Tornado Alley, so spring and summer bring frequent and sometimes severe storms. For homeowners and businesses along Riverview Drive, that means planning around the seasons: durable, water-resistant materials and storm-aware scheduling matter here more than in many inland neighborhoods. Mild, pleasant stretches in spring and fall are the sweet spot for outdoor work, river visits, and trips out onto the Old Chain of Rocks Bridge.

Their facility is equipped with loading docks and a secured, gated parking lot that provides truck and trailer parking. Multiple sources recommend the company for its commitment to safety and its modern fleet, with 95% of their equipment being brand new. Clients and employees alike frequently cite the company’s reliability and the cleanliness of their vehicles. This carrier is ideal for regional businesses requiring dependable freight transportation and secure short-term storage for large-scale shipments.

**Verified Reviews:** 111 reviews

**Service Area:** National (Full Truckload)

6085 W Florissant Ave, St. Louis, MO 63136, USA · (314) 382-9000

Community feedback highlights their dedication to neighborhood vitality and their role as a resource for local alderpersons. ## Shaw — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/shaw

# Shaw Neighborhood Guide – Historic South St. Louis (2026)

## Explore Shaw’s Tree Lined Streets, Missouri Botanical Garden, and Local Services

Updated May 2026.

## Shaw Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

Shaw, St. Louis

Shaw is the near Southside St. Louis neighborhood named for Henry Shaw, the English immigrant whose 1840 land purchase produced both the Missouri Botanical Garden (founded 1859, the oldest in the country) and Tower Grove Park (founded October 20, 1868 as a 276-acre gift to the city). The neighborhood became a Certified Local Historic District in 1985 and is celebrated for its Flora Place private boulevard (1897-1901), the 1878-1883 Shaw Place subdivision, and an 1890-1924 architectural inventory of Victorian, Queen Anne, Colonial Revival, American Foursquare, and Art Deco homes. The corridor offers diverse local cafes, wine bars, ice cream shops, and boutique retail along Shaw Boulevard and adjacent South Grand.

Shaw is one of St. Louis’s 79 official neighborhoods, located on the near Southside at **38.6126° N, 90.2537° W** in zip code 63110. Its boundaries run north to **Interstate 44**, south to **Magnolia Avenue** and **Tower Grove Park**, east to **South Grand Boulevard**, and west to **Tower Grove Avenue** and the **Missouri Botanical Garden**. The neighborhood is bordered by **Botanical Heights and Tiffany** to the north (across I-44), **Compton Heights** to the east, **Tower Grove South** to the south, and **Southwest Garden** to the west. **Shaw Boulevard** and **Flora Place** are the neighborhood’s primary east-west spines; **Tower Grove Avenue** and **South Grand Boulevard** form the major north-south corridors. The 2020 census recorded the neighborhood’s population at 66.2% White, 21.9% Black, 0.2% Native American, 2.8% Asian, 7.7% Two or More Races, and 1.2% Some Other Race.

The land that became Shaw began as part of the French colonial *Prairie des Noyers* (“Meadows of the Walnut Trees”), established in 1769 with long narrow agricultural strips running westward from Grand Boulevard to Kingshighway. After the Louisiana Purchase, the American Government confirmed ownership for the original French families, who later sold to land speculators. By the mid-1800s, two landowners dominated the area: **Henry Shaw** and **Mary L. Tyler** (the daughter of Captain Chambers, the earlier landowner). In **1857**, Shaw and Tyler agreed by deed to promote the “general improvement and salubrity” of their lands by opening **Shaw, Flora, and Tower Grove Avenues**, with restrictions prohibiting offensive factories and other nuisances — deed conditions that produced one of the most architecturally consistent neighborhoods in St. Louis.

**Henry Shaw** arrived in St. Louis from England in 1819 as a young hardware merchant; by 1840 he had purchased a 2,000-acre tract of prairie outside the city limits, and in 1850 he built his Italianate villa **Tower Grove House** (designed by George I. Barnett and Charles H. Peck) as the first building on the land that would become the Missouri Botanical Garden. Inspired by a return trip to England’s Royal Botanical Garden at Kew, Shaw founded the **Missouri Botanical Garden** in 1858-1859 on his land — the oldest botanical garden in continuous operation in the country, today covering 79 acres of flora arranged along waterways, walking paths, and hedge mazes. The Garden is also home to the **Climatron** — the first geodesic dome in the world to be used as a conservatory, designed by Murphy & Mackey Assoc. of St. Louis, housing a tropical rainforest with orchids, coconut trees, cacao plants, and Dale Chihuly glass sculptures and Carl Milles bronze water nymphs.

In 1866, Shaw approached St. Louis mayor James S. Thomas with a proposition: he wanted to donate a still larger tract to the city as a public pleasure ground. **Tower Grove Park** was founded on **October 20, 1868**, as a gift from Shaw to the city — **276 acres** of his country estate, given on two conditions: that it “shall be used as a park forever,” and that an “annual appropriation” be made by the city for its maintenance. Shaw insisted that the park be governed by an independent Board of Commissioners (of which he would be a life member), retaining control over its development as a Victorian walking park. Tower Grove Park’s grand pavilions and Palm House (also designed by George I. Barnett) reflect Shaw’s classical interests and European travels.

Shaw’s residential development began with **Shaw Place** — built between 1878 and 1883 as a re-creation of the English streetscapes of his childhood, designed by George I. Barnett between De Tonty and Shaw avenues just west of Grand. Shaw Place served as rental property, with proceeds endowing the Garden until the Trustees sold the units in 1915; it is now a City Landmark and on the National Register of Historic Places. After a major tornado damaged the Garden in 1896, the Garden’s Board of Trustees asked the City of St. Louis to open **Flora Avenue** for development to finance the repairs and re-plantings. **Flora Place** — the showcase boulevard of the area — became a private street in 1897, with paving, curbs, sewers, trees, and shrubbery installed at a cost of $100,000. The neighborhood’s grand **Flora Place entrance gates** were built in 1897 at a cost of $9,500 ($5,500 more than the first home’s sale price); the boulevard was officially platted in 1901 and lined with fine houses within a few years.

The opening of the **Grand Avenue Viaduct in 1889** and the extension of streetcar lines were the real catalysts for Shaw’s residential development. The majority of the neighborhood was built between 1890 and 1915, with additional development continuing to the north until 1924. **Magnolia Place** opened in 1916, alongside Shaw’s Vandeventer Avenue Addition (north of the Garden), and **Gurney and Heger Courts** opened in 1922-1923, followed by the **Shaw’s Garden Subdivision** on previously under-developed Garden property. Deed restrictions established uniform building lines and limited construction to two-story brick buildings, producing the architectural consistency that residents and visitors still recognize today — a mix of Victorians, Queen Annes, Colonial Revivals, American Foursquares, and Art Decos that Shaw Neighborhood Housing Corporation has compared to “waking up on the set of *Meet Me in St. Louis*.”

In **1985**, Shaw was officially designated as a **Certified Local Historic District** under city ordinance, providing redevelopment incentives and preserving the neighborhood’s 1890-1924 architectural inventory. The Shaw Neighborhood Improvement Association (SNIA), governed by a Board of Directors composed of elected representatives from each neighborhood block, has served as the voice of the neighborhood, advocating before aldermen and the City of St. Louis. The neighborhood’s commercial life is anchored by **Shaw Boulevard** and the adjacent **South Grand** district, with local favorites that include cafes, wine bars, ice cream shops, Bailey’s Range burgers and house-made shakes, and the original **Imo’s Pizza** (opened at the corner of Thurman and Shaw Avenue in 1964). The **Mullanphy Botanical Garden Investigative Learning Center** — a St. Louis Public Schools magnet elementary school with an emphasis on math, science, and technology — is the neighborhood’s single public school campus.

Shaw’s spiritual life is anchored by **St. Margaret of Scotland Catholic Church** (the stunning stone church with rich stained glass and deep community involvement), along with The Word at Shaw United Methodist Church on Shaw Boulevard, South City Church on South Spring, Compton Heights Christian Church on South Grand, Mount Olive Lutheran Church on Shaw Avenue, and the Presbytery of Giddings-Lovejoy on Tower Grove. The annual **Historic Shaw Art Fair**, held on Flora Place and Tower Grove Avenue, draws more than 100 juried artists from across the country, showcasing jewelry, ceramics, fine paintings, and more. The neighborhood was voted St. Louis’s “Best Place to Live” by readers of the Riverfront Times in 2010, and its Shaw Dog Park at Thurman and Cleveland gives Shaw and surrounding residents a fenced space for their dogs.

Image: Streetscape of the Shaw neighborhood — the 1985 Certified Local Historic District with its 1890-1924 architectural inventory of Victorian, Queen Anne, Colonial Revival, and American Foursquare homes — Wikimedia Commons, CC BY 3.0 by Onegentlemanofverona

Shaw sits on the gently rolling near-Southside terrain of St. Louis at 38.6126° N — the same broad upland that the French called the *Prairie des Noyers* in 1769 — with Interstate 44 forming the hard northern boundary that distinguishes Shaw’s interior from adjacent Tiffany and Botanical Heights. The neighborhood experiences St. Louis’s full humid continental weather range: hot, humid summers with frequent 90°F days, cold winters with regular freezing temperatures, and a notably active spring severe weather season — an 1896 tornado damaged the Missouri Botanical Garden and prompted the development of Flora Place, and the neighborhood remains within St. Louis’s real tornado corridor exposure. Peak outdoor activity falls during spring and fall, when Tower Grove Park’s 276 acres of walking paths, ornate Victorian pavilions, picnic shelters, ponds, playgrounds, and the Tower Grove Farmer’s Market draw the heaviest neighborhood use, alongside the Missouri Botanical Garden’s 79 acres of seasonal flora, free summer concerts, and the annual Historic Shaw Art Fair on Flora Place. Cool seasons drive intense indoor activity at the Climatron tropical conservatory, the neighborhood’s cafes and wine bars along Shaw Boulevard, and the South Grand commercial district. Winter shifts commerce indoors to Bailey’s Range, Imo’s Pizza, and the historic St. Margaret of Scotland Catholic Church. The neighborhood’s 1890-1924 brick construction has weathered more than a century of Missouri climate with the consistency typical of well-built late-Victorian residential stock.

## Skinker DeBaliviere — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/skinker-debaliviere

Neighborhood

Skinker-DeBaliviere

Coordinates

38.6513° N, 90.2942° W

Ranked by 10,826+ Google reviews. Updated June 2026.

✓  Anchored by the historic Delmar Loop and the Forest Park edge ✓  A 1900s streetcar neighborhood; a Local Historic District since 1978 ✓  Avg. rating: 4.5/5 across 10,826 reviews

## Skinker-DeBaliviere Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Skinker-DeBaliviere, St. Louis

Tucked into west-central St. Louis between Forest Park and the Delmar Loop, Skinker-DeBaliviere is a compact, leafy neighborhood of early-1900s homes that is best known for the deliberate way its neighbors kept it diverse and stable through decades when many nearby areas came apart.

The neighborhood takes its name from the two streets that bookend it: **Skinker Boulevard** on the west edge, near the City limits, and **DeBaliviere Avenue** on the east. **Delmar Boulevard** forms the northern edge, while **Forsyth and Lindell Boulevards** mark the south, where the neighborhood meets Forest Park. It is officially City Neighborhood #46, covers about 0.52 square miles, and sits within ZIP code 63112.

For most of the 1800s this was farmland. The land began as an eighteenth-century French colonial grant to Marie Louise Chouteau Papin and stayed largely in the Chouteau-Papin family into the late 19th century, kept rural in part because the unruly River Des Peres ran across it. That changed at the turn of the 20th century, when the **1904 World’s Fair** in adjacent Forest Park set off a streetcar-era building boom across the city’s west end.

The neighborhood is generally dated to 1908, and its private subdivisions went up in quick succession: **Parkview platted in 1905**, Washington Heights in 1907, and the Catlin Tract developed in 1909. Hamilton Elementary School opened in 1914, and three churches built in the 1910s — **Grace Methodist, New Cote Brilliante Baptist, and St. Roch Catholic** — still anchor the area today. The Parkview Historic District and the Catlin Tract preserve street after street of architecturally significant early-1900s houses, and Parkview is listed on the National Register of Historic Places.

What put Skinker-DeBaliviere on the national map is its mid-1960s integration story. The neighborhood **became racially integrated in 1964**, and in **1966 residents formed the Skinker-DeBaliviere Community Council** to manage that transition on their own terms and resist the blockbusting and white flight that destabilized so many other St. Louis neighborhoods. The Council’s work is widely credited with keeping the area diverse and steady; the 2020 census still recorded a notably mixed community of about 3,899 residents. In **1978 the City designated the area a Local Historic District**.

The neighborhood’s edges are its biggest draw. **Forest Park** — the roughly 1,300-acre park that hosted the 1904 World’s Fair and is home to the St. Louis Zoo and the Art Museum — runs along the southern boundary, and **Washington University in St. Louis** and its Danforth Campus sit just southwest across the park. Along the northern Delmar Boulevard edge runs the **Delmar Loop**, one of the region’s liveliest dining, shopping, and live-music districts, which straddles the city and University City line.

Getting around is easy by St. Louis standards. Two MetroLink light-rail stations serve the area — the **Forest Park–DeBaliviere station** on the eastern side and the **Skinker station** on the west — putting downtown, the airport, and Washington University a short train ride away, while the Loop and the park are walkable from most blocks. The businesses below reflect that geography: a mix of long-running Loop venues and shops on Delmar, a few spots tucked strictly inside the neighborhood, and trusted service providers that serve residents here.

The Delmar Loop along the northern edge of Skinker-DeBaliviere. Photo: “Delmar Loop - Parkview Gardens Historic District” by Liezl Moss, licensed under CC BY-SA 3.0 via Wikimedia Commons.

Skinker-DeBaliviere shares the humid subtropical climate (Köppen Cfa) of the wider St. Louis region. Summers are warm and humid, with average July highs near **89.6°F**, and the neighborhood’s mature tree canopy and the green expanse of neighboring Forest Park make the hot months noticeably more bearable. Spring and fall are mild and pleasant, though spring also brings the region’s severe-storm season, since the area sits within or near Tornado Alley and sees a real risk of thunderstorms and the occasional tornado. 

Winters are cold but generally manageable, with average January highs around **40.4°F** and roughly **16.6 inches** of snow a year against about **41.7 inches** of annual precipitation. The neighborhood’s solidly built early-1900s brick homes were made for this swing of seasons, and the local businesses below — from restaurants to salons to service providers — keep regular hours through summer heat and winter cold alike.
