With more than 400 reviews, it is a long-standing fixture of the district’s shopping scene.

**Years in Business:** Since 1988

**Verified Reviews:** 429 reviews

**Service Area:** Delmar Loop, University City

**Sells:** Vintage & resale clothing

**Buys:** Gently used clothing & accessories

## Soulard — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/soulard

Ranked by 10,202+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  Home to the 1779 Soulard Farmers Market — oldest west of the Mississippi ✓  St. Louis’s oldest neighborhood, NRHP-listed Soulard Historic District (1972) & Anheuser-Busch HQ ✓  Avg. rating: 4.8/5 across 10,202 reviews

## Soulard Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

Soulard, St. Louis

Soulard serves as St. Louis’s oldest neighborhood — founded in 1779 around the city’s third public marketplace, anchored by the Soulard Farmers Market (oldest west of the Mississippi, donated by Julia Cerré Soulard in 1841), the Anheuser-Busch Brewery (a National Historic Landmark since 1966), St. Vincent de Paul and St. John Nepomuk churches, and the Second Empire and Italianate “flounder house” row homes of the NRHP-listed Soulard Historic District — offering breweries, restaurants, blues bars, wedding venues, coffee, board games, and reputation management across the Russell, 12th, and 9th Street corridors.

Soulard is one of St. Louis’s 79 official neighborhoods and one of **eight certified local historic districts** in the City of St. Louis, located at **38.6028° N, 90.2103° W** on the near south side of the city, just south of Downtown. The neighborhood’s triangular boundaries are defined by **Interstate 55** on the west and north, **7th Street** and **South Broadway** to the southeast, and the **Anheuser-Busch Brewery** complex extending south to Arsenal Street. Surrounding neighborhoods include **LaSalle Park** and **Lafayette Square** to the north (across I-55), **Benton Park West** to the west, and **Benton Park** and the Mississippi River to the south. The neighborhood sits within **ZIP code 63104**. Soulard is divided roughly by Lynch Street — north of which is mostly 19th-century brick row homes, Second Empire Victorians, Italianate town houses, Federal-style buildings, and the unique **“flounder houses”** (narrow homes built with only one slope to the roof) — while south of Lynch Street is largely the campus of **Anheuser-Busch** (the North American headquarters and St. Louis Brewery) and its surviving 19th-century brewery buildings.

The story of Soulard begins with the **founding of the Soulard Farmers Market in 1779** — a flat meadow where farmers from the surrounding French Creole village of St. Louis (founded 1764) came to sell fresh fruit, vegetables, dairy, and livestock. This was the **third public marketplace in St. Louis**, and the meadow lay on land that had been granted by the Spanish government to French merchant **Jean-Gabriel Cerré**. The neighborhood takes its name from Cerré’s son-in-law, **Antoine Pierre Soulard** — born in 1766 in Rochefort, France, a French aristocrat and former French naval officer who escaped the French Revolution and arrived in St. Louis around 1794. In **1795**, Antoine Soulard married **Julia Cerré**, and the couple received a **122-acre plot** of land that included the market grounds; additional land was given to Antoine by the Spanish colonial government as a gift for his service as **Surveyor of Upper Louisiana** under Governor Carlos de Hault de Lassus.

The **1803 Louisiana Purchase** threw the land grant into legal limbo, and following Antoine’s death in **1825**, his widow **Julia Cerré Soulard** fought a **thirty-year courtroom battle** to confirm the deed. She prevailed in **1836**, then began subdividing the land and selling lots — becoming the **first female real estate developer west of the Mississippi River**. Her most enduring gift came in **1841–1842**, when she donated **two city blocks** to the City of St. Louis with the stipulation that the land remain a public marketplace in perpetuity. Julia Soulard died in **1845**; the City formally acquired title in 1850. The first market building was constructed in **1843**, a one-story red brick structure; a second-story meeting hall was added in 1865. The original Soulard Market and many surrounding buildings were heavily damaged by the **Great St. Louis Tornado of May 27, 1896**. For more than three decades, vendors operated from temporary sheds and wagons until the **current Italian Renaissance Grand Hall** opened in **1929**, designed by City Architect **Albert E. Osburg** and modeled on Filippo Brunelleschi’s **Ospedale degli Innocenti** (Florentine Foundling Hospital, 1419).

During the mid-19th century, Soulard transformed from French Creole farmland into one of the most densely settled immigrant neighborhoods in St. Louis. The first wave was **French**; the much larger wave was **German** — particularly during the political upheavals of **1848** — followed by **Syrian, Hungarian, Croatian, Italian, Serbian, Irish, Slovak, and Czech** immigrants. The Eastern European population earned the area the nickname **“Bohemian Hill.”** The principal industry was **beer brewing**: Soulard hosted at least **half a dozen breweries**, including the **Green Tree Brewery** on Sidney Street, the **Excelsior Brewery** on Seventh Street, **Anthony & Kuhn’s Brewery** on Sidney Street, the **Lemp Brewery**, and **Eberhard Anheuser’s brewery** — which his son-in-law **Adolphus Busch** transformed into **Anheuser-Busch**. Most of these breweries sat atop a rumored cave system that runs along Sidney Street. By **Prohibition**, only Anheuser-Busch and Falstaff (formerly Lemp) survived. Notable 19th-century residents included **Max J. Feuerbacher**, **Dr. Franz Artzt**, **Thomas Allen**, **Adam Lemp**, and **Eberhard Anheuser**.

The **Anheuser-Busch Brewery** on Lynch Street, founded in the 1850s as Eberhard Anheuser’s Bavarian Brewery, transformed under **Adolphus Busch** from a small neighborhood brewery into a global icon. The campus — with its elaborate **red-brick German Romanesque buildings**, the original 1885 **Brew House**, the **Clydesdale Stables**, and the historic 19th-century buildings — was designated a **National Historic Landmark in 1966**. Today the brewery offers the **Budweiser Brewery Experience** with the Clydesdale VIP Experience, the Finisher Tour, and a 7-step brewing process tour. The famous **Clydesdale horses** — trademark of Anheuser-Busch since they were used to pull wagons of beer for Prohibition-repeal deliveries in 1933 — are regularly rotated in from the company’s farms near Grant’s Farm and Boonville. Beyond the brewery, the neighborhood is home to several **nineteenth-century church buildings**: **Saints Peter and Paul Catholic Church**, **St. Vincent de Paul Catholic Church** (built on land donated by Julia Soulard to Bishop Joseph Rosati), **St. John Nepomuk Czech Catholic Church** (the first Czech national church in the United States, founded 1854), and **Trinity Lutheran Church**.

In **1972**, the **Soulard Historic District** was placed on the **National Register of Historic Places** and became one of **eight certified local historic districts** in the City of St. Louis. The neighborhood is preserved by the **Soulard Restoration Group** and the **Soulard Business Association**, which publishes the neighborhood newspaper *The Soulard Renaissance*. The district is renowned for its surviving examples of **Greek Revival farmhouses**, **Federal Transitional**, **Federal**, **Italianate**, **Second Empire Victorian** (with characteristic **mansard roofs** inspired by 17th-century French architect François Mansart), and the uniquely St. Louis **flounder houses** — narrow brick homes with only one slope to the roof, built by working-class immigrants on long, narrow lots. The neighborhood is also known for its **Mardi Gras celebration**, the second-largest in the United States (behind New Orleans), the **world’s largest parade of costumed pets** (the Beggin’ Pet Parade), the annual **Oktoberfest**, and the blues / jazz scene that hosted barrelhouse blues piano player **James Crutchfield** from 1984 until his death in 2001.

Today, Soulard remains one of St. Louis’s most vibrant, walkable, and architecturally intact historic neighborhoods. The neighborhood’s key business anchors include the **Soulard Farmers Market** at 730 Carroll Street (140+ vendors, City Landmark 1971, open Wednesday through Saturday year-round), the **Budweiser Brewery Experience** at 1200 Lynch Street, the **Soulard Spice Shop** inside the market (Neighborhood Favorite 2023, 600–800 pounds of spices moved weekly), **Pieces Board Game Bar & Restaurant** at 2727 South 12th Street (1,200+ games), **Goshen Coffee Roasters** at 910 Geyer Avenue (Good Food Award winner), **The Franklin of Soulard** at 816 Allen Avenue (the 1905 wedding venue under Daniel and Christina Walsh), **Soulard Art Gallery** at 2028 South 12th Street (co-op under curator Earl Miller), **International Tap House** (iTap) at 1711 South 9th Street (40 beers on tap, 480+ bottles), **Soulard Market Lofts** at 1531 South 8th Street (managed by Sansone Group), **Unreview** at 1400 Russell Boulevard (reputation management SaaS), the **Pontiac Square Park** (1.50 acres, 1908), the **Soulard Market Park** (1.94 acres, 1908), and dozens of independent restaurants, blues bars, microbreweries, and boutiques.

Image: Soulard Farmers Market — the oldest farmers’ market west of the Mississippi River, founded 1779, donated to the City of St. Louis by Julia Cerré Soulard in 1841. The 1929 Italian Renaissance Grand Hall by City Architect Albert E. Osburg was modeled on Brunelleschi’s 1419 Ospedale degli Innocenti in Florence and designated a St. Louis City Landmark in 1971 — Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA

Soulard sits one mile south of Downtown St. Louis at 38.6028° N along the Mississippi River, with seamless access to Interstate 55 (the western boundary), South Broadway (the eastern boundary along the river), the **Anheuser-Busch Brewery** complex (the southern boundary), Lafayette Square and LaSalle Park to the north, and Benton Park and Cherokee Street to the south. 

The neighborhood experiences St. Louis’s full humid continental climate: hot, humid summers, cold winters, and an active spring severe weather season — punctuated historically by the **Great St. Louis Tornado of May 27, 1896**. Peak outdoor activity falls during late winter, spring, summer, and fall, when the **Soulard Farmers Market** (open Wednesday through Saturday year-round, with Saturday morning crowds of 10,000+) draws shoppers from across the bi-state region; when the **Budweiser Brewery Experience** and **Clydesdale Stables** host daily tours; when **Pontiac Square Park** (1.50 acres, 1908), **Soulard Market Park** (1.94 acres, 1908), and the two neighborhood **dog parks** draw residents; when the annual **Mardi Gras** celebration (the second-largest in the United States with the Beggin’ Pet Parade) takes over the streets each February or March; when **Oktoberfest** arrives in autumn; and when the year-round blues / jazz scene fills the patios of **1860 Saloon**, **Molly’s in Soulard**, **The Shanti**, **Hammerstone’s**, and **McGurk’s**. 

Cool seasons drive intense indoor activity at **International Tap House** on South 9th, **Goshen Coffee Roasters** on Geyer, **Pieces Board Game Bar & Restaurant** on South 12th, **The Franklin of Soulard** on Allen, **Soulard Spice Shop**, **Soulard Art Gallery**, **Soulard Market Lofts**, **Unreview**, and the indoor venues of the 1929 Soulard Market Grand Hall, the Anheuser-Busch Brewery’s 19th-century buildings (including the 1885 Brew House), and the historic 19th-century churches (Saints Peter and Paul, St. Vincent de Paul, St. John Nepomuk, Trinity Lutheran). The neighborhood’s 1830s–1890s housing stock — Federal-style row homes, Greek Revival farmhouses, Italianate town houses, Second Empire Victorians with mansard roofs, and unique flounder houses — has weathered more than 180 years of Missouri climate with the durability of the French Creole, German, and Bohemian masons who built them.

## Southampton — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/southampton

10 Best St Louis Businesses in Southampton, MO (2026)

Southampton Neighborhood Guide – St. Louis History & Businesses

Southampton serves as one of St. Louis's most architecturally distinctive 1905–1930s English-named streetcar subdivisions — anchored by the Macklind Avenue Business District, the 1939 Hampton Village shopping center (one of the first U.S. strip centers), St. Mary Magdalen Catholic Church (1927), and the "Hansel and Gretel," Neo-Tudor, and Arts & Crafts bungalows that define the "SoHa" skyline — offering custom apparel, candles, recording, wine, title insurance, and consulting across the Hampton and Macklind corridors.

✓ Historic 1905–1930s English-named streetcar subdivision (“SoHa”) ✓ Anchored by Macklind Avenue Business District, Hampton Village (1939), & St. Mary Magdalen Church (1927) ✓ Avg. rating: 4.9/5 across 802 reviews

Southampton Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

Southampton, St. Louis

Southampton serves as one of St. Louis’s most architecturally distinctive 1905–1930s English-named streetcar subdivisions — platted by an Anglophile developer who named the streets after Devonshire, Lansdowne, Nottingham, and Sutherland — anchored by the Macklind Avenue Business District, the 1939 Hampton Village shopping center, St. Mary Magdalen Catholic Church (1927), the Hope Lutheran Church, the Buder Branch Library, and the “Hansel and Gretel,” Neo-Tudor, Arts & Crafts, and American Foursquare bungalow homes that define the SoHa skyline — offering custom apparel, candles, recording, wine, title insurance, and community services across the Hampton, Chippewa, Macklind, and Kingshighway corridors.

Southampton is one of St. Louis’s 79 official neighborhoods, located at 38.5869° N, 90.2842° W in southwest St. Louis City. The neighborhood’s boundaries are Chippewa Street (Missouri Route 366, formerly U.S. Route 66) on the north, Hampton Avenue on the west, Eichelberger Street on the south, and South Kingshighway Boulevard on the east. The neighborhood occupies approximately 0.7 square miles and is home to roughly 6,647 residents (2020 census) in 3,718 housing units, making it one of the densest neighborhoods in the city at 9,500 people per square mile. Southampton sits within ZIP code 63109 in the 5th Ward of the City of St. Louis (currently represented by Alderman Matt Devoti). Surrounding neighborhoods include Lindenwood Park to the west across Hampton, St. Louis Hills to the south and southwest, Bevo Mill to the southeast across Kingshighway, Tower Grove South to the northeast, and Northampton to the north across Chippewa. The neighborhood is locally nicknamed “SoHa” (a portmanteau of South Hampton) and is one of the few St. Louis neighborhoods that gained population from 1990 to 2000.

The land that became Southampton was, through the 19th century, a mixture of farms and country estates that remained rural in character despite the conversion of the Christy Estate to the east into clay mines. Everard Horton’s farm occupied most of present-day Southampton, and Devonshire Avenue is named after his original hometown in England. Most of the remainder of the neighborhood was assembled from the Decker Farm, which was acquired and developed by the Mercantile Trust Company. The Mercantile Trust’s superintendent, identified in local histories as Mr. Sihrett, supervised the building of the subdivision and named it “Southampton” after a town in his native England. Many of Southampton’s streets were given the same English (and one Scottish) treatment: Nottingham, Hampton, Devonshire, Lansdowne, Murdoch, Sutherland, Brannon, and Macklind. Other streets carry names of local significance — Bancroft (for historian George Bancroft), Brannon (for landowners Thomas and Louisa Brannon), Delor (for Carondelet’s founder Clement Delor de Treget), and Wherry Avenue, which cuts the neighborhood diagonally on the former path of a creek of the same name.

Early Southampton residents carried water to their homes from a spring at Nottingham and Kingshighway until a water main was constructed. The neighborhood was actually one of several locations considered as a possible site for the 1904 World’s Fair, but it was determined to be almost impossible to run adequate transportation facilities to the Southampton area, and the fair was instead held at Forest Park. Residential development took off with the extension of the streetcar lines. The #50 Southampton streetcar line was established in 1889 as an east-west route between Third & Washington downtown and Macklind & Devonshire, and the #21 Tower Grove line began operating in 1890. The intersection of Macklind and Devonshire became the loop where the Southampton line terminated, and a small business strip and subdivision platting grew up around it. The first homes in the neighborhood were built around 1905–1915, but the great majority of Southampton’s housing stock was constructed in the 1920s and 1930s. Original residents reported that the typical brick Arts & Crafts home took about one year to build.

Southampton’s residential housing stock is one of the most architecturally distinctive collections in St. Louis, defined by brick and stone homes built in styles that include “Hansel and Gretel” Storybook cottages (with gingerbread trim and whimsical storybook detailing), Neo-Tudor revival cottages, Arts & Crafts bungalows, American Foursquare houses, and a small but distinctive collection of Prairie School and Colonial Revival homes. The neighborhood’s 1920s–1930s “Hansel and Gretel” houses, with their steeply pitched roofs, fairy-tale rooflines, and asymmetric stone-and-brick facades, are widely recognized as among the finest concentrations of Storybook-style residential architecture in the Midwest. Southampton also contains the only alley in the City of St. Louis divided by a median — running one block between Chippewa Street and Winona Avenue from Macklind to Brannon avenues, with the median once part of the track for a privately owned rail line.

The neighborhood was historically populated by German immigrants and their descendants in the early 20th century, and was traditionally home to a large number of St. Louis firefighters and police officers as a result of the city’s long-standing residency requirement for employees. Since the 1990s, a new generation of younger residents, artists, and craftsmen has moved into the neighborhood, helping drive a steady increase in property values after 2003. Southampton’s commercial life centers on three corridors: the Macklind Avenue Business District running through the heart of the neighborhood from Devonshire Avenue to Eichelberger Street (a streetcar-era walkable strip of restaurants, delis, bakeries, candle shops, recording studios, the SOHA Gallery, and the Southampton Community Garden); the perimeter Hampton Avenue commercial corridor on the west; the Chippewa Street corridor on the north (the historic Route 66); and the South Kingshighway Boulevard corridor on the east. The neighborhood’s post-war commercial centerpiece is Hampton Village, opened in 1939 at Hampton & Chippewa as one of the first dedicated strip shopping centers in the United States, with off-street parking and one-stop colonial-style retail buildings later expanded into today’s Hampton Village Plaza anchored by Schnucks.

Southampton’s religious life is anchored by several historic congregations: St. Mary Magdalen Catholic Church on South Kingshighway at Bancroft Avenue (the Romanesque Italian-basilica-style church at 5831 South Kingshighway designed by architect Adolph F. Stauder and dedicated February 12, 1927, with its narthex, clerestory, campanile, colonnaded front portico, mosaic altar, and dome, founded in 1919 from a portion of Our Lady of Sorrows parish by Father John J. Thompson to serve the rapidly growing Southampton area); Our Lady of Sorrows Catholic Church on Rhodes Avenue at South Kingshighway (whose parish boundaries cover the southeast portion of the neighborhood); Hope Lutheran Church on Neosho Street (founded 1907); and Kingshighway Baptist Church at South Kingshighway and Eichelberger. The public elementary school serving the neighborhood is Buder Elementary School, named for early-20th-century St. Louis attorney G. A. Buder, and the Buder Branch Library sits on Hampton just south of Chippewa. The Southampton Neighborhood Association (southamptonstl.org), a 501(c)(4) organization established in 1903, coordinates the neighborhood’s legislative advocacy, annual events, landscape committee, and the popular Macklind Days annual street festival.

Today, Southampton is a vibrant residential neighborhood of more than 6,600 residents, with its commercial life concentrated on the Macklind Avenue Business District (anchored by businesses including the Southampton Neighborhood Association headquarters at 5011–5099 Wherry Avenue, Sunburst Logo LLC at 5249 Neosho Street, the Urban Candle Company at 4920 Hampton Avenue, the Red Pill Recording Studio at 4710 Hampton Avenue, the Southampton Community Garden at 4915 Macklind Avenue next door to SOHA Gallery, the Spirit Wine & Craft bottle shop at 5760 Chippewa Street (the 40-year offshoot of The Wine & Cheese Place), Absolute Title Company at 4706 Hampton Avenue (50+ years), and the St. Louis Small Business Monthly publication serving the surrounding South City entrepreneurial community). With its dense walkable streetscape, the only median-divided alley in the city, the 1939 Hampton Village shopping center, the 1927 St. Mary Magdalen church, and the Macklind Days festival, Southampton remains one of the most desirable urban neighborhoods in southwest St. Louis.

Southampton sits about nine miles southwest of Downtown St. Louis at 38.5869° N along the Hampton / Chippewa / Kingshighway / Eichelberger corridor, with seamless access to Forest Park, the Missouri Botanical Garden, Grant’s Farm, Tower Grove Park, Carondelet Park, St. Louis Hills, Bevo Mill, and the broader southwest St. Louis market. The neighborhood experiences St. Louis’s full humid continental climate with hot, humid summers, cold winters, and an active spring severe weather season.

Peak outdoor activity falls during spring, summer, and fall, when the Macklind Avenue Business District hosts the annual Macklind Days street festival, the Southampton Community Garden at 4915 Macklind draws gardeners alongside SOHA Gallery First Fridays, the Southampton Neighborhood Association hosts events at Hope Lutheran, and Hampton Village handles peak retail traffic. Cool seasons drive intense indoor activity at Sunburst Logo LLC, Urban Candle Company, Red Pill Recording Studio, Spirit Wine & Craft, Absolute Title Company, Slalom Consulting in nearby Clayton, the Southwest Area Chamber of Commerce, the St. Louis Small Business Monthly, and the indoor venues of St. Mary Magdalen, Our Lady of Sorrows, Hope Lutheran, Kingshighway Baptist, the Buder Branch Library, and the restaurants and delis lining Macklind. The 1905–1930s housing stock — Storybook cottages, Neo-Tudor revivals, Arts & Crafts bungalows, American Foursquares, and brick-and-stone middle-class homes — has weathered more than a century of Missouri climate with the durability of the German immigrant masons who built them.

Non-Profit501(c)4 Organization Best for: local residents and community advocates

The Southampton Neighborhood Association is a 501(c)4 non-profit organization dedicated to maintaining the character of this historic streetcar subdivision. Established in 1903, the association is headquartered on Delor Street and serves the area bounded by Chippewa and Eichelberger. The organization coordinates numerous community events and manages local initiatives, such as the landscape committee which oversees the neighborhood’s signature tree-lined streets. Community members frequently describe the area as a peaceful and charming enclave, noting the high level of engagement from the association in local legislative matters and neighborhood beautification. They host regular meetings at local venues like Hope Lutheran Church to discuss urban policies and district improvements. This organization is the primary resource for Southampton residents who want to stay informed on local Board Bills and participate in neighborhood-wide social traditions.

Independent$$ Custom Manufacturer Best for: businesses needing custom apparel

## Southwest Garden — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/southwest-garden

8 Best St Louis Businesses in Southwest Garden, MO (2026)

Southwest Garden Neighborhood Guide – St. Louis History & Businesses

Southwest Garden serves as one of St. Louis's most park-adjacent neighborhoods — named for Henry Shaw's 1859-founded Missouri Botanical Garden ("Shaw's Garden"), anchored by the 1960 Climatron geodesic dome, Tower Grove Park (the 1868 Shaw bequest), the 1869 William Rumbold cast-iron-domed former Lunatic Asylum, Sublette Park on the city's highest point, and two National Register Historic Districts (Shaw's Garden and Reber Place) — offering chamber networking, botanical research, manufacturing, accounting, home decor, and direct-mail logistics across the I-44, Kingshighway, and Shaw Boulevard corridors.

Ranked by 17,744+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓ Home of the Missouri Botanical Garden (Henry Shaw, 1859 — National Historic Landmark) ✓ Two National Register Historic Districts: Shaw’s Garden & Reber Place ✓ Avg. rating: 4.9/5 across 17,744 reviews

Southwest Garden Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

Southwest Garden, St. Louis

Southwest Garden serves as one of St. Louis’s most architecturally rich neighborhoods — named for its proximity to Henry Shaw’s 1859-founded Missouri Botanical Garden, anchored by two National Register Historic Districts (Shaw’s Garden and Reber Place), the 1960 Climatron geodesic dome, Tower Grove Park, the 1869 William Rumbold-designed cast-iron-domed former St. Louis Lunatic Asylum, and Sublette Park — offering chamber-of-commerce networking, botanical research, gasket manufacturing, accounting, home decor, direct mail, and neighborhood-association services across the I-44, Kingshighway, and Shaw Boulevard corridors.

Southwest Garden is one of St. Louis’s 79 official neighborhoods, located at 38.6115° N, 90.2837° W in southwest St. Louis City and bisected by Kingshighway Boulevard, one of the city’s major arterial roads. The neighborhood’s boundaries follow the City of St. Louis Planning & Urban Design Agency map: I-44 (Interstate Highway 44) on the northeast, the Missouri Botanical Garden and Tower Grove Park on the east, Scanlan Avenue and Connecticut Street on the south, Hampton Avenue on the west, and a jagged northwestern boundary that follows Columbia Avenue, Southwest Avenue, and South Kingshighway. The neighborhood occupies approximately 0.86 square miles and is home to roughly 5,245 residents (2020 census) at a density of 6,100 people per square mile. Southwest Garden spans ZIP codes 63110 and 63139 and sits in the 5th Ward of the City of St. Louis (currently represented by Alderman Matt Devoti). Surrounding neighborhoods include The Hill and Forest Park Southeast to the north, the Missouri Botanical Garden and Tower Grove Park (and the Shaw neighborhood beyond them) to the east, Lindenwood Park and Clifton Heights to the west, and North Hampton to the south. Vandeventer and Southwest Avenues span the neighborhood and follow the route of Old Manchester Road — the first public road from St. Louis to Jefferson City and an extension of Market Street that was the only way west without crossing the Missouri or Meramec Rivers.

The land that became Southwest Garden was first divided into agricultural arpents in 1769 by Illinois Country French settlers, who established the Prairie des Noyers Commons — a large tract of communal agricultural land between Grand Avenue and Kingshighway Boulevard. Through the 18th and early 19th centuries, French farmers worked these arpents until advancing urbanization led to gradual sales of combined parcels to private landowners. One of these owners would profoundly shape the future of the neighborhood: Henry Shaw (1800–1889), an English-born St. Louis merchant who arrived in 1819 at age 19, made his fortune in hardware and real estate, and retired at age 39. On an 1851 journey to England, Shaw visited the impressive gardens of Chatsworth House in Derbyshire and returned to St. Louis determined to dedicate part of his land holdings to creating a garden worthy of the city’s future greatness. Shaw’s holdings eventually encompassed the modern-day Missouri Botanical Garden and most of the land between modern Tower Grove Park and Vandeventer Avenue.

The Missouri Botanical Garden — long known informally as “Shaw’s Garden” — opened to the public in 1859 at 4344 Shaw Boulevard, transforming a former treeless prairie into one of the great botanical institutions of the world. Shaw bequeathed the adjacent 289 acres of his prairie estate to the City of St. Louis in 1868 as Tower Grove Park, with the stipulation that it would forever remain a park — today the only municipal park in Missouri on the National Register of Historic Places, governed independently of the City Parks Department per Shaw’s will. The Garden bequest spurred the subdivision of nearby tracts, including Tower Grove Place between Kingshighway and Alfred Avenue in 1870, and the “Tower Grove Park Addition” developed slowly over the following three decades due to poor access to the streetcar system (the nearest line ran across Tower Grove Park on Arsenal Street). In 1897, the Missouri Street Railroad Company constructed a new streetcar line to Southampton extending down Vandeventer Avenue and Kingshighway, dramatically increasing the viability of residential development east of Kingshighway.

The neighborhood’s definitive period of architectural development came in the 1910s through the 1950s, under the visionary leadership of Missouri Botanical Garden director George T. Moore (appointed 1912) and chief landscape architect John Noyes (appointed 1915). Moore decided that rather than simply selling off the large land holdings bequeathed to the Garden by Henry Shaw to outside investors, the Garden itself would plat new subdivisions using Noyes’ landscape vision — ensuring that the surrounding neighborhoods would complement the Garden’s aesthetic and the new homes would enhance the Garden setting. The first plat, the “Shaw’s Vandeventer Avenue Addition” north of the Garden (along present-day Interstate 44), was completed in 1916. The larger trapezoidal tract west of the Garden required more substantial planning, and Noyes — following subdivision principles advanced by renowned landscape architect Henry Wright — designed a curved street grid that conformed to the tract’s irregular borders. The resulting “Shaw’s Garden Subdivision”, platted starting in 1922, was fully developed by third-party builders by the end of the 1920s and embodies progressive concepts in suburban planning rarely realized within the city limits of St. Louis. Two private places — Gurney Court and Heger Court — were established by independent developers in the early 1920s along Magnolia Avenue, each containing single-family homes centered around a landscaped court.

Southwest Garden contains two National Register Historic Districts that define its architectural character. The Shaw’s Garden Historic District, listed on February 22, 2012, encompasses approximately 18 city blocks roughly bounded by DeTonty Avenue, Tower Grove Avenue, Shaw Avenue, Alfred Avenue, Magnolia Avenue, Kingshighway Boulevard, and Vandeventer Avenue — built out largely between 1916 and 1955 as a cohesive streetcar-served neighborhood with an exceptionally intact collection of red-brick masonry duplexes and fourplexes from the late 19th and early 20th centuries, mature street trees, defined tree lawns, and landscaped front yards that visually extend the horticultural principles of the adjacent Missouri Botanical Garden and Tower Grove Park. The Reber Place Historic District, listed earlier the same year, sits directly west of Tower Grove Park and contains a diverse mix of frame homes, multifamily buildings, and bungalows from the late 19th and early 20th centuries. Notable architectural styles across Southwest Garden include late 19th-century multifamily duplexes and fourplexes, 1920s American Movements-style duplexes along Shenandoah Avenue with Craftsman-influenced exposed rafters and built-in cabinetry, Queen Anne and Second Empire homes with asymmetrical facades and mansard roofs, Arts & Crafts bungalows, and shotgun houses.

West of Kingshighway, Southwest Garden has a history that closely parallels its neighbor The Hill. After the “Great Fire” of 1849 destroyed fifteen city blocks of St. Louis, a municipal ordinance banning frame construction put a premium on brick. The demand spurred the development of clay deposits discovered west of Kingshighway in the 1830s, including the nearby Russell and Christy coal and clay mines. As the mines attracted immigrant labor in the late 1800s, Italian workers settled in The Hill and German workers settled between The Hill and Arsenal Street, forming St. Aloysius Gonzaga parish in 1892 at Magnolia and January Avenues (razed 2006). This German community built the frame shotgun houses, flats, and block-wide masonry buildings around present-day Sublette Park. Sublette Park is set on the highest point of the City of St. Louis and offers views of the Downtown skyline; it includes a playground, a pavilion built by the Boy Scouts of America, tennis courts, and a soccer field. Also on the highest point of the city is the St. Louis Psychiatric Rehabilitation Center (formerly the St. Louis County Lunatic Asylum), a 196-bed psychiatric hospital on a 32-acre campus on Arsenal Street, including the William Rumbold-designed 1869 Romanesque Revival building topped by a prominent 200-foot cast iron dome visible from up to 30 miles away.

Today, Southwest Garden remains one of St. Louis’s most park-adjacent and architecturally distinctive neighborhoods. The Missouri Botanical Garden draws roughly a million visitors a year to its 79-acre grounds, 1960 Climatron geodesic dome (the world’s first completely air-conditioned greenhouse, designed by St. Louis architects Murphy and Mackey based on R. Buckminster Fuller’s principles, designated a National Historic Landmark in 1976), the Peter H. Raven Library (containing 85% of all literature ever published on systematic botany), the Japanese Garden, the Gladney and Lehmann Rose Gardens, the Tower Grove House, and the seasonal Garden Glow event. Local businesses in the neighborhood include the Southwest Area Chamber of Commerce, the Missouri Botanical Garden Library / Raven Library at 4500 Shaw Boulevard, the manufacturing firm Fairlane Industries Inc at 5033 Southwest Avenue, the long-standing accounting firm Mid-City Accounting Services, LLC at 5701 Southwest Avenue, the boutique Garden District STL, the 35-year direct-mail logistics firm Presort Inc. at 5051 Southwest Avenue, and the Southwest Garden Neighborhood Association at 4950 Southwest Avenue. With Sublette Park, the Kingshighway Branch Library on South Vandeventer Avenue, the South City YMCA, Central Visual and Performing Arts High School, and the Cleveland Naval Junior ROTC Academy serving residents, Southwest Garden offers a rare combination of cultural-landmark proximity and walkable streetcar-era residential character.

Southwest Garden sits about six miles southwest of Downtown St. Louis at 38.6115° N along the I-44 / Kingshighway / Hampton / Shaw Boulevard corridor, with seamless access to The Hill, Forest Park Southeast and Cortex (north), Forest Park (two miles north on Kingshighway), Tower Grove Park (east), and the Shaw, Tower Grove South, Lindenwood Park, Clifton Heights, Bevo Mill, Southampton, and North Hampton neighborhoods.

The neighborhood experiences St. Louis’s full humid continental climate with hot, humid summers, cold winters, and an active spring severe weather season. Peak outdoor activity falls during spring, summer, and fall, when the Missouri Botanical Garden draws its largest crowds for the Japanese Festival, Chinese Culture Days, and the autumn Garden Glow; when Tower Grove Park hosts the Festival of Nations and its weekly farmers market; when Sublette Park sees its peak playground, tennis, and soccer activity; and when the curving streets of the Shaw’s Garden Historic District fill with strollers and dog-walkers admiring the red-brick duplexes and fourplexes.

Cool seasons drive intense indoor activity at the Missouri Botanical Garden Climatron, the Raven Library at 4500 Shaw Boulevard, Mid-City Accounting Services at 5701 Southwest Avenue, Presort Inc. at 5051 Southwest Avenue, Fairlane Industries Inc at 5033 Southwest Avenue, Garden District STL at the City Foundry, the Southwest Area Chamber of Commerce, the Southwest Garden Neighborhood Association at 4950 Southwest Avenue, the Kingshighway Branch Library, the South City YMCA, Central Visual and Performing Arts High School, Cleveland NJROTC High School, and the dozens of restaurants and shops along the Southwest Avenue and Kingshighway corridors. The neighborhood’s 1869 William Rumbold cast-iron dome and 1960 Climatron geodesic dome — visible from up to 30 miles away — have weathered more than 150 years of Missouri climate.

Residents often note the neighborhood’s ideal location, which is bound by I-44 and sits adjacent to the Missouri Botanical Garden and Tower Grove Park. It is a go-to resource for residents who want to stay informed about city services and local safety. This organization is ideal for new and long-term residents looking to engage in local civic life.

Emergency Service: Community forum support

## St Louis Hills — St. Louis Neighborhood

10 Best St Louis Businesses in St. Louis Hills, MO (2026)

St. Louis Hills Neighborhood Guide – St. Louis History & Businesses

St. Louis Hills serves as one of St. Louis's most architecturally cohesive 1930s–1950s residential neighborhoods — the 700-acre Cyrus Crane Willmore "Country living in the City" development platted 1929–1950 on the former David R. Francis farm — anchored by Francis Park (1917 Francis bequest, WPA-improved), Willmore Park (1947), Ted Drewes on Route 66 (since 1941), Hampton Village Plaza, the Art Deco Vedder apartment building, and Stan Musial's longtime home — offering market, barbecue, fuel, IT, real estate, and consulting services.

Ranked by 1,812+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓ Cyrus Crane Willmore-developed 700-acre Art Deco / Mid-Century Modern neighborhood (1929–1950) ✓ Home to Francis Park (1917 Francis bequest), Willmore Park (1947), Ted Drewes on Route 66 (since 1941), & Stan Musial’s longtime home ✓ Avg. rating: 4.8/5 across 1,812 reviews

St. Louis Hills Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

St. Louis Hills, St. Louis

St. Louis Hills serves as one of St. Louis’s most architecturally cohesive 1930s–1950s residential neighborhoods — the 700-acre Cyrus Crane Willmore-developed “Country living in the City” subdivision platted from 1929 through 1950 on the former David R. Francis farm — anchored by Francis Park (1917 Francis bequest, 60 acres, WPA-improved 1935), Willmore Park (1947, 105 acres on the River des Peres), Ted Drewes Frozen Custard on Route 66 (since 1941), Hampton Village Plaza, the Art Deco Vedder apartment building, and the longtime home of Stan Musial — offering publishing, directory, chamber, consulting, dining, fuel, IT, and real estate services across the Hampton, Chippewa (Route 66), Gravois, Eichelberger, and Donovan corridors.

St. Louis Hills is one of St. Louis’s 79 official neighborhoods, located at 38.5782° N, 90.3250° W in southwestern St. Louis City. The neighborhood’s boundaries are Chippewa Street (Missouri Route 366, formerly U.S. Route 66) to the northwest and north, Hampton Avenue to the east, Gravois Avenue to the southeast, and the St. Louis County border (along the River des Peres) to the southwest. The neighborhood occupies approximately 1.63 square miles (about 700 acres) and is home to roughly 7,516 residents (2020 census) in just over 4,000 housing units at a density of 4,610 people per square mile. St. Louis Hills spans ZIP code 63109 (with small portions of 63110 and 63139) and sits in the 2nd Ward of the City of St. Louis (currently represented by Alderman Tom Oldenburg). Surrounding neighborhoods include Lindenwood Park to the north, Southampton and Princeton Heights to the east across Hampton, North Hampton and Bevo Mill to the southeast across Gravois, and the city of Shrewsbury in St. Louis County to the west across the River des Peres. The neighborhood was historically marketed in the 1930s with the phrase “Country living in the City” — a tagline that captured its character as one of the last large expanses of open land within the 1876 city limits to be developed.

The land that became St. Louis Hills dates back to deeds granted in 1768 by St. Louis founder Pierre Laclède to pioneer French colonists Madame Ann Camp and Anton Reihle, originally encompassing 2,471 acres. (A 1797 Spanish land grant later confirmed parts of these holdings to Madame Anne Camp and her son-in-law Antoine Reilhe.) Camp and Reihle’s heirs sold the land to George C. Clarke, who in turn gave it to his two sons. When the village was chartered as the City of St. Louis in 1836 and again when the city and county separated in 1876 (with the western boundary set just west of the River des Peres), the land that would become St. Louis Hills remained open and forested — one of the last large undeveloped expanses within the 1876 boundary. In 1884, David Rowland Francis (1850–1927) purchased part of the property and established his vast country farm. Francis would go on to serve as Mayor of St. Louis (1885–1889), Governor of Missouri (1889–1893), U.S. Secretary of the Interior under President Cleveland, President of the Louisiana Purchase Exposition (the 1904 World’s Fair), and U.S. Ambassador to Russia (1916–1917). Francis originally considered using his farmland as the site for the World’s Fair, but logistics of transportation and construction to his land persuaded fair organizers to use Forest Park instead.

On Christmas Eve 1916 (with the gift recorded in 1917), Francis donated 60.3 acres of his farm to the City of St. Louis as the future Francis Park, located in what would become the geographic heart of the neighborhood. Francis died on January 15, 1927, and his estate sold the remainder of the farm. The buyer was Cyrus Crane Willmore (1889–1949), a young real estate developer who had come to St. Louis in 1912 after graduating from the University of Illinois law school and worked for an existing realty firm until 1922, when he founded the Cyrus Crane Willmore Organization. Willmore had already developed subdivisions in University City, Webster Groves, and Normandy, and in 1926 created Kingshighway Hills along Kingshighway between Fyler and Pernod Avenues. Willmore organized the St. Louis Hills Realty Company to acquire the Francis Farm, engaged surveyor Frederick Pitzman to lay out the subdivision, and platted St. Louis Hills No. 1 in 1929. The first section was bounded by the eastern line of the former Clark tract (about 125 feet west of Hampton Avenue), Eichelberger Street on the south, and Jamieson Avenue on the west.

Willmore’s vision for the neighborhood was explicit and unusually disciplined: a modern (1920s) middle-class and upper-middle-class community with tree-lined residential streets, churches and schools throughout, parks at the center, and commercial development limited to the perimeter on Hampton, Chippewa, and Gravois. (Originally Willmore also envisioned industrial uses along the River des Peres, but industrial development never materialized.) The neighborhood was marketed in the 1930s with the slogan “Country living in the City.” Willmore named streets in tribute to his English heritage — Nottingham Avenue, Devonshire Avenue, Tamm Avenue, Itaska Street, and others — while Eichelberger Street, the main east-west roadway through the center of the neighborhood, was originally named Clarke Road for early landowner George C. Clarke. The Great Depression and World War II altered the timing and content of Willmore’s vision, but the bulk of the area filled in with high-quality, all-brick residential construction from the 1930s through the 1950s. Subdivisions followed in sequence: St. Louis Hills No. 3 in 1942 (fronting Rhodes Avenue from Hampton to Childress); the first St. Louis Hills Estates in 1944 (west of Jamieson and south of Eichelberger); and St. Louis Hills Estates No. 2 in 1945 (bounded by Jamieson, Donovan, Eichelberger, Childress, Clifton, and Loughborough), with the full neighborhood completely platted by 1950.

St. Louis Hills is widely recognized as one of the city’s premier collections of Art Deco residential architecture. The homes and apartments surrounding Francis Park feature curved corners, casement and circular “porthole” windows, glass block trim, zigzag brick patterns, complicated brickwork, and Art Deco massing. The most iconic example is the Vedder apartment building on Nottingham Avenue near Francis Park — rich in Art Deco details with curved corners, casement and circular windows, complicated brickwork, and all six units featuring penthouses. The 6300 block of Bancroft Avenue contains a row of “Hansel and Gretel” Storybook bungalows facing well-maintained four-family Art Deco apartment buildings, all from the 1930s. Just west of Francis Park on Itaska Street stands one of the neighborhood’s most unusual landmarks: an eight-sided Art Deco house, each side a different length. East and west of Francis Park, stately 1.5- and 2-story homes line Tamm and Donovan Avenues with no two homes alike, including a Regency-style home facing the park on Tamm that was featured in a 1936 edition of McCall’s magazine alongside several other Hills homes. South of Francis Park, the architecture changes dramatically: St. Louis Hills Estates contains primarily postwar brick ranch homes on curving streets and generously sized lots, ranging from compact 2-bedroom homes to elegant, sprawling ranches with attached garages, main-floor family rooms, and ornamental fences only.

The neighborhood’s two parks are central to its identity. Francis Park — bounded by Nottingham Avenue (north), Eichelberger Street (south), Tamm Avenue (east), and Donovan Avenue (west) — covers 60.30 acres and sits at the neighborhood’s north-central core. Improvements funded in 1935 enabled the Works Progress Administration (WPA) to build the athletic fields, tennis courts, shelter house, walkways, picnic grounds, lily pond, and landscaping that define the park today. The park is famous for its central lily pond (where the bronze mermaid “Francine” by artist Robin Murez was unveiled July 23, 2002, gift of 26 St. Louis Hills donors) and for the 7-foot bronze statue of David R. Francis by Harry Weber dedicated in 2018. On July 7, 2010, the City of St. Louis Planning Commission adopted the Francis Park Strategic Plan. The annual St. Louis Hills Art in the Park festival, the SLHNA Easter Egg Hunt & Christmas Tree Lighting, the Run for The Hills 5K, and the Tour de Francis Park bike race (part of the St. Louis Gateway Cup series with Benton Park, Lafayette Square, and The Hill) all take place at the park. The 105-acre Willmore Park, bounded by Hampton Avenue (south), Jamieson Avenue (east), and the River des Peres (west), was created in 1947 with a 70-acre land donation from Cyrus Crane Willmore himself; it was the last major park to be developed in the City of St. Louis and was developed using funds from the 1955 Bond Issue.

St. Louis Hills is also home to several of St. Louis’s most beloved cultural landmarks. Ted Drewes Frozen Custard opened its Chippewa Street location at 6726 Chippewa in 1941, on the historic Route 66 alignment through the city — today the flagship location of the family-owned company founded by Ted Drewes Sr. in 1929, famous for its “concrete” (a frozen custard so thick it’s served upside down, invented at the Chippewa stand in 1959 by Ted Drewes Jr. for teenage customer Steve Gamber), expanded from five to twelve service windows by Ted Jr. in 1985, and named the “Best Ice Cream Shop in the World” by Irish marketing firm SoolNua in 2017. St. Louis Cardinals Hall-of-Famer Stan Musial lived in St. Louis Hills for much of his playing career — on Childress Avenue from 1948 to 1955 and on Westway Road from 1955 to 1965. The neighborhood is divided into two Catholic parishes: St. Gabriel the Archangel at Nottingham and Tamm Avenues in the north, and St. Raphael the Archangel at East Jamieson Avenue and Highfield Place in the south. The Nottingham School, an Art Deco / Modernist building with notable mosaics, sits at the northern edge of the west side of Francis Park. The Hampton Village Plaza shopping center (opened 1939 at Hampton and Chippewa, one of the first dedicated strip shopping centers in the United States) and the Southtown Centre on South Kingshighway serve as the neighborhood’s primary commercial anchors.

St. Louis Hills sits about ten miles southwest of Downtown St. Louis at 38.5782° N along the Hampton Avenue / Chippewa Street (Route 66) / Gravois Avenue / Eichelberger Street corridor, with seamless access to Forest Park (home of the Saint Louis Zoo, Saint Louis Art Museum, Missouri History Museum, and The Muny Theater, four miles north on Hampton), the Missouri Botanical Garden (four miles north on Kingshighway), Grant’s Farm (six miles south), Tower Grove Park, the Hampton Village Plaza and Southtown Centre shopping districts, Lindenwood Park, Princeton Heights, Southampton, North Hampton, Bevo Mill, and the city of Shrewsbury across the River des Peres.

The neighborhood experiences St. Louis’s full humid continental climate with hot, humid summers, cold winters with regular freezing temperatures, and an active spring severe weather season. Peak outdoor activity falls during spring, summer, and fall, when Francis Park and Willmore Park draw their largest crowds for the annual St. Louis Hills Art in the Park festival, the Run for The Hills 5K, the Tour de Francis Park bike race (part of the Gateway Cup), the SLHNA Easter Egg Hunt, picnics around the Francine the Mermaid lily pond, soccer and softball games on the WPA-built fields, tennis matches on the Francis Park courts, fishing on Willmore Park’s 5-acre and 3-acre stocked lakes, and walks past the David R. Francis bronze statue.

Cool seasons drive intense indoor activity at Ted Drewes Frozen Custard on Chippewa, LeGrand’s Market and Catering on Donovan, Smoke N Bones BBQ on Hampton, J. D. Streett & Company on Park Avenue, The Miller Group in Des Peres, St.Louis Real Estate Center in Richmond Heights, Slalom Consulting in Clayton, the Heartland St. Louis Black Chamber of Commerce, the Southwest Area Chamber of Commerce, the St. Louis Small Business Monthly, the St Louis Near Me Directory, Hampton Village Plaza, Southtown Centre, the Masses at St. Gabriel and St. Raphael the Archangel, and the restaurants and shops along Hampton, Chippewa, and Gravois. The neighborhood’s 1930s–1950s all-brick housing — Art Deco apartments, Storybook cottages, Tudor Revivals, the eight-sided Itaska Art Deco house, Regency McCall’s magazine homes, and St. Louis Hills Estates postwar ranches — has weathered nearly a century of Missouri climate.

## St Louis Place — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/st-louis-place

10 Best St Louis Businesses in St. Louis Place, MO (2026)

St. Louis Place Neighborhood Guide – St. Louis History & Businesses

St. Louis Place serves as one of St. Louis's most architecturally and historically significant near-North-Side neighborhoods — the heart of the 1850 Union Addition platted by John O'Fallon and Louis A. LaBeaume, anchored by 14.13-acre St. Louis Place Park (the city's first linear park), the Clemens House–Columbia Brewery Historic District (NRHP 1984, 226 buildings), the "Millionaires' Row" mansions of St. Louis Avenue, the Griot Museum, the Vashon Museum, and the rehabbed Brewery Apartments — offering publishing, government, non-profit, chamber, brokerage, and real estate services.

✓ Home of the 1850 Union Addition platted by John O’Fallon & Louis A. LaBeaume, the Clemens House–Columbia Brewery Historic District, and the Griot Museum of Black History ✓ St. Louis Place Park — the city’s first linear park (1850), 14.13 acres ✓ Avg. rating: 4.6/5 across 147 reviews

St. Louis Place Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

St. Louis Place, St. Louis

St. Louis Place serves as one of St. Louis’s most architecturally and historically significant near-North-Side neighborhoods — the heart of the 1850 Union Addition platted by John O’Fallon, Louis A. LaBeaume, and other wealthy St. Louisans, anchored by 14.13-acre St. Louis Place Park (the first linear park in the city), the Clemens House–Columbia Brewery Historic District (NRHP 1984, 226 contributing buildings), the “Millionaires’ Row” mansions of St. Louis Avenue, the Griot Museum of Black History, the George B. Vashon Museum, and the Brewery Apartments (the rehabbed 1892 Columbia/Falstaff Plant No. 5) — offering business publishing, government, non-profit incubation, brokerage, real estate, and chamber-of-commerce services across the Cass Avenue, North Florissant, North Jefferson, and St. Louis Avenue corridors.

St. Louis Place is one of St. Louis’s 79 official neighborhoods, located at 38.6500° N, 90.2135° W on the near North Side, approximately two miles northwest of Downtown St. Louis. The City of St. Louis defines the neighborhood’s boundaries as Palm Street to the north, Cass Avenue to the south, North Florissant Avenue to the east, and North Jefferson Avenue to the west (some sources, including the St. Louis Place Community Association, extend the northern boundary along Farrar Street and Natural Bridge Avenue). The neighborhood’s spine is St. Louis Avenue, running east-west through the center, paired with St. Louis Place Park — the linear “pleasure ground” that gives the neighborhood its name. The neighborhood is bordered by Hyde Park to the north, JeffVanderLou to the west, Carr Square and Columbus Square to the south, and Old North St. Louis to the east. St. Louis Place spans ZIP codes 63106 and 63107. The neighborhood sits in the 5th Ward of the City of St. Louis. As of the 2020 Census, the neighborhood was home to roughly 2,336 residents (a 20% decline from 2010), with a racial makeup that was 89.8% Black, 6.3% White, 0.5% Native American, 0.2% Asian, 2.9% Two or More Races, and 0.4% some other race; 1.4% of the population was of Hispanic or Latino origin.

The land that became St. Louis Place was originally settled by French colonists in the 1700s and early 1800s, who grew wheat and similar crops using the labor of enslaved Africans. In 1850, John O’Fallon, Louis A. LaBeaume, and other wealthy St. Louisans platted out a 54-block residential subdivision as part of the larger Union Addition, the most cohesively planned 19th-century subdivision in north St. Louis. As the centerpiece of the new subdivision, the developers reserved a strip of land down the center for public use, creating St. Louis Place Park — the first linear park in the City of St. Louis, predating Lafayette Square, Hyde Park, and Tower Grove Park. The park was originally one block wide and two blocks long, designed in the Picturesque manner with open, gently sloping lawn, canopy trees, and curvilinear, intersecting pathways. The city began improving the park in 1865, enclosing it and continuing improvements through the 1860s. An 1887 City Ordinance extended the park southward through the old site of the city reservoir to Maiden Lane, doubling it to four blocks long. The park was regraded in 1896, and in 1898 a fountain was added along with a statue of German poet Friedrich Von Schiller, donated by brewer Charles Stifel. (The Schiller statue was later removed and relocated to Memorial Plaza in 1975.) Today the park covers 14.13 acres.

During the 1840s, the first major wave of immigrants — German and Irish — established communities in the area north of downtown. A group of Irish from County Kerry settled in the western portion of present-day Carr Square in an area that became known as “Kerry Patch.” During the same period, Germans settled north of Carr in what is now the southeastern corner of the St. Louis Place neighborhood — an area referred to as “Little Paderhorn” after the German city. The neighborhood saw its first big wave of large-scale development in the 1870s, when prosperous German and Irish immigrants — many of them self-made businessmen and industrialists — built large ornate homes with strong architectural features reflecting their ethnic heritage. The Sheehan family arrived in 1849 and settled around St. Louis Avenue and North 23rd Street; twins John and Patrick Sheehan made a fortune in the 1860s from stone quarries between St. Louis Avenue and North 23rd. By the early 20th century, the neighborhood’s spine — St. Louis Avenue — had earned the nickname “Millionaires’ Row,” lined with stunning Italianate, Second Empire, and Romanesque Revival mansions. Improvements continued as Polish and Jewish immigrants arrived; the area became a center of German religious and cultural life in north St. Louis.

The neighborhood’s religious institutions reflect its layered immigrant history. St. Liborius Catholic Church, on 19th Street, was built in 1889–90, named for the patron saint of a section of Westphalia, Germany, and served the German Catholic population. St. Stanislaus Kostka Polish Church was the first Polish Catholic parish in the city, organized in 1879 for Polish Catholics then residing in the Kerry Patch area. St. Augustine Roman Catholic Church at 3114 Lismore, a towering Gothic Revival structure with a congregation that once boasted thousands of parishioners, was closed by the Archdiocese in 1982. Zion Lutheran Church, formed in 1860 as the last of four congregations to join in a Generalgemeinde (united congregation) under the leadership of Dr. C.F.W. Walther (first president of the Lutheran Church-Missouri Synod), built its present limestone Gothic structure at 21st Street in 1895 (architect Albert Knell). The Zion sanctuary, which seats 1,280, faces St. Louis Place Park and features one tall spire holding a 16-bell carillon — the St. Louis Post-Dispatch at the time predicted Zion Lutheran would be the most impressive church in North St. Louis.

The neighborhood’s most famous landmark was the James Clemens Jr. Mansion at 1849 Cass Avenue, designed by architect Patrick Walsh and built in 1859–60 for banker and millionaire James Clemens Jr. (1791–1878). Clemens was the son-in-law of St. Louis millionaire John Mullanphy and the uncle of Samuel Clemens (Mark Twain). The Italian Renaissance Revival mansion featured extensive use of cast iron for the front portico and window frames, with several female faces cast into the window frames believed to honor Clemens’s late wife Eliza Mullanphy (who died in 1853). In the 20th century the house became a convent under the Sisters of St. Joseph (who added a chapel and dormitory). Abandoned in 2000, it was gutted by fire in 2017 and subsequently demolished. The other anchor of the historic district is the massive Columbia Brewery complex at 2000 Madison Street, built in 1892 in the Romanesque Revival style by St. Louis brewery architects Jungenfeld & Co. — a major neighborhood employer through the era when St. Louis was the largest brewing center in the United States. After Prohibition the brewery reopened in 1933, merged with Falstaff Brewing in 1948 to become Plant No. 5, and brewed beer until 1967. The complex has since been rehabilitated as the Brewery Apartments. The Clemens House–Columbia Brewery Historic District was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1984, expanded in 1986, and contains 226 contributing buildings.

The neighborhood’s civic and educational legacy is anchored by the Frank P. Blair School, built in 1882 overlooking St. Louis Place Park — named for the popular pre-Civil War Missouri political figure and designed by School Board Architect H. William Kirchner as a 12-room schoolhouse in the High Victorian style with built-in capacity for additions. Kirchner designed the first 1888 addition (8 more classrooms) and his younger brother August Kirchner designed the second 1894 addition (bringing the total to 24 classrooms). Located in the predominately German neighborhood, the Blair School offered a daily hour of German-language instruction to attract the children of German residents to the public schools. The Blair School operated for nearly 100 years; in 1982, developer McCormack Baron purchased the building, commissioned a National Register nomination, and completed rehabilitation as the Blair School Apartments. The Community Development Administration funded a 1983 architectural survey of the Union Addition, and the St. Louis Place Historic District was nominated by the Preservation Research Office with principal author Michael R. Allen — the nomination process initiated by former Alderwoman April Ford-Griffin (D-5th) and overseen by Community Renewal Development under Executive Director Sal Martinez. The district’s nomination guide noted that the neighborhood has lost over 60% of the buildings that stood there in 1950.

Today St. Louis Place is undergoing significant transformation. The neighborhood sits in the middle of developer Paul McKee’s NorthSide Regeneration Project, which acquired large swaths of property through shell companies (the “Blairmont” LLCs) over the past two decades. The new National Geospatial-Intelligence Agency (NGA) Western Headquarters / NGA West campus is being constructed just east of the neighborhood, expected to bring approximately 3,000 employees to the area. Cultural institutions have anchored St. Louis Avenue, beginning with The Griot Museum of Black History & Culture (the premier African-American history museum in the region), followed by the George B. Vashon Museum and Research Center, the Frederick Douglass Museum of African American Vernacular Images, and Rudo’s Art Studio. Private owners have rehabbed and moved into historic homes along St. Louis Avenue, and new energy-efficient infill homes have been built. The St. Louis Place Community Association (stlouispca.org) coordinates ongoing advocacy and revitalization. The Murphy-Blair development north of Cass Avenue, the Preservation Square housing project, and the rehab of the Brewery Apartments have brought new residents. The neighborhood remains framed by 1850’s linear-park subdivision plan, its surviving 1870s–1890s mansions and churches, and the steady, persistent work of community activism.

St. Louis Place sits just two miles northwest of Downtown St. Louis at 38.6500° N along the Cass Avenue / North Florissant Avenue / North Jefferson Avenue / St. Louis Avenue corridor, with seamless access to the new NGA West federal campus, the Gateway Arch National Park, the Dome at America’s Center, the City Foundry, Crown Candy Kitchen in Old North St. Louis, Hyde Park, JeffVanderLou, Carr Square, and the Murphy-Blair / Preservation Square housing developments. Interstates 44, 64, 70, and 270 are all within minutes via Cass, Jefferson, and Florissant. The neighborhood experiences St. Louis’s full humid continental climate with hot, humid summers, cold winters with regular freezing temperatures, and an active spring severe weather season averaging 12–14 tornadoes annually across the region per the National Weather Service.

Peak outdoor activity falls during spring, summer, and fall, when St. Louis Place Park draws families to its playgrounds, basketball courts, horseshoe pits, and spray pools; when residents walk St. Louis Avenue admiring the surviving “Millionaires’ Row” Italianate, Second Empire, and Romanesque Revival mansions; when the limestone Gothic Zion Lutheran Church at 21st Street rings its 16-bell carillon; when the Griot Museum of Black History & Culture, the George B. Vashon Museum, the Frederick Douglass Museum of African American Vernacular Images, and Rudo’s Art Studio on St. Louis Avenue welcome school groups and visitors; and when the St. Louis Place Community Association hosts neighborhood block events.

Cool seasons drive intense indoor activity at the cultural institutions, the rehabbed Brewery Apartments in the 1892 Columbia/Falstaff complex on Madison Street, the rehabbed Blair School Apartments overlooking the park, the St. Louis Business Assistance Center downtown at 1200 Market Street, Inroads/St Louis at 10 South Broadway, the St. Louis Regional Chamber at One Metropolitan Square, the St. Louis Place Community Association, and the Murphy-Blair and Preservation Square housing communities. The neighborhood’s 1859 Gothic Revival Patrick Walsh-designed Clemens Mansion and 1892 Jungenfeld & Co. Romanesque Revival Columbia Brewery have weathered over 130 years of Missouri climate.

## The Gate District — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/the-gate-district

Neighborhood

The Gate District

Coordinates

38.6198° N, 90.2340° W

Ranked by 661+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  St. Louis’s largest planned community — named for its ornate iron gates ✓  Four sub-neighborhoods anchored by SLU Medical Campus & the Florist District ✓  Avg. rating: 4.5/5 across 661 reviews

## The Gate District Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 The Gate District, St. Louis

The Gate District serves as one of the most ambitious planned-community redevelopments in St. Louis history — a 400-acre south-central corridor named for the ornate iron gates that line its streets and frame its four sub-neighborhoods — anchored by the Saint Louis University Medical Campus and SSM Health, the Barr Branch Library, the historic Florist District, the Gallaudet School for the Deaf building on South Grand, the Lafayette Avenue restored streetscape, and the Habitat for Humanity and SLU Midtown Redevelopment Corporation single-family infill construction — offering medical and nursing education, B2B consulting and lead generation, secure data and document management, fuel and lubricant manufacturing, creative office leasing, international trade development, industrial flex space, and 24/7 HIPAA-compliant communications across the Chouteau, South Jefferson, Interstate 44, and South Grand corridors.

The Gate District is one of St. Louis’s 79 official neighborhoods, located at **38.6198° N, 90.2340° W** in south-central St. Louis City. The neighborhood’s boundaries are **Chouteau Avenue** on the north, **Interstate 44** on the south, **South Jefferson Avenue** on the east, and **South Grand Boulevard** on the west. The neighborhood sits within **ZIP code 63104**, in the **19th Ward** of the City of St. Louis. Surrounding neighborhoods include **Downtown West** and the **Midtown / Saint Louis University main campus** to the north across Chouteau, **Lafayette Square** to the east across Jefferson, **Tower Grove East** and **Shaw** to the south across I-44, and **Tiffany** and **Compton Heights** to the southwest. The Gate District is uniquely subdivided into **four named sub-neighborhoods** — **Buder Park**, **Eads Park**, **St. Vincent Park**, and **Lafayette Terrace** — each represented by its own neighborhood association in addition to the Gate District East Association.

The name **“The Gate District”** comes directly from the **ornate iron gates** that line the streets and define the entrances to each of the four sub-neighborhoods — including gates that previously stood in front of the **old St. Louis City Hospital** and were preserved as part of the master plan when the planned community was established. By the early 20th century, the area both north and south of Park Avenue contained mostly **two- and four-family flats**, with a diverse working-class mixture of **Irish, Italian, Polish, and other immigrant residents**, rural migrants, and a growing African-American community. The area immediately north of Chouteau — **Mill Creek Valley** — had become a predominantly African-American neighborhood, and as African American residents moved south of Chouteau, their children continued attending **L’Ouverture School** at 2612 Papin Street until the school moved to its current site on Hickory in **1950**.

The neighborhood’s most famous historic resident is Pulitzer Prize-winning poet and memoirist **Maya Angelou**. Born in St. Louis in **1928**, Angelou returned in **1936** from out of state at age eight to live with her maternal grandparents at **2714½ Caroline Street**, in what is now part of The Gate District — an address that figures prominently in her landmark 1969 autobiography *I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings*. The community’s 20th-century history was profoundly shaped by the **1968 federal 235-subsidy program** for low-income homeownership and the predatory **“block busting”** real-estate practices that followed: unscrupulous companies bought homes at low cost from departing white residents and resold or rented them to low-income African-American families, triggering widespread panic. Between 1960 and 1970, the area east of Compton lost **62 percent of its population**. By the early 1970s, much of the area’s historic housing stock had become derelict or been demolished.

A succession of redevelopment plans for the area arose beginning in **1973**, when the first proposal — called **“New Town”** — suggested leveling the area east of Compton, creating a large lake surrounded by expensive homes, and enveloping the whole development in a stone wall. None of those early plans came to fruition. By **1990**, a new plan emerged for the City to acquire the property from Pantheon Corporation and redevelop the area as **six individual “neighborhoods”** (later consolidated to four) making up The Gate District. A highly acclaimed **Miami architecture firm** was retained by the City to formulate the master plan, which incorporated the **restoration of older buildings combined with new construction**, with each sub-neighborhood receiving its own **distinct entrance gates and tiny parks**. The Gate District thus became the **largest planned community in St. Louis City history**.

The most transformative recent chapter began in **2017**, when **Saint Louis University** and **SSM Health** formed the **St. Louis Midtown Redevelopment Corporation** as a joint effort to oversee all development within a **400-acre planning area** that includes The Gate District West. Under executive director **Brooks Goedeker**, the corporation worked with former 19th Ward Alderwoman **Marlene Davis** to re-establish the **Gate District West Neighborhood Association**. In **September 2020**, SLU and the Neighborhood Association held a groundbreaking ceremony that has since produced **40 single-family homes, 12 condos, and seven Habitat for Humanity Saint Louis homes** built on formerly vacant SLU-owned parcels along Vista Avenue and the surrounding streets. As of **2023**, 36 of the new single-family homes had sold, with the neighborhood’s home-improvement fund (created from sale proceeds) supporting renovations to seven of the existing historic homes.

The Gate District’s defining institutional anchor is the **Saint Louis University Medical Campus**, which sits primarily within the Gate District West sub-neighborhood (bordered by Grand to the west, Compton to the east, Chouteau to the north, and I-44 to the south). Most of SLU’s health-sciences education buildings and many of its research facilities are located here, including the **SLU School of Medicine**, the **Trudy Busch Valentine School of Nursing**, the **Doisy College of Health Sciences**, the **Center for Advanced Dental Education**, and the **College for Public Health and Social Justice**. The medical campus shares the corridor with **SSM Health**’s adjacent hospital and clinical operations, making Grand and Vista one of the densest concentrations of medical and health-sciences employment in the State of Missouri. The neighborhood is also home to St. Louis’s historic **Florist District**, a cluster of long-standing wholesale florist operations that has anchored the area for generations.

The Gate District’s civic and cultural institutions include the **Barr Branch Library** on South Jefferson (one of 15 branches in the St. Louis Public Library system), **Hodgen Elementary School** on California Avenue, and the **International Welcome School** on South Grand — a unique St. Louis Public Schools facility that provides immigrant students with accelerated English language training before they transition into mainstream classrooms. The architecturally significant **Gallaudet School for the Deaf** building at 1616 South Grand Boulevard (closed in 2012) continues to add historic beauty to the corridor. Today, The Gate District is home to roughly **3,500 residents** across approximately **1,636 housing units**, with a **72.5% Black, 19.2% White, 1.9% Asian, 5.1% Two or More Races, and 1.1% Some Other Race** demographic mix (2020 census; 3.0% Hispanic or Latino origin). With the SLU Medical Campus expansion, the four sub-neighborhood iron gates, and the steady wave of single-family infill construction, The Gate District is one of the most actively redeveloping corners of the City of St. Louis.

Image: The entrance to The Gate District on Compton Avenue — one of the ornate iron gates that give the neighborhood its name and define the entrances to its four sub-neighborhoods — Wikimedia Commons / CC BY-SA

The Gate District sits just south of Downtown St. Louis along the Chouteau Avenue, South Jefferson, Interstate 44, and South Grand Boulevard corridor, where the climate follows St. Louis’s full humid continental pattern with hot, humid summers, cold winters, and an active spring severe-weather season. Hot summers with highs regularly reaching the upper 80s°F bring peak outdoor activity to the four sub-neighborhood iron-gated entrances (Buder Park, Eads Park, St. Vincent Park, Lafayette Terrace), Terry Park and its restored basketball court, the Lafayette Avenue restored streetscape, and the new Vista Avenue infill homes. Cool springs and falls are the busiest seasons for the Florist District wholesale operations, the Barr Branch Library, and the SLU Medical Campus walking paths along Compton and Grand. 

Cold winters with periodic snow and ice shift commerce indoors to the SLU School of Medicine, SSM Health clinical buildings, the Locust Street and Washington Avenue office complexes, and Hodgen Elementary and the International Welcome School classrooms. The neighborhood’s low-elevation position south of the Mill Creek Valley railroad corridor gives it a sheltered south-central microclimate compared to the more exposed Downtown West and Lafayette Square corridors to the north and east. The late-19th-century brick and stone two- and four-family flats, Lafayette Avenue housing stock, ornate iron gates, and the 1920s-era buildings around the medical campus have weathered nearly a century of Missouri climate with remarkable durability.
