## The Greater Ville — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/the-greater-ville

Neighborhood

The Greater Ville

Coordinates

38.6621° N, 90.2483° W

Ranked by 162+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  10 of 10 verified Greater Ville corridor providers ✓  All businesses verified via Google Business ✓  Avg. rating: 4.8/5 across 162 reviews

## The Greater Ville Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 The Greater Ville, St. Louis

The Greater Ville is the upside-down-U-shaped north St. Louis neighborhood wrapping The Ville on three sides — home to the 1945 Shelley House at 4600 Labadie Avenue (National Historic Landmark and site of the 1948 Supreme Court case *Shelley v. Kraemer* that ended racially restrictive covenants) and to Chuck Berry’s 3137 Whittier Street home (NRHP-listed December 2008) where he wrote “Maybellene,” “Johnny B. Goode,” and “Roll Over Beethoven.” The corridor offers chamber advocacy, business publishing, sustainability consulting, federal contract coaching, managed IT, enterprise consulting, technology integration, regional chamber networking, experiential marketing, and women’s business support services across the broader north St. Louis area.

The Greater Ville is a north St. Louis neighborhood centered at **38.6621° N, 90.2483° W** in zip codes 63107, 63113, and 63115, forming an upside-down-U shape that wraps around the smaller inner neighborhood of The Ville on three sides. Its official boundaries run north to **Natural Bridge Avenue**, south to **Dr. Martin Luther King Drive** and **St. Louis Avenue**, east to **N. Vandeventer Avenue**, and west to **Marcus Avenue**. Interior streets including **Labadie Avenue**, **Whittier Street**, **Dick Gregory Place** (renamed to honor the comedian and activist born at 1803 N. Taylor Avenue just across the boundary in The Ville), and the broad **Natural Bridge Avenue** commercial corridor anchor the neighborhood’s daily life. The 131-acre **Fairground Park** lies along the neighborhood’s northern edge, offering a swimming pool, roller rink, 9-acre stocked fishing lake, walking trails, and athletic fields that have served north St. Louis families since 1908. The 2020 Census recorded nearly 8,000 residents across the neighborhood’s historic brick housing stock.

The Greater Ville is surrounded by seven neighborhoods: **Penrose** and **O’Fallon** to the north, **JeffVanderLou** to the east, **Covenant Blu-Grand Center** to the southeast, **Vandeventer** and **Lewis Place** to the south, and **Kingsway East** to the west. As St. Louis Magazine described it, the neighborhood is “an oddly shaped neighborhood, more of an upside-down U, wrapping itself around the Ville ‘Proper.’” That shape itself encodes the history: before 1948, The Ville (the inner neighborhood) was African American and largely middle class, while The Greater Ville (the outer ring) was white middle class — protected from Black ownership by 1900-1910-era racially restrictive covenants embedded in property deeds.

The case that changed that for the entire country arose from one house in this neighborhood: **4600 Labadie Avenue**. The **J.D. Shelley family** had moved from **Starkville, Mississippi in 1930**, fleeing racially-motivated violence. After renting for a time, in **1945** J.D. and **Ethel Lee Shelley** purchased the home through prominent Black realtor **James Bush** via a sympathetic white intermediary, unaware that the block carried a 1911 covenant prohibiting sale to anyone of the “Negro or Mongolian race” for 50 years. They moved in on **September 11, 1945**. White neighbors **Louis and Fern Kraemer** sued to void the sale. While the trial court held for the Shelleys, the Missouri Supreme Court reversed in 1946; the Shelleys appealed to the U.S. Supreme Court in 1947 with NAACP representation. The U.S. Office of the Solicitor General filed an amicus curiae brief in support of the Shelleys — the first time in a civil rights case. The Court ruled unanimously in *Shelley v. Kraemer* (1948)

The **Shelley House** at 4600 Labadie was declared a **National Historic Landmark in 1990** and is now part of the National Park Service’s African American Civil Rights Network. It remains one of the most consequential private residences in American constitutional history. Twenty years later, the **Fair Housing Act of 1968** — passed six days after the slaying of Martin Luther King Jr. — outlawed racial, religious, and sex discrimination in real estate transactions outright, completing what the Shelley decision had begun.

The opening of The Greater Ville to Black ownership after 1948 brought some of the city’s most significant cultural figures to the neighborhood. **Chuck Berry** — the father of rock and roll — and his wife **Themetta “Toddy” Suggs** purchased the 1910-built, three-bedroom, one-bathroom red-brick bungalow at **3137 Whittier Street** in 1950 for $4,500, shortly after the Shelley decision invalidated the area’s covenants. They lived there from 1950 to 1958, raising daughters Ingrid and Melody, and Chuck Berry composed and rehearsed his landmark songs in this small house — **“Maybellene”** (1955), **“Roll Over Beethoven”** (1956), “Too Much Monkey Business” (1956), **“Rock and Roll Music”** (1957), “School Day” (1957), “Sweet Little Sixteen” (1958), and **“Johnny B. Goode”** (1958) — recordings that defined a genre. During those years he worked at a Chrysler assembly plant and as a photographer for the St. Louis Argus. The **Chuck Berry House** was added to the National Register of Historic Places on December 12, 2008, retains features including the original awning with a letter “B” for Berry on the front porch, and has been vacant since the early 2000s.

The **St. Louis Argus**, founded in 1912 by brothers J. E. and William Mitchell, is the oldest continuously operating Black business in St. Louis and has been a voice for The Greater Ville across more than a century of change. **Holy Ghost Catholic School**, integrated by **Archbishop Joseph Ritter in 1947** — a year ahead of the Supreme Court’s Shelley decision and seven years before *Brown v. Board of Education* — demonstrated what moral courage looked like at the parish level. The **Julia Davis Branch Library** on Natural Bridge Avenue has served The Greater Ville residents through the full arc of the neighborhood’s transformation. The neighborhood is served by St. Louis Public Schools including **Cote Brilliante Elementary School** and **Hickey Elementary School** (both on Cora Avenue), **Farragut Elementary School** on Sullivan Avenue, and the private **De La Salle Middle School** on Kennerly Avenue (a Catholic school).

Today, **Natural Bridge Avenue** is the neighborhood’s primary commercial and institutional corridor. The **Urban League of Metropolitan St. Louis Entrepreneurship and Women’s Business Center**, which opened in July 2024 at 4401 Natural Bridge Avenue in a beautifully restored 1920-era former Commerce Bank building, is the anchor of a new chapter in The Greater Ville’s commercial life — providing startup counseling, the SBA-backed “Ready!” graduation series, and workshops specifically designed for women founders and minority entrepreneurs. Beloved community staples include **Firehouse 282** (a proud Black-owned business in the former 1900s firehouse on Vandeventer Avenue), **Jaden’s Diner**, **St. Louis Fatburger**, and **Midtown Bar & Grill**. Bus routes 19, 4, and 70 connect The Greater Ville to downtown St. Louis just under 4 miles to the east and to the broader regional transit network.

Like its inner neighbor The Ville, The Greater Ville sustained significant damage in the **EF3 tornado on May 16, 2025** and is part of the “A Stronger Northside” neighborhood plan adopted in December 2025, which includes the MLK Cultural Boulevard project and a modular housing pilot now underway. Community organization **4theVille** — co-founded by neighborhood advocate Ms. Ranson and others — gives tours of the neighborhood and advocates for recognition of the Shelley House, the Chuck Berry House, and the full arc of the neighborhood’s civil rights and cultural significance.

Image: The Shelley House at 4600 Labadie Avenue — National Historic Landmark (1990) and site of the 1948 Supreme Court case *Shelley v. Kraemer*, where J.D. and Ethel Lee Shelley’s 1945 home purchase produced the constitutional ruling that ended court enforcement of racially restrictive covenants — Wikimedia Commons, CC BY 3.0 by FrancisNancy

The Greater Ville sits on the flat Grand Prairie glacial till upland of north St. Louis at 38.6621° N, roughly four miles from downtown, with terrain becoming more varied near the western Marcus Avenue edge. The neighborhood shares St. Louis’s full humid continental weather exposure — hot, humid summers, cold winters, and an active spring tornado corridor that the neighborhood now knows from direct experience after the May 16, 2025 EF3 event. Peak outdoor activity falls during spring and fall, when 131-acre Fairground Park’s 9-acre stocked fishing lake, swimming pool, roller rink, walking trails, basketball courts, tennis courts, and athletic fields for baseball, softball, soccer, and football draw the heaviest neighborhood use. Cool seasons drive intense indoor activity at the Urban League of Metropolitan St. Louis Entrepreneurship and Women’s Business Center on Natural Bridge Avenue, the Julia Davis Branch Library, the St. Louis Argus offices, and the neighborhood’s community-anchor businesses. Winter shifts commerce indoors to Firehouse 282 on Vandeventer Avenue, Jaden’s Diner, St. Louis Fatburger, and Midtown Bar & Grill. The neighborhood’s early-20th-century brick construction has weathered more than a century of Missouri climate, and the post-tornado rebuilding continues through the 2026 season.

## The Hill — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/the-hill

Coordinates

38.6184° N, 90.2870° W

Ranked by 6,502+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  America’s largest most intact Italian neighborhood — 52-block Little Italy ✓  Anchored by St. Ambrose Catholic Church (1903) & the Hill 2000 Neighborhood Association ✓  Avg. rating: 4.9/5 across 6,502 reviews

## The Hill Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 The Hill, St. Louis

The Hill serves as the largest and most intact Italian-American neighborhood in the United States — a 52-block Little Italy in south-central St. Louis built on the fire-clay deposits that drew German, Irish, and African-American immigrants in the 1830s and Northern Italian and Sicilian immigrants from the 1880s onward — anchored by St. Ambrose Catholic Church (1903, current Lombard Romanesque Revival building 1926, modeled after the Basilica of Sant’Ambrogio in Milan), the Hill 2000 Neighborhood Association, Yogi Berra and Joe Garagiola’s adjacent boyhood homes on Elizabeth Avenue, Berra Park, the Italian Heritage Parade & Festa, and the green-white-and-red fire hydrants painted in Italian flag colors — offering Italian bakeries and confectioners, family-owned restaurants and taverns, plant-based soap manufacturers and artisanal goods, design-build construction firms, antique markets, craft breweries, professional services, and community-led cultural commerce across the Marconi, Macklind, Shaw, and Sublette Avenue corridors.

The Hill is one of St. Louis’s 79 official neighborhoods, located at **38.6184° N, 90.2870° W** in south-central St. Louis City. The neighborhood’s official boundaries are **Manchester Avenue (Route 100)** on the north, **South Kingshighway Boulevard** on the east, **Columbia and Southwest Avenues** on the south, and **Hampton Avenue** on the west. Total area is **0.97 square miles**, with a 2020 census population of **2,487 residents** and a density of approximately **2,600 per square mile**. ZIP code is part of **63110**, and the neighborhood sits in the **5th Ward** of the City of St. Louis under Alderman Matt Devoti. Surrounding neighborhoods include **Kings Oak** and **Cheltenham** to the north, **Forest Park Southeast** and **Southwest Garden** to the east, **Southwest Garden** and **North Hampton** to the south, and **Ellendale** and **Clifton Heights** to the west. The neighborhood’s defining streets are **Marconi Avenue** (the cultural and commercial spine), **Macklind Avenue**, **Shaw Avenue**, **Daggett Avenue**, **Wilson Avenue**, **Edwards Street**, and **Sublette Avenue**.

The neighborhood’s name comes from its proximity to the highest point in the City of St. Louis — about an eighth of a mile south of the official boundaries at the intersection of **Arsenal Street and Sublette Avenue**, near the former **St. Louis State Hospital** (today the St. Louis Psychiatric Rehabilitation Center, opened 1869). The area was originally called **Cheltenham**, a rugged stretch of land on the city’s western edge five miles from the Mississippi River. The pivotal event came in **1849**, when a massive fire destroyed 15 blocks of downtown St. Louis and new city building codes required brick construction — triggering an explosive demand for fire brick. After the **Evens and Howard Fire Brick Company** and its clay mines opened in **1854–1855**, more than a dozen brick and tile factories sprang up along the banks of the River Des Peres, drawing waves of German, Irish, and African-American workers. Italians from **Northern Italy (especially Lombardy) and later Sicily** began arriving in the **1880s** to work in the clay mines, initially living in boarding houses before bringing families and building permanent homes. By 1910, six foundries were fully operational.

By the **1920s**, The Hill had become a self-sustaining Italian-American community where residents could find everything within walking distance — grocers, bakeries, butchers, tailors, barbers, and doctors. **St. Ambrose Catholic Parish**, founded in 1903 by members of St. Aloysius Gonzaga Parish to serve the area’s growing Lombard immigrant population, became the spiritual and cultural heart of the neighborhood. After the first wooden church burned in **1921**, a brick-and-terracotta replacement designed by architect **Angelo Corrubia** in the **Lombard Romanesque Revival** style was completed in **1926**, modeled after the **Basilica of Sant’Ambrogio in Milan**. Parishioners bought individual bricks and pews to fund construction. The **parish school** opened in **1906** and helped bridge the divide between northern and southern Italian families. The Italian Immigrants sculpture by Rudolph Edward Torrini, installed in front of the church, commemorates the 100-year history of both Northern and Southern Italians settling here. Across the street is **Piazza Imo**, the neighborhood’s informal central square.

The Hill’s most celebrated native sons are baseball Hall of Famer **Yogi Berra** (1925–2015), an 18-time All-Star and 10-time World Series champion with the New York Yankees, and his lifelong friend **Joe Garagiola** (1926–2016), a Major League catcher turned broadcaster — their **boyhood homes face each other on Elizabeth Avenue**. The neighborhood also produced four of the five St. Louisans on the U.S. soccer team that famously defeated England in the **1950 FIFA World Cup** — **Frank Borghi**, **Charlie Colombo**, **Gino Pariani**, and Frank “Pee Wee” Wallace — a story told in the book and 2005 film *The Game of Their Lives*. Other notable residents include MLB player **Frank Crespi** (the first Hill resident on a World Series championship team, the 1942 Cardinals), NFL player **Ben Pucci**, and singer **Toni Carroll**. The neighborhood was historically known by the disparaging nickname **“Dago Hill”** — the Mississippi blues singer **Luella Miller** recorded “Dago Hill Blues” in 1926, **Charlie Patton** referenced the area in his 1934 single “Love My Stuff,” and **Tampa Red and Georgia Tom Dorsey** sang of The Hill’s Prohibition-era bootlegging in their 1932 “You Can’t Get That Stuff No More.”

The Hill thrived through the 1950s and 1960s. **More than 1,020 men from The Hill served in World War II**; 23 did not return, and their names are inscribed on a bronze plaque in the back of St. Ambrose Church and on a column in Piazza Imo. Manufacturing companies including **Magic Chef**, **Marlo Coil**, and **Style Craft** employed hundreds. Politics shifted from Republican to Democratic in the 1930s, and leaders like **Midge Berra** used political connections to improve the neighborhood. By the **mid-1970s**, however, two threats emerged: in **1971**, the USDOT began construction on a section of **Interstate 44** that cut through nearly a third of The Hill, demolishing **109 homes**. Under the leadership of **Father Salvatore Polizzi**, residents raised **$50,000** to build a car bridge on Edwards Street crossing over the highway to reconnect the community. The collective action gave rise to the **Hill 2000 Neighborhood Association** in **1970** with the motto **“Don’t Move, Improve”** — the campaign that ensured The Hill survived while other Little Italys across America disappeared.

Today The Hill remains a tightly knit, multi-generational community where homes are often sold privately rather than listed on the open market and where Italian-American families pass houses down across four or five generations. The 2020 census recorded a racial makeup of **90.4% White, 2.5% Black, 1.1% Asian, 4.5% Two or More Races, and 1.2% Some Other Race** (3.3% Hispanic or Latino origin). The neighborhood is famous for the **fire hydrants painted green, white, and red** in the colors of the Italian flag, the **Italian-flag crosswalks at Shaw and Marconi** (completed 2022), the **Italian Heritage Parade & Festa**, **Carnevale** (February), **Ferragosto** (August), the **Hill Wine Walk**, the **Soap Box Derby**, the **Christmas on the Hill** festivities, and the annual **Giro della Montagna** cycling race (part of the Gateway Cup over Labor Day weekend). The **St. Louis Italian Language Program** is housed at Gateway Science Academy on Fyler Avenue. St. Louis’s Italian sister city is **Bologna**. **Berra Park**, the neighborhood’s only green space, covers nearly five acres with soccer and softball fields, a playground, and seasonal events.

The Hill’s commercial life is concentrated along Marconi Avenue, Macklind Avenue, Shaw Avenue, and Daggett Avenue, where the businesses listed on this page operate. The neighborhood’s defining anchors include the **Hill 2000 Neighborhood Association** at 1935 Marconi (volunteer-led since 1970), **Herbaria** at 2016 Marconi (24 years of plant-based, vegan, chemical-free handcrafted soaps), **Missouri Baking Co** at 2027 Edwards (the 1924 Italian bakery famous for cassata cake, cannoli, gooey butter cake, and chocolate drops), **Anthonino’s Taverna** at 2225 Macklind (the Greek-Italian taverna with 3,957+ Google reviews famous for its toasted ravioli), **The Hill Antique Market** at 4923 Daggett (75+ vendors in a renovated brick warehouse), **2nd Shift Brewing** at 1601 Sublette (the craft brewery and tasting room founded by Steve Crider), **Turner & Associates** at 2215 Macklind, **The Korte Company** at 5700 Oakland (66 years of design-build construction), the **Hill Neighborhood Center** at Daggett and Marconi, the **1929 Big Club building** at Shaw and Marconi (today a photography studio), and dozens of family-run grocers, butchers, bakeries, salons, and bocce gardens that have made The Hill an internationally recognized Little Italy destination.

Image: St. Ambrose Catholic Church on The Hill — the 1926 Lombard Romanesque Revival church modeled after the Basilica of Sant’Ambrogio in Milan and the cultural heart of St. Louis’s Italian-American community — Wikimedia Commons / CC BY-SA

The Hill sits on high ground in south-central St. Louis just south of Forest Park, a few blocks north of the highest point in the city at Arsenal and Sublette, where the climate follows a classic Midwest four-season pattern. Hot, humid summers with highs regularly reaching the upper 80s°F bring peak outdoor activity to Berra Park, the Italian Heritage Parade & Festa, the Hill Wine Walk, Carnevale and Ferragosto, the Soap Box Derby, the Giro della Montagna cycling race, and the patios along Marconi and Macklind Avenues. Cool springs and falls are the busiest seasons for the green-white-and-red-painted fire hydrants and Italian-flag crosswalk strolls, St. Ambrose feast days, and Christmas on the Hill. 

Cold winters with periodic snow and ice shift commerce indoors to St. Ambrose Catholic Church, Missouri Baking Co, the Hill Antique Market warehouse, Anthonino’s Taverna, the family-owned grocers and butchers along Marconi, 2nd Shift Brewing’s industrial tasting room, and the Hill Neighborhood Center. The neighborhood’s elevated position on the city’s highest ridge gives it slightly drier conditions and faster drainage than the lower-lying corridors to the east and south. The late-19th- and early-20th-century brick shotgun and bungalow homes — built from the same fire clay that drew the original immigrants — have weathered nearly a century and a half of Missouri climate with the durability that gave the neighborhood its industrial start.

## The Ville — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/the-ville

## The Ville Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 The Ville, St. Louis

The Ville is the historic 42-square-block north St. Louis neighborhood that served from 1911 to 1950 as the cultural center of Black St. Louis — home to Sumner High School (the oldest Black high school west of the Mississippi), Homer G. Phillips Hospital (which trained more Black doctors than any institution in the world), and Annie Malone’s Poro College. Notable alumni include Chuck Berry, Tina Turner, Arthur Ashe, Dick Gregory, Grace Bumbry, and Robert Guillaume. The corridor offers business publishing, chamber advocacy, executive consulting, AI/cloud technology, business district management, adaptive-reuse hospitality, enterprise IT, fuel manufacturing, B2B marketing, and experiential agency services across the broader north St. Louis area.

The Ville is one of the most historically significant neighborhoods in American urban history — a compact **42-square-block rectangle** centered at **38.6591° N, 90.2446° W** in zip codes 63113 and 63107 on the city’s north-central plateau, bounded north by **St. Louis Avenue**, south by **Dr. Martin Luther King Drive**, east by **Sarah Street**, and west by **Taylor Avenue**. Interior streets including **Whittier Street** (formerly Wash), **Annie Malone Drive** (formerly Goode Avenue, renamed to honor the neighborhood’s greatest entrepreneur), **Cottage Avenue**, **W. Kennerly Avenue**, **Aldine Avenue**, **Maffitt Avenue**, and **St. Ferdinand Avenue** form a tight residential grid that, at its peak in the 1930s and 1940s, was among the most prosperous Black communities in America. The name itself is a shortening of “Elleardsville,” the old settlement named for horticulturist Charles M. Elleard who arrived from San Francisco in the 1860s; the area was annexed by St. Louis in 1876, and Black residents coined “The Ville” in the late 1880s as the neighborhood became one of the few parts of St. Louis free of racially restrictive covenants.

The transformation was rapid and well-documented. In 1873, St. Louis built **Elleardsville Colored School No. 8** (later renamed Simmons School) — the neighborhood’s first Black institution and the earliest African American institution in the community. After Missouri’s 1911 introduction of racially restrictive housing covenants concentrated Black residents in the few open neighborhoods, The Ville’s African American population went from **8% in 1920 to 86% by 1930** and reached **95% by 1950** — the Ville of proud Black St. Louis folklore. James Kennerly had owned a large **Cote Placquemine (Persimmon Hill) plantation** near present-day Kennerly and Taylor avenues, which was destroyed by fire in 1863. Original street names like Kennerly, Wash (now Whittier), Goode (now Annie Malone Drive), and Taylor preserve traces of those early landowners.

The institutions that defined The Ville’s golden era are without parallel in the story of Black St. Louis. **Sumner High School** (4248 West Cottage Avenue) was founded in 1875, named for abolitionist U.S. Senator Charles H. Sumner, and relocated to The Ville in 1910 (current building constructed 1908). It is the **oldest Black high school west of the Mississippi River**, and was added to the National Register of Historic Places in December 2025. Sumner alumni include **Chuck Berry**, who was born and raised in The Ville before recording the songs that shaped rock and roll; comedian and activist **Dick Gregory**, born at 1803 N. Taylor Avenue; actress and singer **Tina Turner** (born Anna Mae Bullock), who moved to The Ville at 16 and graduated from Sumner in 1958; opera diva **Grace Bumbry**; tennis champion **Arthur Ashe**, who completed his senior year at Sumner; Emmy-winning actor **Robert Guillaume**; civil rights attorney **Frankie Freeman**; activist Julia Davis; African Methodist Episcopal pioneer Charleton Tandy; and operatic baritone Robert McFerrin. Lloyd Lionel Gaines — plaintiff in the 1938 *Gaines v. Canada* Supreme Court case — was also tied to the neighborhood.

Across St. Ferdinand Avenue from Sumner stood **Poro College**, founded in **1917** by **Annie Turnbo Pope Malone** — one of America’s first Black female millionaires, whose hair-care and cosmetics empire (the “Poro System”) employed nearly 200 African Americans in St. Louis alone and reached 75,000 worldwide. Annie Malone arrived in St. Louis from Illinois in 1902; one of her early salespeople was Sarah Breedlove, who later became **Madam C. J. Walker**. The Poro Building — a million-dollar complex housing her Poro College of Beauty Culture, manufacturing plant, and numerous community organizations — became the symbol of African American enterprise in St. Louis. Malone was a generous philanthropist to the St. Louis Colored Orphans’ Home (which moved to The Ville in 1922 thanks to her gift and was later renamed the **Annie Malone Children’s Home**, now the Annie Malone Children and Family Service Center at 2612 Annie Malone Drive, NRHP-listed), St. James A.M.E. Church, and successfully pushed the city to pave streets in The Ville.

**Homer G. Phillips Hospital** (2601 North Whittier Street, NRHP-listed) opened in **1937** after a 15-year campaign led by attorney Homer G. Phillips — who was **assassinated in 1931**, four years before he could see the hospital built in his name. By 1961, Homer G. Phillips had trained more Black doctors and nurses than any other institution in the world, employing more than 800 people at peak as a fully Black-staffed teaching hospital at a time when racial segregation barred Black physicians from white St. Louis hospitals. It closed in 1979 amid community protests and is now a 220-unit senior living facility; its red brick towers remain the visual anchor of the neighborhood, with the Homer G. Phillips Ambulatory Care Center continuing health services in the building. The Ville was designated a **local historic district in 1987**.

**Tandy Park** (5.6 acres, established 1918, at Kennerly and Billups) is the neighborhood’s central green space, featuring the **Arthur Ashe Tennis Courts**, the **Tuskegee Airman Football Field**, basketball courts, a skating rink, and a playground. The adjacent **Tandy Community Center** (4206 West Kennerly Avenue, built 1938 as Public Works Administration Project No. 8483 by the same architect who designed Homer G. Phillips Hospital) is an Art Deco recreation facility on the National Register of Historic Places (NRHP since 1999), remaining in active use. **St. James African Methodist Episcopal Church** (organized 1885) and **Antioch Baptist Church** (organized 1884) anchor the neighborhood’s spiritual and civic life. Other landmarks include the **Marshall School** (4342 Aldine Avenue, built 1918, NRHP-listed), **Turner Middle School** (4235 Kennerly, originally the Charles Henry Turner Open Air School for Crippled Children, opened 1925 and named for the distinguished entomologist who taught at Sumner from 1908-1922), and the former **Lincoln University Law School** (1939-1955, later absorbed into the University of Missouri Law School).

The Ville today faces real challenges alongside real momentum. An **EF3 tornado struck the neighborhood on May 16, 2025**, causing significant damage including the temporary displacement of Sumner High School students. A **“Stronger Northside”** neighborhood revitalization plan was formally adopted in December 2025, a **$3.2 million ARPA-funded modular housing pilot** is underway on Aldine Avenue, and community organization **4theVille** is developing an MLK Cultural Boulevard project to anchor the southern corridor. The 2000 census recorded 97% Black residency among 2,695 residents; the neighborhood today continues working to rebuild and reinvest. The Annie Malone Children and Family Service Center continues its century-long mission, and the work of rebuilding is ongoing.

Image: Homer G. Phillips Hospital, the 1937 NRHP-listed landmark at 2601 North Whittier Street that trained more Black doctors than any institution in the world and is now a 220-unit senior living facility — Wikimedia Commons, CC BY 3.0 by Onegentlemanofverona

The Ville sits on the flat urban plateau of north St. Louis at roughly 460 to 490 feet elevation — open terrain that proved tragically exposed when the May 16, 2025 EF3 tornado cut directly through the neighborhood. The area experiences St. Louis’s full humid continental weather range: hot, humid summers with frequent 90°F days, cold winters with regular freezing temperatures, and a spring severe weather season with real tornado corridor exposure that the neighborhood now knows firsthand. Peak outdoor activity falls during spring and fall, when Tandy Park’s Arthur Ashe Tennis Courts, Tuskegee Airman Football Field, basketball courts, skating rink, and playground draw the heaviest neighborhood use, alongside the Annie Malone Children and Family Service Center grounds and the surrounding institutional spaces. Cool seasons drive intense indoor activity at the Tandy Community Center (1938 Art Deco PWA building), Sumner High School, the Homer G. Phillips senior living complex, St. James A.M.E. Church, and Antioch Baptist Church. Winter shifts commerce indoors to the institutional anchors that have weathered more than a century of Missouri climate. The neighborhood’s late-19th and early-20th-century brick construction has shown remarkable resilience.

## Tiffany — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/tiffany

Coordinates

38.6218° N, 90.2459° W

Ranked by 1,380+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  8 of 8 verified Tiffany corridor providers ✓  All businesses verified via Google Business ✓  Avg. rating: 4.4/5 across 1,380 reviews

## Tiffany Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Tiffany, St. Louis

Tiffany is the south-central St. Louis neighborhood named for the 1890 Tiffany streetcar line that ran along the former Tiffany Street (now 39th Street) and transformed Mary McRee’s 1888-sold farmland into a middle-class commuter suburb. The neighborhood’s 215-structure Tiffany Neighborhood Historic District (NRHP-listed December 1978) is anchored by the $550 million SSM Health Saint Louis University Hospital that opened in 2020 on Grand Boulevard. The corridor offers boutique retail, B2B marketing, diner hospitality, HIPAA-compliant telecom, commercial printing, and luxury jewelry services across the broader SLU Medical Center area.

Tiffany is one of St. Louis’s 79 official neighborhoods, centered at **38.6218° N, 90.2459° W** in zip code 63110 on the city’s south-central section. Its boundaries run north to **Chouteau Avenue**, south to **Interstate 44**, east to **Grand Boulevard**, and west to **39th Street** — the same corridor once called Tiffany Street, from which the neighborhood takes its name. That western boundary, 39th Street, was home to the massive United Railways Company car shop complex, the hub of St. Louis’s entire streetcar network, and the Tiffany streetcar line that gave the area its identity long before the neighborhood was formally named. The eastern spine, **Grand Boulevard**, carries the region’s most-used bus route and anchors the SLU/SSM Health medical campus that now defines the neighborhood’s eastern face. Interior streets — **McRee Avenue, Lafayette Avenue, Blaine Avenue, and Folsom Avenue** — weave through a residential fabric that has been on the **National Register of Historic Places since December 1978**. Adjacent neighborhoods include **Forest Park Southeast** to the north, **Shaw** to the south across I-44, the medical district to the east, and Botanical Heights (the rebranded former McRee Town) to the west.

The name Tiffany has nothing to do with jewelry. It derives from **Louis and P. D. Tiffany**, a family of landowners whose tract ran along what is now 39th Street. That street was called Tiffany Street until 1926; the Tiffany streetcar line took the same name. Before any of this, the land was part of the French colonial *Prairie des Noyers*, laid out in 1769 as communal agricultural strips west of the early St. Louis settlement. After the Louisiana Purchase, the tract passed through various hands before **William McRee** — a retired Army officer and Surveyor General of the Missouri and Illinois District — assembled most of it between 1825 and 1832. His widow **Mary Urquhart McRee** developed a subdivision called **McRee City in 1869** (Mary married Samuel McRee at age 14, lived in New Orleans during the Mexican-American War, and inherited the land when Samuel died of cholera). Mary sold her remaining land in 1888, just before her death at 83. The purchaser was the **Dundee Land and Investment Company**, run by **Thomas A. Scott**, a young Canadian real estate developer described by contemporaries as “one of the most daring real estate operators St. Louis has ever known.”

Scott platted the land as **Dundee Place** in June 1889 (covering the area between McRee and Park Avenues, westward from Grand to Old Manchester Road, now Vandeventer) and held a grand auction in June 1891, naming two new streets in a deliberate bipartisan gesture: **Folsom** (for Frances Folsom Cleveland, wife of President Grover Cleveland) and **Blaine** (for James G. Blaine, the Republican nominee Cleveland had just defeated). With the completion of the **Grand Avenue Viaduct in 1890** and electrified streetcar lines by the turn of the century, Tiffany developed rapidly as a middle-class commuter suburb. Nearly all of the **215 contributing structures** in the Tiffany Neighborhood Historic District were built between 1891 and 1928 — a catalogue of late-Victorian and early-20th-century brick construction, from the celebrated rows of **blonde brick homes on Blaine Avenue** to Dutch colonial houses on **Rutger Street** to classical ornament reflecting the post-1904 World’s Fair confidence in the city’s future.

Architectural landmarks within Tiffany include the **Bell Telephone Exchange Building** (1902, architect F.C. Bonsack) at McRee and Grand and the **Marquette Apartments** (1912, Lafayette and Spring) which anchor the historic commercial and residential character of the neighborhood’s core. Parallel to all of this, the medical corridor on Grand Boulevard was taking shape: the **Josephine Heikamp Hospital** opened in 1900, **Saint Louis University** acquired its medical school in 1903, and the landmark **Firmin Desloge Hospital** — a 15-story green-roofed tower — opened in 1933. The $550 million **SSM Health Saint Louis University Hospital** that replaced Desloge opened **September 1, 2020**, and a new $300 million **Cardinal Glennon Children’s Hospital** is planned for the former **Pevely Dairy site** at Grand and Chouteau with a projected 2027 completion. As one local urbanist put it, “residential Tiffany is in reality a different neighborhood than ‘Tiffany’” — the neighborhood is more than half owned by SSM Health and Saint Louis University, with the residential core compressed into the six square blocks south of the medical campus.

The neighborhood’s single park, **Tiffany Park** (3801 McRee Ave, 1.1 acres, established 1980), was created by the **Midtown Medical Center Redevelopment Corporation** as part of a late-1970s stabilization effort. Its western boundary was deliberately designed to **block through-traffic on Blaine Avenue** — a practical crime-reduction measure that also gave the park an unusual triangular presence on the residential block. The park features a tiered concrete semi-circle, a pavilion, a playground, and a basketball court, and remains the neighborhood’s primary outdoor gathering point. The **Tiffany Community Association**, formed in the early 1990s when the MMCRC program stalled, developed the long-range plan that helped stabilize reinvestment through the 2000s and into the present. In 2017, the Association partnered with Park Central Development on a new neighborhood plan addressing the residential core’s future.

Today Tiffany is anchored on its eastern edge by the SLU/SSM Health medical campus — a Level 1 trauma center that draws patients from across the region — while the residential core continues to fill in with renovations and adaptive reuse along McRee, Folsom, and Blaine Avenues. About **75% of Tiffany residents rent their properties**, with many being SLU medical residents who buy homes during residency and either sell upon graduation or stay as permanent neighborhood residents. Downtown St. Louis sits just over 3 miles to the east; Forest Park is approximately 2 miles to the west; the Missouri Botanical Garden is just across I-44 in adjacent Shaw.

Image: Skyline of the Tiffany Neighborhood showing the SLU/SSM Health medical campus along Grand Boulevard and the surrounding 215-structure NRHP-listed historic district — Wikimedia Commons, CC BY 3.0 by Onegentlemanofverona

Tiffany sits on gently rolling terrain at 38.6218° N that drops noticeably from Grand Boulevard westward to 39th Street, an elevation change visible in the way some blocks of Folsom Avenue sit perched well above street level. The neighborhood experiences St. Louis’s full range of humid continental weather — hot, humid summers with many days above 90°F and a spring severe weather season that brings real tornado risk — while **Interstate 44’s southern boundary creates a hard acoustic and atmospheric edge** that distinguishes the neighborhood’s interior from the more open terrain of Shaw to the south. Peak outdoor activity falls during spring and fall, when Tiffany Park’s tiered concrete semi-circle, pavilion, basketball court, and playground draw the heaviest neighborhood use. Cool seasons drive intense indoor activity at the SLU/SSM Health Saint Louis University Hospital, Cardinal Glennon Children’s Hospital, and the surrounding professional services along Grand Boulevard. Winter shifts commerce indoors to Tiffany’s Original Diner on Manchester (open late on Friday and Saturday nights), the Bell Telephone Exchange Building, the Marquette Apartments, and the SLU/SSM Health medical complex. The neighborhood’s 1891-1928 brick construction has weathered more than a century of Missouri climate with the durability typical of well-built late-Victorian residential stock.

## Tower Grove East — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/tower-grove-east

Ranked by 9,256+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

## Tower Grove East Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Tower Grove East, St. Louis

Tower Grove East serves as one of south St. Louis’s most architecturally splendid Victorian-era neighborhoods — a 0.51-square-mile residential and commercial corridor settled by 1850s German Catholic dairy farmers and built up by the relatives of the prominent German immigrants who founded Compton Heights and Flora Place — anchored by Tower Grove Park (the 289-acre 1868 Henry Shaw gift just across South Grand Boulevard), the South Grand Business District’s internationally renowned restaurant row, Roosevelt High School, the Carpenter Branch Library, KDHX 88.1 FM, Stray Dog Theatre at the Tower Grove Abbey, and the four-square Victorian and Italianate flats with carved fretwork, stained-glass windows, and elaborate staircases — offering historic-restoration general contractors, eco-friendly retail and zero-waste marketplaces, biotechnology and lab services, boutique real estate, handyman services, scratch-kitchen restaurants, and community-led cultural organizations across the South Grand, Shenandoah, Arsenal, and Gravois Avenue corridors.

Tower Grove East is one of St. Louis’s 79 official neighborhoods, located at **38.6029° N, 90.2364° W** in south-central St. Louis City. The neighborhood’s official boundaries are **Shenandoah Avenue** on the north, **Nebraska Avenue** on the east, **Gravois Avenue (MO Route 30)** on the south, and **South Grand Boulevard** on the west. Total area is **0.51 square miles**, with a 2020 census population of **5,708 residents** and a density of approximately **11,000 per square mile**. ZIP codes are parts of **63104** and **63118**, and the neighborhood sits in the **7th Ward** of the City of St. Louis under Alderwoman Alisha Sonnier. Surrounding neighborhoods include **Compton Heights** to the north across Shenandoah, **Fox Park** and **Benton Park West** to the east, **Gravois Park** and **Dutchtown** to the south across Gravois, and **Shaw** and **Tower Grove South** to the west across South Grand. The neighborhood is approximately six miles south of downtown St. Louis.

Tower Grove East was originally part of **La Petite Prairie**, settled by the **French** in the early 1700s. Grazing land was held in common, and farming land was divided into long narrow tracts. The commons system was abandoned around 1800, and the land began to be sold into private hands. By the **1850s**, much of the property was owned by **German Catholics** — recent immigrants fleeing the aftermath of Germany’s 1848 civil war — who found the area ideal as pasture land for their dairy farming operations. The neighborhood takes its name from its position bordering the east entrance of **Tower Grove Park** across South Grand Boulevard. The park was deeded to the City of St. Louis in **1868** as a gift from **Henry Shaw** (1800–1889), the 19th-century English-born businessman and botanist who owned much of the surrounding land and also founded the adjacent **Missouri Botanical Garden**.

As the German immigrants prospered, the wealthiest began building mansions in the adjacent **Compton Heights** and along **Flora Place** — the city’s exclusive private streets just north of Shenandoah. Their siblings, cousins, and extended family members later settled the pastures of what is now Tower Grove East and built beautiful homes and flats of their own, using similar architectural detail to what is found in the wealthier Compton Heights and Flora Place. The typical Tower Grove East house was built on the **four-square plan** — a pyramid or hipped roof on a two-story cube, often with a pressed-brick or limestone course separating the stories. Buyers chose architectural elements from **pattern books** that illustrated multiple styles of windows, doors, stairways, and fireplace mantels. The often austere exterior facades typically hide a wealth of richly designed entries with **carved fretwork**, built-in hall benches, mirrors, and bookcases, wood paneling, **stained-glass windows**, and elaborate staircases.

The neighborhood’s civic and educational anchors include **Roosevelt High School** on Hartford Street (opened 1925, designed by Rockwell M. Milligan in an impressive Collegiate Gothic style), **Shenandoah Elementary School** on Shenandoah Avenue, and the **Carpenter Branch Library** on South Grand Boulevard (one of 15 branches in the St. Louis Public Library system). The neighborhood is home to St. Louis’s most-listened-to community radio station, **KDHX 88.1 FM** (historically located at 3504 Magnolia and later moved to Grand Center), and the **Stray Dog Theatre** at the **Tower Grove Abbey** on Tennessee Avenue. A monument in nearby Reservoir Park commemorates **The Westliche Post**, the German-language newspaper that Joseph Pulitzer later acquired and merged to form the **St. Louis Post-Dispatch**. Houses of worship include **St. Wenceslaus Church** on Oregon Avenue, **St. Pius V Catholic Church** and **Messiah Lutheran Church** on South Grand Boulevard, **Holy Trinity Baptist Church** on Minnesota Avenue, and the **Body of Christ Temple** on Louisiana Avenue.

The neighborhood’s commercial heartbeat is the **South Grand Business District** (also known simply as **South Grand**) — a six-block stretch of South Grand Boulevard that has been branded and improved through the South Grand Community Improvement District. The district anchors one of the most internationally diverse restaurant rows in the Midwest, with **Vietnamese, Thai, Ethiopian, Italian, Chinese, Middle Eastern, Japanese, Lebanese, Afghan, and Mexican** restaurants alongside specialty grocers, rare bookstores, jewelry boutiques, natural food stores, and international gift shops. Many storefronts retain their original late-19th-century commercial architecture, and most restaurants offer outdoor dining that animates the streetscape year-round.

Tower Grove East’s history through the second half of the 20th century followed the trajectory of many south-side St. Louis neighborhoods. The 2000 census recorded **7,211 residents** across **3,485 housing units** (79% occupied, 39% owner-occupied, 61% renter-occupied). Like much of south St. Louis, the neighborhood lost 8% of its population between 1990 and 2000. By 2020, the population had stabilized at **5,708 residents** with a racial makeup of **55.6% White, 28.9% Black, 5.0% Asian, 5.3% Hispanic/Latino**, and other backgrounds. The neighborhood’s late-20th-century recovery accelerated in the 2010s and 2020s, with extensive historic restoration along Shenandoah, Arsenal, Humphrey, and the smaller cross streets, and with the South Grand business district’s emergence as a regional destination for international dining and independent retail.

Today, Tower Grove East’s active redevelopment is driven by neighborhood and immigrant-service organizations, historic-restoration and property-management firms, eco-friendly landscaping, floral design, scratch-kitchen pubs, live theatre, and the steady pull of Tower Grove Park’s programming. The businesses listed on this page include the **Tower Grove East Neighborhood Association (TGENA)**, the **St Louis Near Me Directory**, **Stray Dog Theatre** at the Tower Grove Abbey on Tennessee Avenue, **Seasons Cycle** landscaping on Louisiana Avenue, **Heritage Properties** on Shenandoah Avenue (the historic-home rehab and property-management firm founded by Warren Popp and Tristan Mackenzie), the **International Institute of St. Louis** on Arsenal Street (the 1919-founded immigrant and refugee service hub and host of the Festival of Nations), **Botanicals Design Studio** on South Grand (the custom florist founded by Keith McDonnell and Dean Riebeling), **Riley’s Pub** on Arsenal Street, **Tick Tock Tavern** on Magnolia Avenue, and the irreplaceable **Tower Grove Park** itself with 7,868+ Google reviews, 13 restored early-20th-century picnic pavilions, the Piper Palm House, the Tower Grove Farmers’ Market, “sip and stroll” events, and Food Truck Friday.

Roosevelt High School — the 1925 Collegiate Gothic landmark on Hartford Street, designed by district architect Rockwell M. Milligan, in the Tower Grove East neighborhood of St. Louis, Missouri. Photo: St Louis Near Me Directory.

Tower Grove East sits roughly six miles south of downtown St. Louis between South Grand Boulevard and Nebraska Avenue, where the climate follows a classic Midwest four-season pattern. Hot, humid summers with highs regularly reaching the upper 80s°F bring peak outdoor activity to Tower Grove Park’s 289 acres, the Tower Grove Farmers’ Market, Food Truck Friday, the “sip and stroll” events, the Piper Palm House, the 13 restored Victorian picnic pavilions, and the South Grand restaurant patios. Cool springs and falls are the busiest seasons for the historic four-square architectural walking tours along Shenandoah, Arsenal, and Humphrey, Carpenter Branch Library programming, the International Institute’s outdoor events, and Stray Dog Theatre productions at the Tower Grove Abbey. 

Cold winters with periodic snow and ice shift commerce indoors to the South Grand restaurant row’s Vietnamese, Thai, Ethiopian, Italian, Chinese, Middle Eastern, Japanese, Afghan, Lebanese, and Mexican restaurants, the neighborhood pubs like Riley’s Pub and Tick Tock Tavern, Botanicals Design Studio’s floral work, KDHX 88.1 FM, and the Stray Dog Theatre seasonal program. The neighborhood’s position at one of the higher residential elevations in south St. Louis gives it good drainage and slightly drier conditions than the lower corridors along Mississippi-side neighborhoods to the east. The late-19th-century pressed-brick and limestone four-square Victorian housing stock — with stained-glass windows, carved fretwork, and elaborate staircases — has weathered nearly 150 years of Missouri climate with remarkable durability, sustaining the work of historic-restoration and property-management firms like Heritage Properties.

## Tower Grove South — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/tower-grove-south

Ranked by 9,542+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  10 of 10 verified Tower Grove South corridor providers ✓  All businesses verified via Google Business ✓  Avg. rating: 4.9/5 across 9,542 reviews

## Tower Grove South Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Tower Grove South, St. Louis

Tower Grove South sits in the heart of south St. Louis as one of the city’s most populous and culturally diverse neighborhoods — formerly known as Oak Hill, anchored by the 289 acres of Henry Shaw’s Tower Grove Park (one of the largest existing Victorian walking parks in America), bordered by the international South Grand Business District and the Morganford Road corridor — offering restoration services, craft brewing, women-led design and creative agencies, sustainable retail, smart-home integration, event venues, weekly farmers’ markets, and historic-neighborhood community amenities for residents and businesses across the central-south St. Louis corridor.

Tower Grove South sits in south-central St. Louis at **38.5968° N, 90.2681° W** in zip code 63116. Its boundaries form a rectangle: **Arsenal Street** to the north (along the southern edge of Tower Grove Park), **Chippewa Street** to the south, **Grand Boulevard** to the east, and **Kingshighway Boulevard** to the west. The neighborhood’s defining streets are **South Grand Boulevard** (the international restaurant and retail spine on the east), **Morganford Road** (the secondary commercial corridor running roughly north-south through the western half), **Arsenal Street** (the northern boundary along the park), and **Chippewa Street** (the southern boundary). Tower Grove South is bordered by **Tower Grove East** across South Grand Boulevard, **Shaw and Southwest Garden** across Tower Grove Park to the north, **Dutchtown and Bevo Mill** to the south, and **Southampton** to the west. The neighborhood encompasses approximately 1.49 square miles and is one of the most densely populated neighborhoods in St. Louis, with a 2010 Census count of **13,333 residents** — placing it consistently among the top three most populated neighborhoods in the city.

The neighborhood’s earliest known name was **Prairie des Noyers**, a rich agricultural area in the nineteenth century divided into long strips called **“common fields”** and sold to anyone willing to farm the land. The **Russell family** acquired a significant portion of these common fields to build their family estate, **Oak Hill** — the name by which Tower Grove South was originally known. Shortly after building their estate, the Russells discovered coal in the fields and founded the **Parker-Russell Mining Company**, employing many in the community as miners. The **1849 cholera epidemic** decimated the community and effectively closed the mines; Parker-Russell shifted to extracting fire clay, and the successful brick industry brought jobs and German immigrants to the area in the 1850s. Many of these German immigrants were dairy farmers from the 1848 civil war who found Prairie des Noyers ideal as pasture land. In **1876 the city limits of St. Louis expanded to include Tower Grove South**, and following the opening of the Grand viaduct and the 1872 dedication of Tower Grove Park, the area saw rapid real estate speculation. The McDonalds were the first major landowners between Grand and Gustine to sell out to a major subdivision — **Tower Grove Heights**.

Most of Tower Grove South was built between the 1860s and the 1930s, following the extension of streetcar lines from downtown St. Louis. The neighborhood’s housing stock is dominated by **two-and-a-half-story Italianate and Victorian brick homes** featuring stained glass, intricate staircases, and the red brick mined from the surrounding clay fields. Closer to the park in the **Tower Grove Heights** sub-neighborhood (extending from Arsenal to Utah Street, east of Gustine to Grand), single-family brick Victorian houses are most prominent, complete with their own neighborhood association and branding. The 1920s brought **Cape Cod-style houses**, while older **shotgun houses** are still found in the western part of the neighborhood near Kingshighway. The architectural showpiece of the Grand Boulevard business district is the **1928 South Side National Bank building** — one of St. Louis’s best examples of Art Deco architecture — which nearly faced the wrecking ball before grassroots preservationists rescued it. Today this 10-story structure houses **The Noble by Culinary Canvas**, an elegant event venue retaining the original bank vault, high ceilings, and ornate gold detailing inspired by Greek and Roman design.

The defining institution of Tower Grove South is **Tower Grove Park**, the 289-acre Victorian-era walking park donated to the City of St. Louis by **Henry Shaw** on October 20, 1868 and opened to the public in 1872. Shaw — the English-born merchant, botanist, and philanthropist who also founded the adjacent **Missouri Botanical Garden** — designed the park with conditions that it “shall be used as a park forever” and that the city make an “annual appropriation” for its maintenance. As per Shaw’s estate, Tower Grove Park remains the only public city park in St. Louis managed by an independent Board of Commissioners. The park features **32 picturesque Victorian-era pavilions**, including the chinoiserie Chinese Pavilion (Pagoda), the Turkish Pavilion with its onion-domed roof, the identical Cypress Pavilions, the large Sons of Rest Pavilion, and the 1931 Henry Shaw Memorial Pavilion. Other landmarks include the **Piper Palm House** (1878) — the oldest standing greenhouse west of the Mississippi River, designed by George I. Barnett, the “dean” of Missouri architects — its identical 1885 sister the **Piper Plant House**, **The Ruins** (constructed by Shaw from limestone blocks of the 1867 Lindell Hotel fire), the three-tiered cast-iron Fountain, and **Café Madeleine**. The park is home to nearly 400 species of trees, bushes, and flowering plants, is part of the **Mississippi Flyway**, and is widely renowned for spring and fall bird migration watching.

The **South Grand Business District** on the eastern edge of the neighborhood is one of St. Louis’s most beloved international destinations. After a serious slump in the 1970s, the district became a magnet for new **Vietnamese immigrants** who opened restaurants and shops along Grand Boulevard, beginning what locals describe as having “more ethnicities represented on one street than Epcot.” Today the corridor hosts **Café Mochi** (Japanese/Vietnamese), **King & I** (Thai), **Grand Bistro** (the 2025 reopening of the legendary **Pho Grand**, which served Vietnamese cuisine for 33 years from 1989 to 2022 under Michael and Tami Trinh), **Cafe Natasha’s** (Persian), **Snō** (modern Asian dim sum), **Meskerem Ethiopian Restaurant**, **Sameem Afghan Restaurant**, **Apotheosis Comics & Lounge**, **The Gin Room** (founded by Natasha Bahrami, the only American inducted into the Gin Hall of Fame), and **Dunaway Books**. Each August, Tower Grove Park hosts the **Festival of Nations**, the region’s largest multicultural celebration, with 75 nations represented through food, dance, and music across the Centene Charitable Foundation Forest Stage, the Enterprise Magnolia Stage, and the World Music Stage. The **Tower Grove Farmers’ Market** has run from May through October every Saturday morning since 2006, just west of the Wading Pool Pavilion, and is widely regarded as the best farmers’ market in the St. Louis region.

The **Morganford Road business district** on the western flank of the neighborhood emerged after 2000 with substantial reinvestment in restaurants, bars, retail, and a neighborhood-scale storefront grocery store specializing in natural and locally grown foods. **Civil Life Brewing Co.** (established 2011 by Jake Hafner) anchors the southern end on Holt Avenue, serving Cask Marque-certified traditional English and American ales. Other key Morganford businesses include **Black Sheep Whiskey & Wine**, **Road Crew Coffee & Cycles**, **Mama 2’s Biscuits**, and the 45-year-old **A&M Cyclery**. Tower Grove South’s children attend **Mann Elementary**, **Fanning Middle Community Education Center**, and **Roosevelt High School** in the St. Louis City School District. The neighborhood is also home to **Artscope**, a non-profit offering art classes and camps from a small cottage surrounded by nature, and several active community organizations through the **Tower Grove South Neighborhood Association**.

Tower Grove South’s most recent census data (2020) records the neighborhood as **57.6% White, 21.8% Black, 7.7% Asian, 7.6% two or more races, and 8.4% Hispanic/Latino** — making it one of the most racially and ethnically diverse neighborhoods in St. Louis. The median age is **36**, with 35.2% of residents between 25 and 44, average household size of 2, and approximately 5,027 households. Median home values are approximately **$225,600**, with park-front renovated brick homes commanding premium prices. The neighborhood is a major urban-revitalization success story — once in decline by the 1970s and 1980s, it has been steadily rehabilitated since the 1990s, with the South Grand corridor leading first followed by Morganford after 2000. Today Tower Grove South is consistently ranked a top-10 place to live in St. Louis. Because of its walkability, mixed-use commercial spine, and proximity to Tower Grove Park, the neighborhood draws a service ecosystem of restoration, design, smart-home, hospitality, and boutique-retail providers that reflect both its historic stock and its young, diverse, urban resident base. The listings on this page draw from that broader Tower Grove South ecosystem.

Image: The Turkish Pavilion at Tower Grove Park, with its distinctive onion-domed roof — one of the 32 Victorian-era pavilions in the 289-acre park donated to the City of St. Louis in 1868 by Henry Shaw — Wikimedia Commons / CC BY-SA 3.0 by TMS63112

Tower Grove South sits on flat-to-gently-rolling terrain just south of Tower Grove Park, where the city follows a classic Midwest four-season pattern. Hot, humid summers with highs in the upper 80s°F bring peak outdoor activity to the park’s 32 Victorian pavilions, the Saturday-morning Tower Grove Farmers’ Market (May through October), Civil Life Brewing’s pet-friendly patio, the South Grand patios at Café Mochi and Grand Bistro, and the August Festival of Nations — the region’s largest multicultural festival, drawing crowds from across the metro.

Spring and fall are the busiest seasons for the park’s renowned bird-migration watching (Tower Grove Park sits on the Mississippi Flyway), tennis and pickleball on the grass and hard courts, and walks along Morganford Road’s independent retail. Cold winters with periodic snow shift commerce indoors to the Piper Palm House (the oldest greenhouse west of the Mississippi), The Social Goods Marketplace and Urban Matter on South Grand, the historic 1928 South Side National Bank building (now The Noble), and Tower Grove South’s many late-19th and early-20th-century brick Italianate and Victorian homes — built from the very clay mined locally by the Parker-Russell Mining Company — that have weathered well over a century of Missouri seasons.

## Vandeventer — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/vandeventer

38.6500° N, 90.2516° W

Ranked by 208+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

## Vandeventer Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Vandeventer, St. Louis

Vandeventer is the historic north-central St. Louis neighborhood that gave its name to Vandeventer Place (the 1870 Julius Pitzman-designed Gilded Age private street whose limestone gates now stand in Forest Park) and was the West Belle Place childhood home of Lloyd Lionel Gaines, whose 1938 Supreme Court case *Missouri ex rel. Gaines v. Canada* became a landmark precursor to *Brown v. Board of Education*. The corridor offers chamber advocacy, event venue rental, industrial calibration, IT consulting, senior housing, fuel manufacturing, and experiential marketing for residents and businesses across the broader Grand Center / Central West End area.

Vandeventer is City of St. Louis **Neighborhood #58**, centered at **38.6500° N, 90.2516° W** on the city’s north-central plateau in zip code 63113/63108. Its official boundaries run north to **Dr. Martin Luther King Drive**, south to **Delmar Boulevard**, east to **N. Vandeventer Avenue**, and west to **N. Newstead Avenue** — a compact rectangle of rectilinear streets that local urbanists have called a “true rectilinear beauty.” The neighborhood’s internal spine, **N. Sarah Street**, runs north-south through the center and anchors the area’s primary park (Turner Park) and most significant recent development. **N. Spring Avenue** runs parallel one block west, serving as the address of the Urban League’s **North Spring Empowerment Center** at 929 N. Spring. The neighborhood lies between the **Central West End** to the south, **The Ville** to the north, and just west of the **Grand Center Arts District** (home to the Fox Theatre and Powell Symphony Hall) — within easy reach of every Vandeventer address.

The land that became Vandeventer began as part of St. Louis’s French colonial *Grande Prairie Commonfield*, organized into long narrow arpent strips that extended westward from the original riverfront settlement and were maintained under common enclosure until about 1800. By 1850 the strips had been consolidated into larger tracts suitable for subdivision. In the mid-1850s, the **Cote Brilliante** subdivision was laid out to the west and **Prairie Place along Belle Glade** to the north. In **1858**, florist and horticulturist **Charles M. Elleard** purchased a tract bounded by present-day Martin Luther King Drive, Annie Malone Drive, Cote Brilliante, and Newstead Avenues just north of today’s Vandeventer, establishing a conservatory and greenhouses. By 1870, a small town called **Elleardsville** — later known as **The Ville** — had emerged around Elleard’s floral nursery, with its town hall at the corner of present MLK Drive and Whittier Street. An **Abbey Trotting Race Track** occupied the neighborhood’s northern section between roughly Page, Whittier, MLK, and Taylor avenues until about 1877. German and Irish immigrants settled the area through the turn of the 20th century, and streetcar lines — including the **Hodiamont line** — linked Vandeventer to the rest of the growing city.

The neighborhood’s most celebrated landmark was **Vandeventer Place**, a privately maintained gated enclave platted on **June 4, 1870**, on land purchased from the estate of New York stockbroker **Peter Lewis Vandeventer** (who came to St. Louis in the 1860s with his brothers William and Henry Barnum Vandeventer from their Wall Street firm at 6 Wall St., New York City). Funded by businessmen Napoleon Mulliken, Charles H. Peck, and John McCune, the street was designed by **Julius Pitzman** — St. Louis’s most influential private street designer — with a rigid rectilinear plan featuring a grass-median boulevard, fountains at each end, and lavish mansions on each side. The first houses were built in 1871. The **monumental limestone entry gates** were added in 1894, designed by architect **Louis Mullgardt**. At its peak, the enclave’s 50 mansions housed the city’s wealthiest citizens: Governor and U.S. Ambassador to Russia **David R. Francis** (mansion No. 16, designed by Charles K. Ramsey in 1880), oil magnate **H. Clay Pierce** (whose mansion at No. 40 reportedly cost $800,000), chemical patriarch **Edward Mallinckrodt** (No. 26), banker **John Lionberger** (No. 27, designed by the great American architect **Henry Hobson Richardson** in 1886), and tobacco heir Hiram Shaw Liggett (No. 32).

Vandeventer Place declined after 1900 as its residents moved west to the newer private places of Portland and Westmoreland (where David Francis and the Mallinckrodts built new Central West End mansions), and by the 1920s the rise of Grand Center as a theater and streetcar hub had largely emptied the street. By **1947** the Veterans’ Administration demolished the eastern half for the John Cochran VA Hospital; the western half fell to the Juvenile Justice Center around **1950**. Only the **east Grand Boulevard limestone gate** by Louis Mullgardt was preserved and relocated to **Forest Park near the Jewel Box**, where it stands today as a Forest Park Statue & Monument. The story is captured in the 2024 book *Vandeventer Place — Gilded Age St. Louis: A Social, Economic, Architectural and Urban History of the Legendary Post-Civil War Enclave* by Mike Boyd and Thomas Grady.

Vandeventer carries one of the most important civil rights histories of any St. Louis neighborhood. **Lloyd Lionel Gaines** (1911–1939?), who lived with his family in a house on **West Belle Place** within the neighborhood, graduated first in his class from Vashon High School and became the first African American to apply to the **University of Missouri School of Law** — then a segregated institution. After rejection, with NAACP support, he pursued the case to the U.S. Supreme Court. The resulting *Missouri ex rel. Gaines v. Canada* (1938) ruling held that Missouri must provide Black students equal legal education within the state — a landmark precursor to the eventual dismantling of *Plessy v. Ferguson* through *Brown v. Board of Education*. Gaines disappeared in Chicago on **March 19, 1939**, and was never found; his case remains among the most consequential and haunting in American legal history.

**Turner Park**, the neighborhood’s 1.4-acre public park placed into ordinance in **1937**, is located just southwest of the intersection of C.D. Banks Avenue and N. Sarah Street. It is named for **James Milton Turner** (c. 1839–1915), born into slavery in St. Louis County in 1839 or 1840, freed in 1843, educated in secret schools (despite the 1847 Missouri law prohibiting teaching Black people), and a graduate of Oberlin College. Turner became Missouri’s foremost post-Civil War Black leader: he established **32 schools for African Americans statewide**, helped found **Lincoln University**, and was appointed by President Ulysses S. Grant in **1871** as the first African American **U.S. Minister to Liberia**. He died from blood poisoning in Ardmore, Oklahoma on November 1, 1915, after a tank-car explosion injury, with large crowds attending his funeral in St. Louis. The park today features a picnic pavilion, modern playground with splash pad and musical instruments, soft safe playground surface, a softball field, and a loop trail winding around the 1.4-acre property.

Today Vandeventer is a neighborhood in active renewal. The **North Sarah Apartments** — 120 mixed-income units developed by McCormack Baron Salazar — anchor a residential revitalization along N. Sarah Street. **Pamoja Preparatory Academy at Cole Elementary School** is the only district school in the neighborhood, providing African-centered education with Swahili language instruction. The **Guardian Angel Settlement Association** provides early childhood education and social services, and the **Advanced Manufacturing Innovation Center (AMICSTL)** — a $60 million facility near the neighborhood’s eastern edge — is bringing aerospace, transportation, and agricultural manufacturing training jobs to the area. After eighth grade, students head a mile north to **Sumner High School** in The Ville. The neighborhood is anchored on its east side by **Firehouse 282** on Vandeventer Avenue — a former fire station now serving cold drinks, live music, and fried buffalo fish filets.

The 2020 Census recorded **2,041 residents**, an 18% gain from 2010’s low of 1,682, reflecting renewed confidence in a neighborhood with an exceptional depth of story. As Vandeventer resident and urban planner Judith Arnold notes, the neighborhood has been on the revitalization path for decades, resulting in a stable community full of personally invested residents who can mobilize quickly: “I can snap my finger and have 100 residents ready to come help clean up.” Vandeventer aspires to serve as a model for neighborhoods to the north still working on redevelopment.

Image: Vandeventer Place — the 1870 Julius Pitzman-designed Gilded Age private street that gave the neighborhood its name, demolished 1947-1950; its 1894 Louis Mullgardt limestone gate now stands in Forest Park near the Jewel Box — Wikimedia Commons, Public Domain

Vandeventer sits on the gently rolling interior plateau of St. Louis — the same high open prairie the French called the *Grande Prairie* — at 38.6500° N, well removed from the Mississippi River’s humidity and temperature extremes. The neighborhood experiences St. Louis’s full humid subtropical range: hot, humid summers with high temperatures regularly reaching 90°F, cold winters with January lows near 24°F, and a notably active spring severe weather season with frequent thunderstorms and real tornado exposure. Peak outdoor activity falls during spring and fall, when the renovated Turner Park playground and splash pad, the loop trail around the 1.4-acre park, and the surrounding North Sarah Apartments green spaces draw the heaviest neighborhood use. Cool seasons are also the busiest pace for ongoing redevelopment work at the Advanced Manufacturing Innovation Center, the Guardian Angel Settlement Association, and the North Spring Empowerment Center. Winter shifts commerce indoors to Firehouse 282 on Vandeventer Avenue, the Pamoja Preparatory Academy, and the chamber and IT consulting offices along the corridor.

Forest Park Statue & Monument

The Vandeventer Place Gates serve as a significant historical landmark located in Forest Park, just outside the Jewel Box. Originally the 1894 east entrance to Vandeventer Place — one of St. Louis’s most luxurious Gilded Age private streets — the limestone gate was designed by architect Louis Mullgardt and salvaged when the enclave was demolished (1947-1950). Visitors often include the gates as part of a walking tour through Forest Park’s “green oasis,” frequently pairing the visit with a trip to the nearby peaceful lily pond or the Korean War Memorial. Reviewers describe the experience as “walking through history” and recommend the site for its serene atmosphere and preservation of local heritage. This landmark is a must-visit for history enthusiasts and those looking for a quiet, scenic spot for photography within the park.
