## Visitation Park — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/visitation-park

Neighborhood

Visitation Park

Coordinates

38.6552° N, 90.2831° W

Ranked by 214+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  5 of 5 verified Visitation Park corridor providers ✓  All businesses verified via Google Business ✓  Avg. rating: 4.3/5 across 214 reviews

## Visitation Park Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Visitation Park, St. Louis

Visitation Park is a compact half-mile-square historic district on St. Louis’s north-central side — anchored by the 1909 Soldan High School, the 1908 Carnegie-funded Cabanne Branch Library, three private places (Windermere, Beverly, Savoy), and Ivory Perry Park (formerly Visitation Park) named for the civil rights activist who led the 1963 Jefferson Bank protests and authored St. Louis’s first lead-paint legislation in 1970. The corridor offers neighborhood retail, HIPAA-compliant telecommunications, regional tourism services, and commercial logistics for residents and businesses across the broader West End and Central West End area.

Visitation Park is a compact residential neighborhood on St. Louis’s north-central side at **38.6552° N, 90.2831° W** in zip code 63112. Its boundaries run north to **Maple Avenue**, south to **Delmar Boulevard**, east to **Union Boulevard**, and west to **Belt Avenue** — a roughly half-mile-square pocket that sits just north of **Forest Park** and west of the DeBaliviere corridor, approximately six miles outside downtown St. Louis. The neighborhood’s internal spine, **Cabanne Avenue**, runs east–west through its core and once served as the primary address for the institutions that gave the area its identity. The quiet residential lanes of **Windermere Place**, **Beverly Place**, and **Savoy Court** — three of the city’s historic private places — add a distinctive character that distinguishes Visitation Park from the broader West End grid. Adjacent neighborhoods include the **West End** to the north, the **Academy** neighborhood to the east, the **Skinker-DeBaliviere** neighborhood to the south, and **Hamilton Heights** to the west.

The land that became Visitation Park was historically part of **Survey 378**, held by early St. Louis figures including **Lucien Cabanne, James Clemens Jr., and Emanuel de Hodiamont** — names that still echo in the neighborhood’s street names and legal descriptions. Much of the area was originally uncultivated meadow land owned by **Dr. J. Sheppard Cabanne**, with Windermere Place developed beginning in 1895 on what had previously been a farm owned by the Thomas Wright family. By the 1870s, the western edge of Union Boulevard north of Delmar was lined with substantial family estates belonging to the **Blossom, Monks, Cabanne, Colman, and Gay** families. Most of the rest of the neighborhood was developed during and just prior to the **1904 World’s Fair**, producing an eclectic mix of architectural styles and an unusual concentration of large institutional buildings primarily located along Union Boulevard — what local historians call the **“Union Avenue Promenade.”**

The neighborhood’s defining moment came in **1892** with the opening of **Visitation Academy**, a French Renaissance chateau-style institution at Cabanne and Belt designed by the prominent firm **Barnett, Haynes & Barnett** (the same firm that designed the Cathedral Basilica of Saint Louis). The Academy of the Visitation had been incorporated in 1858 by Visitation Nuns who arrived in St. Louis from Kaskaskia following the flood of 1844, originally housed on Cass Avenue on land donated by Mrs. Anne Biddle. The 1892 westward move educated daughters of St. Louis society as well as children of South American diplomats whose families were in the city during and after the 1904 World’s Fair. After 70 years, the Academy made another westward move in 1962 to 3020 North Ballas Road in St. Louis County, and the City demolished its French Renaissance chateau to create what was originally **Visitation Park** — the small public park (city ordinance 1961) that gave the neighborhood its name.

In **1989**, the park was renamed **Ivory Perry Park** to honor the civil rights activist who had spent decades fighting for racial justice from this very neighborhood. **Ivory Perry** arrived in St. Louis in 1954, led the landmark **Jefferson Bank protests of 1963** demanding the hiring of Black bank tellers, championed tenant rights throughout the 1960s, and discovered the connection between lead paint and childhood health problems while visiting renters in their homes — spearheading the city’s **first lead-paint legislation in 1970** and becoming a nationally recognized pioneer in what would later be called environmental justice and “environmental racism.” The park today (officially 0.29 acres but the working footprint is closer to 10 acres including surrounding green space) features basketball courts, a softball field, a picnic pavilion, and a playground — bordered by Cabanne Avenue to the north, Belt Avenue to the west, Arlington Avenue to the east, and Clemens Place to the south. The park hosts an annual summer concert series June through August.

The neighborhood’s institutional legacy is remarkable for its scale. **Soldan High School**, opened in 1909 at **918 N. Union Blvd** on the neighborhood’s eastern edge, was celebrated at its founding as the “brightest gem in the city’s public school system.” The school reopened in 1993 as **Soldan International Studies Magnet School**, today featuring six foreign languages, a science-focused magnet curriculum, and strong partnerships with St. Louis University, Washington University, and the University of Missouri-St. Louis. At roughly the same time as Soldan’s opening, **Smith Academy & Manual Training School** (1905, originally built for Washington University) operated as an elite boys’ preparatory school — the male counterpart to Mary Institute — and **St. Philomena’s Technical School** (1910) provided vocational training for young women. The **Cabanne Branch Library**, designed in 1908 by the distinguished firm **Mauran, Russell & Garden** and funded through a **Carnegie grant**, became the second Carnegie library built in St. Louis. The **William Clark Elementary School** (1906) and **Frederick von Harten House** (1896) are additional city landmarks within the district.

Visitation Park’s religious and cultural institutions include the **St. Louis Artists Guild** (1908) and the **Young Men’s Hebrew Association** (1927), which added cultural and civic dimensions that drew residents from across the region. A wave of development starting in 1906 saw the creation of **four separate churches** within the neighborhood: **Pilgrim Congregational Church** (1906), **Union Avenue Christian Church** (1907), the former **Church of the Messiah** (1907), and **Westminster Presbyterian Church** (1913). The neighborhood was further distinguished when **Pilgrim Congregational Church** became, in **1953**, one of the first St. Louis congregations to formally welcome integrated membership, choosing to remain in the area rather than follow other institutions to the western suburbs. The **Visitation Park Historic District**, first designated in 1975 by Alderman C.B. Broussard and expanded in 1987, recognizes this layered architectural and social history.

Among Visitation Park’s most celebrated childhood residents is poet, playwright, and novelist **Ntozake Shange**, whose landmark choreopoem *for colored girls who have considered suicide / when the rainbow is enuf* brought national attention to Black women’s experiences, and whose novel *Betsy Brown* draws directly from her girlhood in St. Louis during the court-ordered school integration era of the 1950s and 1960s. Her life here — in a neighborhood shaped equally by academic excellence, civic activism, and architectural ambition — speaks to the uncommon depth of character that defines Visitation Park. The neighborhood’s demographic trajectory shifted significantly in the 1950s and 1960s as the removal of restrictive covenants in the late 1940s greatly increased housing options for African American families and many white middle-class residents moved out of the city. As of **2020**, Visitation Park’s racial makeup was **77.6% Black, 18.1% White, 0.2% Native American, 1.2% Asian, 2.3% Two or More Races, and 0.7% Some Other Race**, with the neighborhood’s total population well under 1,000 residents.

Today, the neighborhood is experiencing a renewed wave of restoration and investment alongside long-standing community organizations like **WeCollab** that are working on 20-year preservation plans across Visitation Park and the adjacent West End. New businesses like **Deli Divine** on Delmar Boulevard reflect the corridor’s growing momentum, while restoration projects in the **Winston Churchill Apartments** (1927) and 1898-era Foursquares continue the work of preserving Visitation Park’s extraordinary architectural inheritance.

Image: Soldan High School, 918 N. Union Blvd — opened 1909 by architect William B. Ittner, celebrated at its founding as the “brightest gem in the city’s public school system,” reopened as Soldan International Studies Magnet School in 1993 — Wikimedia Commons, CC BY 2.0 by Paul Sableman

Visitation Park sits on gently rolling terrain in the interior of St. Louis’s north-central neighborhoods at 38.6552° N, sheltered from the Mississippi River’s temperature extremes by several miles of dense urban fabric. Summers are warm and humid — typical of the St. Louis continental climate with highs regularly in the upper 80s°F — while winters bring occasional ice and snow with temperatures frequently dipping below freezing. The mature tree canopy along Cabanne Avenue and the private places (Windermere, Beverly, and Savoy) provides meaningful shade in summer and wind buffering in colder months. Peak outdoor activity falls during the spring and fall, when Ivory Perry Park’s playground, basketball courts, and summer concert series draw the heaviest neighborhood use, alongside the adjacent Forest Park (1,300+ acres) to the south. Winter shifts commerce indoors to the Delmar Boulevard retail corridor, the Cabanne Branch Library, and the Soldan International Studies Magnet School. The neighborhood’s pre-1920 brick and stone construction has weathered more than a century of Missouri climate with the durability typical of well-built turn-of-the-century residential and institutional stock.

## Walnut Park East — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/walnut-park-east

Neighborhood

Walnut Park East

Coordinates

38.6980° N, 90.2614° W

Ranked by 75+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  10 of 10 verified Walnut Park East corridor providers ✓  All businesses verified via Google Business ✓  Avg. rating: 4.9/5 across 75 reviews

## Walnut Park East Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Walnut Park East, St. Louis

Walnut Park East is the central neighborhood in the historic Walnut Park corridor of northwest St. Louis — anchored by the 9.6-acre Dwight Davis Park (named for the St. Louis-born founder of the international tennis Davis Cup) and home to both century-old logistics firms and Intramotev, the world’s only company converting freight cars to autonomous battery-electric railcars. The corridor offers contracting, logistics, manufacturing, neighborhood retail, chamber advocacy, and enterprise technology services for residents and businesses across the broader Northwest Side.

Walnut Park East sits in northwest St. Louis at **38.6980° N, 90.2614° W** in zip code 63120, occupying the central position in the cluster of three linked Northwest Side neighborhoods alongside **Walnut Park West** to the northwest and the **Mark Twain** neighborhood to the southeast. Its borders are **West Florissant Avenue** (along the edge of Calvary Cemetery) to the northeast, **Emerson Avenue** to the southeast, **Interstate 70 (I-70)** to the southwest and west, and **Riverview Boulevard** to the northwest. The **Mark Twain/I-70 Industrial** neighborhood sits to the south-southwest. The neighborhood developed as a working-class residential community filled in steadily from the 1890s through the 1950s, with the last small truck farm operating in the 5800 block of Woodland Avenue as late as 1954.

Until the late 1890s the land was a mix of farms and wooded creek bottoms. The arrival of streetcar lines along West Florissant and the extended **Bernays Avenue** (now Union Boulevard) opened the area to development, and people of German stock led the first wave — joined steadily by Polish, Irish, and other European families. The neighborhood’s Catholic character was strong and specific: the **Nativity parish** was founded on Christmas Day **1904**, and later-arriving Polish Catholics petitioned for and established their own congregation at **St. Adalbert’s in 1913** (the former church site at the 5700 block of Woodland has since been redeveloped as four new homes). The 1922 Nativity School structure at 5827 Harney still stands. **St. Matthew Lutheran Church**, with roots in the earliest days of Walnut Park, built its current church (5402 Wren) and first school (5403 Wren) in 1950. Among the neighborhood’s earliest large structures was the **St. Mary’s Home for Girls**, a large copper-domed Catholic orphanage constructed in 1900 on five acres at Emerson and Harvey Avenues, which later became the San Francisco Christian Assembly Multi-Plex Center.

From the beginning, commercial land use was comparatively modest and industrial development was practically nonexistent in Walnut Park East proper. The single greatest concentration of storefronts ran between **Robin and Wren along West Florissant**, originally anchored by a nearby trolley turnaround. A larger number of stores lined **Lilian Avenue**, most clustered from Emerson northwest to Thrush, with smaller grocery stores, taverns, and confectioneries scattered at key intersections throughout. The defining streets of Walnut Park East are West Florissant Avenue (the diagonal northeast boundary and primary commercial corridor, home to the Walnut Park Branch Library); **Riverview Boulevard** (the northwest boundary, one of north St. Louis’s most historic drives); **Lilian Avenue** (the main interior east-west street and historic commercial concentration); **Davison Avenue** (an important residential spine home to Walbridge Elementary School); and **Bircher Boulevard** (the industrial corridor along the southern edge where Intramotev is building autonomous freight-rail technology on the very ground where industrial St. Louis once manufactured and shipped goods).

The neighborhood’s signature public space is **Dwight Davis Park** — 9.6 acres at the heart of the community offering a playground, basketball court, tennis courts, soccer, softball and baseball fields, a multi-purpose athletic court, and a spray pool. The park is named for **Dwight Filley Davis**, the St. Louis-born Harvard graduate who donated the international tennis trophy that bears his name (the Davis Cup), competed in the 1904 St. Louis Olympics, and served as City Parks Commissioner beginning in 1911 — famously removing the “Keep Off the Grass” signs from city parks and building dozens of free public tennis courts. The park that carries his name in Walnut Park East was undeveloped except for ballfields until 1962 and has been a community hub ever since. Smaller **Walnut Park**, located at Lilian and Thrush with two basketball courts and over 2 acres of parkland, rounds out the neighborhood’s recreational offerings. For several years in the 1970s and 1980s, the **Walnut Park Community Center** — a municipal facility — operated in converted buildings at Wren and Lilian (now mostly closed except for a Head Start center).

Educational institutions with deep roots in the neighborhood include **Walnut Park Elementary School** at 5814 Thekla (built 1909) and **Cyrus P. Walbridge Elementary School** at 5000 Davison (1922), both of which replaced older or portable public schools. The local fire house at **5437 Partridge** also dates to 1922. The **Northwest Academy of Law High School**, a college preparatory institution, sits on Riverview Boulevard — opened originally as **Northwest High School** in February 1964 after 25 years of community advocacy, expanded in 1968, and reopened in 1993 as Northwest Accelerated Middle School after a brief closure. The now-historic **Laboure High School** building at 5421 Thekla — opened as an all-girls school in 1948 and later co-ed — became the highly regarded **Cardinal Ritter College Preparatory High School**, one of St. Louis’s most prominent Catholic college-prep institutions before its 2001 relocation to a new midtown campus. The **Walnut Park Branch Library** on West Florissant Avenue, one of 15 branches in the St. Louis Public Library system, anchors learning and community life along the neighborhood’s main commercial edge.

The neighborhood’s industrial layer — modest historically but increasingly important today — is concentrated along the rail corridors near its southern edge. **FW Logistics** on Semple Avenue has operated as a major asset-based warehousing and logistics provider for **32 years**, offering dry, cold, food-grade, and hazmat-certified storage with nationwide coverage. At the leading edge of transportation technology, **Intramotev** on Bircher Boulevard is the only company in the world converting conventional freight cars to **battery-electric, self-propelled autonomous railcars** — a St. Louis startup operating in the heart of north St. Louis that is winning contracts in the mining and intermodal industries globally, supported by the St. Louis Development Corporation’s Business Retention & Expansion program. And **J. D. Streett & Company, Inc.**, headquartered nearby on Park Avenue, has been an industry leader in wholesale fuel, oxygenate, and lubricant distribution for over **100 years**, operating a gasoline terminal that serves the regional supply chain across Missouri and Illinois.

Walnut Park East’s demographic trajectory shifted with the construction of **I-70** (in service from 1961), which took numerous local homes and businesses, sped suburban commutes, and accelerated north St. Louis County’s residential development. By the mid-1960s, the Walnut Park East community was transitioning from a white to an African American population, a process largely completed by the early 1970s. As of **2020**, Walnut Park East’s racial makeup was **93.9% Black, 3.4% Two or More Races, 1.4% White, 0.1% Native American, and 0.6% Some Other Race**, with 1.6% of the population of Hispanic or Latino origin. The neighborhood’s population is approximately **2,593 residents**, with a sparse-suburban feel and most residents renting their homes. While much of the housing stock remains sound — small frame and brick single-family homes built between 1900 and 1930, mostly by German immigrants — some blocks face blight and board-ups, particularly along the Union Boulevard corridor in adjacent Mark Twain.

Like its neighbors to the north and southeast, Walnut Park East has always drawn on a broader network of services. The businesses on this page serve a connected north St. Louis corridor — from neighborhood staples on Lilian Avenue to logistics firms on Semple, tech innovators on Bircher, and community organizations that operate across Ward 13 and beyond. The Walnut Park East corridor combines century-old industrial reliability with cutting-edge transportation technology in the same square miles.

Image: View of Walnut Park East from Walnut Park — showing the predominantly small frame and brick single-family housing stock built between 1900 and 1930 — Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0 by LittleT889

Walnut Park East occupies the north-central tier of the city at 38.6980° N, where St. Louis’s humid continental climate brings hot, humid summers with highs regularly in the upper 80s°F and cold winters with periodic ice and snow. The neighborhood’s relatively flat terrain and open residential streetscapes — punctuated by the bluffs of Calvary Cemetery to the northeast and the rail corridor to the south — mean seasonal conditions arrive with little geographic buffer. Mature street trees along Lilian and Davison provide some summer canopy. Peak outdoor activity falls during spring and fall, when Dwight Davis Park’s 9.6 acres of athletic facilities, the smaller Walnut Park at Lilian and Thrush, and the Walnut Park Branch Library draw the heaviest neighborhood use. Cool seasons also drive intense indoor activity at Intramotev’s Bircher Boulevard facility, FW Logistics’s Semple Avenue warehouses, and the Goodfellow / West Florissant residential corridor. The neighborhood’s early-20th-century brick and frame construction has weathered more than a century of Missouri climate with the durability typical of well-built working-class residential stock.

## Walnut Park West — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/walnut-park-west

Neighborhood

Walnut Park West

Coordinates

38.7057° N, 90.2567° W

Ranked by 3,144+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  10 of 10 verified Walnut Park West corridor providers ✓  All businesses verified via Google Business ✓  Avg. rating: 5.0/5 across 3,144 reviews

## Walnut Park West Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Walnut Park West, St. Louis

Walnut Park West serves as one of the city’s most historically distinctive Northwest Side neighborhoods — platted from the 1888 Walnut Park and Jennings Heights subdivisions on former dairy farms, anchored by 470-acre Calvary Cemetery (one of the great historic landscapes of the Midwest) along its eastern edge, and built almost entirely to between-the-wars suburban standards with wider lots, generous setbacks, and an almost complete absence of corner commercial sites along Goodfellow Boulevard — offering chamber advocacy, EPC engineering, general contracting, community development, transportation services, and cultural-destination support for residents and businesses across the broader Walnut Park / North City corridor.

Walnut Park West sits along the northwestern edge of the City of St. Louis at **38.7057° N, 90.2567° W** in zip code 63136. Its boundaries are **West Florissant Avenue** to the northeast, **Riverview Boulevard** to the southeast, **Interstate 70 (I-70)** to the south, and the **City of St. Louis limits** to the west and northwest, where the neighborhood abuts St. Louis County and the City of **Jennings, Missouri**. (The city limits stay close to **Acme Avenue**, which can be visually traced by the difference between city and county dumpsters and trash carts.) Walnut Park West is the westernmost of three linked neighborhoods north of outbound I-70, alongside **Walnut Park East** and the **Mark Twain** neighborhood to the southeast. Together these three form the historic Walnut Park corridor of northwest St. Louis, named for the walnut and fruit trees that once covered the land in abundance before European settlement. Surrounding neighborhoods include **North Pointe** to the northeast and **Mark Twain/I-70 Industrial** to the south.

The land that became Walnut Park West passed through the hands of some of the most prominent family names in St. Louis history — the **St. Cyr, Clemens, Mullanphy, Shreve, Switzer, and Jennings** families among them. By the 1850s, most of the area was under cultivation or used as dairy pasture. One of the largest farms belonged to **Charles E. Bircher**, who established his homestead on the present Small Arms Plant site in 1849. A typical farm of the era was the **Wipperman Farm**, which covered about eighty acres near the present intersection of **Davison Avenue and West Florissant** — complete with orchards, a pond, alfalfa fields, and a well that served as a welcome refreshment stop for travelers en route to nearby Calvary Cemetery. Platting of these farms into residential subdivisions began in **1888** with the Walnut Park and Jennings Heights developments. The decade after 1900 saw most of the area’s subdividing accomplished — West Walnut Park (between Riverview and Mimika), **Acme Heights** (west of Goodfellow and north of Emma), Bircher’s Subdivision (bounded by Riverview, Theodore, Thrush, and Bircher), Florissant Avenue Hills, Westfield, Union Avenue Heights, and the Strodtman Heights additions all date to this period. Most of the neighborhood’s residential blocks were built after World War I.

Public transit reached the Walnut Park area in **1893**, when the Bellefontaine branch of the Union Depot Railroad Company was extended out Florissant Avenue from Grand to Calvary Avenue — an electric line intended to serve visitors to the cemeteries. The St. Louis and Suburban Railway soon placed in service an extension running north on Union from Forest Park to Florissant Avenue, and the **Florissant Avenue Electric Railway Company** built a line west on Florissant from Calvary to Robin Avenue. About 1911, a locally owned trolley line known as “The Dinky” (so named for its small cars) began operations on Florissant Avenue westward from Robin Avenue to Jennings Station Road, later running a branch on Helen Avenue from Florissant to Lilian. Lack of business forced the Dinky into bankruptcy in 1925. Industrial development in the northwestern portion of St. Louis was primarily driven by the **St. Louis Terminal Railroad** (now the Terminal Railroad Association), which began acquiring right-of-way through the area in 1892 and completed laying track in 1900. This belt line connected the river-corridor railroads in the Hall Street section with lines in St. Louis County, attracting many new industries to the vast tracts of vacant land.

One of Walnut Park West’s most distinctive characteristics is what it deliberately was *not*. Unlike most St. Louis neighborhoods, it was developed entirely to **suburban standards**: single-family brick and frame homes on wider-than-usual lots, with very few duplex, four-family flat, or apartment buildings (less than 10 percent of the residential mix). The area bounded by **Emma Avenue, Mimika Avenue, Lalite Avenue, and the city limits** was built to middle-to-upper-middle-class suburban specifications, with generous setbacks, structural dimensions, and exterior finishes that distinguished it from the denser urban fabric to the southeast. The early German/German-American influence that defined Walnut Park East and Mark Twain was less pronounced here. No public park, public school, library, post office, firehouse, or recreation center was ever established within Walnut Park West’s official boundaries — residents obtained those services from surrounding communities and, partly because of this institutional absence, the neighborhood never developed a clear independent identity. As one historical overview put it: “It adjoined the ‘real’ Walnut Park but was not quite Walnut Park or anything else.” **Goodfellow Boulevard**, a major north-south St. Louis artery, runs through the neighborhood’s heart almost entirely free of commercial development between Lilian and West Florissant — block after block of strictly residential frontage.

The most iconic landmark on Walnut Park West’s doorstep is **Calvary Cemetery**, which directly abuts the neighborhood along West Florissant Avenue and Riverview Boulevard. Founded in **1854 on land purchased from Senator Henry Clay’s estate** (and formally established in 1858 to receive remains from Old Cathedral cemetery and other Catholic burial grounds being moved for city expansion), Calvary covers **470 acres** and holds more than **300,000 graves** — including those of **General William Tecumseh Sherman, Dred Scott, Tennessee Williams, Kate Chopin, René Auguste Chouteau, and Pierre-Jean De Smet**. The cemetery’s northern corner contains the **only remnant tall-grass prairie within the City of St. Louis** — a 25-acre preserve of native species that has been protected in partnership with the Missouri Department of Conservation and the Missouri Botanical Garden since the 1990s. Calvary is one of the great historic landscapes of the Midwest, and it gives Walnut Park West a quiet, green eastern gateway that few urban neighborhoods can claim. Directly to the north of Calvary is **Bellefontaine Cemetery** (1849), forming a continuous historic cemetery district that has shaped the eastern boundary of the entire Walnut Park corridor since the 19th century.

Because Walnut Park West has no public park within its official boundaries, residents have historically drawn on the parks in adjacent neighborhoods. **Walnut Park** (in Walnut Park East at Lillian and Thrush) offers two basketball courts and over 2 acres of parkland; **Dwight Davis Park** (now also known as **Gregory J. Carter Park**, established 1951) in Walnut Park East provides 9.6 acres with baseball, softball, and soccer fields, tennis courts, a spray pool, a multipurpose court, and a playground; and **Vivian Astra Park** in adjacent North Pointe provides additional “greenspace” parkland. The **Walnut Park Branch Library** — one of the 15 branches in the St. Louis Public Library system — is located on West Florissant Avenue serving the broader Walnut Park corridor. The St. Louis Public School District serves the neighborhood; while no district schools are within Walnut Park West boundaries, **Herzog Elementary** (Pamplin Place) and **Nance Elementary** (Riverview Blvd) in North Pointe, the **Northwest Academy of Law** college-preparatory high school, and **Walbridge Elementary School** in Walnut Park East all serve area residents. **Cardinal Ritter Preparatory High School** (originally the all-girls Laboure High School, opened 1948 at 5421 Thekla) operated in the area until its 2001 relocation to a new midtown campus.

Walnut Park West’s demographic trajectory followed the familiar Northwest Side pattern but with unique acceleration. The neighborhood’s first African American family moved to the **5100 block of Goodfellow in 1963**; by the mid-1970s the racial transition was largely complete. The construction of **I-70** (in service from 1961) demolished approximately 200 homes and five businesses along its right-of-way and effectively cut Walnut Park West off from the rest of the city to the south. By 2000, the neighborhood was 98% Black with 4,235 residents (down 18% from 1990), and by 2010 the population had dropped to **3,349 residents** with a racial makeup of **95.1% Black, 0.8% White, 0.1% Asian, 0.2% American Indian, 3.4% Two or More Races, and 0.4% Some Other Race**. The 2020 Census continued the trend with an additional 24% population loss. Despite the depopulation pressure, the neighborhood maintains one of the strongest **72% owner-occupancy rates** on the North Side, reflecting both the suburban character of the original development and the long-term commitment of generations of homeowners to the corridor.

In recent decades the neighborhood has shown more stability than some neighboring communities, supported by active organizations including the **Heartland St. Louis Black Chamber of Commerce**, the **East-West Gateway Council of Governments (EWGCOG) Great Streets Initiative**, the City’s ongoing **Mark Twain & Walnut Park neighborhood plan** adoption process, and various community development partnerships committed to stabilizing housing and improving commercial corridors. Because Walnut Park West has always obtained its services from beyond its official boundaries, the businesses listed on this page reflect the broader north St. Louis service area — from community organizations anchored in the Walnut Park corridor and Mark Twain to professional services, contractors, and cultural destinations that residents and business owners throughout the corridor regularly use.

Image: Walnut Park West houses near Goodfellow Boulevard and Emma Avenue — built to between-the-wars suburban standards with wider lots and generous setbacks, distinct from the denser urban fabric to the southeast — Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0 by LittleT889

Walnut Park West sits at the far northwestern edge of the city at 38.7057° N — among the highest-latitude neighborhoods in St. Louis — where the flat terrain meets the city line at Jennings and the gentle bluffs of Calvary Cemetery rise along Riverview Boulevard. The neighborhood shares St. Louis’s humid continental climate, with hot and humid summers regularly reaching the upper 80s°F and cold winters with periodic snow and ice. Its position near the airport flight path and open, tree-sparse streetscapes along West Florissant and Goodfellow mean residents feel the full seasonal extremes of the north city with limited natural windbreak. Peak outdoor activity falls during the spring and fall, when the Calvary Cemetery tall-grass prairie, the Walnut Park Branch Library, Dwight Davis / Gregory J. Carter Park, and the Vivian Astra Park greenspace in adjacent North Pointe draw the heaviest neighborhood use. Winter shifts commerce indoors to the Goodfellow Boulevard commercial corridor, West Florissant retail, the Walnut Park Branch Library, and the cultural destinations downtown. The neighborhood’s post-World War I brick and frame construction has weathered more than a century of Missouri climate with the durability typical of well-built between-the-wars residential stock.

## Wells Goodfellow — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/wells-goodfellow

Neighborhood

Wells Goodfellow

Coordinates

38.6774° N, 90.2707° W

Ranked by 2,133+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  10 of 10 verified Wells Goodfellow corridor providers ✓  All businesses verified via Google Business ✓  Avg. rating: 4.5/5 across 2,133 reviews

## Wells Goodfellow Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Wells Goodfellow, St. Louis

Wells Goodfellow serves as one of the city’s most historically layered Northwest Side neighborhoods — rooted in John W. Burd’s 1868 Arlington Grove subdivision, anchored by the 13-acre Barrett Brothers Park, the William B. Ittner-designed 1914 Laclede Elementary School, the former GM Corvette assembly plant (now Union-70 Center Industrial Park) on its northern edge, and the 112-unit Arlington Grove mixed-income redevelopment around the historic NRHP-listed Arlington School — offering minority chamber advocacy, youth mentorship, soul food and catering, tax and financial services, neighborhood retail, manufacturing employment, and community grocery for residents and businesses across the Arlington/Northwest corridor.

Wells Goodfellow sits along the northwestern edge of the City of St. Louis at **38.6774° N, 90.2707° W** in zip code 63120. Its boundaries form a distinctive quadrilateral: the **City of St. Louis limits** on the northwest, **Natural Bridge Avenue** on the northeast, **Union Boulevard** on the southeast, and **Dr. Martin Luther King Drive** (formerly Easton Avenue) on the southwest. The neighborhood is bordered by the **Mark Twain/I-70 Industrial** neighborhood to the north, **Hamilton Heights** to the southwest, **Academy** to the south, and **Kingsway West** to the southeast. Across the western city line lie the St. Louis County municipalities of **Hillsdale**, **Wellston**, and **Pine Lawn**. The neighborhood’s name combines two of its most prominent street and corridor references: **Goodfellow Boulevard**, the major north-south commercial and residential spine that runs through the heart of the neighborhood, and a tribute to **Erastus Wells**, the 19th-century St. Louis streetcar magnate. Interestingly, “Goodfellow” also happens to be the maiden name of Eliza Goodfellow, wife of pioneer subdivider William Burd (1818–1885), who platted the original 1868 Arlington Grove subdivision that gave the broader area its historic identity.

Wells Goodfellow is part of the historic **Arlington** section of north St. Louis — an early-19th-century district whose name comes from **John W. Burd’s Arlington Grove subdivision of 1868**. The neighborhood’s roots run deeper still: portions of the land predate the Civil War, when the area transitioned from open farmland to one of St. Louis’s most productive brick-making districts. **More subdivisions were built in the mid-1880s**, with residential construction continuing through 1910; the last residential subdivisions opened by the mid-1920s. The neighborhood’s most distinctive housing pattern is its mix of **brick structures and wood-frame homes** — the wood-frame stock now increasingly rare across the city, making Wells Goodfellow one of the most architecturally distinctive working-class neighborhoods on the North Side. Single-family dwellings in the **Dutch and shotgun styles**, alongside two-family and four-family flats on the major streets, define the neighborhood’s residential fabric, with **57.6% of the residential real estate built no later than 1939**. Before World War II, Wells Goodfellow was considered a particularly attractive community, home to a thriving mix of Catholic, Jewish, and other families drawn to its quiet residential streets and strong schools.

A defining piece of Wells Goodfellow’s 20th-century history sits at the neighborhood’s northern edge: the former **General Motors Corvette assembly plant** at Union Boulevard and Natural Bridge Avenue, which manufactured the iconic American sports car from **1954 to 1981** before closing due to environmental compliance issues. The site is now the **Union-70 Center Industrial Park**, which continues to anchor employment and logistics activity for the broader north-central corridor. The plant’s 1981 closure — combined with the broader post-war suburban exodus and the early-1970s relocation of public-housing residents following the demolition of Pruitt-Igoe — reshaped Wells Goodfellow significantly. Manufacturing tradition persists through firms still operating along **Semple Avenue**, including **Triad Manufacturing Inc.**, a precision custom-fixture builder serving national retail brands. The neighborhood’s key streets, beyond Goodfellow Boulevard, include **Natural Bridge Avenue** (the major east-west northern corridor), **Dr. Martin Luther King Drive** (formerly Easton Avenue, the major southwest corridor), **Semple Avenue** (the industrial backbone running through the midsection), and **Union Boulevard** (the eastern boundary).

The neighborhood was the site of one of the most tragic disasters in St. Louis history. On **October 4, 1916**, the **Christian Brothers College** building at the corner of North Kingshighway and Easton Avenue (now Dr. Martin Luther King Drive) was destroyed by fire — one of the worst fires in the City’s history, taking **10 lives**. The site has since been redeveloped, but the disaster remains a touchstone for the broader Arlington-area heritage. Wells Goodfellow’s school history includes some of the most architecturally significant Public Schools structures in the city: **Laclede Elementary School** at **5821 Kennerly Avenue**, designed by the renowned St. Louis Public Schools architect **William B. Ittner** in 1914 (Ittner is the same architect responsible for several of the most beloved school buildings in the city); **Langston Middle School** at 5511 Wabada, a notable mid-century modern structure; and the former **Gundlach School** at 2931 Arlington, designed by architect George Sanger in 1931.

The neighborhood’s religious institutions remain among its most important community anchors. **Friendly Temple Missionary Baptist Church** has served as a key partner in major neighborhood redevelopment efforts and was a leading institutional partner on the Arlington Grove project. **Mount Pleasant Baptist Church**, **New Bible Days-United Church of God in Christ**, and a large **Islamic Center** round out the religious landscape. The historic **Arlington Elementary School** on Burd Avenue — the only building in Wells Goodfellow listed on the **National Register of Historic Places** — was rescued from abandonment and converted to housing as part of the **$41 million, 112-unit Arlington Grove mixed-income redevelopment** completed in 2013. Spearheaded by developer **McCormack Baron Salazar (MBS)** in partnership with the **St. Louis Housing Authority**, **KAI Design & Build**, and **Friendly Temple Missionary Baptist Church**, Arlington Grove is one of the first **Enterprise Green Communities-certified projects in Missouri** and a sister project to North Sarah. Healthcare access is served by the **St. Louis Comprehensive Health Center** and **Mound City Medical Center**, both serving Wells Goodfellow residents.

The primary public green space in Wells Goodfellow is **Barrett Brothers Park** at the corner of **St. Louis Avenue and Goodfellow Boulevard** — a **13.16-acre park established in 1947** featuring a picnic pavilion, playground, and athletic fields for baseball, softball, and soccer. Nearby residents also access **Hamilton Heights Park** at Clara Avenue and Wells Avenue (with a Cardinal Care baseball field, softball field, playground, and multi-purpose field) and the **22-acre Sherman Park** in adjacent Kingsway West, which includes the **David P. Wohl Community and Recreation Center** with an indoor swimming pool, basketball and tennis courts, a football field, and playgrounds. The **Cabanne Branch Library** on Union Boulevard (one of 15 St. Louis Public Library branches) is conveniently located just across the Academy neighborhood line, and **Soldan High School** — the International Studies Magnet School with six foreign languages — is in nearby Academy. Since 2016, the **Green City Coalition’s Urban Greening Program**, funded by the EPA Region 7, has transformed long-vacant lots in Wells Goodfellow into restored green-space properties, supported by a $322,310 grant from the Robert J. Trulaske Jr. Family Foundation and a $50,000 grant from the National Fish & Wildlife Foundation. First demolitions and installations began in March 2017 and Spring 2019.

Wells Goodfellow’s 20th-century demographic trajectory followed a familiar but particularly intense north-city pattern. Before World War II, the neighborhood was a stable, mixed Catholic, Jewish, and Protestant community. By the 2000 Census, the neighborhood was 98% Black and 1% White, with 8,193 residents (down 29% from 1990). The neighborhood lost another 28% of its population during 2000–2010. By 2020, Wells Goodfellow had one of the highest residential vacancy rates in the entire United States — **39.5% (higher than 97.6% of U.S. neighborhoods)** — and **49.8% of households did not own a car** (higher than 98.7% of U.S. neighborhoods), reflecting both economic challenges and the strong walkable urban fabric the neighborhood retains. The current median home price of $74,185 makes Wells Goodfellow one of the most affordable historic neighborhoods in Missouri. The City has since partnered with the **St. Louis Blight Authority** on targeted blight-elimination zones to reclaim and stabilize occupied blocks. The neighborhood remains one of the most architecturally significant pre-1939 residential corridors on the North Side, with active revitalization through Arlington Grove, the Green City Coalition, ongoing municipal investment, and the steady work of its religious institutions and community organizations.

The businesses on this page reflect the full range of Wells Goodfellow’s economic ecosystem — from the neighborhood staples on Goodfellow Boulevard and Dr. Martin Luther King Drive to the manufacturing employers on Semple Avenue and the nonprofit chambers, foundations, and faith-based organizations that form the backbone of community life. Several listings serve the broader north St. Louis corridor, consistent with how residents here have always lived: drawing on a connected network of services across neighboring Hamilton Heights, Academy, Kingsway West, Mark Twain/I-70 Industrial, and the adjacent St. Louis County suburbs of Hillsdale, Wellston, and Pine Lawn.

Image: Barrett Brothers Park at Goodfellow Boulevard and St. Louis Avenue — the 13.16-acre Wells Goodfellow city park established in 1947, featuring a picnic pavilion, playground, and athletic fields — Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0 by LittleT889

Wells Goodfellow sits in the northwestern quadrant of St. Louis at 38.6774° N, one of the city’s higher-latitude neighborhoods, where the flat terrain transitions toward the St. Louis County line at Pine Lawn and Wellston. The neighborhood shares St. Louis’s humid continental climate — humid summers with high temperatures regularly in the upper 80s°F and cold winters with periodic snow and ice — but its open, largely treeless streetscapes along Natural Bridge and Goodfellow Boulevard mean residents feel the full force of summer heat and winter wind with little canopy buffer. Peak outdoor activity falls during the spring and fall, when Barrett Brothers Park’s playground and athletic fields, the Hamilton Heights Park Cardinal Care baseball field, and the Green City Coalition’s restored green-space properties draw the heaviest neighborhood and visitor use. Cool seasons are also the busiest pace for ongoing rehabilitation of the neighborhood’s pre-1939 housing stock and the Green City Coalition’s Urban Greening Program work. Winter shifts commerce indoors to the Price Chopper on Goodfellow Boulevard, the St. Louis Comprehensive Health Center, Mound City Medical Center, Mom’s Soul Food Kitchen, and the Sherman Park David P. Wohl Community and Recreation Center indoor swimming pool. The neighborhood’s historic brick and wood-frame construction has weathered more than a century of Missouri climate, with continued investment from the Arlington Grove redevelopment, Friendly Temple Missionary Baptist Church, and the Green City Coalition.

## West End — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/west-end

Coordinates

38.6620° N, 90.2971° W

Ranked by 792+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  8 of 8 verified West End corridor providers ✓  All businesses verified via Google Business ✓  Avg. rating: 4.8/5 across 792 reviews

## West End Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 West End, St. Louis

The West End serves as one of the city’s most architecturally distinguished North Side neighborhoods — built on the original Cabanné Spanish land grants, anchored by the 1829 Emmanuel DeHodiamont House (one of the two oldest surviving structures in St. Louis), the West Cabanne Place NRHP Historic District, the 1927 Winston Churchill Apartments, and the Ruth Porter Mall greenway — offering biotech incubation, digital marketing, custom signage, century-old hardware, historic apartment living, entrepreneurship education, and community park amenities along the central corridor that connects the West End to Cortex Innovation District and the Central West End.

The West End sits along the northwestern edge of the City of St. Louis at **38.6620° N, 90.2971° W** in zip code 63112. Its boundaries are defined by **Page Boulevard** on the north, **Delmar Boulevard** on the south, **Belt Avenue** and **Union Boulevard via Maple Avenue** on the east, and the **City of St. Louis limits** on the west, where the neighborhood meets the suburbs of **Wellston** and **University City**. The West End is bordered by **Hamilton Heights** to the north, **Visitation Park** and **Academy** to the east, and **Skinker DeBaliviere** and **DeBaliviere Place** to the south. **Delmar Boulevard** at the southern boundary is the widely acknowledged dividing line between north and south St. Louis — placing the West End firmly on the North Side, with all the historical complexity that distinction carries in St. Louis. Despite the similar names, the West End is a separate neighborhood from the **Central West End**, which sits south of Delmar near Forest Park and contains the Cathedral Basilica, Barnes-Jewish Hospital, and the surrounding institutions. The two neighborhoods, however, function as one connected corridor along the central spine of the city.

The neighborhood’s name comes from its origins in the broader 19th-century community of **Cabanné**, which itself was carved out of **three old Spanish land grants**. The West End is the largest segment of that historic Cabanné community, which also encompassed what are today the **Skinker DeBaliviere**, **DeBaliviere Place**, **Visitation Park**, and **Hamilton Heights** neighborhoods. **Jean Pierre Cabanné**, a French-Creole fur trader who came to St. Louis in 1806, held the land for the entire family of neighborhoods. His descendants retained the holdings until the 1870s, when the family began to subdivide the property for residential development. **West Cabanne Place** — the neighborhood’s most distinguished private street — was platted in 1888 and is now listed on the **National Register of Historic Places** (designated 1980) as the West Cabanne Place District. It features several “Shingle Style” houses, a then-new American architectural style of the late 1800s. **Theodore C. Link**, the famed architect of St. Louis Union Station, built a Shingle Style home for his own family on West Cabanne (since razed); other prominent St. Louis architects also built personal residences on the street. Most of the neighborhood’s existing housing stock was constructed between 1890 and 1920, with development continuing through the 1920s.

The West End’s most remarkable historic landmark is the **Emmanuel DeHodiamont House** on Maple Place — a stone Gothic Revival house built for Emmanuel de Hodiamont in **1829** and designated a St. Louis City Landmark. The DeHodiamont House is one of the **two oldest surviving structures in the entire City of St. Louis**, predating the Civil War, the Gateway Arch, and even the city’s incorporation as a major commercial center. The neighborhood’s architectural identity expanded dramatically during the 1920s, when developers built large luxury apartment buildings on what had been the large single-family lots of the late-19th-century mansion blocks. The **Winston Churchill Apartments** at the northwest corner of **Cabanne and Belt**, built in 1927, is one of the largest and most distinctive of those apartment buildings, offering middle-class St. Louisans the opportunity to live in a previously exclusive part of the city. Other private streets in the neighborhood include **Thornby Place** (notable for its concentration of preserved Victorian-era homes and contemporary infill), **Oakley Place**, and **Oakherst Place**.

The West End’s 20th-century history followed a pattern familiar across many inner-ring St. Louis neighborhoods, but with particular intensity. In 1950, the neighborhood was 98.2% white. By 1960, after a wave of **blockbusting** by speculative real-estate investors that systematically destabilized the local market, the neighborhood was only 30% white. Across the larger central St. Louis area, an estimated 57,450 white families left and 56,150 Black families moved in during this period, with most of the transition occurring in the final three years before 1960. As the 1960s progressed, middle-class Black families who had moved into the area also began to leave as the housing stock deteriorated. Landlords found that local rents could not cover operating costs, triggering widespread “milking” (the practice of collecting rent without performing maintenance) and disinvestment. After the **Pruitt-Igoe** housing project was emptied and demolished beginning in 1972, many of its former residents were relocated to the West End. By the early 1990s, many of the large abandoned apartment buildings on Cabanne and adjoining streets had deteriorated to the point where roofs, floors, and interiors had collapsed into the basements, leaving open masonry shells. These shells were eventually demolished and new single-family homes began to rise on the cleared parcels. By 2020, West End residents were **71.5% Black, 10.5% White, 6.8% Asian, 5.4% Two or More Races, and 5.4% Some Other Race** — reflecting both ongoing diversity gains and the slow return of investment to North Side neighborhoods south of Page Boulevard.

The neighborhood’s park system spans five city parks providing over 20 acres of green space. The **Ruth Porter Mall** — an 8-acre linear greenway running along Delmar Boulevard from DeBaliviere to Etzel Avenue — is the neighborhood’s most distinctive open space, named for the late civic leader and one of the most-used public spaces in north-central St. Louis. **Amherst Park** at Hodiamont and Julian, **Gwen B. Giles Park** bounded by Hodiamont, Catalpa, and Maple, **Frank Williamson Sr. Park** (Parkland Park, 2.35 acres, established 1968) at Hamilton and Maple, **Russell Park** at Cabanne and Goodfellow, and **Greg Freeman Park** (0.85 acres, established 1980) round out the neighborhood’s park network, with playgrounds at all locations plus basketball and volleyball courts, a softball field, a multipurpose field, horseshoe pits, and a picnic pavilion. The world-renowned **Forest Park** is just half a mile south of the West End’s southern boundary at Delmar — a straight shot south down DeBaliviere — bringing the Saint Louis Zoo, Saint Louis Art Museum, Missouri History Museum, Saint Louis Science Center, The Muny, and the Forest Park Golf Course within an easy walk or short drive.

Although the West End sits on the North Side, its immediate proximity to the central corridor — with the **Delmar Loop** retail and entertainment district to the south, the **Cortex Innovation District** a short drive southeast, and the **Central West End** medical and cultural campus just below Forest Park — means many of the businesses that serve West End residents and professionals operate along this connected corridor. The **Cortex Innovation District** — a 200-acre purpose-built innovation campus anchored along Duncan Avenue between Forest Park Southeast and the Central West End — is the defining economic engine of the broader central corridor. A collaboration between Washington University, BJC HealthCare, the University of Missouri-St. Louis, Saint Louis University, and the Missouri Botanical Garden, Cortex has generated more than $2.1 billion in economic output and houses biotech startups, researchers, and global companies in flexible lab and office spaces. The **Cortex MetroLink Station** connects the district to the broader regional transit network, making it one of the most transit-accessible innovation zones in the Midwest. The **Cathedral Basilica of Saint Louis** on Lindell Boulevard (in the Central West End), with the second-largest collection of mosaics in the world, and the **World Chess Hall of Fame** on Euclid Avenue are among the most distinctive cultural landmarks of the corridor. Literary figures **Tennessee Williams, T.S. Eliot, Kate Chopin, and William S. Burroughs** all had roots in the Central West End just south of the West End boundary.

The neighborhood is served by the St. Louis Public School District; while no district schools sit within West End boundaries themselves, **Hamilton Elementary School**, **Yeatman-Liddell Preparatory Middle School**, and the magnet **Collegiate School of Medicine and Bioscience** on Des Peres Avenue all serve the area from surrounding neighborhoods. The West End has been part of St. Louis’s **Continued Rise / Sustained Reversal** Central Corridor population trend identified in the 2020 Census analysis, with new construction, rehabilitation of historic apartment buildings, and selective revitalization concentrated in the southern and western parts of the neighborhood. As longtime resident and local broker Keaira Caldwell-Anderson described it: “We call it the W.E. — because WE do things for each other.” That community ethos, combined with the neighborhood’s remarkable architectural inventory and central-corridor location, makes the West End one of the most distinctive and historically significant neighborhoods on the North Side of St. Louis.

Image: Historic mansions on Cabanne Avenue with their distinctive large setbacks — the central residential corridor of the West End, platted from the original Cabanné Spanish land grants and developed between 1890 and 1920 — Wikimedia Commons, CC BY 2.0 by Paul Sableman

The West End occupies the north-central portion of the city at 38.6620° N, where St. Louis’s characteristic humid continental climate delivers hot, humid summers in the upper 80s°F and cold winters with occasional ice storms — a pattern shared across the central urban core. Sitting on relatively flat terrain between Delmar Boulevard and Page Boulevard, the neighborhood benefits from the urban canopy of its older tree-lined streets, which moderate temperatures along residential blocks like Cabanne, Enright, and Thornby Place while the commercial corridors along Delmar and Goodfellow run more openly exposed. Peak outdoor activity falls during the spring and fall, when the Ruth Porter Mall greenway, Frank Williamson Sr. Park, the West Cabanne Place private street, and the Forest Park trail system half a mile south draw the heaviest neighborhood and visitor use. Cool seasons are also the busiest pace for ongoing rehabilitation work on the neighborhood’s 1890–1920 housing stock and the Winston Churchill Apartments. Winter shifts commerce indoors to Gallery CWE and the Central West End and Cortex coworking centers a short drive south. The neighborhood’s historic brick, stone, and Shingle Style construction has weathered more than a century of Missouri climate with remarkable durability.

## Wydown Skinker — St. Louis Neighborhood
Source: https://stlouismissourinearme.com/st-louis-neighborhoods/wydown-skinker

Ranked by 319+ Google reviews. Updated May 2026.

✓  10 of 10 verified Wydown Skinker corridor providers ✓  All businesses verified via Google Business ✓  Avg. rating: 5.0/5 across 319 reviews

## Wydown Skinker Businesses & Neighborhood Guide | St Louis Near Me Directory

 Wydown Skinker, St. Louis

Wydown Skinker serves as the city’s smallest and most internationally connected far-west neighborhood — built entirely on the grounds of the 1904 World’s Fair, anchored by Forest Park, Washington University, the National Register Wiltshire and Versailles Apartments, the world’s largest Amoco sign at Clayton and Skinker, and the seamless transition into Clayton’s legal and financial district — offering coworking and flexible offices, boutique grocers, wellness and psychotherapy, industrial engineering, university-adjacent professional services, and historic-neighborhood community amenities for residents and businesses across the west-central St. Louis corridor.

Wydown Skinker sits along the western edge of the City of St. Louis at **38.6465° N, 90.3065° W** in zip code 63105. Its boundaries form a compact strip: **Forsyth Boulevard** to the north, **Clayton Road** to the south, **Skinker Boulevard** to the east (along Forest Park’s western flank), and the **City of St. Louis limits** to the west — the city extends roughly 600 feet west of Skinker, with the city/county dividing line famously visible through the difference in pavement materials and trash carts. The neighborhood’s defining streets are **Skinker Boulevard** (the high-rise corridor along Forest Park), **Wydown Boulevard** (the tree-lined residential spine), **Forsyth Boulevard** at the north, and **Clayton Road** at the south. Wydown Skinker is St. Louis’s smallest neighborhood by area — less than a half square mile — and one of its most distinctive: bordered by **Skinker DeBaliviere** to the north, **Hi-Pointe** to the south, the world-renowned **Forest Park** directly across Skinker, and the City of Clayton and St. Louis County to the west. The neighborhood seamlessly transitions into Clayton, one of Missouri’s most prominent legal, financial, and business centers and the seat of St. Louis County government.

The entire neighborhood was once part of the grounds of the **1904 Louisiana Purchase Exposition** — St. Louis’s legendary World’s Fair — which transformed Forest Park and the surrounding land into a 1,272-acre temporary city that drew 19.7 million visitors over its seven-month run. A large **Ferris wheel** stood near what is now Clayton Road and Skinker Boulevard, parts of which have been excavated in recent archaeological explorations. The complex that housed the **Filipino Igorot tribes** — brought from the Philippines and presented to fairgoers as “representatives of a new American colony” in one of the Fair’s most troubling exhibits — was also located within the modern Wydown Skinker footprint. **Washington University in St. Louis** relocated to its current Forsyth campus in 1905, immediately after the Fair closed; several Wash U buildings including **Brookings Hall** and **Bixby Hall** were used as Fair facilities. **Francis Olympic Field** at Washington University was the main venue for the **1904 Summer Olympics**, which were held in conjunction with the Fair. The 1904 site of the Fair fundamentally created the modern street grid, lot pattern, and architectural identity of Wydown Skinker.

Most of Wydown Skinker’s buildings rose during the 1920s, as the City of Clayton developed and middle- and upper-middle-class buyers sought tree-lined residential blocks adjacent to Forest Park and the new university campus. The first homes in **Hillcrest** (the northern sub-area) were built before World War I, followed by post-World War I construction in **Hi-Pointe** and **Ellenwood** (the southern sub-areas), and the three-story walk-up apartments of **DeMun Park** through the 1920s. The first **high-rise apartment buildings on Skinker Boulevard** appeared around 1929. The architectural style is dominated by **colonial, Tudor, and Craftsman homes with red-brick exteriors and terracotta-tile roofs**, with the high-rises offering coveted Forest Park views from upper floors. Two of these buildings on Skinker are listed on the **National Register of Historic Places**: the **Wiltshire Apartment Building** and the **Versailles Apartment Building**, both built during a mid-1920s frenzy of luxury apartment construction. The National Register also designated the **Wydown/Forsyth Historic District** — bounded by Skinker, Forsyth, University Lane, and Farquier Boulevard — encompassing 180+ homes and buildings, with a small portion within the city limits and the majority in the county.

The neighborhood’s most recognizable landmark is also one of the most beloved roadside icons in the entire Midwest: the **giant Amoco sign at the intersection of Clayton Road, Skinker Boulevard, McCausland Avenue, and Interstate 64/U.S. Highway 40**. Standing near the highest point of the city since the 1930s, this is the **largest Amoco sign ever built**, both before and after the company’s merger with British Petroleum — visible up to two miles away on the interstate, and kept aloft by BP specifically to maintain trademark rights to the Amoco and Standard brand names. The sign sits on the boundary between Wydown Skinker, Hi-Pointe, and the Cheshire Inn complex. The historic **Cheshire Inn** — with its iconic restaurant, bar, and themed “fantasy rooms” given new exposure in the 2009 Oscar-nominated film “Up in the Air” (which used the restaurant for filming) — is partially in Wydown Skinker, with the restaurant and bar inside the city and the hotel rooms across the line in Richmond Heights.

Public green space is dominated by **Forest Park** directly across Skinker Boulevard — 1,300+ acres that rank among the largest urban parks in the United States, drawing approximately **13 million visitors annually**. The park is home to the **Saint Louis Zoo** (free admission, top-ranked among U.S. zoos), the **Missouri History Museum**, the **Saint Louis Art Museum** (the Cass Gilbert-designed Beaux-Arts building, also free), the **Saint Louis Science Center**, **The Muny** (the oldest and largest outdoor musical theatre in America, since 1919), the **Dwight Davis Tennis Center**, the **Norman K. Probstein Golf Course** (three nine-hole courses), the **1904 World’s Fair Pavilion**, miles of walking, jogging, in-line skating, and cycling trails along the park’s perimeter, and the **Mildred Lane Kemper Art Museum** on the adjacent Washington University campus. Within Wydown Skinker itself, residents access the **Charing Cross Branch Library** on North Skinker Boulevard (one of 15 branches in the St. Louis Public Library system) and the **Missouri Historical Society Library & Research Center**, which occupies a magnificent building originally constructed as the **United Hebrew Congregational Temple between 1926 and 1927**. **DeMun Park**, just across the Clayton line at 810 De Mun Avenue, serves as the neighborhood’s effective center for outdoor family recreation.

The neighborhood’s religious and cultural landmarks reflect its early-20th-century building boom. The **Gothic Revival stone Memorial Presbyterian Church** on Skinker Boulevard, the **Eighth Church of Christ, Scientist** at Wydown and Skinker (an Italian Gothic brick building on Alexander), and the **Vedanta Society of St. Louis** (a Hindu temple) all anchor different corners of the neighborhood’s spiritual life. The **Dorchester Apartments** on Skinker, where Cardinals Hall of Fame outfielder **Lou Brock** once lived during his playing years, is one of the most storied addresses on the corridor. Although Wydown Skinker itself contains no public schools, residents have access to some of the highest-rated schools in Missouri immediately across the Clayton line: **Ralph M. Captain Elementary School** (Niche A+, 8:1 student-teacher ratio), **Wydown Middle School** (Niche A+, 9:1), and **Clayton High School** (Niche A+, 10:1). **The Wilson School** private school (Niche A+, 7:1) is nearby. The neighborhood is exceptionally educated: **86.8% of residents hold a college degree**, the median age is 44, and the average household income exceeds $134,000.

Washington University in St. Louis, immediately to the north on Forsyth, is a constant influence — drawing faculty, researchers, graduate students, and university affiliates into the local housing market. The neighborhood is notably international in character: **14% of residents were born outside the United States**, with only 37% born in Missouri. Average home sale prices peaked near $700,000 in 2019; condo prices in the iconic Versailles and Wiltshire buildings on Skinker today range from the low $250s to over $420,000. The neighborhood maintains a **54%/46% owner/renter split** as of the most recent census data, and roughly **100% of K-12 children in Wydown Skinker attend private schools**, reflecting both the area’s exceptional family wealth and the strength of the surrounding private-school options. The 2000 Census recorded 1,056 residents; the 2010 Census showed virtually no change at all (a loss of only two people) with slight diversity gains (86% White, 7% Asian, 3% Black, 3% Hispanic/Latino). The neighborhood remains one of the most stable and prosperous corners of the City of St. Louis.

Because Wydown Skinker is so geographically compact, many of the businesses that serve its residents and professionals are located just across the boundary in Clayton, Richmond Heights, and the DeMun district — within easy walking or short driving distance. The listings on this page reflect that practical reality, drawing from the broader service area that Wydown Skinker residents and professionals actually use every day, with coworking centers, professional services, wellness providers, boutique grocers, and the neighborhood’s adjacent green spaces all represented in their natural geographic relationship to the corridor.

Image: Wydown Skinker neighborhood streetscape — the city’s smallest neighborhood by area, built on the grounds of the 1904 Louisiana Purchase Exposition (the World’s Fair) — Wikimedia Commons / CC BY 3.0 by Onegentlemanofverona

Wydown Skinker sits at the southwestern edge of the city near the highest point of St. Louis, where the city meets Clayton and the climate follows a classic Midwest four-season pattern. Hot, humid summers with highs regularly reaching the upper 80s°F bring peak outdoor activity to Forest Park’s 1,300 acres, the Saint Louis Zoo, The Muny’s open-air summer season, and the patios along DeMun Avenue and Wydown Boulevard. Cool springs and falls are the busiest seasons for the tree-lined streetscapes along Wydown Boulevard, Saint Louis Art Museum exhibitions, and the Forest Park trail system. 

Cold winters with periodic snow and ice shift commerce indoors to the high-rise lobbies along Skinker, the Charing Cross Branch Library, Box Hill Grocer, and the coworking centers in adjacent Clayton. The neighborhood’s position directly east of the Skinker Boulevard ridge and adjacent to Forest Park gives it a slightly more sheltered, parkside microclimate compared to more exposed north and south St. Louis corridors. The Cass Gilbert-designed brick and limestone construction of the Wiltshire and Versailles apartments, Memorial Presbyterian Church, and Eighth Church of Christ, Scientist has weathered nearly a century of Missouri climate with remarkable durability.
